topics.title: Properties of Fluid: Specific Gravity, Specific Weight, Specific Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fluid: Specific Gravity, Specific Weight, Specific Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Fluid Properties

Fluid mechanics rests upon a small set of **intensive properties** that characterize any fluid parcel regardless of how much fluid we sample. Before we can apply Newton's laws, Bernoulli's equation, or any momentum balance, we must first quantify how "heavy" a fluid is, how much space its mass occupies, and how its heaviness compares against a universal benchmark (water at **4 °C**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** *"Properties of fluids – density, specific gravity, specific weight, specific volume, viscosity, vapour pressure, compressibility and bulk modulus."* Mastery of the four properties covered in this note is the prerequisite for every later module.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU-Standard Terminology)

### (a) Mass Density (ρ)
The mass of the fluid contained per unit volume.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

- **SI Unit:** kg/m³
- **FPS Unit:** slug/ft³
- Standard reference value: **ρ_water at 4 °C = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³**

### (b) Specific Weight (γ) — also called *Weight Density*
The weight of the fluid contained per unit volume.

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \rho \cdot g$$

- **SI Unit:** N/m³ (or kN/m³)
- **FPS Unit:** lbf/ft³
- Standard reference value: **γ_water at 4 °C = 9810 N/m³ ≈ 9.81 kN/m³**

### (c) Specific Volume (v)
The volume occupied per unit mass of the fluid. It is the reciprocal of density.

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

- **SI Unit:** m³/kg
- **FPS Unit:** ft³/slug
- Standard reference value: **v_water at 4 °C = 0.001 m³/kg**

### (d) Specific Gravity (S) — also called *Relative Density*
The dimensionless ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of a standard reference fluid (water at 4 °C for liquids; air at standard atmospheric conditions for gases).

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at 4°C}}}$$

- **Unit:** Dimensionless (no unit)
- Standard reference value: **S_water = 1** (by definition)

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a sealed jar of honey vs. a sealed jar of cooking oil of identical size (1 litre each).**
>
> - The **jar of honey feels heavier** because honey packs more *mass* into the same *volume* → its **mass density (ρ)** is larger.
> - The **weight of each jar on a weighing scale** divided by its volume gives the **specific weight (γ)** — this is literally "how heavy a litre feels on Earth."
> - If you ask "how much space does 1 kg of this liquid occupy?", the answer is the **specific volume (v)**. Honey (being denser) will give you a *smaller* jar of space than oil for the same 1 kg.
> - The phrase **"specific gravity"** simply answers: *"Compared to water, is this fluid heavier or lighter, and by what factor?"* Mercury is about 13.6 times heavier than water → S = 13.6.

| Fluid (real-world feel) | ρ (kg/m³) | γ (kN/m³) | S | v (m³/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Air (almost weightless) | 1.225 | 0.01201 | 0.001225 | 0.8163 |
| Petrol (lighter than water) | 680 | 6.67 | 0.68 | 0.00147 |
| Water (baseline) | 1000 | 9.81 | 1.00 | 0.00100 |
| Glycerine (heavier than water) | 1260 | 12.36 | 1.26 | 0.00079 |
| Mercury (very heavy) | 13600 | 133.42 | 13.60 | 0.0000735 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

### Step 1 — Start from the *base* property: Mass Density (ρ)
Density is the *fundamental* property because it depends purely on the fluid's molecular packing. It does **not** change with location (mass and volume are intrinsic).

### Step 2 — Multiply by *g* to get Specific Weight (γ)
Specific weight is **location-dependent** because it incorporates the gravitational field. A fluid on the Moon has the same ρ but only 1/6 of Earth's γ.

### Step 3 — Invert density to get Specific Volume (v)
Useful in **thermodynamics and gas dynamics**, where engineers work with mass flow and prefer a "volume per kg" figure (e.g., steam tables).

### Step 4 — Normalize against water to get Specific Gravity (S)
A **dimensionless** number that allows engineers to speak in *ratios* without carrying units. Critical in problems involving **manometry**, **buoyancy**, and **hydrometers**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Property | Formula | SI Unit | FPS Unit | Reference Value (Water, 4 °C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass Density | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ | slug/ft³ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| 2 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | lbf/ft³ | **9810 N/m³** |
| 3 | Specific Volume | $v = V/m = 1/\rho$ | m³/kg | ft³/slug | **0.001 m³/kg** |
| 4 | Specific Gravity | $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}}/\rho_{\text{water}}$ | dimensionless | dimensionless | **1 (by definition)** |
| 5 | Linking relation | $\gamma_{\text{fluid}} = S \cdot \gamma_{\text{water}}$ | N/m³ | lbf/ft³ | — |
| 6 | Reciprocal relation | $\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \cdot \rho_{\text{water}}$ | kg/m³ | slug/ft³ | — |
| 7 | Reciprocal relation | $v_{\text{fluid}} = v_{\text{water}}/S$ | m³/kg | ft³/slug | — |

### 2.3 Standard Reference Conditions (Memorize for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Water at 4 °C:** ρ = 1000 kg/m³, γ = 9810 N/m³, S = 1 (defines the SI baseline).
> - **Air at 15 °C, 1 atm:** ρ ≈ 1.225 kg/m³, γ ≈ 12.01 N/m³, S ≈ 1.225 × 10⁻³.
> - **Standard gravity:** *g* = 9.81 m/s² (use **9.81** unless the problem specifies otherwise; for higher precision use **9.80665**).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Property | Where Engineers Use It |
|---|---|
| **Density (ρ)** | Pipe-flow mass balance ($\dot{m} = \rho A V$), Reynolds number, settling tanks, sedimentation design. |
| **Specific Weight (γ)** | Hydrostatic pressure ($p = \gamma h$), dam-design, storage-tank wall thickness. |
| **Specific Volume (v)** | Steam tables, gas-turbine calculations, compressible-flow nozzles. |
| **Specific Gravity (S)** | Battery-acid testing, alcohol content in wine, milk-fat measurement, manometer problems, hydrometer calibration. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Relation $\gamma = \rho g$

Starting from the definition of weight, $W = m \cdot g$ (Newton's second law under gravity):

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V}$$

Substitute $W = m g$:

$$\gamma = \frac{m \cdot g}{V}$$

Recognise that $m/V = \rho$:

$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \, g}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Specific-Gravity Link

By definition $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}}/\rho_{\text{water}}$. Multiplying both numerator and denominator by $g$ (a positive scalar):

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}} \cdot g}{\rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g} = \frac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water}}}$$

Hence the three equivalent forms:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water}}} = \frac{v_{\text{water}}}{v_{\text{fluid}}}}$$

This single identity is the most-tested link in KTU Module 1 problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem:** A liquid has specific gravity S = 0.85. Calculate its (a) density in kg/m³, (b) specific weight in N/m³, (c) specific weight in lbf/ft³, (d) specific volume in m³/kg.

### Part (a) — Density
Using $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}}/\rho_{\text{water}}$:

$$\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \cdot \rho_{\text{water}} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Part (b) — Specific Weight in N/m³

$$\gamma = \rho \, g = 850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5 \text{ N/m}^3 \approx 8.34 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

### Part (c) — Specific Weight in lbf/ft³ (Unit Conversion)

> Conversion factor: **1 lbf/ft³ = 157.087 N/m³**, so 1 N/m³ = 1/157.087 lbf/ft³.

$$\gamma = \frac{8338.5}{157.087} = 53.08 \text{ lbf/ft}^3$$

### Part (d) — Specific Volume

$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{850} = 1.176 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Reverse Problem

**Problem:** A fluid occupies 0.004 m³ and has a mass of 5.2 kg. Find its density, specific weight, and specific gravity. Take *g* = 9.81 m/s².

### Step 1 — Density

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{5.2}{0.004} = 1300 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Specific Weight

$$\gamma = \rho g = 1300 \times 9.81 = 12753 \text{ N/m}^3 = 12.753 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Specific Gravity

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{1300}{1000} = 1.30$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** S = 1.30 tells us instantly that the fluid is **30 % heavier than water** (e.g., concentrated brine, or sea water ≈ 1.025).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Choosing the Right Property)

| If the problem gives you… | And asks for… | Use this direct relation |
|---|---|---|
| Mass $m$, Volume $V$ | $\rho$ | $\rho = m/V$ |
| $\rho$ | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \rho g$ |
| $\rho$ | $v$ | $v = 1/\rho$ |
| $\rho$ | $S$ | $S = \rho/1000$ |
| $S$ | $\gamma$ (in kN/m³) | $\gamma = 9.81 \times S$ |
| $\gamma$ (in N/m³) | $\rho$ | $\rho = \gamma / 9.81$ |
| $\gamma$ | $S$ | $S = \gamma / 9810$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inter-relationships of Fluid Properties

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional dependencies** between the four fluid properties, the standard reference (water at 4 °C), and the gravitational constant.

```mermaid
graph TD
    massA["MASS m (kg)"]
    volA["VOLUME V (m3)"]
    weightA["WEIGHT W (N)"]
    gravA["GRAVITY g = 9.81 m/s2"]
    refWaterA["REFERENCE WATER AT 4 C"]
    densA["MASS DENSITY rho (kg per m3)"]
    spWtA["SPECIFIC WEIGHT gamma (N per m3)"]
    spVolA["SPECIFIC VOLUME v (m3 per kg)"]
    spGrA["SPECIFIC GRAVITY S (dimensionless)"]

    massA --> densA
    volA --> densA
    densA --> spWtA
    gravA --> spWtA
    weightA --> spWtA
    massA --> spVolA
    volA --> spVolA
    densA --> spVolA
    refWaterA --> spGrA
    densA --> spGrA

    densA -- "gamma equals rho times g" --> spWtA
    densA -- "v equals one over rho" --> spVolA
    densA -- "S equals rho divided by rho water" --> spGrA
    spWtA -- "S equals gamma divided by gamma water" --> spGrA
    refWaterA -- "rho water equals 1000" --> densA
    refWaterA -- "gamma water equals 9810" --> spWtA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this chart:** Start with the three *primitive* inputs (mass, volume, weight + gravity). The three *derived* properties (density, specific weight, specific volume) emerge. Specific gravity is then computed by comparing the fluid's density against the water reference. Every arrow is a valid KTU-level computation path.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Property Tree (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["FLUID PROPERTIES MODULE 1"]
    intA["INTENSIVE PROPERTIES (independent of sample size)"]
    extA["EXTENSIVE PROPERTIES (scale with sample size)"]
    intD["Density rho"]
    intE["Specific Weight gamma"]
    intF["Specific Volume v"]
    intG["Specific Gravity S"]
    intH["Viscosity mu"]
    intI["Vapour Pressure"]
    intJ["Bulk Modulus K"]
    extK["Mass m"]
    extL["Volume V"]
    extM["Weight W"]

    rootA --> intA
    rootA --> extA
    intA --> intD
    intA --> intE
    intA --> intF
    intA --> intG
    intA --> intH
    intA --> intI
    intA --> intJ
    extA --> extK
    extA --> extL
    extA --> extM
    intD -- "rho equals m divided by V" --> extK
    intD -- "rho equals m divided by V" --> extL
    intE -- "gamma equals W divided by V" --> extM
    intE -- "gamma equals W divided by V" --> extL
    intF -- "v equals V divided by m" --> extL
    intF -- "v equals V divided by m" --> extK
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four properties covered in this note — ρ, γ, v, S — are **intensive**: a drop and a lake of the same fluid share the same values. This is why specific gravity of milk (measured by a hydrometer on the farm) is identical to that of milk in a tanker truck.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define specific gravity and specific volume. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Specific Gravity (S):** It is the ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of a standard reference fluid (water at 4 °C for liquids; air for gases). $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}}/\rho_{\text{water}}$. It is **dimensionless** (no units). [1.5 marks]
- **Specific Volume (v):** It is the volume of the fluid per unit mass. $v = V/m = 1/\rho$. Its **SI unit is m³/kg**. [1.5 marks]

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**The mass density of an oil is 920 kg/m³. Find its (i) specific weight in kN/m³ and (ii) specific gravity. Take g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (i) $\gamma = \rho g = 920 \times 9.81 = 9025.2 \text{ N/m}^3 = 9.025 \text{ kN/m}^3$ [2 marks]
- (ii) $S = \rho_{\text{oil}}/\rho_{\text{water}} = 920/1000 = 0.92$ [1 mark]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Pick Either A or B)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (READ CAREFULLY):**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit of *g*:** KTU examiners *expect* $g = 9.81$ m/s² stated explicitly. Omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up ρ_water and ρ_air:** Specific gravity for liquids is referenced to water (1000 kg/m³), but for gases to air (1.225 kg/m³). Using the wrong reference = 0 marks for that sub-part.
> 3. **Not writing units in the final answer:** KTU strictly follows *unit-aware marking*. A bare "9.025" without "kN/m³" loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Conversion to FPS:** Use the standard factor **1 lbf/ft³ = 157.087 N/m³**. Writing a wrong conversion factor (e.g., 9.81 or 1000) loses full marks.
> 5. **Formula ≠ Substitute:** Always write the formula first, *then* substitute the values. Jumping straight to a numerical answer loses the formula mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Define the following fluid properties and state their SI units: mass density, specific weight, and specific gravity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid has a specific gravity of 0.78. Calculate:
- (i) Its mass density in kg/m³
- (ii) Its specific weight in N/m³ and lbf/ft³
- (iii) Its specific volume in m³/kg

Take $g = 9.81$ m/s² and 1 lbf/ft³ = 157.087 N/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A

**Part (a) — Definitions [7 marks]**

1. **Mass Density (ρ):** Mass per unit volume. $\rho = m/V$. SI unit: **kg/m³**. [2 marks]
2. **Specific Weight (γ):** Weight per unit volume. $\gamma = W/V = \rho g$. SI unit: **N/m³**. [2 marks]
3. **Specific Gravity (S):** Dimensionless ratio of fluid density to water density at 4 °C. $S = \rho_{\text{fluid}}/\rho_{\text{water}}$. SI unit: **dimensionless (no unit)**. [3 marks]

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

(i) **Density:**
$$\rho = S \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 0.78 \times 1000 = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad [\text{2 marks}]$$

(ii) **Specific Weight in N/m³:**
$$\gamma = \rho g = 780 \times 9.81 = 7651.8 \text{ N/m}^3 \quad [\text{2 marks}]$$

**Specific Weight in lbf/ft³:**
$$\gamma = \frac{7651.8}{157.087} = 48.71 \text{ lbf/ft}^3 \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]$$

(iii) **Specific Volume:**
$$v = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{780} = 1.282 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the terms density, specific weight, specific volume, and specific gravity. Give one practical application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An oil of specific gravity 0.86 occupies a volume of 4 m³. Find:
- (i) The mass of the oil
- (ii) The specific weight of the oil in kN/m³
- (iii) The weight of the oil in kN

Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B

**Part (a) — Explanations and Applications [7 marks]**

| Property | Explanation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Density | Mass per unit volume | Pipeline mass-flow rate design [1.75 m] |
| Specific Weight | Weight per unit volume | Hydrostatic pressure calculation ($p=\gamma h$) [1.75 m] |
| Specific Volume | Volume per unit mass | Steam-table thermodynamics [1.75 m] |
| Specific Gravity | Ratio of fluid density to water density | Hydrometer calibration in dairy industry [1.75 m] |

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

(i) **Density:**
$$\rho = S \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]$$

**Mass of oil:**
$$m = \rho \times V = 860 \times 4 = 3440 \text{ kg} \quad [\text{2 marks}]$$

(ii) **Specific Weight:**
$$\gamma = \rho g = 860 \times 9.81 = 8436.6 \text{ N/m}^3 = 8.437 \text{ kN/m}^3 \quad [\text{2 marks}]$$

(iii) **Weight of oil:**
$$W = \gamma \times V = 8.437 \times 4 = 33.748 \text{ kN} \quad [\text{1.5 marks}]$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Revision — Read 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Density ρ = m/V**, units kg/m³, water at 4 °C → 1000 kg/m³ (memorize).
- **Specific Weight γ = ρg**, units N/m³, water at 4 °C → 9810 N/m³ ≈ 9.81 kN/m³.
- **Specific Volume v = 1/ρ**, units m³/kg, water at 4 °C → 0.001 m³/kg.
- **Specific Gravity S = ρ_fluid/ρ_water**, dimensionless, water → 1.
- **Master Link:** $\gamma_{\text{fluid}} = S \times \gamma_{\text{water}} = S \times 9810$ N/m³.
- **Unit Conversion to remember:** 1 lbf/ft³ = 157.087 N/m³ (use it for cross-system problems).
- **Always state g = 9.81 m/s²** explicitly in your solution to gain full credit.
- **Specific gravity is dimensionless** — never write units for S.
- **Specific weight is location-dependent** (changes with *g*); density is *not* (purely a property of matter).
- **Fluids with S < 1 float on water**; fluids with S > 1 sink in water.
- **For gases**, S is referenced to **air** (ρ_air ≈ 1.225 kg/m³), not water.
- **Distinguish** *relative density* (synonym for specific gravity) from *density*.
- **Quick sanity check:** If S < 1, then ρ < 1000, γ < 9810, and v > 0.001 — all four properties move in consistent directions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dynamic Viscosity (Absolute Viscosity)

**Dynamic Viscosity**, denoted by the Greek letter **μ** (mu), is the fundamental property of a real fluid that quantifies its **internal resistance to deformation (shear)** when subjected to an applied tangential force. It is the proportionality constant in Newton's Law of Viscosity that relates the shear stress in a fluid to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *"Dynamic viscosity is defined as the tangential force per unit area (shear stress) required to maintain a unit velocity gradient between two parallel layers of a fluid, separated by a unit distance apart."*

**SI Unit of Dynamic Viscosity:** **N·s/m²** or equivalently **Pa·s** (Pascal-second) or **kg/(m·s)**.

**CGS Unit:** **Poise** (P), where **1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s**. A commonly used sub-unit is **Centipoise (cP)**, where **1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s**.

## 1.2 Kinematic Viscosity

**Kinematic Viscosity**, denoted by the Greek letter **ν** (nu), is defined as the ratio of the dynamic viscosity of a fluid to its mass density. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under the influence of **gravity alone** (i.e., without any external force driving the flow).

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

where:
- **μ** = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid [Pa·s]
- **ρ** = Mass density of the fluid [kg/m³]

**SI Unit of Kinematic Viscosity:** **m²/s**.

**CGS Unit:** **Stokes (St)**, where **1 Stoke = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s**. The sub-unit **Centistokes (cSt)** is widely used (**1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s**).

> [!NOTE]
> The term "**kinematic**" arises because this form of viscosity involves only **kinematic quantities** (length and time) and excludes the force-based dynamic term, making it essential in **Reynolds number** calculations for characterizing flow regimes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine two extreme fluids placed on an inclined plate:

- **Honey / Thick Syrup:** When you tilt the plate, honey barely moves. Its molecules strongly resist sliding past each other — it has a **high dynamic viscosity**.
- **Pure Water:** Water slides readily. Its molecules slide past each other easily — it has a **low dynamic viscosity**.
- **Air:** Air flows even more freely than water — it has a very **low dynamic viscosity**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of dynamic viscosity as the "**internal friction or stickiness**" of a fluid — the more "sticky" the fluid, the harder it is for one layer to slide over another. Kinematic viscosity, on the other hand, is like a "**fluidity score**" that accounts for the fluid's heaviness (density). A heavy, sticky fluid (like mercury) can have a low kinematic viscosity if its density is high enough!

### Standard Reference Values at 20°C (for Intuition)

| Fluid | Dynamic Viscosity μ (Pa·s) | Density ρ (kg/m³) | Kinematic Viscosity ν (m²/s) |
|-------|---------------------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| Water | **1.002 × 10⁻³** | **998** | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶** |
| Air | **1.81 × 10⁻⁵** | **1.204** | **1.50 × 10⁻⁵** |
| Glycerine | **1.49** | **1260** | **1.18 × 10⁻³** |
| Mercury | **1.55 × 10⁻³** | **13550** | **1.14 × 10⁻⁷** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Notice how **glycerine** has a much higher dynamic viscosity than mercury, yet mercury has a far lower kinematic viscosity because of mercury's enormous density. This contrast highlights why both quantities are essential.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundation

For a fluid flowing in a streamline manner over a flat stationary plate, experimental observation by **Sir Isaac Newton** revealed that the shear stress **τ** (tau) acting on any fluid layer is:

1. **Directly proportional** to the velocity gradient **du/dy** in the direction perpendicular to the flow.
2. **Directly proportional** to the dynamic viscosity **μ** of the fluid.

This is mathematically expressed as:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- **τ** = Shear stress at any fluid layer [N/m² or Pa]
- **μ** = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid [Pa·s]
- **du/dy** = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow [s⁻¹]

### Logical Reasoning Behind Each Component:

- **The shear stress τ** exists because the faster-moving upper layer drags the slower-moving layer beneath it through intermolecular cohesive forces.
- **The velocity gradient du/dy** captures how rapidly velocity changes from one fluid layer to the next. A steeper gradient (e.g., near a wall) means more "rubbing" between layers.
- **The proportionality constant μ** is unique to each fluid and quantifies how strongly the molecules resist this relative motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign sometimes appears?** In some textbook conventions, the law is written as **τ = −μ (du/dy)** because the shear stress on a lower (slower) layer acts in the direction of the upper layer's motion, opposite to the positive y-direction. However, in most engineering practice (including KTU solutions), the magnitudes are used without the negative sign.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic of Dynamic Viscosity from Force Balance

Consider two parallel fluid layers of area **A** separated by a small distance **dy**:
- Lower layer moves at velocity **u**.
- Upper layer moves at velocity **u + du**.

The force **F** required to maintain this velocity difference (overcoming internal friction) is:

$$F = \mu \cdot A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Dividing by area **A** to obtain shear stress:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From this equation, by definition:
> $$\mu = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)}$$
> This means **μ is numerically equal to the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient**.

## 2.3 Derivation Logic of Kinematic Viscosity

The kinematic viscosity is **not derived** from Newton's law directly but is a **derived quantity** defined to simplify dimensional analysis in fluid flow problems:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \right]$$

The physical reasoning is:
- **μ** alone carries both *inertial* and *frictional* characteristics.
- Dividing by **ρ** (which carries only *inertial* characteristics) isolates the *diffusive* nature of momentum transfer due to viscosity.

This is why kinematic viscosity is interpreted as the **diffusivity of momentum** in the fluid.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | SI Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$ | Pa | Fundamental constitutive equation for viscous fluids |
| 2 | Dynamic Viscosity Definition | $\mu = \tau \div (du/dy)$ | Pa·s or N·s/m² | Internal friction coefficient |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s | Momentum diffusivity |
| 4 | Force on Fluid Layer | $F = \mu \cdot A \cdot (du/dy)$ | N | Force to overcome viscous drag |
| 5 | SI to CGS Conversion (μ) | $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise}$ | — | Unit conversion |
| 6 | Sub-unit Conversion (μ) | $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$ | — | Unit conversion |
| 7 | SI to CGS Conversion (ν) | $1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 10^4 \text{ St}$ | — | Unit conversion |
| 8 | Sub-unit Conversion (ν) | $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | — | Unit conversion |
| 9 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$ | Dimensionless | Uses kinematic viscosity |
| 10 | Newton's Number (Capillarity) | $Nm = \rho g D^2 / \mu$ | — | Dynamic viscosity usage |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** When substituting values into formulas, **always convert to SI units first**. The KTU examiner will strictly deduct marks if a student mixes SI and CGS units in a single calculation.

## 2.5 Variation of Viscosity with Temperature

| Fluid Type | Behaviour with Increasing Temperature | Reason |
|------------|----------------------------------------|--------|
| **Liquids** (water, oil) | Dynamic viscosity **DECREASES** sharply | Molecular cohesive forces weaken with heat |
| **Gases** (air, steam) | Dynamic viscosity **INCREASES** slightly | Molecular momentum exchange rises with temperature |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** (both) | **DECREASES** for liquids, **INCREASES** for gases | Follows μ trend, modulated by ρ |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Engineering Utility:**
> - Lubricating oils are chosen based on **dynamic viscosity** at operating temperature.
> - Pipeline flow calculations and **Reynolds number** determination use **kinematic viscosity** extensively.
> - In HVAC and aerodynamics, **kinematic viscosity of air** is critical for boundary layer analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from First Principles

### Step 1: Setting up the Physical Model
Consider a fluid flowing steadily over a fixed horizontal plate. The fluid layer in immediate contact with the plate (at $y = 0$) has zero velocity (no-slip condition). As we move upward in the y-direction, fluid velocity $u$ increases until it reaches a free-stream value $U$.

### Step 2: Defining the Velocity Gradient
The rate of change of velocity with respect to the perpendicular distance $y$ is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}$$

This is the **velocity gradient** or **rate of shear strain**.

### Step 3: Experimental Observation (Newton's Postulate)
Newton experimentally established that the shear stress $\tau$ at any horizontal plane within the fluid is **linearly proportional** to the velocity gradient at that plane. Mathematically:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 4: Introducing the Proportionality Constant
To convert the proportionality into an equation, we introduce the constant of proportionality $\mu$:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

### Step 5: Identifying the Constant $\mu$
The constant $\mu$ is named **dynamic viscosity** (or **absolute viscosity** or **coefficient of viscosity**). Its value depends solely on the type of fluid and its temperature/pressure.

### Step 6: Solving for $\mu$ to Get its Physical Meaning
Rearranging the equation:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy}$$

**Interpretation:** $\mu$ equals the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient in the fluid.

### Step 7: Computing the Force on a Fluid Layer
If the fluid layer has surface area $A$, the tangential force $F$ acting on it is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

This force must be applied externally to overcome viscous resistance and maintain the velocity gradient.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity from Dimensional Analysis

### Step 1: Start with Dynamic Viscosity
Dynamic viscosity has SI units:

$$[\mu] = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^2} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$$

### Step 2: Dimensional Form of $\mu$
Using [M, L, T] notation:

$$[\mu] = M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-1}$$

### Step 3: Density Dimensional Form

$$[\rho] = M \cdot L^{-3}$$

### Step 4: Forming the Ratio $\mu / \rho$
Dividing the two:

$$\left[ \frac{\mu}{\rho} \right] = \frac{M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-1}}{M \cdot L^{-3}} = L^2 \cdot T^{-1}$$

### Step 5: Recognising the Result
The ratio $\mu / \rho$ has units of $L^2 / T$, which is precisely $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. This is the dimension of **diffusivity**, motivating the term "**kinematic**".

### Step 6: Final Definition

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \right]}$$

## 3.3 Detailed Unit Conversion Derivation

### Conversion of Dynamic Viscosity: Poise to Pa·s

By definition in CGS:
$$1 \text{ Poise} = 1 \frac{\text{dyne} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{cm}^2}$$

Convert to SI:
- $1 \text{ dyne} = 10^{-5} \text{ N}$
- $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

$$1 \text{ Poise} = \frac{10^{-5} \text{ N} \cdot \text{s}}{10^{-4} \text{ m}^2} = 10^{-1} \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

And consequently:

$$\boxed{1 \text{ cP} = 0.001 \text{ Pa·s} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

### Conversion of Kinematic Viscosity: Stokes to m²/s

By definition in CGS:
$$1 \text{ Stokes} = 1 \frac{\text{cm}^2}{\text{s}} = 10^{-4} \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Computing Viscosity Quantities (Analytical)

**Problem:** A fluid of density $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ has a kinematic viscosity of $0.35 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate:
1. The dynamic viscosity in Pa·s.
2. The shear stress on a layer if the velocity gradient is $4 \text{ s}^{-1}$.
3. The force required on a fluid patch of area $0.5 \text{ m}^2$ to maintain this gradient.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute Dynamic Viscosity**

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho = (0.35 \times 10^{-4}) \times 850$$

$$\mu = 297.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s} = 0.02975 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Shear Stress**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.02975 \times 4 = 0.119 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute Force on Patch**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 0.119 \times 0.5 = 0.0595 \text{ N}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification of Units:** $\mu$ in Pa·s $\times$ $du/dy$ in s⁻¹ = Pa·s × s⁻¹ = Pa = N/m² ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Viscosity Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID PROPERTIES]:::root --> B[Viscosity]
    B --> C[Dynamic Viscosity mu]:::branch
    B --> D[Kinematic Viscosity nu]:::branch
    B --> E[Other Properties like Bulk Modulus]:::branch

    C --> C1[Symbol: mu]
    C --> C2[Unit: Pa s]
    C --> C3[CGS Unit: Poise]
    C --> C4[Represents Internal Friction]
    C --> C5[Governed by Newtons Law: tau equals mu times du by dy]

    D --> D1[Symbol: nu]
    D --> D2[Unit: m squared per s]
    D --> D3[CGS Unit: Stokes]
    D --> D4[Represents Momentum Diffusivity]
    D --> D5[Formula: nu equals mu divided by rho]

    E --> E1[Density rho in kg per m cubed]
    E --> E2[Pressure p in Pa]
    E --> E3[Surface Tension sigma in N per m]

    C5 --> F[Relationship Bridge]:::bridge
    D5 --> F
    F --> F1[Reynolds Number Re uses nu]
    F --> F2[Lubrication Analysis uses mu]
    F --> F3[Boundary Layer Theory uses both]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef bridge fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map: Newton's Law of Viscosity Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stationary Plate at y equals zero]:::in --> P2[Velocity u increases with y]:::in
    P2 --> P3[Velocity Gradient du by dy exists]:::proc
    P3 --> P4[Shear Stress tau generated between layers]:::proc
    P4 --> P5[Newton Law: tau equals mu times du by dy]:::out
    P5 --> P6[Dynamic Viscosity mu is the proportionality constant]:::out
    P6 --> P7[Combine with density rho to get kinematic viscosity nu]:::out

    classDef in fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#5e35b1,stroke:#5e35b1,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description: Velocity Profile of Viscous Flow

For a clear conceptual understanding, the following ASCII schematic illustrates the **velocity profile** for a viscous fluid flowing over a stationary wall. This is the geometric interpretation of the velocity gradient $du/dy$ used in Newton's law.

```
Velocity u
   ^
   |              Free Stream (U = max)
   |______________________________
   |             /  /
   |           /   /                <- Velocity Profile
   |         /    /                    (parabolic or linear
   |       /     /                      depending on flow type)
   |     /      /
   |   /       /
   | /        /
   |/________/________________________> y (distance from wall)
   |        Wall (u = 0, no-slip condition)
```

**Interpretation:** Each "layer" or "filament" of fluid moves at a slightly different velocity. The rate of change of velocity between adjacent layers (the slope of this profile at any point) is the **velocity gradient $du/dy$** that appears in Newton's law.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Engineering Applications of Viscosity Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Viscosity Concepts]:::src --> V1[Dynamic Viscosity mu]
    V --> V2[Kinematic Viscosity nu]

    V1 --> A1[Engine Lubrication Design]
    V1 --> A2[Hydraulic System Analysis]
    V1 --> A3[Polymer Processing]
    V1 --> A4[Viscometer Calibration]

    V2 --> A5[Reynolds Number Calculation]
    V2 --> A6[Pipe Flow Classification]
    V2 --> A7[Boundary Layer Analysis]
    V2 --> A8[Wind Tunnel Experiments]

    A5 --> R1[Flow Regime: Laminar or Turbulent]
    A6 --> R1
    A7 --> R2[Aerodynamic Drag Estimation]
    A8 --> R2

    classDef src fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams visually establish that **dynamic viscosity** governs problems involving **forces and friction**, while **kinematic viscosity** governs problems involving **flow characterization and dimensionless analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units and CGS units.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is defined as the tangential force per unit area required to maintain a unit velocity gradient between two parallel layers of a fluid separated by unit distance.

- **SI Unit:** Pa·s or N·s/m²
- **CGS Unit:** Poise (P), where 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s

**Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

- **SI Unit:** m²/s
- **CGS Unit:** Stokes (St), where 1 Stokes = 10⁻⁴ m²/s

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. What is the physical significance of dynamic viscosity?**

**Model Answer:**

**Newton's Law of Viscosity:** The shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient $du/dy$).

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Physical Significance:** The dynamic viscosity $\mu$ represents the **internal resistance** of a fluid to flow or deformation. It indicates how "thick" or "sticky" a fluid is.

- A high value of $\mu$ means the fluid resists flow strongly (e.g., honey).
- A low value of $\mu$ means the fluid flows easily (e.g., water, air).
- Mathematically, $\mu$ equals the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient in the fluid.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Assume Option A)*

**(a) Derive Newton's law of viscosity. Explain the physical significance of dynamic viscosity with a suitable example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid flowing steadily over a stationary horizontal plate. The fluid layer in contact with the plate is at rest, and successive layers move with increasing velocity $u$ as we move away from the plate.

**Step 2:** Let two consecutive fluid layers at distance $dy$ apart have velocities $u$ and $u + du$ respectively.

**Step 3:** The relative velocity between layers is $du$, and the velocity gradient is $du/dy$.

**Step 4:** According to Newton, the shear stress $\tau$ developed in the fluid is directly proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5:** Introducing the proportionality constant $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity):

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**.

**Step 6:** Physical Significance of $\mu$:
- From the equation, $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$, so $\mu$ equals the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient.
- High $\mu$ ⇒ high internal friction (e.g., honey, glycerine).
- Low $\mu$ ⇒ low internal friction (e.g., water, air).

**Example:** Consider two oils — SAE 10W and SAE 40W. SAE 40W has higher $\mu$, hence flows slower and provides a thicker lubricating film in engines.

**(b) A fluid of density 900 kg/m³ has a dynamic viscosity of 0.15 Pa·s. Calculate: (i) its kinematic viscosity, (ii) the shear stress if the velocity gradient is 5 s⁻¹, and (iii) the viscous force on a plate of area 0.25 m². (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.15 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $du/dy = 5 \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $A = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$

**(i) Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.15}{900}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.15 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 0.75 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

**(iii) Viscous Force:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 0.75 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{F = 0.1875 \text{ N}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` *(Alternative Option)*

**(a) Differentiate between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. Discuss the variation of viscosity with temperature for liquids and gases. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Differences between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity:**

| Aspect | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Internal resistance to deformation | Ratio of $\mu$ to density $\rho$ |
| **Formula** | $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$ | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
| **SI Unit** | Pa·s | m²/s |
| **CGS Unit** | Poise | Stokes |
| **Depends on** | Fluid's molecular structure | Both viscosity and density |
| **Represents** | Internal friction force | Momentum diffusivity |
| **Used in** | Force-related problems | Reynolds number and flow classification |

**Variation with Temperature:**

- **For Liquids:** As temperature increases, the cohesive forces between molecules weaken, allowing layers to slide more easily. Hence, **dynamic viscosity of liquids DECREASES** with temperature.
  - Example: Engine oil becomes thinner when hot.
  - Empirical formula (for oils): $\mu = A \cdot e^{B/T}$

- **For Gases:** As temperature increases, molecular momentum exchange between layers increases due to higher kinetic energy. Hence, **dynamic viscosity of gases INCREASES** with temperature.
  - Example: Hot air is "thicker" in dynamic sense than cold air.
  - Empirical relation (Sutherland's): $\mu \propto T^{3/2} / (T + S)$

- **Kinematic Viscosity:** For liquids, $\nu$ decreases with temperature (faster than $\mu$ because $\rho$ also decreases). For gases, $\nu$ **increases** because $\mu$ increases faster than $\rho$.

**(b) The dynamic viscosity of a fluid is given as 0.05 Pa·s. Convert it into (i) Poise, (ii) centipoise, and (iii) N·s/m². If the fluid's specific gravity is 0.85, calculate the kinematic viscosity in m²/s and Stokes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\mu = 0.05 \text{ Pa·s}$, Specific Gravity = 0.85, so $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Conversion to Poise:**

$$1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise}$$

$$0.05 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.05 \times 10 = \boxed{0.5 \text{ Poise}}$$

**(ii) Conversion to Centipoise:**

$$1 \text{ Pa·s} = 1000 \text{ cP}$$

$$0.05 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.05 \times 1000 = \boxed{50 \text{ cP}}$$

**(iii) N·s/m² (Same as Pa·s):**

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.05 \text{ N·s/m}^2}$$

**(iv) Kinematic Viscosity in m²/s:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.05}{850}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 5.882 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**(v) Kinematic Viscosity in Stokes:**

$$1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 10^4 \text{ Stokes}$$

$$5.882 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 5.882 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^4 = \boxed{0.5882 \text{ Stokes}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mixing Disaster (–2 marks):** Students often calculate $\nu = \mu/\rho$ with $\mu$ in Poise and $\rho$ in kg/m³ without conversion. ALWAYS convert $\mu$ to Pa·s before dividing.
> 2. **Confusing Centi- and Milli- Prefixes (–1 mark):** 1 cP (centipoise) = 0.01 Poise = 0.001 Pa·s. Don't confuse it with milliPoise (mP).
> 3. **Forgetting Density in Kinematic Viscosity (–2 marks):** Some students report $\nu = \mu$ (incorrect). Kinematic viscosity MUST involve dividing by $\rho$.
> 4. **Incorrect Sign of Velocity Gradient (–1 mark):** The negative sign in $\tau = -\mu(du/dy)$ is convention-based. KTU accepts both positive and negative conventions if the magnitude is correctly computed. State your convention clearly.
> 5. **No-Slip Condition Ignorance (–1 mark):** When drawing velocity profiles, ALWAYS mark the wall velocity as zero. Examiners check this rigorously.
> 6. **Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Confusion (–1 mark):** The Newton's law applies ONLY to Newtonian fluids (water, air, oils). Blood, paints, and slurries are non-Newtonian and require different constitutive equations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$ — always the starting point for any viscosity problem.
- ✅ **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** Unit is **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**; CGS unit is **Poise (1 Pa·s = 10 Poise)**.
- ✅ **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** Unit is **m²/s**; CGS unit is **Stokes (1 m²/s = 10⁴ St)**.
- ✅ **Golden Formula:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — convert all units to SI before substituting.
- ✅ **Temperature Behaviour:** Liquids ↓, Gases ↑ for dynamic viscosity.
- ✅ **Centipoise & Centistokes:** 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s; 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.
- ✅ **Physical Meaning of $\mu$:** Numerically equal to shear stress at unit velocity gradient.
- ✅ **Physical Meaning of $\nu$:** Represents momentum diffusivity of the fluid.
- ✅ **No-slip condition** at walls — fluid velocity equals wall velocity (zero for stationary walls).
- ✅ **Standard Values:** Water at 20°C → $\mu \approx 1$ cP; Air at 20°C → $\mu \approx 0.018$ cP.
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Reynolds number uses $\nu$; force/stress problems use $\mu$.
- ✅ **Always write units** in the final answer; KTU strictly enforces this.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** State clearly whether you are using $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$ or $\tau = -\mu(du/dy)$.
- ✅ **Typical 3-mark question focus:** Definitions + units + Newton's law statement.
- ✅ **Typical 14-mark question focus:** Derivation + numerical computation involving unit conversions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 Weightage Insight:** From Module 1, viscosity concepts consistently appear every semester — usually as a **3-mark definition** in Part A and as a **sub-part of a 14-mark derivation** in Part B. Mastering unit conversions and the difference between $\mu$ and $\nu$ is the single most important step to scoring full marks on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Fluid Mechanics - Types of Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Fluid Mechanics - Types of Fluids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fluid?

A **fluid** is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows) under the action of an applied shear (tangential) stress**, however small that stress may be. A fluid is a state of matter that includes both **liquids** and **gases**, and it cannot sustain shear stress when in static equilibrium — the internal shear resistance arises only when the fluid is in motion (this property is called *viscosity*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A fluid is a substance which deforms continuously under the action of an applied shear stress, or an external force."*
> The defining mechanical property is the **inability to resist a static shear stress** — this is what fundamentally separates a fluid from a solid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey Test 🐝

Imagine pushing a spoon across three substances on a table:
- A **steel block** (solid) — the spoon will not penetrate; the block resists shear up to a fixed elastic limit.
- **Water** (Newtonian liquid) — the spoon moves easily, and the resistance is directly proportional to how fast you move it.
- **Toothpaste** (Bingham plastic) — the spoon won't move until you push hard enough, then it flows.

This everyday "spoon test" intuitively captures the entire classification of fluids: some need a threshold stress to flow, some flow easily, and some resist flow differently depending on motion speed. That is the heart of fluid classification.

## 1.3 Distinction Between Liquids and Gases

| Property | Liquid | Gas (or Vapour) |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Has a free surface (in open containers) | Fills the entire container |
| **Volume** | Practically incompressible | Highly compressible |
| **Density (kg/m³)** | Water ≈ **1000**, Mercury ≈ **13600** | Air ≈ **1.225** |
| **Molecular spacing** | Closely packed | Widely spaced |
| **Bulk modulus (N/m²)** | Very high (≈ **2.1 × 10⁹** for water) | Low |
| **Surface tension** | Significant | Negligible |

## 1.4 The Continuum Concept

Although fluids are made of discrete molecules, in engineering fluid mechanics we treat them as a **continuous medium (continuum)** — meaning fluid properties like density $\rho$, velocity $V$, and pressure $p$ are defined at every point in space.

> [!NOTE]
> The continuum assumption is valid when the **Knudsen number** $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L} < 0.01$, where $\lambda$ is the mean free path of molecules and $L$ is the characteristic length of the flow. For most engineering flows (pipe flow, open-channel flow, aerodynamics of vehicles), this assumption holds extremely well.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Fluid Properties

### 2.1.1 Mass Density ($\rho$)

Mass per unit volume. It is an intensive property.

$$\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to \Delta V^{*}} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V}$$

where $\Delta V^{*}$ is the smallest volume over which a statistically meaningful average of molecular mass can be taken (the *continuum limit*).

**SI Unit:** $\text{kg/m}^3$
**Typical values:** Water at $4^\circ\text{C}$ — $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (this is a **primary standard** in SI).

### 2.1.2 Specific Weight ($\gamma$ or $w$)

Weight per unit volume.

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

**SI Unit:** $\text{N/m}^3$
**For water at standard gravity** ($g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$): $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$

### 2.1.3 Specific Gravity ($S$ or $SG$)

A **dimensionless** ratio that compares a fluid's density to that of water at $4^\circ\text{C}$.

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4^\circ\text{C}}}$$

### 2.1.4 Viscosity — The Internal "Stickiness"

Viscosity is the property that represents the fluid's **internal resistance to deformation (flow)**. It originates from the **intermolecular cohesive forces** and the **momentum exchange between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities**.

#### 2.1.4.1 Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity ($\mu$)

Consider a fluid element trapped between two parallel plates. The lower plate is fixed; the upper plate moves at velocity $U$ under applied tangential force $F$ per unit area.

Newton's law of viscosity states:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress $[\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa$]$
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity $[\text{N·s/m}^2$ or Pa·s or kg/(m·s)$]$
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow $[1/\text{s}]$

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's postulate (1687):** Shear stress is *directly proportional* to the *rate of angular deformation* (shear strain rate). Fluids that obey this linear relation are called **Newtonian fluids**.

#### 2.1.4.2 Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**SI Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
A memory aid: **μ** = absolute (uses force), **ν** = kinematic (no force, only geometry — flows under its own weight).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Conversion: $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s} = 0.1 \text{ N·s/m}^2$. Water at $20^\circ\text{C}$ has $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.

### 2.1.5 Compressibility ($\beta$) and Bulk Modulus ($K$)

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V} \frac{dV}{dP} = \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{dP}$$

$$K = \frac{1}{\beta} = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**For water** at standard conditions: $K \approx 2.1 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 2.1 \text{ GPa}$.

### 2.1.6 Surface Tension ($\sigma$)

A tensile force per unit length that exists at the interface of a liquid and a gas (or two immiscible liquids). It arises from **cohesive forces** between liquid molecules.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L} \quad [\text{N/m}]$$

**For water–air at $20^\circ\text{C}$:** $\sigma \approx 0.0728 \text{ N/m}$.

### 2.1.7 Capillarity — Capillary Rise

$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

where $\theta$ is the **contact angle** and $r$ is the tube radius. This explains why water rises in a glass tube (wetting, $\theta < 90^\circ$) but mercury depresses (non-wetting, $\theta > 90^\circ$).

### 2.1.8 Vapour Pressure ($P_v$)

The pressure at which a liquid and its vapour are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** at a given temperature. It is strongly temperature dependent. When local absolute pressure falls below $P_v$, **cavitation** occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation** is the formation, growth, and violent collapse of vapour cavities in a flowing liquid when local pressure drops below vapour pressure. It is a **major failure mode in pumps, turbines, and ship propellers**.

## 2.2 Types of Fluids — The KTU Classification

The shear stress–shear strain rate behaviour is the **primary classification criterion** in fluid mechanics.

### 2.2.1 Ideal Fluid vs Real Fluid

| Aspect | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity | Zero ($\mu = 0$) | Non-zero |
| Compressibility | Zero (incompressible) | May be compressible |
| Surface tension | Zero | Non-zero |
| Use | Theoretical analysis (Euler equations) | Real engineering problems (Navier–Stokes) |

> [!NOTE]
> An **ideal fluid** is a hypothetical, frictionless, incompressible fluid. It is a pedagogical model — it does *not exist*. Real engineering design *always* accounts for viscosity.

### 2.2.2 The Three Main Categories of Fluids

#### A. Newtonian Fluids
These obey **Newton's law of viscosity** — linear relationship between $\tau$ and $du/dy$, passing through the origin.
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$
**Examples:** Water, air, mercury, oils (light), alcohol, gasoline, glycerine.

#### B. Non-Newtonian Fluids
These do **not** follow Newton's law. The slope of $\tau$ vs $du/dy$ (the *apparent viscosity* $\mu_a$) varies with shear rate.
$$\tau = \mu_a \frac{du}{dy}, \quad \text{where } \mu_a = \mu_a\!\left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Sub-classification of Non-Newtonian fluids (very high yield):**
>
> | Type | $\tau$–$du/dy$ curve | Examples | Engineering notes |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning)** | Curves downward; $\mu_a$ decreases with shear rate | Blood, ketchup, paint, milk, nail polish | Easier to pump at high shear |
> | **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** | Curves upward; $\mu_a$ increases with shear rate | Wet sand, starch suspension, mud | Quicksand behaviour |
> | **Bingham plastic** | Linear after a finite **yield stress** $\tau_y$ | Toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge, mayonnaise | Will not flow below $\tau_y$ |
> | **Thixotropic** | Apparent viscosity **decreases over time** under constant shear | Gel, honey (over long times), some paints | Time-dependent — structure breaks down |
> | **Rheopectic** | Apparent viscosity **increases over time** under constant shear | Gypsum paste, printer's ink | Time-dependent — structure builds up |
> | **Viscoelastic** | Combines viscous flow with elastic recovery | Egg white, slingshot polymer, bread dough | Recovers shape after deformation |

#### C. Ideal Plastic (Bingham Plastic) — Already covered above as a non-Newtonian type.

### 2.2.3 Real Fluid Summary Table

| Fluid | Viscosity | Compressibility | Surfaces | Exists in nature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal fluid** | None | None | None | **No** (theoretical) |
| **Real Newtonian** | Yes | Some | Yes | **Yes** (water, air) |
| **Real Non-Newtonian** | Variable | Some | Yes | **Yes** (blood, paint) |

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | SI Unit | Notes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Hydraulic engineering**: design of dams, spillways, channels — requires accurate $\rho$, $\gamma$, $\mu$.
- **Aerospace**: viscous drag on aircraft skin — governed by $\mu$ and $\rho$ via Reynolds number.
- **Biomedical devices**: blood is a *non-Newtonian* (pseudo-plastic) fluid — using water viscosity in a blood pump gives large errors.
- **3D printing & food industry**: thixotropic and shear-thinning behaviour governs extrusion of pastes and sauces.
- **Pipeline transport of crude oil**: Bingham plastics like waxy crudes must be heated or sheared to flow.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Specific Weight Relation

**Given:** A fluid of mass density $\rho$ under gravitational acceleration $g$.

**Step 1.** Mass of an elemental fluid volume $V$ is
$$m = \rho V$$

**Step 2.** Weight of this element is
$$W = m \cdot g = \rho V g$$

**Step 3.** Specific weight, by definition, is *weight per unit volume*, so divide by $V$:
$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{\rho V g}{V} = \rho g$$

**Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho g}$$

This relation is used in **hydrostatics** to compute pressure at depth, $P = \rho g h = \gamma h$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Newton's Law of Viscosity from a Force Balance

**Setup.** Two large parallel plates separated by a fluid film of thickness $h$. The lower plate is fixed; the upper plate of area $A$ is pulled with a tangential force $F$ and moves at steady velocity $U$.

**Step 1.** The fluid layers adhere to the plates (**no-slip condition**):
- Lower plate layer has velocity $0$.
- Upper plate layer has velocity $U$.

**Step 2.** For a thin film, the velocity distribution is linear:
$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

**Step 3.** The velocity gradient is therefore constant:
$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 4.** Newton postulated that the **shear stress** on the fluid is proportional to the velocity gradient:
$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5.** The constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 6.** The required pulling force is the shear stress multiplied by the contact area:
$$F = \tau A = \mu A \frac{U}{h}$$

**Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{F = \mu A \frac{U}{h}}$$

This is the **Couette flow** result, and it is the *experimental origin* of Newton's law of viscosity. It also explains why $\mu$ has units of $\text{Pa·s}$:
$$\mu = \frac{F h}{A U} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [\mu] = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m/s}} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} \cdot \text{s} = \text{Pa·s}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bulk Modulus of an Ideal Gas (Isothermal Process)

**Step 1.** For an ideal gas, the equation of state is
$$PV = mRT \quad \text{(per unit mass: } P = \rho R T \text{)}$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate $P = \rho R T$ with respect to $\rho$ at constant $T$ (isothermal):
$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = RT = \frac{P}{\rho}$$

**Step 3.** By definition of bulk modulus:
$$K = \rho \frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute the derivative:
$$K = \rho \cdot \frac{P}{\rho} = P$$

**Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{K_{\text{isothermal}} = P}$$

**For air at standard atmosphere** ($P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$):
$$K_{air} \approx 1.01 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

This is **~20,000 times smaller** than that of water — that's why gases are highly compressible while liquids are practically incompressible.

> [!NOTE]
> For *adiabatic* processes, the corresponding relation is $K_{\text{adiabatic}} = \gamma P$, where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats. For air, $\gamma \approx 1.4$, so $K_{adiabatic} \approx 1.4 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Relation Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

**Given:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ and $\gamma = \rho g$.

**Step 1.** Rearrange Newton's law for $\mu$:
$$\mu = \tau \cdot \frac{dy}{du}$$

**Step 2.** Define kinematic viscosity as the ratio $\nu = \mu / \rho$:
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 3.** Units:
$$[\nu] = \frac{\text{Pa·s}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{N·s/m}^2}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \frac{\text{kg·m/s}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{with units } \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Capillary Rise

**Setup.** A glass tube of radius $r$ dipped in a wetting liquid (contact angle $\theta$).

**Step 1.** Surface tension acts along the contact line of circumference $2\pi r$. The vertical component of this force is
$$F_{\text{up}} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 2.** This upward force supports the weight of the liquid column of height $h$:
$$W = \rho g \cdot (\pi r^2 h)$$

**Step 3.** At equilibrium, $F_{\text{up}} = W$:
$$\sigma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta = \rho g \pi r^2 h$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $h$:
$$h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

**Conclusion.**
$$\boxed{h = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- **Smaller tubes → greater rise** (inverse with $r$).
- **Wetting liquids** ($\theta < 90^\circ$) → positive $h$ (rise).
- **Non-wetting liquids** like mercury ($\theta > 90^\circ$) → $\cos\theta < 0$ → **depression**.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Specific Weight and Viscosity Calculations

**Problem.** A storage tank contains $0.5 \text{ m}^3$ of a lubricant oil. The mass of the oil is $440 \text{ kg}$. A thin layer of $0.5 \text{ mm}$ thickness between two plates of $1 \text{ m}^2$ area requires $0.985 \text{ N}$ to move the top plate at $0.3 \text{ m/s}$. Find:
(a) the specific weight of the oil,
(b) the dynamic viscosity,
(c) the kinematic viscosity.

**Solution (a):**
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{440}{0.5} = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$\gamma = \rho g = 880 \times 9.81 = 8632.8 \text{ N/m}^3$$
$$\boxed{\gamma \approx 8.63 \text{ kN/m}^3}$$

**Solution (b):**
From Newton's law: $F = \mu A \dfrac{U}{h}$, so
$$\mu = \frac{F h}{A U} = \frac{0.985 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 0.3} = 1.642 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$$
$$\boxed{\mu \approx 1.642 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**Solution (c):**
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{1.642 \times 10^{-3}}{880} = 1.866 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$
$$\boxed{\nu \approx 1.87 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare with water at $20^\circ\text{C}$: $\mu_w = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, so this oil is **about 1.64 times more viscous** than water — a typical result for a light machine lubricant.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fluid Property Relations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mass Density rho] --> B[Specific Weight gamma]
    A --> C[Specific Gravity S]
    A --> D[Kinematic Viscosity nu]
    E[Dynamic Viscosity mu] --> D
    E --> F[Newton Law of Viscosity]
    F --> G[Shear Stress tau]
    A --> H[Compressibility beta]
    H --> I[Bulk Modulus K]
    J[Surface Tension sigma] --> K[Capillary Rise h]
    L[Contact Angle theta] --> K
    A --> K
    M[Vapour Pressure Pv] --> N[Cavitation Event]
    O[Local Pressure P] --> N
    A --> P[Ideal Gas PV = mRT]
    P --> I
    N --> Q[Pump and Turbine Damage]
```

**How to read the architecture:**
- **Source properties** (boxes A, E, J, M) are the *roots*.
- **Derived properties** (boxes B, C, D, I, K) are computed from roots.
- **Engineering consequences** (boxes N, Q) branch off the critical threshold variable (vapour pressure).

## 4.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Bottom Plate fixed, u=0] --> B[Linear Velocity Profile u_y = U*y/h]
    B --> C[Velocity Gradient du/dy = U/h constant]
    C --> D[Shear Stress tau = mu * U/h]
    D --> E[Required Force F = tau * A]
    E --> F[Apply Tangential Pull on Top Plate]
    F --> G[Top Plate moves at Velocity U]
```

## 4.3 Types of Fluids — Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    X[Fluid] --> Y[Ideal Fluid]
    X --> Z[Real Fluid]
    Y --> Y1[Zero viscosity]
    Y --> Y2[Incompressible]
    Z --> Z1[Newtonian]
    Z --> Z2[Non-Newtonian]
    Z1 --> Z1a[Water, Air, Mercury, Light Oils]
    Z2 --> Z2a[Pseudo-plastic shear-thinning]
    Z2 --> Z2b[Dilatant shear-thickening]
    Z2 --> Z2c[Bingham Plastic yield stress]
    Z2 --> Z2d[Thixotropic time-dep decrease]
    Z2 --> Z2e[Rheopectic time-dep increase]
    Z2 --> Z2f[Viscoelastic elastic recovery]
```

## 4.4 Behavioural Curves — Shear Stress vs Shear Strain Rate

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Shear Strain Rate du/dy] --> B[Shear Stress tau]
    B --> C1[Newtonian: straight line through origin]
    B --> C2[Ideal Bingham: straight line with yield stress offset]
    B --> C3[Pseudo-plastic: concave down curve]
    B --> C4[Dilatant: concave up curve]
    C1 --> D1[Slope = mu constant]
    C2 --> D2[Slope = mu after tau_y]
    C3 --> D3[Apparent viscosity decreases]
    C4 --> D4[Apparent viscosity increases]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these curves for KTU 2024:**
> - The **slope** of the curve at any point = *apparent viscosity* $\mu_a$.
> - **Pseudo-plastic** curves bow *downward* (slope decreases with shear rate).
> - **Dilatant** curves bow *upward* (slope increases with shear rate).
> - **Bingham plastic** has a *horizontal intercept* on the $\tau$ axis (the yield stress $\tau_y$).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme note:** Part A questions are *direct*, *definition-based*, and *formula-based*. They require crisp 3–5 line answers, no lengthy derivations. A well-structured answer with a labelled diagram (where relevant) fetches **full marks**.

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model 1]

**Define a fluid. How does it differ from a solid with respect to the application of shear stress?**

**Model Answer:**
A fluid is a substance that *continuously deforms* under the action of an applied shear stress, no matter how small that stress is. When a shear stress is applied, the fluid layers slide relative to each other and the deformation grows with time.
A **solid** resists shear elastically up to a fixed limit (yield point) and then either deforms plastically or fractures. A **fluid** has *no fixed shear limit* — even an infinitesimally small shear stress produces a finite *rate* of deformation. This is the fundamental mechanical distinction between the two states of matter.

> **Examiner's tip:** Always mention that the deformation is *continuous* and *time-dependent* in a fluid — that single phrase often differentiates a 3-mark answer from a 2-mark answer.

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model 1]

**What is meant by Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids? Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Newtonian fluid** obeys Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$, where $\mu$ is constant (independent of shear rate). The shear stress–shear strain rate relationship is a *straight line through the origin*.
**Examples:** Water, air, mercury, gasoline, alcohol.

A **Non-Newtonian fluid** does **not** obey Newton's law. Its apparent viscosity $\mu_a$ varies with the applied shear rate. The $\tau$ vs $du/dy$ relationship is **non-linear** (or has a yield stress).
**Examples:** Blood (pseudo-plastic), tooth paste (Bingham plastic), paint (pseudo-plastic or thixotropic), wet sand (dilatant), quicksand (thixotropic).

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme regulation:** Each Module question (14 marks) carries an *internal choice* — candidates must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B, *not both*. Each part (a) and (b) is typically 7 marks. Sub-questions may have further sub-parts (i) and (ii).

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]

**(a)** Distinguish between the following pairs of properties, with one practical example for each:
   (i) Mass density and specific weight (3 marks)
   (ii) Dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity (2 marks)
   (iii) Bulk modulus and compressibility (2 marks)

**(b)** A liquid of mass $1500 \text{ kg}$ fills a volume of $1.5 \text{ m}^3$. The liquid is placed between two parallel plates $1.2 \text{ mm}$ apart. The upper plate of area $0.6 \text{ m}^2$ is pulled with a force of $0.45 \text{ N}$ and moves at a steady velocity of $0.25 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate:
   (i) The specific weight of the liquid. (2 marks)
   (ii) The dynamic viscosity $\mu$. (2 marks)
   (iii) The kinematic viscosity $\nu$. (2 marks)
   (iv) Comment on whether this liquid behaves as a Newtonian fluid. (1 mark)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Solution to (a)

**(i) Mass density vs Specific weight:**
Mass density $\rho$ is mass per unit volume ($\text{kg/m}^3$). Specific weight $\gamma$ is weight per unit volume ($\text{N/m}^3$). They are related by $\gamma = \rho g$. **Example:** Water — $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$.

**(ii) Dynamic viscosity vs Kinematic viscosity:**
Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is the *absolute* resistance to shear — has units $\text{Pa·s}$. Kinematic viscosity $\nu = \mu/\rho$ incorporates density — has units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and represents momentum diffusivity. **Example:** Air at $20^\circ\text{C}$ — $\mu \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$, $\nu \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

**(iii) Bulk modulus vs Compressibility:**
Bulk modulus $K$ is the ratio of pressure increase to *volumetric strain* (unit: Pa) — measures the *resistance* to compression. Compressibility $\beta = 1/K$ (unit: $\text{Pa}^{-1}$) measures the *ease* of compression. **Example:** Water — $K \approx 2.1 \text{ GPa}$, $\beta \approx 4.75 \times 10^{-10} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$.

#### Solution to (b)

**(i) Specific weight:**
Given $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$, $V = 1.5 \text{ m}^3$, so

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{1500}{1.5} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\gamma = \rho g = 1000 \times 9.81 = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3}$$

**(ii) Dynamic viscosity:**
From $F = \mu A \dfrac{U}{h}$,

$$\mu = \frac{F h}{A U} = \frac{0.45 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.6 \times 0.25}$$

$$\mu = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.15} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**(iii) Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1000} = 3.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 3.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**(iv) Newtonian behaviour:**
The problem assumes a *linear* velocity profile and a *single value* of $\mu$ across the gap. This is consistent with a Newtonian fluid. The computed value $\mu = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$ is approximately **3.6 times** that of water at $20^\circ\text{C}$, suggesting a *light oil* — a common Newtonian liquid. Hence, the liquid can be treated as a **Newtonian fluid** for engineering purposes.

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Alternative]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the Newton's law of viscosity. Define the following:
   (i) Coefficient of dynamic viscosity (2 marks)
   (ii) Coefficient of kinematic viscosity (2 marks)
   (iii) State and explain three types of non-Newtonian fluids with examples. (3 marks)

**(b)** A small capillary tube of internal diameter $0.8 \text{ mm}$ is inserted into a liquid of specific gravity $1.2$ and surface tension $0.072 \text{ N/m}$. The contact angle between the liquid and glass is $20^\circ$. Calculate:
   (i) The capillary rise of the liquid in the tube. (3 marks)
   (ii) If the same tube is dipped into mercury of surface tension $0.51 \text{ N/m}$ and contact angle $130^\circ$, what will be the capillary depression? (3 marks)
   (iii) Briefly state two practical situations where capillary rise/depression is significant. (1 mark)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Solution to (a)

**Newton's law of viscosity (sketch required):**

A thin fluid film of thickness $h$ is sandwiched between two large parallel plates. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate is pulled with a tangential force $F$ over area $A$ and moves at steady velocity $U$. Due to the **no-slip condition**, the fluid in contact with the upper plate moves at $U$ and the fluid in contact with the lower plate is at rest. The velocity profile is linear (for thin film, low Reynolds number):

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

Newton's law states that the shear stress $\tau$ is proportional to the velocity gradient:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

The proportionality constant $\mu$ is the **coefficient of dynamic viscosity**.

**(i) Coefficient of dynamic viscosity:**
$\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ — the shear stress required to produce a unit velocity gradient. It is a measure of the *internal friction* of the fluid.
**SI Unit:** $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa·s}$.
**Physical meaning:** High $\mu$ → "thick" or "sticky" fluid (e.g., honey, tar); low $\mu$ → "thin" fluid (e.g., water, air).

**(ii) Coefficient of kinematic viscosity:**
$\nu = \mu / \rho$ — dynamic viscosity per unit density.
**SI Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
**Physical meaning:** Represents the *diffusivity of momentum* in a fluid — it is the kinematic analogue of thermal diffusivity. Used in Reynolds number $Re = VL/\nu$.

**(iii) Three types of Non-Newtonian fluids:**

1. **Pseudo-plastic (shear-thinning):** Apparent viscosity *decreases* with increasing shear rate (curve bows downward). **Examples:** Blood, paint, ketchup, milk, polymer solutions. *Practical note:* This is why paints are easy to brush but stay put on walls after application.

2. **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** Apparent viscosity *increases* with increasing shear rate (curve bows upward). **Examples:** Wet sand, corn starch suspension, quicksand (under sudden load). *Practical note:* Dilatant fluids resist sudden impact — used in body armour composites.

3. **Bingham plastic:** Behaves like a solid until a *yield stress* $\tau_y$ is exceeded, then flows linearly. The $\tau$–$du/dy$ plot is a straight line with a *positive intercept* on the $\tau$ axis. **Examples:** Toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise, sewage sludge. *Practical note:* Must be pushed out of the tube (above $\tau_y$) but then flows smoothly.

#### Solution to (b)

**(i) Capillary rise:**

Given: $r = d/2 = 0.4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $S = 1.2$, $\sigma = 0.072 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 20^\circ$, $\rho = S \cdot \rho_w = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times \cos 20^\circ}{1200 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$h = \frac{0.144 \times 0.9397}{4.7088} = \frac{0.1353}{4.7088}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 0.02873 \text{ m} = 28.73 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Capillary depression in mercury:**

Given: $\sigma_{Hg} = 0.51 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta_{Hg} = 130^\circ$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} = \frac{2 \times 0.51 \times \cos 130^\circ}{13600 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$h = \frac{1.02 \times (-0.6428)}{53.3664} = \frac{-0.6556}{53.3664}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx -0.01229 \text{ m} = -12.29 \text{ mm (depression)}}$$

The negative sign indicates a *depression* (mercury level inside the tube is **below** the outside level), because mercury does not wet glass ($\theta > 90^\circ$).

**(iii) Practical situations of capillary rise/depression:**

1. **Rise of oil in lamp wicks and plant xylem** — both depend on capillary action in narrow channels.
2. **Depression of mercury in a barometer tube** — the height of the mercury column is reduced by capillary depression, requiring a correction factor in precise barometric measurements.
3. (Bonus) **Moisture rising in building walls** from the ground, and **ink absorption in paper** during writing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units**: $r$ must be in **metres** (not mm), $\sigma$ in N/m. A common mistake is using $r = 0.4$ mm directly in the formula — this gives an answer 1000× too large. Always convert mm to m **first**.
> 2. **Sign of $\cos\theta$**: For $\theta > 90^\circ$, $\cos\theta$ is **negative** — explicitly write the negative sign in your calculation. Many students write $h = +12.29 \text{ mm}$ for mercury without indicating the depression.
> 3. **Forgetting $g$**: Some students write $h = 2\sigma \cos\theta / (\rho r)$ — that's dimensionally wrong; gravity is essential.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of Newtonian behaviour**: A one-line comment ("we assume a linear velocity profile, hence the fluid is Newtonian") is often required to score the last mark.
> 5. **Mixing up bulk modulus and compressibility**: $K$ has units of Pa, $\beta$ has units of $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ — they are reciprocals, not synonyms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **fluid** is defined by its *inability to sustain a static shear stress* — it deforms continuously under any applied shear.
- [ ] A **Newtonian fluid** obeys $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ with constant $\mu$; **non-Newtonian** fluids have a shear-rate-dependent apparent viscosity.
- [ ] The three primary **Newtonian-fluid** properties: $\rho$, $\mu$, and the relationship $\gamma = \rho g$.
- [ ] The **five sub-types of non-Newtonian fluids** (KTU 2024 high yield): pseudo-plastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic, thixotropic, rheopectic (plus viscoelastic).
- [ ] **Bingham plastic** has a finite **yield stress** — below it, the fluid behaves as a solid.
- [ ] **Pseudo-plastic** = *shear-thinning* (curve bows downward); **dilatant** = *shear-thickening* (curve bows upward).
- [ ] **Thixotropic** = viscosity *decreases over time* at constant shear; **rheopectic** = viscosity *increases over time* at constant shear.
- [ ] **Bulk modulus** $K$ (Pa) is the *resistance* to compression; **compressibility** $\beta$ ($\text{Pa}^{-1}$) is its reciprocal.
- [ ] For an **ideal gas isothermal process**: $K = P$ — so gases at higher pressure are stiffer.
- [ ] **Capillary rise**: $h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ — wetting liquids rise, non-wetting liquids depress.
- [ ] **Vapour pressure** is the threshold below which **cavitation** occurs — critical in pump and turbine design.
- [ ] The **continuum hypothesis** is valid when the Knudsen number $Kn < 0.01$ — this is the standard assumption in all engineering fluid flows.
- [ ] **Common conversion:** $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$; $1 \text{ stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (CGS to SI).
- [ ] **Reference values**: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$, $\mu_w(20^\circ\text{C}) \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for non-Newtonian types**: *"P-D-B-T-R-V"* → **P**seudo-plastic, **D**ilatant, **B**ingham, **T**hixotropic, **R**heopectic, **V**iscoelastic.
- [ ] **Real engineering fluids** are *viscous* and *compressible*; **ideal fluid** is a teaching model only.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton's Law of Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton's Law of Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress ($\tau$) acting between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the rate of change of velocity (velocity gradient) with respect to the distance measured perpendicular to the direction of flow.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity coefficient (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) (s⁻¹)

Fluids that obey this linear relationship are termed **Newtonian Fluids**, and $\mu$ remains constant for a given fluid at a specified temperature and pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
