> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Newton's Law of Viscosity is the foundational postulate of classical fluid mechanics. The coefficient $\mu$ characterizes the internal resistance of a fluid to deformation. For KTU 2024 Scheme, students MUST memorize the SI and CGS units of $\mu$ and $\nu$ (kinematic viscosity), as direct unit-conversion questions are routinely asked in the university exam.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine sliding a heavy book across a table, but instead of a solid book, picture a deck of playing cards glued together on the table. When you push the top card, the friction between cards causes the second one to slide slightly, the third even less, and so on — until the bottom card is stationary (held by the table).

A **real fluid** behaves the same way:
- The layer touching the **stationary boundary** has zero velocity (this is called the **no-slip condition**).
- Each successive layer slides over the one below, with a small relative velocity.
- The faster-moving layers "drag" the slower ones along, transmitting shear stress down the column.

> **The "honey test"**: Pour water and honey down a tilted glass. Water runs fast (low $\mu$), honey creeps slowly (high $\mu$). Both obey Newton's Law, but the **magnitude** of their $\mu$ differs by a factor of ~10,000.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Typical Range (Water at 20°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | **N·s/m² (= Pa·s)** | **poise (P) or g/(cm·s)** | ~1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **m²/s** | **stokes (St) or cm²/s** | ~1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | **N/m² (= Pa)** | **dyne/cm²** | Depends on flow |
| Velocity Gradient | $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ | **s⁻¹** | **s⁻¹** | Depends on flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Quick-Recall:** 1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s = 1 g/(cm·s). 1 stoke = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s. The relation between dynamic and kinematic viscosity is $\nu = \mu / \rho$, where $\rho$ is the fluid density.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physical Postulate

Newton postulated that for a flowing real fluid, the resistance to deformation arises due to **internal molecular cohesion and momentum exchange** between adjacent fluid layers. The key logical steps are:

- **Step 1 — Layered Flow Assumption:** Fluid particles move in parallel layers (laminar flow regime), with no crossing between layers.
- **Step 2 — Velocity Variation Exists:** The velocity $u$ of a fluid layer depends on its perpendicular distance $y$ from a fixed boundary.
- **Step 3 — Newton's Hypothesis:** The shear stress $\tau$ required to maintain this layered motion is proportional to the **velocity gradient** $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ (rate of shear strain).
- **Step 4 — Proportionality Constant:** The constant of proportionality $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity) is a fluid property that depends on temperature (and marginally on pressure for liquids, more so for gases).
- **Step 5 — No-Slip Condition:** Fluid in direct molecular contact with a solid boundary has zero relative velocity with respect to that boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why proportional and not equal?** Because the velocity gradient is a *kinematic* property (geometry of flow), while shear stress is a *dynamic* quantity (force per area). They are linked by a fluid-specific property — the dynamic viscosity $\mu$ — which is essentially a measure of how strongly a fluid resists being "pulled apart" at the molecular level.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity (fundamental) | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity definition | m²/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Shear strain rate (velocity gradient) | s⁻¹ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ | Dynamic viscosity in terms of $\nu$ | Pa·s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Alternate form using shear rate notation | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $1 \text{ poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ | CGS to SI conversion of $\mu$ | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $1 \text{ stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | CGS to SI conversion of $\nu$ | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force on a fluid layer | N | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Newton's Law is the cornerstone for deriving the **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation** (flow through pipes), **Stokes' Law** (sphere drag), **Navier-Stokes Equations** (governing equations of fluid motion), and **lubrication theory**. Without this single postulate, modern fluid mechanics, hydraulic machinery design, and aerodynamics would not exist.

### 2.3 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids — A Critical Distinction

| Fluid Type | $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ Behavior | Examples | Constant $\mu$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | Linear (straight line through origin) | Water, air, gasoline, alcohol, glycerin | **Yes** |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Linear, but with a yield stress | Toothpaste, drilling mud | No (has $\tau_0$) |
| **Pseudo-plastic (Shear-thinning)** | Non-linear, slope decreases | Blood, ketchup, paint | No (apparent $\mu$) |
| **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | Non-linear, slope increases | Starch suspensions, wet sand | No (apparent $\mu$) |
| **Thixotropic / Rheopectic** | Time-dependent | Printing ink, gypsum paste | No (time-dependent) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The phrase "Newton's Law of Viscosity" applies **ONLY** to Newtonian fluids. If a problem mentions blood, sludge, or polymer solutions, the fluid is non-Newtonian, and $\mu$ is NOT a constant. Read the problem statement carefully before applying $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic System Design:** Predicting pressure drops in pipelines carrying lubricating oils.
- **Aerospace:** Computing boundary-layer shear stress on aircraft wings.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling blood flow in arteries (blood is mildly non-Newtonian, but Newtonian assumption is valid for large vessels).
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Sizing pumps for viscous fluids like polymers or syrups.
- **Meteorology:** Wind shear stress on the ocean surface drives ocean currents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Dimensional Check of Newton's Law

We will verify that $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ is dimensionally homogeneous.

**Step 1: Dimensions of $\tau$ (Shear Stress)**
Since $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$, and $F$ has dimensions of $[M L T^{-2}]$, while $A$ has $[L^2]$:

$$[\tau] = \frac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$$

**Step 2: Dimensions of $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ (Velocity Gradient)**
Velocity $u$ has dimensions $[L T^{-1}]$, and $y$ has dimensions $[L]$. Therefore:

$$\left[\frac{du}{dy}\right] = \frac{[L T^{-1}]}{[L]} = [T^{-1}]$$

**Step 3: Solve for the Dimensions of $\mu$**

$$[\mu] = \frac{[\tau]}{[du/dy]} = \frac{[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$$

**Step 4: Verify SI Units**
From the dimensional result $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$, the SI unit must be:

$$1 \text{ kg·m}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1} = 1 \text{ N·s/m}^2 = 1 \text{ Pa·s} \;\;\checkmark$$

This confirms the dimensional homogeneity of Newton's Law of Viscosity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity

By definition, the **kinematic viscosity** $\nu$ is the ratio of dynamic viscosity $\mu$ to fluid density $\rho$:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step-by-step dimensional verification:**

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]}{[M L^{-3}]} = [L^2 T^{-1}]$$

In SI units:

$$[\nu] = \text{m}^2/\text{s} \;\;\checkmark$$

In CGS units, $\nu$ is expressed in **stokes (St)**, where 1 stoke = 1 cm²/s. To convert:

$$1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity Profile for Linear Shear Flow (Couette-Type)

Consider a fluid between two parallel plates separated by a small distance $h$. The lower plate is stationary ($y = 0$, $u = 0$); the upper plate moves with constant velocity $U$ ($y = h$, $u = U$).

**Step 1: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

For steady, fully developed laminar flow with no pressure gradient, $\tau$ is constant throughout the fluid.

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for $du$**

$$du = \frac{\tau}{\mu} \, dy$$

**Step 3: Integrate both sides with appropriate boundary conditions**

$$\int_{0}^{u} du = \frac{\tau}{\mu} \int_{0}^{y} dy$$

$$u = \frac{\tau}{\mu} \, y$$

**Step 4: Apply the upper boundary condition** $u = U$ at $y = h$:

$$U = \frac{\tau}{\mu} \, h \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{\mu U}{h}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back to get the velocity profile**

$$\boxed{u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}}$$

This is a **linear velocity profile** — characteristic of pure Couette flow (plane Couette flow). The shear stress is uniform and equal to $\tau = \mu U / h$, and the velocity gradient (shear rate) is constant: $\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{U}{h}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Force on a Moving Plate (Analytical Matrix)

Consider a rectangular plate of area $A$ floating on a fluid film of thickness $h$, with the plate moving at velocity $U$.

**Step 1: Velocity gradient assumption** — Linear (Couette-type) profile:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{h - 0} = \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Newton's Law of Viscosity**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 3: Compute the viscous resistance force on the plate**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \frac{U}{h}$$

This result is the foundation of **viscous drag** calculations in lubrication and slider-bearing analysis.

| Quantity | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Shear stress | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{U}{h}$ | Force per unit area resisting motion |
| Total drag force | $F = \mu A \dfrac{U}{h}$ | Integrated viscous resistance |
| Power dissipated | $P = F \cdot U = \mu A \dfrac{U^2}{h}$ | Energy lost per unit time as heat |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A fluid with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.9$ N·s/m² flows between two parallel plates 6 mm apart. The upper plate moves at $U = 1.5$ m/s while the lower plate is fixed. Determine:
1. The shear stress on the fluid.
2. The velocity at $y = 2$ mm from the fixed plate.
3. The viscous force on an area of $0.25$ m².

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Shear stress:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h} = 0.9 \times \frac{1.5}{0.006}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 225 \text{ Pa}}$$

**Part 2 — Velocity at $y = 2$ mm:**

Using the linear profile $u(y) = U \cdot \dfrac{y}{h}$:

$$u(0.002) = 1.5 \times \frac{0.002}{0.006}$$

$$\boxed{u = 0.5 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part 3 — Viscous force on $A = 0.25$ m²:**

$$F = \tau \cdot A = 225 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{F = 56.25 \text{ N}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Newton's Law Concept Map

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical architecture of Newton's Law of Viscosity, from physical observation to mathematical formulation and engineering application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Observation: Layered Fluid Motion] --> B[No-Slip Condition at Boundaries]
    A --> C[Velocity Variation Between Layers]
    B --> D[Velocity Gradient du/dy Exists]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Newtons Hypothesis: tau proportional to du/dy]
    E --> F[Mathematical Form: tau = mu times du/dy]
    F --> G[Parameter mu: Dynamic Viscosity]
    F --> H[Parameter rho: Fluid Density]
    H --> I[Derived Quantity: nu = mu divided by rho]
    G --> I
    F --> J[Validation: Dimensional Homogeneity]
    F --> K[Classification: Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian]
    K --> L[Newtonian: mu constant, Linear Behavior]
    K --> M[Non-Newtonian: mu variable, Non-Linear Behavior]
    F --> N[Engineering Applications]
    N --> O[Hagen-Poiseuille Pipe Flow]
    N --> P[Stokes Drag on Sphere]
    N --> Q[Boundary Layer Theory]
    N --> R[Lubrication and Bearing Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at node A (top — physical observation), trace downward through the hypothesis to the formula (node F), and then branch right to see how this single law propagates into two parallel downstream paths — fundamental properties (nodes G–I), and applied engineering domains (nodes N–R).

### 4.2 Schematic Velocity Profile (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fixed Lower Plate: y=0, u=0] --> P2[Linear Velocity Profile: u increases with y]
    P2 --> P3[Moving Upper Plate: y=h, u=U]
    P3 --> P4[Shear Stress Constant Throughout: tau = mu U/h]
    P4 --> P1
```

This represents the classic **plane Couette flow** configuration: the linear profile between two plates generates a uniform shear stress, which is the simplest physical realization of Newton's Law of Viscosity.

### 4.3 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behavior — Schematic Differentiation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Under Shear] --> B{Does tau versus du/dy follow a straight line through origin?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Newtonian Fluid]
    B -- No --> D{What is the shape of the curve?}
    D -- Curves downward: slope decreases with shear --> E[Pseudoplastic: Shear-Thinning]
    D -- Curves upward: slope increases with shear --> F[Dilatant: Shear-Thickening]
    D -- Linear but offset: requires yield stress --> G[Bingham Plastic]
    D -- Time-dependent --> H[Thixotropic or Rheopectic]
    C --> I[Examples: Water, Air, Glycerin]
    E --> J[Examples: Blood, Ketchup, Paint]
    F --> K[Examples: Starch Slurry, Wet Sand]
    G --> L[Examples: Toothpaste, Drilling Mud]
    H --> M[Examples: Printing Ink, Gypsum Paste]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to identify the fluid type, the *first* check is whether the $\tau$ vs. $\dot\gamma$ relationship is **linear and passes through the origin**. If yes, it is Newtonian and Newton's Law applies directly with constant $\mu$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**State Newton's Law of Viscosity. Mention the SI units of dynamic and kinematic viscosity.**

**Model Answer:**

Newton's Law of Viscosity states that the shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, i.e., the velocity gradient ($\dfrac{du}{dy}$) perpendicular to the direction of flow.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\mu$ is the dynamic (absolute) viscosity of the fluid.

- **SI unit of dynamic viscosity ($\mu$):** N·s/m² or Pa·s
- **SI unit of kinematic viscosity ($\nu$):** m²/s, where $\nu = \mu / \rho$

**(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ vs. $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Linear, passes through origin | Non-linear OR has yield stress |
| Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ | Constant (depends only on T, P) | Variable (apparent viscosity) |
| Examples | Water, air, gasoline, glycerin | Blood, ketchup, toothpaste, drilling mud |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 Choice A]

**(a)** Derive Newton's Law of Viscosity from the concept of momentum transfer between adjacent fluid layers. Explain the physical significance of the coefficient of dynamic viscosity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 400 mm × 200 mm plate, weighing 50 N, slides down an inclined surface inclined at 30° to the horizontal, with a constant velocity of 3 m/s. The inclined surface is lubricated by a 0.4 mm thick oil film. Determine the dynamic viscosity of the oil. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

Consider two adjacent fluid layers A (lower) and B (upper) separated by a small distance $dy$. The upper layer B moves with velocity $u + du$ relative to the lower layer A which moves with velocity $u$.

**Step 1:** The faster-moving layer B experiences a **retarding viscous force** $F$ from the slower layer A.

**Step 2:** By Newton's third law, layer A experiences an equal and opposite **dragging force** from B.

**Step 3:** Newton hypothesized that this force $F$ is:
- Proportional to the **area of contact** $A$ between layers.
- Proportional to the **relative velocity** $du$ between layers.
- Inversely proportional to the **distance** $dy$ between layers.

**Step 4:** Mathematically:

$$F \propto A \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5:** Introducing the proportionality constant $\mu$:

$$F = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 6:** Dividing both sides by area $A$:

$$\frac{F}{A} = \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

This is Newton's Law of Viscosity.

**Physical Significance of $\mu$:**

$\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity coefficient. Its physical significance:
1. It is a measure of the fluid's **resistance to deformation** (shear).
2. Higher $\mu$ means the fluid flows slowly (e.g., honey vs. water).
3. It depends primarily on **temperature** — for liquids, $\mu$ decreases with rising T; for gases, $\mu$ increases with rising T.
4. Its value characterizes whether the fluid is Newtonian (constant $\mu$) or non-Newtonian (variable apparent $\mu$).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Plate dimensions: $L \times b = 0.4$ m × $0.2$ m
- Plate weight: $W = 50$ N
- Inclination angle: $\theta = 30°$
- Velocity: $U = 3$ m/s (constant → equilibrium)
- Oil film thickness: $h = 0.4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m

**Step 1: Compute the area of contact**

$$A = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply equilibrium (constant velocity → net force = 0)**

The component of weight acting down the incline must equal the viscous resistance:

$$W \sin\theta = F_{\text{viscous}}$$

$$F_{\text{viscous}} = 50 \times \sin 30° = 50 \times 0.5 = 25 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Express viscous force using Newton's Law**

$$F_{\text{viscous}} = \mu A \frac{U}{h}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\mu$**

$$\mu = \frac{F_{\text{viscous}} \cdot h}{A \cdot U} = \frac{25 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.08 \times 3}$$

$$\mu = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{0.24} = \frac{0.01}{0.24}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.04167 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 Choice B]

**(a)** Define the following with their SI units: (i) Dynamic viscosity, (ii) Kinematic viscosity, (iii) Fluidity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of diameter 80 mm rotates at 1800 rpm inside a journal bearing. The bearing is 120 mm long, and the radial clearance (oil film thickness) is 0.15 mm. If the dynamic viscosity of the lubricating oil is 0.05 Pa·s, find: (i) the shear stress in the oil film, and (ii) the viscous torque required to overcome friction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions:**

**(i) Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the ratio of shear stress to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) in a fluid. It represents the fluid's internal resistance to flow.

- **SI unit:** N·s/m² = Pa·s
- **Mathematical form:** $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$

**(ii) Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under the action of gravity.

- **SI unit:** m²/s
- **Mathematical form:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$

**(iii) Fluidity ($\phi$):** It is the reciprocal of dynamic viscosity. It is a measure of how easily a fluid flows.

- **SI unit:** m²/(N·s) or 1/Pa·s or m²/kg
- **Mathematical form:** $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Shaft diameter: $D = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- Rotational speed: $N = 1800$ rpm
- Bearing length: $L = 120$ mm $= 0.12$ m
- Oil film thickness: $h = 0.15$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s

**Step 1: Compute the tangential velocity of the shaft surface**

$$U = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.08 \times 1800}{60}$$

$$U = \frac{452.39}{60} = 7.54 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the velocity gradient (shear rate)**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h} = \frac{7.54}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.027 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Compute the shear stress in the oil film**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.05 \times 5.027 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 2513.27 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.513 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the viscous force on the shaft surface**

The contact area is the curved surface of the shaft inside the bearing:

$$A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.08 \times 0.12 = 0.03016 \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{\text{viscous}} = \tau \cdot A = 2513.27 \times 0.03016 = 75.80 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Compute the viscous torque**

The torque is the force multiplied by the shaft radius ($r = D/2 = 0.04$ m):

$$T = F_{\text{viscous}} \times r = 75.80 \times 0.04$$

$$\boxed{T = 3.032 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert mm to m in oil film thickness ($h$) is the most common mistake. Always work in SI units.
> 2. **Mixing up Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity (–2 marks):** If the question provides density, you must use $\nu$ correctly. Do NOT equate $\mu$ with $\nu$.
> 3. **Forgetting "no-slip condition" (–1 mark):** The boundary layer velocity is zero at the fixed wall. Explicitly state this assumption.
> 4. **Wrong formula for rotational shear rate (–1 mark):** For a rotating shaft, use $U = \pi D N / 60$, NOT $U = 2 \pi r N$.
> 5. **Sign convention for shear stress (–1 mark):** The viscous force on the shaft opposes motion; do not write it as an aiding force.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity** is written as $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$. The constant $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity) has units of **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**.
- ✅ **Kinematic viscosity** is $\nu = \mu / \rho$ with SI unit **m²/s**. CGS unit is the **stoke** (1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s).
- ✅ **1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s**. Common exam conversion: dynamic viscosity of water ≈ 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s.
- ✅ The law applies **only to Newtonian fluids** (linear $\tau$ vs. $\dot\gamma$ relation). Non-Newtonian fluids have variable apparent viscosity.
- ✅ **No-slip condition:** Velocity of fluid = velocity of bounding solid surface (zero at a fixed wall).
- ✅ For a **plane Couette flow** between two plates, the velocity profile is **linear** and the shear stress is **uniform**: $\tau = \mu U / h$.
- ✅ **Effect of temperature:** For liquids, $\mu$ **decreases** with temperature; for gases, $\mu$ **increases** with temperature.
- ✅ **Fluidity** $\phi = 1/\mu$ is the reciprocal of dynamic viscosity.
- ✅ **Shear stress is tangential**, not normal — always parallel to the fluid layer surface, not perpendicular.
- ✅ The law forms the basis of **Hagen-Poiseuille flow, Navier-Stokes equations, and lubrication theory**.
- ✅ Memorize the linear velocity profile equation $u(y) = U \cdot (y/h)$ — it appears in over 60% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.
- ✅ The dimensional formula of $\mu$ is $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$; of $\nu$ is $[L^2 T^{-1}]$.
- ✅ Viscous force on a moving plate: $F = \mu A \cdot \dfrac{U}{h}$. Viscous power dissipated: $P = \mu A \cdot \dfrac{U^2}{h}$.
- ✅ **For an inclined plane sliding problem:** equate the component of weight along the incline to the viscous resistance force.
- ✅ **For a journal bearing problem:** use $U = \pi D N / 60$ for the linear velocity of the shaft surface.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Measurement: Fluid Pressure, Pressure Head and Types of Pressures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Pressure Measurement: Fluid Pressure, Pressure Head and Types of Pressures # 📘 MODULE 1 — PRESSURE MEASUREMENT ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🔷 What is Fluid Pressure? **Fluid Pressure (P)** is defined as the **normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area** on any surface (real or imaginary) in contact with the fluid. It is a **scalar quantity** because pressure at a point acts equally in all directions (Pascal's Principle). $$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$ Its **SI unit** is **Pascal (Pa)** equivalent to **N/m²**, with practical multiples being **kPa** and **MPa**. In the CGS system, it is expressed in **bar** or **kgf/cm²**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)** > The fundamental triad in this topic is: > 1. **Fluid Pressure** (the physical force per area) > 2. **Pressure Head** (height of fluid column equivalent to a given pressure) > 3. **Types of Pressures** (Atmospheric, Gauge, Vacuum, Absolute) --- ### 🌊 Intuitive Analogy — "The Diver and the Balloon" Imagine you are a diver descending into a swimming pool: - At the surface, water gently pushes on your eardrums (low pressure). - The deeper you go, the more your ears hurt (high pressure). **Why?** Because the weight of all the water *above* you is being supported by your body. Pressure is essentially the **"weight of the fluid column stacked above a point."** > A simple **balloon** is a perfect visual: when you squeeze a balloon in one spot, it bulges everywhere else. This is because fluid pressure acts **equally in all directions** at a point inside the fluid. --- ### 🔷 What is Pressure Head? **Pressure Head (h)** is the **height of a fluid column** that would produce a given pressure at its base. It is a convenient way to express pressure in terms of **length (meters of fluid)** rather than force per area. $$h = \frac{P}{\rho g}$$ where $\rho$ is fluid density and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Note** > Pressure head and pressure are not the same thing! Pressure is an *intensity* (Pa), while pressure head is a *length* (m). They are related through the fluid's specific weight ($\gamma = \rho g$). --- ### 🔷 The Four Fundamental Types of Pressures | Type | Symbol | Definition | |------|--------|------------| | **Atmospheric Pressure** | $P_{atm}$ | Pressure exerted by the **weight of the earth's atmosphere** (≈ 101.325 kPa at sea level). | | **Absolute Pressure** | $P_{abs}$ | Pressure measured from the **absolute zero** (perfect vacuum) reference. | | **Gauge Pressure** | $P_{gauge}$ | Pressure measured **above atmospheric** pressure. | | **Vacuum / Negative Pressure** | $P_{vac}$ | Pressure **below atmospheric** pressure (a "suction" condition). | The **master relationship** is: $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$ $$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** > In **all manometer problems** and **hydraulic machine questions** in the KTU University Exam, you MUST clarify whether the given pressure is **absolute or gauge** before substituting into formulas. A common error costs 1–2 marks. # 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure — The Foundation of Pressure Measurement When a fluid is **at rest**, the only stress acting on it is **normal stress** (pressure). The shear stress is zero. The variation of pressure with depth forms the cornerstone of all pressure measurement devices. ### 🔶 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Hydrostatic Pressure 1. **Isolating a fluid element:** Consider a small vertical column of fluid of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $h$. 2. **Force balance:** The vertical forces are the weight of the column (downward) and the pressure forces on top and bottom. 3. **Equilibrium condition:** $\sum F_{vertical} = 0$. 4. **Result:** Pressure increases **linearly** with depth. For a fluid of constant density $\rho$: $$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$ where: - $P_0$ = pressure at the free surface (reference) - $\rho$ = mass density of fluid (kg/m³) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) - $h$ = vertical depth below the free surface (m) --- ## 2.2 Pascal's Law — The Operating Principle of Hydraulic Systems > [!NOTE] > **Pascal's Law (1651):** > *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the walls of the containing vessel."* **Mathematical Statement:** $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$ This is the working principle behind **hydraulic lifts, hydraulic brakes, hydraulic jacks, and hydraulic presses**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Basic Pressure | $P = F/A$ | Pa or N/m² | Scalar quantity | | 2 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa | Linear with depth | | 3 | Pressure Head | $h = P/(\rho g)$ | m of fluid | Length representation | | 4 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | For water: **9810 N/m³** | | 5 | Absolute ↔ Gauge | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Pa | Standard atmosphere = **101.325 kPa** | | 6 | Vacuum Pressure | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Pa | Always positive value | | 7 | Pascal's Law | $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$ | — | Force multiplication | | 8 | Total Pressure on Submerged Surface | $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ | N | $\bar{h}$ = depth of centroid | | 9 | Manometer Equation (U-tube) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Pa | $\rho_m$ = manometer fluid | | 10 | Piezometer Reading | $h = P/(\rho g)$ | m | Open tube manometer | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Atmospheric Pressure values for KTU problems:** > - $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa > - $P_{atm} = 1.01325$ bar > - $P_{atm} = 10.33$ m of water column > - $P_{atm} = 760$ mm of Hg > - $P_{atm} = 1.033$ kgf/cm² --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Pressure Principle Used | |-------------|-------------------------| | **Hydraulic Lift / Jack** | Pascal's Law — small force lifts heavy loads | | **Car Brake System** | Pascal's Law — pedal force amplified at wheel cylinders | | **Water Supply Tank** | Hydrostatic pressure — tank bottom pressure = $\rho g h$ | | **Barometer** | Atmospheric pressure balances mercury column | | **Bourdon Gauge** | Gauge pressure deforms curved tube into pointer movement | | **Pitot Tube** | Measures flow velocity from stagnation + static pressure | | **Manometers in pipelines** | Differential pressure measurement using fluid columns | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Numerical problems on **U-tube manometers, inclined manometers, and differential manometers** appear almost every semester in the ESE Module 1 paper. Master the **sign convention** for height differences first. # 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Variation with Depth ### Statement *Prove that in a static incompressible fluid, pressure varies linearly with depth according to $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.* ### Setup Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ at a depth $h$ below the free surface of an open tank containing a liquid of density $\rho$. ### Forces Acting on the Element 1. **Pressure force from above** (pushing down): $P_0 \cdot dA$ 2. **Weight of the fluid element** (pulling down): $W = (\text{mass}) \cdot g = (\rho \cdot dA \cdot dh) \cdot g = \rho g \, dh \, dA$ 3. **Pressure force from below** (pushing up): $P \cdot dA$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply static equilibrium** (sum of vertical forces = 0, since fluid is at rest): $$ \begin{aligned} \sum F_{vertical} &= 0 \\ P \, dA - P_0 \, dA - \rho g \, dh \, dA &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Cancel the common term $dA$ from all sides:** $$ \begin{aligned} P - P_0 - \rho g \, dh &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $P$:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= P_0 + \rho g \, dh \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate over a finite depth** from the free surface (where $P = P_0$, $h = 0$) to a depth $h$ (where pressure is $P$): $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{P_0}^{P} dP &= \int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dh \\ P - P_0 &= \rho g \, h \\ P &= P_0 + \rho g h \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Head Relationship ### Statement *Derive the relationship between pressure and pressure head: $h = P / (\rho g)$.* ### Derivation **Step 1 — Start from the hydrostatic equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= P_0 + \rho g h \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — If the surface is exposed to atmospheric pressure, $P_0 = P_{atm}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{gauge} &= P_{abs} - P_{atm} = \rho g h \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $h$:** $$ \begin{aligned} h &= \frac{P_{gauge}}{\rho g} = \frac{P}{\gamma} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ where $\gamma = \rho g$ is the **specific weight** of the fluid. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Absolute and Gauge Pressure Relationship ### Statement *Establish the relationship between absolute, gauge, and atmospheric pressures.* ### Derivation **Step 1 — Define absolute pressure** as the total pressure measured from absolute zero (vacuum): $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Define gauge pressure** as the pressure above atmospheric: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{gauge} &= P_{abs} - P_{atm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Define vacuum pressure** as the shortfall below atmospheric: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{vac} &= P_{atm} - P_{abs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Combine the relations:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs} + P_{vac} &= 2 P_{atm} \end{aligned} $$ > This last relation is a **favourite KTU question** for 3-mark or short-answer sections. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — U-Tube Manometer Equation (Differential) ### Statement *Derive the equation for a simple U-tube differential manometer connecting two points A and B in a pipe carrying a fluid of density $\rho_f$, with a manometer fluid (mercury, $\rho_m$) showing a deflection $h$.* ### Derivation **Step 1 — Start at point A** and trace the manometer fluid column. Apply **hydrostatic balance** as we go down and up. **Step 2 — Going from A down to the mercury interface on the left limb:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= P_A + \rho_f \, g \, z_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Crossing the manometer fluid** (mercury), going up on the right limb: $$ \begin{aligned} P_2 &= P_1 - \rho_m \, g \, h \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Going up through the pipe fluid to point B:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_B &= P_2 - \rho_f \, g \, z_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Combining and eliminating $z_1, z_2$** (taking elevation difference as $z_1 - z_2 = z$): $$ \begin{aligned} P_A - P_B &= (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h + \rho_f g z \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — For a horizontal pipe** ($z = 0$): $$ \begin{aligned} P_A - P_B &= (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention used in KTU Board Valuations:** > - $P_A > P_B$ → $h$ is taken as **positive** (mercury depressed on the higher-pressure side). > - $P_A < P_B$ → $h$ is taken as **negative** (mercury rises on the higher-pressure side). > - Heights of the **lighter fluid (pipe fluid)** are added; heights of the **heavier fluid (manometer fluid)** are subtracted. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Pressure Measurement Devices | Device | Measures | Working Principle | Best For | Limitation | |--------|----------|-------------------|----------|------------| | **Piezometer** | Gauge pressure of liquid only | Hydrostatic column rise | Simple lab setups | Cannot measure gas pressure or vacuum | | **U-Tube Manometer** | Gauge / differential pressure | Two-liquid column balance | Pipeline differential | Bulky, slow response | | **Inclined Manometer** | Small gauge pressures | Magnified reading by inclination | Low pressures (draft) | Needs careful levelling | | **Bourdon Gauge** | Gauge pressure | Curved tube straightens under pressure | Industrial, wide range | Mechanical wear over time | | **Dead-Weight Gauge** | Absolute / gauge (calibration) | Known weights on piston | Calibration standard | Not portable, expensive | | **Barometer** | Atmospheric pressure | Mercury column balanced by $P_{atm}$ | Meteorology | Fixed reference only | | **Pirani / Strain Gauge** | Very low pressures | Thermal / strain-based | Vacuum systems | Specialized calibration | # 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Pressure Variation System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Free Surface
Pressure P0] --> B[Fluid Element
Cross-section dA] B --> C[Depth h below surface] C --> D[Weight of column
W = rho g h dA] D --> E[Apply Force Balance
sum F vertical = 0] E --> F[Pressure at depth
P = P0 + rho g h] F --> G[Pressure Head
h = P divided by rho g] G --> H[Output: Total Pressure
on submerged surface
F = rho g h_bar times A] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Types of Pressures and Their Relationships ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pressure at a Point] --> B{Measured w.r.t. what?} B -->|Absolute Vacuum| C[Absolute Pressure
Pabs] B -->|Atmospheric Reference| D[Gauge Pressure
Pgauge] B -->|Below Atmospheric| E[Vacuum Pressure
Pvac] C --> F[Master Relation
Pabs = Patm + Pgauge] D --> F E --> G[Vacuum Relation
Pvac = Patm minus Pabs] F --> H[KTU Board Use:
Always state reference!] G --> H ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — U-Tube Manometer Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Point A in Pipe
Pressure PA] --> A1[Pipe Fluid
Density rho f] A1 --> B1[Left Limb
descends] B1 --> C[Manometer Fluid
Mercury rho m] C --> B2[Right Limb
ascends by h] B2 --> A2[Point B in Pipe
Pressure PB] A2 --> A3[Pipe Fluid
Density rho f] C --> D[Deflection h
read by observer] D --> E[Equation:
PA minus PB equals rho m minus rho f times g times h] E --> F[Output:
Differential Pressure] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Pressure Measurement Device Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pressure Measurement Devices] --> B[Liquid Column Type] A --> C[Mechanical Type] A --> D[Electronic Type] B --> B1[Piezometer] B --> B2[U-Tube Manometer] B --> B3[Inclined Manometer] B --> B4[Differential Manometer] C --> C1[Bourdon Tube Gauge] C --> C2[Diaphragm Gauge] C --> C3[Dead Weight Tester] D --> D1[Strain Gauge Transducer] D --> D2[Pirani Gauge] D --> D3[Capacitance Manometer] ``` > [!TIP] > **Study Tip:** The four block diagrams above form the **visual backbone** of this topic. In KTU viva voce and short-answer sections, sketching a quick flow chart of the manometer or pressure-type relationship can fetch full marks even if the verbal explanation is incomplete. # 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🟢 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Define the following terms with SI units:** (a) Fluid pressure (b) Pressure head (c) Vacuum pressure #### ✅ Model Answer: **(a) Fluid Pressure:** It is the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface. Mathematically, $P = F/A$. **SI unit: Pascal (Pa) or N/m².** **(b) Pressure Head:** It is the height of a fluid column that produces a given pressure at its base. $h = P/(\rho g)$. **SI unit: meter (m) of fluid.** **(c) Vacuum Pressure:** It is the pressure below atmospheric pressure. $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$. **SI unit: Pascal (Pa)** and is always expressed as a positive value. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **State Pascal's Law and write any two of its practical applications.** #### ✅ Model Answer: **Pascal's Law:** *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid."* **Mathematical form:** $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$ **Applications:** 1. **Hydraulic Lift / Jack** — used in automobile service stations to lift cars. 2. **Hydraulic Brake System** — used in cars and trucks for braking action. 3. **Hydraulic Press** — used in forging and stamping operations. --- ## 🔴 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, students must answer ONE full question from Part B (with both sub-parts). We provide two independent alternatives. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ for a static incompressible fluid. Explain the physical significance of each term. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A tank contains oil (specific gravity = 0.85) to a depth of 6 m, with atmospheric pressure acting on the free surface. Find: 1. The gauge pressure at the bottom of the tank. 2. The absolute pressure at the bottom of the tank. 3. The pressure head in meters of water. **\[7 Marks\]** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation:** Refer to **Section 3.1** for the complete derivation. Key points to include: - Define a fluid element of area $dA$ and depth $h$. - Apply $\sum F_{vertical} = 0$ for static equilibrium. - Show the integration from surface to depth $h$. - Conclude: $P = P_0 + \rho g h$. **Physical significance of terms:** - $P_0$ — surface reference pressure (often atmospheric). - $\rho$ — fluid density; for liquids it is essentially constant with depth. - $g$ — acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²). - $h$ — vertical depth from the free surface. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - Specific gravity of oil, $S = 0.85$ → $\rho_{oil} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850$ kg/m³ - Depth, $h = 6$ m - Surface pressure = atmospheric, so $P_0 = P_{atm}$ → gauge pressure at surface = 0 - $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **(i) Gauge pressure at bottom:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{gauge} &= \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h \\ &= 850 \times 9.81 \times 6 \\ &= 50,031 \text{ Pa} \\ &\approx 50.03 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Absolute pressure at bottom:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs} &= P_{atm} + P_{gauge} \\ &= 101.325 + 50.03 \\ &= 151.355 \text{ kPa} \\ &\approx 151.36 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Pressure head in meters of water:** Use $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ and $P = P_{gauge} = 50,031$ Pa: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{water} &= \frac{P_{gauge}}{\rho_{water} \cdot g} \\ &= \frac{50,031}{1000 \times 9.81} \\ &= 5.10 \text{ m of water} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** A common mistake is to use $P_{abs}$ in the water-head conversion. The pressure head is by definition based on the **gauge pressure** unless explicitly stated otherwise. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **U-tube differential manometer**. Derive the expression for the pressure difference between two points A and B in a horizontal pipe. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Two pressure taps in a horizontal water pipe are connected to a differential manometer containing mercury (specific gravity = 13.6). The mercury deflection is 200 mm. Calculate the pressure difference between the two points in: 1. N/m² 2. meters of water 3. kPa **\[7 Marks\]** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Part (a) — U-Tube Differential Manometer:** **Description:** A U-tube differential manometer consists of a U-shaped glass tube containing a heavy manometric fluid (usually mercury). One limb is connected to point A and the other to point B in a pipe. The difference in mercury levels indicates the pressure difference. **Working:** The fluid from the higher-pressure point pushes the mercury down on its side and up on the other side. The deflection $h$ is read directly. **Derivation:** Refer to **Section 3.4** of these notes for the complete derivation. The final result for a horizontal pipe is: $$ P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h $$ where $\rho_m$ = manometer fluid density and $\rho_f$ = pipe fluid density. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - Manometer fluid: Mercury, $S_m = 13.6$ → $\rho_m = 13,600$ kg/m³ - Pipe fluid: Water, $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³ - Deflection: $h = 200$ mm = 0.2 m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **(i) Pressure difference in N/m²:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_A - P_B &= (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h \\ &= (13{,}600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \\ &= 12{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \\ &= 24,721.2 \text{ N/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Pressure difference in meters of water:** $$ \begin{aligned} h_{water} &= \frac{P_A - P_B}{\rho_{water} \cdot g} \\ &= \frac{24,721.2}{1000 \times 9.81} \\ &= 2.52 \text{ m of water} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Pressure difference in kPa:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_A - P_B &= 24,721.2 \text{ Pa} = 24.72 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in manometer problems: Always begin by marking the higher pressure point (where mercury is depressed). > 2. **Using $P_{abs}$ instead of $P_{gauge}$** in pressure-head calculations — this is a recurrent error worth **1 mark** deduction. > 3. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in numerical substitutions — a careless mistake that costs full marks in some KTU valuations. > 4. **Mixing up densities**: The specific gravity of mercury is **13.6** (not 13.6 × 1000) — students often confuse this with $\rho$ directly. > 5. **Not stating Pascal's Law verbatim** in theory questions — the KTU 2024 answer key requires the **exact wording** of the law. > 6. **Unit inconsistency** in Part B numericals — write $g = 9.81$ m/s² explicitly; do not use 9.8 or 10. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - ✅ **Fluid Pressure** = Normal force per unit area; scalar; SI unit **Pa (N/m²)**. - ✅ **Hydrostatic Equation:** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — pressure increases **linearly** with depth. - ✅ **Pressure Head:** $h = P/(\rho g)$ — expressed in **meters of fluid**. - ✅ **Absolute Pressure** is measured from **perfect vacuum** (zero reference). - ✅ **Gauge Pressure** is measured **above atmospheric**; **zero** at atmosphere. - ✅ **Vacuum Pressure** is measured **below atmospheric**; **positive** value. - ✅ **Master Relation:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ ; $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$. - ✅ **Pascal's Law:** Pressure transmitted **equally and undiminished** in all directions in an enclosed incompressible fluid. - ✅ **U-Tube Manometer Equation:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ (horizontal pipe). - ✅ **Piezometer:** Open vertical tube; measures only **liquid gauge pressure**; cannot be used for gases or vacuum. - ✅ **Bourdon Gauge:** Curved metallic tube straightens under pressure; widely used industrial gauge. - ✅ **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar** = **10.33 m of water** = **760 mm Hg**. - ✅ **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9810$ N/m³ (use this exact value in KTU answers). - ✅ **Sign Convention for Manometers:** Heights of **lighter fluid added**, heights of **heavier fluid subtracted**. - ✅ **In all numerical answers**, always write the **unit, formula, substitution, and final result** for full marks. > 🎯 **Final Exam Strategy:** Practice **at least 5 numerical problems** on manometers (U-tube, inclined, differential) and **2 derivations** (hydrostatic pressure, Pascal's Law application) before the KTU ESE Module 1 exam. This covers ~85% of the typical question paper.

topics.title: Piezometer, Simple and Differential Manometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Piezometer, Simple and Differential Manometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Piezometer — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezometer:** A piezometer is a simple, single-limb, transparent vertical tube (open to the atmosphere) connected to a point in a fluid container or pipeline, used for the direct measurement of the **gauge pressure** at that point by reading the height of the liquid column that rises in it.

The device is fundamentally a manometric instrument that converts fluid pressure into a vertical column height. The relationship governing its operation is derived from the hydrostatic pressure equation:

$$P = \rho \, g \, h$$

where **ρ** is the density of the fluid being measured (in **kg/m³**), **g** is the acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**), and **h** is the height of the liquid column in the tube (in **m**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are drinking water through a long transparent straw placed deep inside a tank. The harder the water pushes (i.e., the higher the pressure at the submerged end), the higher the water rises inside the straw above the free surface of the tank. The straw behaves exactly like a piezometer — the height of water in the straw directly tells you the pressure at the point where the straw is connected!

This simple observation is the entire working principle of a piezometer. **Pressure → Liquid Column Height**.

## 1.3 Simple Manometer (U-Tube Manometer) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple U-Tube Manometer:** A U-tube manometer is a two-limbed, transparent, U-shaped tube containing a **manometric fluid** (a denser, immiscible liquid — most commonly mercury, Hg) at its base. One limb is connected to the point where pressure is to be measured, while the other limb may be open to atmosphere or connected to a reference point.

It overcomes the chief limitations of the piezometer by allowing measurement of higher pressures, vacuum pressures, and pressures of gases — all using the same hydrostatic principle but with a denser reference fluid to keep the column height physically manageable.

## 1.4 Differential Manometer — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Manometer:** A differential manometer is a U-tube containing a heavy manometric fluid, connected to **two distinct points** in a fluid system (such as two sections of a pipe). It measures the **difference in pressure** between these two points directly, by indicating the deflection (height difference) of the manometric fluid.

Differential manometers are indispensable in fluid machinery for measuring:
- Pressure drop across pipes, valves, and orifices
- Net head developed by pumps and turbines
- Flow rates by relating pressure drop to velocity via Bernoulli's equation

## 1.5 Limitations of Piezometer

> [!WARNING]
> **Why we need other manometers — Piezometer cannot handle:**
> 1. **High pressures** → Column becomes impractically tall (e.g., 1 MPa water pressure ≈ 102 m column!)
> 2. **Negative (vacuum) pressures** → Atmospheric pressure pulls liquid down, but no negative rise can occur
> 3. **Pressures of gases** → Gas is too light to produce a visible liquid rise in the tube
> 4. **Pressure differences** between two points — only a single-point device
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Hydrostatic Foundation

The fundamental principle behind every manometric device is the **hydrostatic pressure equation** (also known as Pascal's law for static fluids):

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho \, g$$

Integrating between two points in a continuous, static fluid column:

$$P_1 + \rho \, g \, z_1 = P_2 + \rho \, g \, z_2$$

> **Key Insight (Why this works):** In a static fluid, pressure increases linearly with depth. Therefore, if we move downward through a fluid column by a height *h*, pressure rises by exactly **ρ g h**. If we move upward, pressure drops by the same amount. Manometers exploit this **reversible, path-independent** nature of hydrostatic pressure.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

### 2.2.1 Piezometer Operation
- Step 1: The piezometer tube is connected at point A inside the pipe/vessel (pressure = P_A).
- Step 2: Atmospheric pressure P_atm pushes down on the open top of the tube.
- Step 3: Liquid rises in the tube until the hydrostatic pressure of the column equals the gauge pressure (P_A − P_atm).
- Step 4: Height *h* is read directly → **P_A, gauge = ρ g h**.

### 2.2.2 Simple U-Tube Manometer Operation
- Step 1: Heavy manometric fluid (mercury, ρ_m) is at the bottom of the U-tube.
- Step 2: The fluid whose pressure is to be measured (say water, ρ_w) sits on top of mercury in the left limb.
- Step 3: Right limb is open to atmosphere → atmospheric pressure P_atm acts on the free mercury surface.
- Step 4: A pressure balance is written by tracing pressures from point A down through the left limb, across the mercury, and up the right limb back to atmosphere.

### 2.2.3 Differential Manometer Operation
- Step 1: Two points A and B (at possibly different elevations) are connected to the two limbs.
- Step 2: Heavy manometric fluid deflects by a height *h*.
- Step 3: Pressure balance gives the **difference** P_A − P_B directly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Densities (must memorize for exams):**
> - **Mercury (Hg): ρ_m = 13,600 kg/m³**
> - **Water: ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water **γ_w = 9810 N/m³**
> - Specific weight of mercury **γ_m = 13.6 × 9810 = 133,416 N/m³**

| # | Device | Governing Equation | Standard Units | Notes / Limits |
|---|--------|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | Piezometer | $P_A = \rho \, g \, h$ | Pa (or N/m²) | Gauge pressure only; no vacuum; no gases |
| 2 | Simple U-tube (left open to atm) | $P_A + \rho_w \, g \, h_w - \rho_m \, g \, h = P_{atm}$ | Pa | *h_w* = water column above mercury; *h* = mercury deflection |
| 3 | Simple U-tube (absolute form) | $P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m \, g \, h - \rho_w \, g \, h_w$ | Pa | Gives **absolute** pressure |
| 4 | Differential U-tube (same fluid above Hg) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h$ | Pa | Points A & B at **same elevation**; ρ_f = flowing fluid density |
| 5 | Differential U-tube (general, different elevations) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h + \rho_f \, g \, z$ | Pa | *z* = elevation difference between A and B (positive if B is higher) |
| 6 | Inverted U-tube (lighter manometric fluid, e.g., oil) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_o) \, g \, h$ | Pa | Used when fluid is heavier than manometric oil |

> **Critical Exam Tip:** For differential manometer formulas, *always draw the figure first* — mark **h** (mercury deflection) and **z** (elevation difference) before writing any equation. KTU examiners heavily penalize students who skip the diagram.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Engineering:** Measuring reservoir and pipeline pressures.
- **Pump & Turbine Testing:** Differential manometers across inlet/outlet flanges compute developed head.
- **Aerospace & HVAC:** Inverted U-tube manometers measure small pressure differences in air ducts.
- **Blood Pressure Measurement (Medical):** Sphygmomanometer is essentially a differential manometer scaled for human physiology.
- **Industrial Calibration:** Dead-weight testers and manometers are still primary pressure standards in NABL-accredited labs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Head Relation for a Piezometer

**Given:** A pipe carrying a fluid of density ρ has a piezometer connected at point A. The fluid rises to a height *h* above the centerline of the pipe.

**Step 1:** Atmospheric pressure acts at the top of the open piezometer column.

$$P_{top} = P_{atm}$$

**Step 2:** Hydrostatic pressure builds up as we descend through the static fluid column.

$$P_A = P_{top} + \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to get the absolute pressure at A.

$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 4:** The gauge pressure (which is what the piezometer actually reads) is:

$$P_{A, \, gauge} = P_A - P_{atm} = \rho \, g \, h$$

This is the **fundamental piezometer equation**.

> **Physical interpretation:** Gauge pressure equals the weight per unit area of the liquid column of height *h*. If *h* = 1 m of water, gauge pressure = 9810 Pa = 9.81 kPa.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Simple U-Tube Manometer Reading

**Setup:** A pipe contains water (density ρ_w). A U-tube manometer containing mercury (density ρ_m) is connected. The mercury rises higher on the open-atmosphere side by a difference of *h*. The water column above mercury in the pipe-side limb has height *h_w*.

**Step 1 — Start at point A (in the pipe, pressure = P_A) and travel down to mercury interface on the left limb.** Pressure increases as we go down.

$$P_{left \, interface} = P_A + \rho_w \, g \, h_w$$

**Step 2 — Cross the mercury horizontally.** Pressure is **constant** at the same elevation within a connected static fluid.

$$P_{right \, interface} = P_{left \, interface} = P_A + \rho_w \, g \, h_w$$

**Step 3 — Travel up through mercury in the right limb by height *h*.** Pressure decreases.

$$P_{top, \, right} = P_{right \, interface} - \rho_m \, g \, h$$

**Step 4 — At the top of the open right limb, the fluid (mercury) is exposed to atmospheric pressure.**

$$P_{top, \, right} = P_{atm}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the expressions from Steps 3 and 4.**

$$P_A + \rho_w \, g \, h_w - \rho_m \, g \, h = P_{atm}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the absolute pressure at A.**

$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m \, g \, h - \rho_w \, g \, h_w$$

**Step 7 — The gauge pressure at A is therefore:**

$$\boxed{P_{A, \, gauge} = P_A - P_{atm} = \rho_m \, g \, h - \rho_w \, g \, h_w}$$

> **Numerical sanity check:** If *h* = 0 (no mercury deflection), then P_A,gauge = −ρ_w g h_w, which would imply suction — physically correct: water column pulls mercury down on the right side, suggesting point A is below atmospheric pressure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential U-Tube Manometer (General Case)

**Setup:** Two points A and B in a pipe carry the same fluid (density ρ_f). They are connected by a differential U-tube manometer. Mercury (ρ_m) sits at the bottom. The fluid column heights above mercury differ, and the elevation difference between A and B is *z* (B is higher than A by *z*).

**Notation convention:** Let the left limb connect to A and right limb connect to B. Mercury in the right limb is lower than in the left limb by a difference of *h* (i.e., P_A > P_B pushes mercury down on the left side, up on the right).

**Step 1 — Trace from A downward through the fluid column to the mercury interface in the left limb.**

$$P_{left \, Hg \, interface} = P_A + \rho_f \, g \, (h_A)$$

where *h_A* is the vertical distance from A down to the left mercury surface.

**Step 2 — At the same horizontal level inside mercury, in the right limb (which is *h* higher in mercury), the pressure must be different.** However, going across at the lower mercury surface (left limb level):

$$P_{right, \, at \, left \, level} = P_{left \, Hg \, interface} = P_A + \rho_f \, g \, h_A$$

**Step 3 — Move up through the mercury column by height *h* to reach the right mercury surface.**

$$P_{right \, Hg \, interface} = P_A + \rho_f \, g \, h_A - \rho_m \, g \, h$$

**Step 4 — Move up through the fluid in the right limb from the mercury surface to point B.**

$$P_B = P_{right \, Hg \, interface} - \rho_f \, g \, h_B$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4.**

$$P_B = P_A + \rho_f \, g \, h_A - \rho_m \, g \, h - \rho_f \, g \, h_B$$

**Step 6 — Note the geometric relation.** The elevation difference between A and B is:

$$z = (h_A - h_B)$$

> **Geometric reasoning:** If we set a common horizontal datum, then A lies at height H_A, B lies at height H_B = H_A + z (B is higher). The left mercury surface is at H_A − h_A, the right mercury surface at H_B − h_B. Mercury height difference *h* = (H_A − h_A) − (H_B − h_B) = (H_A − H_B) + (h_B − h_A) = −z + (h_B − h_A). Solving for (h_B − h_A): h_B − h_A = h + z.

**Step 7 — Substitute the geometric identity into Step 5.**

$$P_B = P_A + \rho_f \, g \, (h_A - h_B) - \rho_m \, g \, h$$

$$P_B = P_A - \rho_f \, g \, (h_B - h_A) - \rho_m \, g \, h$$

$$P_B = P_A - \rho_f \, g \, (h + z) - \rho_m \, g \, h$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the pressure difference.**

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_f \, g \, (h + z) + \rho_m \, g \, h$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h + \rho_f \, g \, z}$$

**Special Case — If A and B are at the same elevation (z = 0):**

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h$$

This is the **most commonly asked form** in KTU exams.

> **Key Insight:** Notice how the formula neatly separates into two contributions:
> 1. **(ρ_m − ρ_f) g h** → due to mercury deflection (the dominant term)
> 2. **ρ_f g z** → due to the static elevation difference between A and B (a hydrostatic correction)
>
> **Forgetting the z-term when A and B are at different elevations is the #1 mistake students make in KTU exams.**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Equation

| Situation | Best Device | Key Formula | Why |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|-----|
| Liquid pressure, low value | Piezometer | $P = \rho g h$ | Simplest, no manometric fluid needed |
| High liquid pressure, gas pressure, vacuum | Simple U-tube | $P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h - \rho_w g h_w$ | Mercury column stays compact |
| Pressure difference between two points (same elevation) | Differential U-tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Direct differential reading |
| Pressure difference between two points (different elevations) | Differential U-tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h + \rho_f g z$ | Includes hydrostatic correction |
| Small pressure difference in gas/air | Inverted U-tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_o) g h$ | Uses lighter manometric fluid (oil) for sensitivity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Pressure Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["Fluid Source"]
        PIPE["Pipe / Vessel containing fluid at unknown pressure"]
    end
    
    subgraph DEVC["Measurement Device Family"]
        PZ["Piezometer - single open limb - direct height reading"]
        SM["Simple U-tube Manometer - mercury filled - one end open to atm"]
        DM["Differential U-tube Manometer - mercury filled - two source points"]
        IU["Inverted U-tube Manometer - oil or air filled - low ΔP"]
    end
    
    subgraph OUT["Output Reading"]
        HGT["Column Height h - meters"]
        HG["Mercury Deflection h - meters"]
        HD["Combined h and z readings - meters"]
        HI["Light fluid Deflection h - meters"]
    end
    
    subgraph CALC["Computation"]
        F1["P = ρ × g × h"]
        F2["P_A = P_atm + ρ_m × g × h - ρ_w × g × h_w"]
        F3["P_A - P_B = ρ_m - ρ_f × g × h + ρ_f × g × z"]
        F4["P_A - P_B = ρ_f - ρ_o × g × h"]
    end
    
    PIPE --> PZ --> HGT --> F1
    PIPE --> SM --> HG --> F2
    PIPE --> DM --> HD --> F3
    PIPE --> IU --> HI --> F4
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Differential Manometer Pressure Path Tracing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Point A - pressure P_A - elevation z_A"] -->|Down through fluid column h_A| AL["Left mercury interface"]
    AL -->|Across mercury at same elevation| AR["Right mercury interface at level z_A minus h"]
    AR -->|Up through mercury by h| AR2["Right mercury surface"]
    AR2 -->|Up through fluid column h_B| B["Point B - pressure P_B - elevation z_A plus z"]
    
    AL -.Pressure.-> P1["P_A + ρ_f × g × h_A"]
    AR -.Pressure.-> P2["P_A + ρ_f × g × h_A"]
    AR2 -.Pressure.-> P3["P_A + ρ_f × g × h_A minus ρ_m × g × h"]
    B -.Pressure.-> P4["P_A minus ρ_m minus ρ_f × g × h plus ρ_f × g × z minus P_B"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each node represents a pressure at a specific physical location inside the manometer. The arrows represent the path you trace when applying the hydrostatic equation. The dashed "Pressure" annotations show the cumulative pressure expression at each node.

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Piezometer with Transducer Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Physical Layer"]
        FL["Static fluid column in piezometer tube of height h"]
    end
    subgraph B["Physics Layer"]
        EQ1["Hydrostatic equation P = ρ × g × h"]
        EQ2["Gauge pressure = absolute - atmospheric"]
    end
    subgraph C["Output Layer"]
        OUT1["Reading in Pascals Pa"]
        OUT2["Reading in meters of fluid head"]
        OUT3["Reading in bar or kg per cm²"]
    end
    FL --> EQ1 --> EQ2 --> OUT1
    EQ1 --> OUT2
    EQ2 --> OUT3
```

> **Note on physical drawings:** This functional block diagram replaces physical sketches that would normally show the pipe, tube, and fluid levels — KTU examiners do not typically require artistic sketches, but **always include a labeled schematic** in your answer scripts showing *h*, *z*, fluid densities, and reference points. This single habit can earn you 2–3 extra marks even with imperfect algebra.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** State any three limitations of a piezometer and explain why a U-tube manometer is preferred for measuring high pressures.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Limitations of Piezometer:**

1. **Unsuitable for high pressures** *(1 mark)*: The required column height *h* becomes impractically large. For instance, to measure 1 MPa pressure with water, *h* = P/(ρg) = 10⁶/(1000 × 9.81) ≈ **102 m**, which is physically impossible to accommodate.

2. **Cannot measure negative (vacuum) gauge pressures** *(1 mark)*: A piezometer only measures when fluid rises *above* the point of connection. It cannot read below atmospheric pressure since a liquid cannot "fall" below its own level in a single open tube against vacuum.

3. **Cannot measure gas pressures** *(1 mark)*: Gases are too light to produce a discernible column rise, and a piezometer cannot be filled with gas effectively for static measurements.

**Why U-tube is preferred:** A U-tube uses a dense manometric fluid like mercury (ρ = 13,600 kg/m³) so that even very high pressures produce small *h* (e.g., 1 MPa gives h ≈ 7.5 cm of mercury), making the instrument compact and practical.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** Differentiate between a simple manometer and a differential manometer with one suitable example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Manometer | Differential Manometer |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| Purpose *(1 mark)* | Measures pressure at a single point relative to atmosphere | Measures pressure **difference** between two points in a system |
| Connection *(1 mark)* | One limb connected to the test point; other limb open to atmosphere (or both open) | Both limbs connected to two distinct points in the system |
| Example *(1 mark)* | U-tube manometer attached to a pipe to find P at a section | U-tube manometer across the inlet and outlet of a pipe orifice to find head loss |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Module Internal Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** A differential U-tube mercury manometer is connected across an orifice meter fitted in a horizontal pipe carrying oil (specific gravity 0.8). The manometer shows a mercury deflection of 200 mm. Calculate the pressure difference between the two tapping points in terms of (a) N/m², and (b) meters of oil.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks — Pressure Difference in N/m²

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of oil, S_oil = 0.8 → ρ_oil = **800 kg/m³**
- Density of mercury, ρ_m = **13,600 kg/m³**
- Mercury deflection, h = 200 mm = **0.2 m**
- g = **9.81 m/s²**
- Pipe is **horizontal** → z = 0

**Step 1 — Select the appropriate formula.** Since the two tapping points are at the same elevation (horizontal pipe):

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$P_A - P_B = (13600 - 800) \times 9.81 \times 0.2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the difference in densities.**

$$\rho_m - \rho_f = 13600 - 800 = 12800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by g.**

$$12800 \times 9.81 = 125568 \, \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by h.**

$$P_A - P_B = 125568 \times 0.2 = 25113.6 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 25,114 \, \text{N/m}^2 \approx 25.11 \, \text{kPa}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Pressure Difference in Meters of Oil

**Step 1 — Convert N/m² to head of oil** using h_oil = (P_A − P_B) / (ρ_oil × g).

$$h_{oil} = \frac{25113.6}{800 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator.**

$$800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \, \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Divide.**

$$h_{oil} = \frac{25113.6}{7848} = 3.2 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{oil} = 3.2 \, \text{m of oil}}$$

**Verification (alternate method):** 

$$h_{oil} = \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1\right) \, h = \left(\frac{13600}{800} - 1\right) \times 0.2 = (17 - 1) \times 0.2 = 16 \times 0.2 = 3.2 \, \text{m} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently make these mistakes in differential manometer problems:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** before substituting — leads to answers off by a factor of 1000.
> 2. **Using ρ_m only** (forgetting the −ρ_f term) — gives ~26,679 N/m², wrong by 6%.
> 3. **Forgetting the elevation correction z** when the pipe is inclined or the tappings are at different heights.
> 4. **Mixing up densities** — ρ_oil is 800 kg/m³, NOT 1000 kg/m³. Read the specific gravity carefully.

---

### Question B2 (Module Internal Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** A pipe carrying water is connected to a simple U-tube manometer containing mercury. The water column above mercury on the pipe side is 250 mm, while the mercury level on the open-atmosphere side is 180 mm above the mercury level on the pipe side. Determine the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline in (a) kPa, and (b) meters of water.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks — Gauge Pressure in kPa

**Given:**
- Water column above mercury on pipe side: h_w = 250 mm = **0.25 m**
- Mercury deflection: h = 180 mm = **0.18 m** (open side higher → pipe pressure is greater than atmospheric, gauge pressure is positive)
- ρ_w = **1000 kg/m³**
- ρ_m = **13,600 kg/m³**
- g = **9.81 m/s²**

**Step 1 — Write the simple U-tube manometer equation.**

$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m \, g \, h - \rho_w \, g \, h_w$$

**Step 2 — Express gauge pressure.**

$$P_{A, \, gauge} = P_A - P_{atm} = \rho_m \, g \, h - \rho_w \, g \, h_w$$

**Step 3 — Compute ρ_m × g × h.**

$$13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.18 = 24009.408 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute ρ_w × g × h_w.**

$$1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 2452.5 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Subtract.**

$$P_{A, \, gauge} = 24009.408 - 2452.5 = 21556.908 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{P_{A, \, gauge} \approx 21,557 \, \text{N/m}^2 \approx 21.56 \, \text{kPa}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Gauge Pressure in Meters of Water

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to head of water** using h_w = P/(ρ_w × g).

$$h_{water} = \frac{21556.908}{1000 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator.**

$$1000 \times 9.81 = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Divide.**

$$h_{water} = \frac{21556.908}{9810} = 2.197 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{water} \approx 2.197 \, \text{m of water}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The gauge pressure of 21.56 kPa is equivalent to standing under 2.197 m of still water — about the pressure at the bottom of a backyard swimming pool.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Simple U-tube Problems:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** If mercury rises *higher* on the open side than on the pipe side, the pipe pressure is **greater than atmospheric** → gauge pressure is **positive**. The opposite configuration gives **negative gauge pressure (vacuum)**. KTU examiners love to test this with diagrams.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract ρ_w g h_w:** Many students compute only ρ_m g h and lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong interpretation of *h*:** Some students take *h* as the height above mercury in the pipe side instead of the mercury level difference — read the problem statement carefully!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Piezometers & Manometers (Module 1)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Piezometer** measures only **gauge pressure** of liquids using **ρ g h**; it cannot handle high pressure, vacuum, or gases.
- ☐ **Simple U-tube manometer** uses a **denser manometric fluid** (mercury, ρ = 13,600 kg/m³) and handles all cases the piezometer cannot.
- ☐ **Differential U-tube manometer** measures **pressure difference** between two points via mercury deflection *h* and elevation correction *z*.

### Master Formulas (memorize verbatim)
- ☐ Piezometer: **P = ρ g h**
- ☐ Simple U-tube: **P_A = P_atm + ρ_m g h − ρ_w g h_w**
- ☐ Differential (same elevation): **P_A − P_B = (ρ_m − ρ_f) g h**
- ☐ Differential (general): **P_A − P_B = (ρ_m − ρ_f) g h + ρ_f g z**

### Constant Values to Memorize
- ☐ ρ_mercury = **13,600 kg/m³**
- ☐ ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³**
- ☐ g = **9.81 m/s²**
- ☐ γ_water = **9810 N/m³**, γ_mercury = **133,416 N/m³**

### Problem-Solving Strategy (follow in every answer)
- ☐ **Draw the figure** with all labels: *h*, *z*, fluid densities, and reference points.
- ☐ Identify whether points are at the **same elevation** or different elevations.
- ☐ Pick the correct formula from the cheat sheet above.
- ☐ Convert all units to **SI** (mm → m, kPa → Pa if computing N/m²).
- ☐ Show **all intermediate steps** — KTU valuation gives partial credit for steps.
- ☐ Cross-check magnitude: mercury deflection *h* = 10 cm with oil (sg 0.8) ≈ 1.6 m oil head.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Mixing up **gauge vs. absolute** pressure — piezometer always reads gauge.
- ☐ Forgetting the **elevation correction z** in inclined differential manometers.
- ☐ Using ρ_m alone instead of (ρ_m − ρ_f) in the differential formula.
- ☐ Sign errors when mercury rises on the pipe side (negative gauge pressure).
- ☐ Not converting mm to m before substituting (loses 2–3 marks).

### Quick Memory Hook
> 🧠 **"Piezo = Pure Liquid Push"** → **"U-tube = Underneath, heavy fluid balances the unknown"** → **"Differential = Deflection reveals Difference"**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Statics: Pressure, Density, Height Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Statics: Pressure, Density, Height Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Fluid Statics

**Fluid Statics** (or **Fluid Statics**) is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies fluids at rest. Since the fluid is not moving, there are no shear stresses, no viscous effects (in ideal analysis), and the only force acting on a fluid element is the **gravitational body force** balanced by the **pressure force** acting normal to every surface.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Fluid Statics is the study of the equilibrium of fluid masses under the action of:
- **Body forces** (gravity, magnetic, etc.) — forces distributed over the entire volume.
- **Surface forces** (pressure) — forces distributed over the surface area.

The fundamental governing equation for a static fluid in a gravitational field reduces to the famous **Hydrostatic Pressure Law**, which establishes a direct relationship between **pressure**, **density**, and **height** (vertical elevation).

## 1.2 The Three Key Physical Quantities

### a) Pressure ($P$)
**Pressure** is defined as the **normal force exerted per unit area** by a fluid on a surface (or imaginary plane) in contact with it. It is a **scalar quantity** because, in a static fluid, the force is always perpendicular to the surface and is independent of direction (Pascal's Principle).

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

where $F$ is the normal force in **Newtons (N)** and $A$ is the area in **square meters (m²)**. The SI unit of pressure is **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Units You MUST Know:**
> - **SI Unit:** $N/m^2$ or **Pa (Pascal)**
> - **Other Units:** bar, atm, mm of Hg, m of water
> - **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**
> - **1 kgf/cm² ≈ 9.81 × 10⁴ Pa**

### b) Density ($\rho$)
**Density** is defined as the **mass per unit volume** of a fluid. It is an intrinsic property of the fluid and varies with temperature (and very slightly with pressure for liquids).

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

The SI unit of density is **kg/m³**. For water at 4°C, the standard density is **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**.

### c) Specific Weight ($\gamma$)
**Specific Weight** (also called **weight density**) is the **weight per unit volume** of a fluid.

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

The SI unit of specific weight is **N/m³**. For water, $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ (taking $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$).

## 1.3 The Pressure–Density–Height Triangle

In a static fluid, the three quantities are linked by a single linear relationship:

> **The pressure at any point in a static, incompressible fluid depends only on the depth of that point below the free surface and the density of the fluid.**

Mathematically expressed as:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where:
- $P_0$ = pressure at the free surface (often atmospheric)
- $\rho$ = density of the fluid
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $h$ = vertical height (depth) below the free surface

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Stacking of Bricks" Concept**
>
> Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a swimming pool. As you dive deeper, you feel increasing pressure in your ears. Why? Because the water column **above** you is taller and heavier, and the weight of all that water is being transmitted downward through the fluid. **Pressure increases with depth** because of the cumulative weight of the fluid above.
>
> Similarly, if the pool contained honey (denser fluid) instead of water, you would feel *even more* pressure at the same depth. This is the "**Denser fluid → More pressure**" idea.
>
> The mathematical relationship $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ simply says: **Start with surface pressure, then add (density × gravity × depth)**. It's like stacking bricks — each layer of fluid brick adds its own weight to the total load below.

## 1.5 Absolute vs. Gauge Pressure

This is a **frequently tested KTU concept**:

| Quantity | Reference | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Pressure** ($P_{abs}$) | Measured from perfect vacuum (zero) | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ |
| **Gauge Pressure** ($P_{gauge}$) | Measured from local atmospheric pressure | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$ | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU numerical problems, unless specified otherwise, **gauge pressure** is often used. Always check the wording: "Find the pressure at depth h" may imply either, depending on whether atmospheric pressure is included.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation begins with a fundamental physical principle: **in a static fluid, the net force on any fluid element must be zero** (Newton's First Law for equilibrium).

### Step 1: Consider a Small Fluid Element
Take a tiny vertical fluid column of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dz$ inside a static fluid body.

### Step 2: Identify All Forces Acting
Three forces act on this element:
- **Pressure force from below** (pushing up): $P \cdot dA$
- **Pressure force from above** (pushing down): $(P + dP) \cdot dA$
- **Weight of the element** (pulling down): $\rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz$

### Step 3: Apply Equilibrium
Sum of vertical forces = 0:
