$$(P + dP) \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

### Step 4: Simplify
$$dP \cdot dA = \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz$$

$$dP = \rho g \cdot dz$$

### Step 5: Integrate Between Two Points
Integrating from point 1 (at elevation $z_1$, pressure $P_1$) to point 2 (at elevation $z_2$, pressure $P_2$):

$$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1)$$

The negative sign indicates that **pressure decreases as elevation increases** (i.e., pressure increases with depth).

### Step 6: Final Standard Form
For a free surface at elevation $z_s$ with pressure $P_0$, and a point at depth $h = (z_s - z)$:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this "the most important equation in fluid statics"?**
> Because it gives you the **pressure at ANY depth** in a static fluid, regardless of the shape of the container. This is the famous "**hydrostatic paradox**" — pressure depends on **depth only**, not on the shape or volume of the fluid.

## 2.2 Pressure Variation in Compressible Fluids (Atmospheric Gases)

For a compressible fluid like air, density $\rho$ is itself a function of elevation. Assuming an isothermal atmosphere and using the ideal gas law ($\rho = P/RT$):

$$P = P_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{gz}{RT}}$$

where $R$ is the specific gas constant (287 J/kg·K for air) and $T$ is absolute temperature.

For a **constant temperature lapse rate** (adiabatic atmosphere):

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = \left(1 - \frac{Lz}{T_0}\right)^{\frac{g}{RL}}$$

where $L$ is the lapse rate. KTU generally tests the **incompressible** form ($P = P_0 + \rho g h$) for liquids and applies the simpler form for gases.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU ESE preparation. These are the most frequently tested formulas in Module 1.**

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area | $Pa = N/m^2$ |
| 2 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Density = Mass / Volume | $kg/m^3$ |
| 3 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific Weight = Density × Gravity | $N/m^3$ |
| 4 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific Gravity (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 6 | $dP = \rho g dz$ | Differential pressure form | Pa |
| 7 | $P_1 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \rho g z_2$ | Pressure between two points (steady) | Pa |
| 8 | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gauge pressure relation | Pa |
| 9 | $P_{vacuum} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure relation | Pa |
| 10 | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$ | Equivalent head of fluid | m |
| 11 | $P = \rho g h_{fluid}$ | Pressure from manometer (head form) | Pa |
| 12 | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Atmospheric pressure | Pa |

**Critical Note on Pipe Symbol Usage:** In all equations above, the standard "**pressure difference**" or "**modulus**" is denoted as $\Delta P$ (not $\vert P \vert$) to avoid markdown table-breaking issues. KTU notation in answer sheets prefers $\Delta P$ for clarity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The Pressure–Density–Height relationship is the foundation of countless engineering systems:

1. **Water Supply Systems** — Engineers calculate the pressure at the tap based on the height of the overhead tank.
2. **Dam Design** — The hydrostatic pressure on the vertical face of a dam increases linearly with depth; this determines the structural thickness required at the base.
3. **Submarines** — Hulls are designed to withstand the crushing pressure at deep depths using $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.
4. **Barometers and Manometers** — The mercury barometer (Torricelli) works because the column height of Hg is balanced by atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$.
5. **Hydraulic Presses** — Pascal's law (a direct consequence of hydrostatics) allows multiplication of force.
6. **Aircraft Altimeters** — They use the inverse relationship — pressure decreases with altitude.
7. **Blood Pressure Measurement** — The sphygmomanometer measures the gauge pressure of blood relative to atmospheric.

## 2.5 The Hydrostatic Paradox — KTU Conceptual Question Favorite

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Question (often 2 marks in KTU):**
> *"Three vessels of different shapes but the same base area and same fluid level contain water. Why is the force on the base of each vessel the same?"*

**Answer:** Because pressure at the base depends only on the **vertical height** of the fluid column (i.e., $P = \rho g h$), not on the shape of the vessel or the total weight of the fluid. The force on the base is:

$$F = P \cdot A_{base} = \rho g h \cdot A_{base}$$

This is **independent of shape** — the "hydrostatic paradox."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: The Hydrostatic Equation $P = P_0 + \rho g h$

### Setup
Consider a static fluid of constant density $\rho$ in a tank open to atmosphere. Let point **A** be at the free surface (pressure $P_0 = P_{atm}$, elevation $z_0$) and point **B** be at a depth $h$ below the free surface (elevation $z_B = z_0 - h$, pressure $P_B$).

### Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law (Static Equilibrium)
A fluid element in static equilibrium experiences zero net force. Considering a small fluid slab of cross-section $dA$ and thickness $dz$:

**Forces in the vertical (z) direction:**
- Force on bottom face (acts upward): $+P \cdot dA$
- Force on top face (acts downward): $-(P + dP) \cdot dA$
- Weight of the element (acts downward): $-\rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz$

**Equilibrium Condition:**

$$\sum F_z = 0$$

$$(P \cdot dA) - (P + dP) \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

### Step 2: Algebraic Simplification
Expanding the first two terms:

$$P \cdot dA - P \cdot dA - dP \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

The $P \cdot dA$ terms cancel:

$$-dP \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $dA$:

$$-dP - \rho g \cdot dz = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$dP = -\rho g \cdot dz$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Sign Convention
The negative sign tells us that **pressure increases as elevation decreases** (as we go deeper, elevation $z$ becomes smaller, so $dz$ is negative, making $dP$ positive).

### Step 4: Integrate Between Two Points
For an **incompressible fluid** (constant $\rho$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P_B} dP = -\rho g \int_{z_0}^{z_B} dz$$

$$P_B - P_0 = -\rho g (z_B - z_0)$$

### Step 5: Substitute Depth
Let $h = z_0 - z_B$ (depth of point B below the surface):

$$P_B - P_0 = -\rho g (z_0 - h - z_0)$$

$$P_B - P_0 = -\rho g (-h)$$

$$P_B - P_0 = \rho g h$$

### Step 6: Final Result

$$\boxed{P_B = P_0 + \rho g h}$$

This is the **fundamental equation of fluid statics**. It states that the pressure at any depth in a static, incompressible fluid equals the surface pressure plus the product of fluid density, gravitational acceleration, and the depth below the surface.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Pressure Variation in a Compressible Atmosphere (Isothermal Case)

### Setup
For the atmosphere, density $\rho$ is not constant — it decreases with elevation. Assuming **isothermal conditions** (constant temperature $T$):

$$P = \rho R T$$

where $R$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**.

### Step 1: Express Density in Terms of Pressure
From the ideal gas law:

$$\rho = \frac{P}{RT}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Hydrostatic Equation
From earlier:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

Substituting $\rho = P / (RT)$:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{P g}{R T}$$

### Step 3: Separate Variables
$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{RT} dz$$

### Step 4: Integrate
Integrate from the ground level (where $P = P_0$ at $z = 0$) to an arbitrary elevation $z$ (pressure $P$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{g}{RT} \int_{0}^{z} dz$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{gz}{RT}$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate Both Sides

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = e^{-\frac{gz}{RT}}$$

### Step 6: Final Form

$$\boxed{P = P_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{gz}{RT}}}$$

This is the **barometric formula** for an isothermal atmosphere. KTU typically asks for this derivation when introducing pressure measurement in gases (altimeters, barometers).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pressure Variation in Common Scenarios

| Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid in open tank | $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Incompressible, constant $\rho$ |
| Liquid in closed tank with gas pressure $P_0$ | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | $P_0$ replaces $P_{atm}$ |
| Static manometer | $P_A + \rho_1 g h_1 - \rho_2 g h_2 = P_B$ | Hydrostatic balance |
| Compressible gas (isothermal) | $P = P_0 e^{-gz/RT}$ | Ideal gas, constant $T$ |
| Compressible gas (adiabatic) | $P/P_0 = (1 - Lz/T_0)^{g/(RL)}$ | Constant lapse rate $L$ |
| Submerged curved surface | $F_V = \rho g V$ (vertical force) | Volume method |
| Buoyancy (Archimedes) | $F_B = \rho_{fluid} g V_{displaced}$ | Hydrostatic pressure integration |

---

## 3.4 Sample KTU-Style Derivation Practice: Manometer Equation

Consider a U-tube manometer connected between two points A and B containing different fluids. The pressure balance yields:

$$P_A + \rho_1 g h_1 - \rho_2 g h_2 - \rho_3 g h_3 = P_B$$

**Derivation Logic (step-by-step):**
1. Start at point A with pressure $P_A$.
2. Move down through fluid 1 by $h_1$: pressure increases by $\rho_1 g h_1$.
3. Move up through fluid 2 by $h_2$: pressure decreases by $\rho_2 g h_2$.
4. Move down through fluid 3 by $h_3$: pressure increases by $\rho_3 g h_3$.
5. End at point B with pressure $P_B$.

**Final Equation:**

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_2 g h_2 + \rho_3 g h_3 - \rho_1 g h_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Manometer Problems:** When moving **down** through a fluid column, **add** $\rho g h$. When moving **up**, **subtract** $\rho g h$. This is the "down is positive" rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **conceptual model** of hydrostatic pressure buildup in a static fluid column, mapping the key physical interactions and mathematical relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Surface of Liquid] --> B[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm acts on surface]
    B --> C[Pressure at depth h: P = P_atm + rho times g times h]
    C --> D[Fluid Element: cross section dA, thickness dz]
    D --> E[Force from below pushes UP: P times dA]
    D --> F[Force from above pushes DOWN: P + dP times dA]
    D --> G[Weight of element pulls DOWN: rho times g times dA times dz]
    E --> H[Net Force = 0 for static equilibrium]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Equation: dP = negative rho g dz]
    I --> J[Integration yields: P = P_0 + rho g h]
    J --> K[Key Insight: Pressure depends on DEPTH only, not on shape]
    K --> L[Application: Dam Design, Manometry, Submarines]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pressure Measurement System

The following diagram shows how a **manometer** (a hydrostatic pressure measurement device) works as an integrated system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Stage"]
        A1[Point A: Unknown Pressure P_A]
        A2[Point B: Unknown Pressure P_B]
    end

    subgraph Transmission["Transmission Stage - Fluid Columns"]
        B1[Fluid 1: density rho_1, height h_1]
        B2[Fluid 2: density rho_2, height h_2]
        B3[Fluid 3: density rho_3, height h_3]
    end

    subgraph Output["Output Stage - Pressure Equation"]
        C1[Apply Hydrostatic Balance]
        C2[P_A + rho_1 g h_1 - rho_2 g h_2 - rho_3 g h_3 = P_B]
        C3[Solve for Unknown: P_A or P_B]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> A2
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Relationships Among Key Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forces on Fluid Element] --> B[Surface Forces: Pressure]
    A --> C[Body Forces: Gravity]
    B --> D[Hydrostatic Equation: dP = negative rho g dz]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Integrated Form: P = P_0 + rho g h]
    E --> F[Liquid Case: rho constant]
    E --> G[Gas Case: rho varies with z]
    F --> H[Liquid Manometers, Dams, Hydraulic Press]
    G --> I[Barometric Formula, Altimeters]
    H --> J[Engineering Application: Water Distribution]
    I --> K[Engineering Application: Aviation and Weather]
```

## 4.4 Pressure Variation Visualization Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Top of Container: P = P_atm at z = h_max] --> Mid1[Mid Layer: P_1 = P_atm + rho g h_1]
    Mid1 --> Mid2[Lower Layer: P_2 = P_atm + rho g h_2]
    Mid2 --> Base[Base of Container: P_base = P_atm + rho g H]
    Base --> Note1[Pressure varies LINEARLY with depth]
    Mid1 --> Note2[Pressure acts PERPENDICULAR to all surfaces]
    Mid2 --> Note3[Force on base F = P_base times A = rho g H times A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The Mermaid diagrams are designed to map the **functional flow** of the physical concepts, since static fluid systems cannot easily be rendered as physical 2D drawings. The arrows show the logical flow from physical setup to mathematical formulation to engineering application — the precise way KTU board questions are structured.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define the following terms with their SI units:
(a) Pressure
(b) Density
(c) Specific Weight

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Pressure ($P$):** Pressure is defined as the **normal force exerted per unit area** by a fluid on a surface. It is a scalar quantity acting equally in all directions at a point in a static fluid.

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

**SI Unit:** $N/m^2$ or **Pascal (Pa)**.

**(b) Density ($\rho$):** Density is the **mass per unit volume** of a fluid. It is a measure of how compact the fluid's mass is.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

**SI Unit:** $kg/m^3$.

**(c) Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Specific weight is the **weight per unit volume** of a fluid. It relates the gravitational force per unit volume.

$$\gamma = \rho g$$

**SI Unit:** $N/m^3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU follow-up:** The examiner may also ask for the **relationship between specific weight and specific gravity**: $SG = \gamma_{fluid} / \gamma_{water} = \rho_{fluid} / \rho_{water}$. Specific gravity is **dimensionless**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State and explain the **hydrostatic pressure law**. Why is the pressure in a static fluid the same at all points on a horizontal plane?

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The hydrostatic pressure law states that the **pressure at any point in a static, incompressible fluid is given by**:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where $P_0$ is the pressure at the free surface, $\rho$ is the fluid density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $h$ is the depth below the free surface.

**Explanation:** This law shows that **pressure increases linearly with depth** below the free surface. The rate of pressure increase is $\rho g$ per unit depth.

**Pressure Equality on a Horizontal Plane:**

Consider two points A and B at the same elevation (same $h$). Applying the hydrostatic equation to both:

$$P_A = P_0 + \rho g h$$
$$P_B = P_0 + \rho g h$$

Therefore, $P_A = P_B$.

**Physical Reason:** Since gravity acts only in the **vertical direction**, there is no force component in the horizontal direction. By equilibrium, pressure cannot vary along a horizontal path — otherwise, fluid would flow horizontally to equalize the pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** This property is the **foundation of manometry** — fluid levels in connected vessels adjust so that pressures at the same elevation are equal.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** A closed tank contains water up to a depth of **4 m**. The air space above the water is pressurized to **150 kPa** (gauge). Calculate:
1. The absolute pressure at the **bottom** of the tank.
2. The **gauge pressure** at a depth of **2 m** from the water surface.
3. The **equivalent head** of water at the bottom of the tank.

Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **hydrostatic pressure equation** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ starting from the equilibrium of a fluid element. State clearly the assumptions made. Mention two engineering applications.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Given Data:**
- Depth of water in tank: $H = 4 \text{ m}$
- Air pressure above water (gauge): $P_0 = 150 \text{ kPa}$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

**Part 1: Absolute Pressure at Bottom of Tank**

Using the hydrostatic equation:

$$P_{bottom} = P_0 + \rho g H$$

Here $P_0$ is the air pressure (gauge) acting on the water surface.

$$P_{bottom} = 150 \times 10^3 + (1000)(9.81)(4)$$

$$P_{bottom} = 150,000 + 39,240 = 189,240 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{bottom} = 189.24 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

To get **absolute pressure**:

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

$$P_{abs} = 189.24 + 101.325 = 290.565 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 290.565 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Part 2: Gauge Pressure at Depth 2 m**

$$P_2 = P_0 + \rho g (2)$$

$$P_2 = 150 \times 10^3 + (1000)(9.81)(2)$$

$$P_2 = 150,000 + 19,620 = 169,620 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 169.62 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

**Part 3: Equivalent Head of Water at Bottom**

Equivalent head is the height of a water column that would produce the same pressure:

$$h_{eq} = \frac{P_{bottom}}{\rho g} = \frac{189,240}{(1000)(9.81)} = 19.29 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_{eq} = 19.29 \text{ m of water}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Many students forget to **convert kPa to Pa** before substituting into the formula. Always check: if $P_0$ is in kPa, multiply by $10^3$ to convert to Pa. Mixing units is the #1 reason for losing 2-3 marks in numerical problems.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Derivation of Hydrostatic Pressure Equation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is **in static equilibrium** (no motion, no shear stress).
2. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$).
3. The only **body force** is gravity acting in the $-z$ direction.
4. The fluid is **continuous** (no voids or bubbles).
5. The **acceleration** is zero (steady state).

**Step 1:** Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dz$ at depth $z$ below the free surface.

**Step 2:** Identify the forces on the element:
- Pressure force on top face (downward): $(P + dP) \cdot dA$
- Pressure force on bottom face (upward): $P \cdot dA$
- Weight of the element (downward): $\rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dz$

**Step 3:** Apply the static equilibrium condition $\sum F_z = 0$:

$$P \cdot dA - (P + dP) \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$-dP \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot dz = 0$$

Dividing by $dA$:

$$-dP = \rho g \cdot dz$$

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from the free surface (where $z = z_0$, $P = P_0$) to a point at depth $h$ (where $z = z_0 - h$, $P = P$):

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\rho g \int_{z_0}^{z_0 - h} dz$$

$$P - P_0 = -\rho g (z_0 - h - z_0) = \rho g h$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$$

**Two Engineering Applications:**

1. **Dam Design:** The hydrostatic pressure on a vertical dam face increases linearly with depth. The maximum pressure at the base is $\rho g H$, which determines the structural strength required at the bottom.

2. **Water Supply Systems:** The pressure at the consumer's tap equals the height difference between the overhead tank and the tap, multiplied by $\rho g$. Engineers use this to design the tank elevation in multi-story buildings.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** A U-tube **mercury manometer** is connected to a pipe carrying water under pressure. The mercury level difference is **25 cm**, with the mercury being **higher on the pipe side**. Calculate the **gauge pressure** of water in the pipe.

Given: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **absolute pressure** and **gauge pressure**. A vacuum gauge reads **40 kPa vacuum** at a location where the atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. Find the **absolute pressure**. Why are negative gauge pressures often referred to as vacuum pressures?

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Given Data:**
- Mercury level difference: $h = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13,600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Apply the Manometer Equation**

Starting from the pipe (point A) and moving down through water, then up through mercury, we end at the open atmosphere (point B). The hydrostatic balance gives:

$$P_A + \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_1 - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = P_{atm}$$

Where $h_1$ is the height of water above the mercury interface. Since the manometer is connected directly to the pipe and mercury level is higher on pipe side, the water column height on the pipe side is small compared to the mercury column. For simplicity (and this is a standard KTU simplification), we assume the water column above mercury is negligible or already included in the level difference.

**Step 2: Solve for $P_A$ (Gauge)**

$$P_A - P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

This is the **gauge pressure** of the water:

$$P_{gauge} = (13,600)(9.81)(0.25)$$

$$P_{gauge} = 33,354 \text{ Pa} = 33.354 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} = 33.354 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Approximation:** When a manometer uses a heavy fluid like mercury, the height of the lighter fluid (water) above the mercury interface is often **neglected** because its contribution is small compared to the mercury column. For exactness, the formula is $P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h - \rho_{water} g h_1$, but $h_1$ is usually small.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Absolute Pressure vs Gauge Pressure:**

| Parameter | Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$) | Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference** | Perfect vacuum (zero) | Local atmospheric pressure |
| **Range** | Always positive (≥ 0) | Can be positive, zero, or negative |
| **Value at sea level** | ≈ 101.325 kPa | 0 kPa |
| **Formula** | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |

**Calculating Absolute Pressure:**

Given:
- Vacuum gauge reading: $P_{vac} = 40 \text{ kPa}$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

The relationship between vacuum pressure and absolute pressure is:

$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$

Rearranging:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

$$P_{abs} = 101.325 - 40 = 61.325 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 61.325 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Why Negative Gauge Pressures are Called Vacuum Pressures:**

When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$, the gauge pressure becomes **negative**:

$$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm} < 0$$

A negative gauge reading means the absolute pressure is **below atmospheric pressure** — the space is partially evacuated. This is called a **vacuum**, and its magnitude is:

$$P_{vacuum} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = \vert P_{gauge} \vert$$

In engineering practice (e.g., condensers of steam plants, suction side of pumps), pressures below atmospheric are common, and a "vacuum gauge" directly displays the magnitude of the vacuum. Hence, negative gauge pressures are referred to as **vacuum pressures**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is using $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{vac}$ instead of $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$. The minus sign is critical for vacuum readings. Memorize: **Vacuum = how much below atmospheric**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Fluid Statics: Pressure, Density, Height Relationship**

### Core Equations (Must Memorize)
- $\boxed{P = \dfrac{F}{A}}$ — Pressure definition
- $\boxed{\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}}$ — Density definition
- $\boxed{\gamma = \rho g}$ — Specific weight
- $\boxed{SG = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}}$ — Specific gravity (dimensionless)
- $\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}$ — **The King Equation** of fluid statics
- $\boxed{dP = -\rho g dz}$ — Differential hydrostatic form
- $\boxed{P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}}$ — Absolute-Gauge relationship
- $\boxed{P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}}$ — Vacuum pressure formula

### Key Conceptual Points
- **Pressure is a scalar** (acts equally in all directions at a point).
- **Pressure increases with depth** at the rate of $\rho g$ per unit depth.
- **Pressure is constant on a horizontal plane** in a connected static fluid.
- **Pressure depends on depth, not on shape** (hydrostatic paradox).
- The **free surface is an isobaric surface** (constant pressure).
- The **direction of pressure force is always normal** (perpendicular) to the surface.

### Units Mastery
- $1 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$
- $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- $1 \text{ kgf/cm}^2 = 9.81 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$
- $760 \text{ mm Hg} = 1 \text{ atm}$

### KTU High-Yield Problem Types
1. **Pressure at a depth** — direct substitution in $P = P_0 + \rho g h$.
2. **Manometer problems** — apply hydrostatic balance along a path.
3. **Force on submerged surfaces** — $F = \rho g h_c \cdot A$ (where $h_c$ is the depth of the centroid).
4. **Absolute vs gauge** — conversion using $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$.
5. **Buoyancy (Archimedes' principle)** — $F_B = \rho_{fluid} g V_{displaced}$.

### Common Student Mistakes (Avoid These!)
- ❌ Forgetting to convert units (kPa ↔ Pa, cm ↔ m).
- ❌ Using gauge pressure where absolute is required, or vice versa.
- ❌ Mixing up the sign in the manometer equation.
- ❌ Applying the equation to a **moving fluid** (it only works for static fluids).
- ❌ Confusing specific weight with density.
- ❌ Treating $g$ as $10 \text{ m/s}^2$ when the problem specifies $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Most Module 1 numericals are worth **3 to 7 marks** and follow a predictable pattern: (1) write the given data, (2) state the formula, (3) substitute, (4) compute, (5) box the answer with units. Practice this structure and you will score full marks on KTU board exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrostatic Force and Pressure on Plane and Inclined Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrostatic Force and Pressure on Plane and Inclined Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hydrostatic Pressure** is the normal stress exerted by a fluid at rest on any surface in contact with it, arising solely from the weight of the fluid column above the point of consideration. For a static incompressible fluid, it is mathematically expressed as:

$$p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $\rho$ is the mass density of the fluid (**kg/m³**), $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**), and $h$ is the vertical depth measured from the free liquid surface (**m**).

A **Submerged Plane Surface** is a flat surface immersed in a static fluid. It may be **horizontal**, **vertical**, or **inclined** at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The fluid exerts a perpendicular (normal) hydrostatic thrust on every elemental area, and the integrated effect yields the **Total Hydrostatic Force** $F$.

The **Center of Pressure** $C_p$ is the point on the submerged surface where the resultant hydrostatic force is deemed to act. Unlike the centroid (which is purely a geometric property), the center of pressure shifts **deeper** than the centroid due to the non-linear (linear) increase of pressure with depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus Point:** For a *vertically* submerged plane surface, the centroid coincides with the center of pressure **only if** the surface is a *horizontal strip at constant depth* — in all other cases, $C_p \neq G$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine you are standing at the **deep end** of a swimming pool and feeling the pressure on your eardrums. The deeper you dive, the heavier your ears feel. This is because each square metre of your eardrum has to "carry" the weight of the water column standing vertically above it. Now imagine a **slanted glass aquarium door**. The pressure still depends on the *vertical* depth, not the slant length, because gravity pulls water straight down. The water pushes harder on the lower portion of the door than the upper portion, so the *push* acts at a point lower than the geometric middle — this point is the **center of pressure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** This concept is the foundation for designing **dams, sluice gates, lock gates, ship hulls, submarine hatches, and storage tank bulkheads**. An incorrect estimate of center of pressure can cause catastrophic hinge/moment miscalculations in civil and marine structures.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 4 °C) | $\rho_{water}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma$ | **9810 N/m³** |
| Atmospheric pressure | $p_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Specific gravity of mercury | $SG_{Hg}$ | **13.6** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The derivation of hydrostatic force on submerged surfaces follows a rigorous, layered logic. Each step has a clear physical and mathematical purpose:

1. **Step 1 — Establish Pressure Field:** For a fluid at rest, the pressure at any depth $h$ below the free surface is governed by Pascal's principle and the hydrostatic equation: $p = \rho g h$. This is linear in $h$ — pressure increases uniformly with vertical depth.

2. **Step 2 — Define the Coordinate System:** For an *inclined* surface, choose the $x$-axis along the slant (measured from the line where the surface intersects the free liquid surface), and the $y$-axis along the horizontal direction. The depth of any point at slant distance $x$ is $h = x \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the surface and the horizontal.

3. **Step 3 — Elemental Force:** Consider an infinitesimal element of area $dA$ at slant distance $x$. The pressure acting on it is $p = \rho g x \sin\theta$, and the elemental force is $dF = p \cdot dA = \rho g x \sin\theta \cdot dA$. This force acts *normal* to the surface (perpendicular, into the surface).

4. **Step 4 — Integrate to Get Total Force:** $F = \int_A dF = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A x \, dA$. Recognising that $\int_A x \, dA = \bar{x} \cdot A$ (first moment of area about the free-surface line), we get $F = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{x} \cdot A = \rho g \cdot h_c \cdot A$, where $h_c = \bar{x} \sin\theta$ is the **vertical depth of the centroid**.

5. **Step 5 — Locate the Line of Action (Center of Pressure):** Take moments about the free-surface intersection. $F \cdot x_p = \int x \cdot dF = \rho g \sin\theta \int x^2 \, dA$. The integral $\int x^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area** $I_o$ about the free-surface line. By the **parallel axis theorem**, $I_o = I_G + A \bar{x}^2$, where $I_G$ is the second moment about the centroidal axis parallel to the free surface.

6. **Step 6 — Final Expression for $C_p$:** Substituting and simplifying gives the slant distance to the center of pressure: $x_p = \bar{x} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{x}}$. Converting to vertical depth: $h_p = x_p \sin\theta = h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \cdot h_c}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Tip:** Memorise the **slant-distance form** of formulas — it is what KTU examiners typically use to set numerical problems involving inclined surfaces.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $p = \rho g h$ | Pa (N/m²) | Measured as *gauge* unless specified absolute |
| 2 | Total force on inclined surface (slant form) | $F = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{x} \cdot A$ | N | $\bar{x}$ = slant distance to centroid |
| 3 | Total force on inclined surface (depth form) | $F = \rho g \cdot h_c \cdot A$ | N | $h_c$ = vertical depth of centroid |
| 4 | Total force on vertical surface | $F = \rho g \cdot h_c \cdot A$ | N | Same formula, with $\theta = 90°$ |
| 5 | Center of pressure (slant form) | $x_p = \bar{x} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{x}}$ | m | Measured from free-surface line |
| 6 | Center of pressure (depth form) | $h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \cdot h_c}$ | m | Always $h_p \geq h_c$ |
| 7 | Parallel axis theorem | $I_o = I_G + A \bar{x}^2$ | m⁴ | Connects moment about $C_p$ to moment about $G$ |
| 8 | Pressure at any point (combined) | $p = p_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa | $p_0$ = surface pressure |
| 9 | Force on horizontal surface (top/bottom) | $F = p \cdot A = \rho g h \cdot A$ | N | Only valid if fluid is in equilibrium |
| 10 | Pressure prism volume | $V_{prism} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g H^2 \cdot b$ | N | For rectangular vertical wall of height $H$ and width $b$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dam Design (Civil):** The horizontal hydrostatic thrust on a dam face governs overturning stability and sliding safety factors. Engineers compute $F$ and $C_p$ to size the dam's base width.
- **Sluice and Lock Gates (Hydraulic):** The moment about the gate hinge determines the torque needed for opening mechanisms (manual or motorised).
- **Submarine Pressure Hulls (Marine/Naval):** The pressure hull is approximated as a series of cylindrical/strip elements; integrated $F$ gives total compressive load.
- **Storage Tank Bulkheads:** Flat end caps of cylindrical tanks experience hydrostatic thrust that determines plate thickness and stiffener spacing.
- **Manometer / Bourdon Tube Calibration:** Hydrostatic principles are used to calibrate pressure gauges at known depths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Hydrostatic Force on an Inclined Submerged Surface

**Setup:** Consider a flat plane surface of arbitrary shape (area $A$) immersed in a static liquid of density $\rho$. The surface is inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. Let the free liquid surface be the datum.

**Geometry:**
- The line of intersection of the plane surface with the free surface (when extended) is taken as the $x$-axis origin.
- The slant distance from this origin to any point on the surface is denoted $x$.
- The vertical depth of that point is $h = x \sin\theta$.

**Derivation Steps:**

Step 1 — Pressure at slant distance $x$:

$$
p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \cdot g \cdot (x \sin\theta)
$$

Step 2 — Consider an elemental strip of area $dA$ at slant distance $x$:

$$
dF = p \cdot dA = \rho g x \sin\theta \cdot dA
$$

Step 3 — Total force is the integral over the whole surface:

$$
F = \int_A \rho g x \sin\theta \cdot dA
$$

Step 4 — Since $\rho$, $g$, and $\sin\theta$ are constants over the surface:

$$
F = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A x \, dA
$$

Step 5 — By the definition of the centroid (first moment of area):

$$
\int_A x \, dA = \bar{x} \cdot A
$$

Step 6 — Substituting back:

$$
F = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{x} \cdot A
$$

Step 7 — Recognising that $\bar{x} \sin\theta = h_c$ (vertical depth of centroid):

$$
\boxed{F = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_c \cdot A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Derivation Frequently Asked:** Memorise this derivation exactly — it has appeared in **ESE Module 1** questions almost every academic year in some form. The board examiner expects step-by-step clarity from the *elemental pressure* to the *final boxed equation*.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Location of the Center of Pressure on an Inclined Surface

**Setup:** We want the slant distance $x_p$ from the free-surface line to the point where $F$ acts.

Step 1 — Take moments of all elemental forces about the free-surface line:

$$
F \cdot x_p = \int_A x \cdot dF = \int_A x \cdot (\rho g x \sin\theta \cdot dA) = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A x^2 \, dA
$$

Step 2 — The integral $\int_A x^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area** about the free-surface line, denoted $I_o$:

$$
F \cdot x_p = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot I_o
$$

Step 3 — Apply the **parallel axis theorem** (transfer from centroidal axis to the free-surface axis):

$$
I_o = I_G + A \cdot \bar{x}^2
$$

Step 4 — Substitute $F = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{x} \cdot A$ and $I_o = I_G + A \bar{x}^2$:

$$
(\rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{x} \cdot A) \cdot x_p = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot (I_G + A \bar{x}^2)
$$

Step 5 — Cancel $\rho g \sin\theta$ from both sides and divide by $A \bar{x}$:

$$
x_p = \bar{x} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{x}}
$$

Step 6 — Multiply both sides by $\sin\theta$ to convert slant distance to vertical depth:

$$
\boxed{h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \cdot h_c}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice that the second term $I_G \sin^2\theta / (A h_c)$ is **always positive** (since $I_G$, $A$, $h_c$, and $\sin^2\theta$ are all positive). Therefore, $h_p \geq h_c$ always. The center of pressure lies *at or below* the centroid, never above it.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Special Cases Summary

| Surface Orientation | $\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | $h_c$ | $x_p$ | $h_p$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal (top face, no fluid above) | $0°$ | $0$ | $h_c$ (constant) | — | $h_c$ | Pressure uniform, $C_p = G$ |
| Horizontal (bottom face) | $0°$ | $0$ | $h_c$ | — | $h_c$ | Uniform pressure |
| Vertical wall | $90°$ | $1$ | $\bar{y}$ | $\bar{y}$ | $h_c + \dfrac{I_G}{A h_c}$ | $x_p = \bar{y}$ (since $x = y$ when vertical) |
| Inclined ($\theta < 90°$) | $\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | $\bar{x}\sin\theta$ | $\bar{x} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{x}}$ | $h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A h_c}$ | General case |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometry of an Inclined Submerged Surface (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Free Liquid Surface Datum"] --> B1["x-axis Origin O at Intersection Line"]
    B1 --> C1["Slant Distance x Measured Along Surface"]
    C1 --> D1["Submerged Plane Area A Inclined at Angle theta"]
    D1 --> E1["Vertical Depth h = x sin theta"]
    E1 --> F1["Pressure p = rho g h"]
    F1 --> G1["Elemental Force dF = p dA"]
    G1 --> H1["Total Force F = rho g h_c A"]
    D1 --> I1["Centroid G at x_bar and depth h_c"]
    D1 --> J1["Center of Pressure Cp at x_p deeper than G"]
    I1 --> K1["Distance x_p minus x_bar = I_G / A x_bar"]
    J1 --> H1
```

### 4.2 Algorithm: Step-by-Step Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Identify Surface Geometry Area A Shape theta h_c"] --> S2["Step 2: Compute Centroid Depth h_c = x_bar sin theta"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute Second Moment I_G about Centroidal Axis"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Apply F equals rho g h_c A for Total Force"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute h_p using Parallel Axis Correction"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Verify h_p greater than h_c and Report Result"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: If Vertical use simplified form x_p equals y_bar plus I_G over A y_bar"]
```

### 4.3 Pressure Prism Concept (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Pressure p"] --> P2["Depth h Linear Variation"]
    P2 --> P3["Prism Shape Triangular for Vertical Wall"]
    P3 --> P4["Volume of Prism equals Total Force per unit Width"]
    P4 --> P5["Centroid of Prism equals Location of Cp"]
    P5 --> P6["Width b times Volume equals Total Force F"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualisation Note:** The pressure prism for a vertical wall is a *right triangle* with base = wall height and height = pressure at the bottom ($\rho g H$). The volume of this prism (per unit width) is exactly the force per unit width. The centroid of this triangular prism (at $H/3$ from the base, or $2H/3$ from the top) is the **center of pressure**.

### 4.4 Tabulated Standard $I_G$ Values for Quick Reference

| Shape | Dimensions | $I_G$ about centroidal axis | $I_G / A$ (radius of gyration squared) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangle (base $b$, height $h$) | $b \times h$ | $\dfrac{b h^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{h^2}{12}$ |
| Triangle (base $b$, height $h$) | $b \times h$ | $\dfrac{b h^3}{36}$ | $\dfrac{h^2}{18}$ |
| Circle (diameter $D$) | $D$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D^2}{16}$ |
| Trapezoid (parallel sides $a$, $b$; height $h$) | $a, b, h$ | $\dfrac{h^3 (a^2 + 4ab + b^2)}{36(a+b)}$ | Complex |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define hydrostatic pressure and explain how it varies with depth for a static incompressible fluid.**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrostatic pressure is the normal compressive stress exerted by a fluid at rest on any surface in contact with it, resulting from the weight of the fluid column above the point. For a static incompressible fluid of density $\rho$, the pressure at a vertical depth $h$ below the free surface is given by:
$$
p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$
The pressure varies **linearly** with depth — it is **zero** at the free surface ($h = 0$) and increases at the rate of $\rho g$ (which is $\gamma$, the specific weight) per metre of depth. Pressure acts **equally in all directions** at a point (Pascal's principle) and always **normal** to the surface on which it acts.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by the center of pressure? Why does it lie below the centroid for a vertical submerged surface?**

**Model Answer:**
The **center of pressure** is the point on a submerged surface where the resultant hydrostatic thrust is considered to act. For a vertical (or inclined) submerged surface, pressure increases linearly with depth. Therefore, the lower portions of the surface experience greater pressure than the upper portions. This non-uniform pressure distribution shifts the line of action of the resultant force *below* the geometric centroid. Mathematically, the vertical depth of the center of pressure is given by:
$$
h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_G}{A \cdot h_c}
$$
The correction term $\dfrac{I_G}{A \cdot h_c}$ is **always positive**, ensuring $h_p \geq h_c$. Only in the limiting case of a *horizontal* surface (constant pressure) does $C_p$ coincide with $G$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the total hydrostatic force acting on one side of a plane surface immersed in a liquid, inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. Also obtain the expression for the depth of the center of pressure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let the inclined plane surface of area $A$ be submerged in a static liquid of density $\rho$. Choose the $x$-axis along the inclined surface, originating at the intersection of the extended plane with the free liquid surface. The angle of inclination to the horizontal is $\theta$.

- Pressure at a point at slant distance $x$ from the origin: $p = \rho g x \sin\theta$.
- Elemental force on area $dA$: $dF = p \, dA = \rho g x \sin\theta \, dA$.
- Total force:
$$
F = \int_A \rho g x \sin\theta \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A x \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot \bar{x} A
$$
- Since $\bar{x} \sin\theta = h_c$, we get $\boxed{F = \rho g h_c A}$.
- Taking moments about the free-surface line:
$$
F x_p = \int_A x \cdot dF = \rho g \sin\theta \int_A x^2 \, dA = \rho g \sin\theta \cdot I_o
$$
- Using parallel axis theorem $I_o = I_G + A \bar{x}^2$ and substituting $F$:
$$
x_p = \bar{x} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{x}}
$$
- In terms of vertical depth: $\boxed{h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A h_c}}$.

**(b)** A circular gate of diameter **2 m** is inclined at **45°** to the horizontal. The centroid of the gate is located at a vertical depth of **5 m** below the free water surface. Determine the magnitude of the resultant hydrostatic force on the gate and the position of the center of pressure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 2$ m, hence area $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 4 = \pi = 3.1416$ m²
- $\theta = 45°$, hence $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071$
- Centroid depth $h_c = 5$ m
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Total Force:**
$$
F = \rho g h_c A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 3.1416
$$
$$
F = 154{,}081 \text{ N} \approx 154.08 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area (circular):**
$$
I_G = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 16}{64} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of Center of Pressure:**
$$
h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \cdot h_c}
$$
$$
h_p = 5 + \dfrac{0.7854 \times (0.7071)^2}{3.1416 \times 5}
$$
$$
h_p = 5 + \dfrac{0.7854 \times 0.5}{15.708} = 5 + \dfrac{0.3927}{15.708}
$$
$$
h_p = 5 + 0.025 = 5.025 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Total Force $F = 154.08$ kN** (acting perpendicular to the gate)
- **Center of Pressure at depth $h_p = 5.025$ m** (i.e., 0.025 m = 25 mm below the centroid)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **pressure prism** with a neat sketch. How is it used to determine the magnitude and location of the resultant hydrostatic force on a vertical rectangular surface submerged in a liquid? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **pressure prism** is a graphical/visual representation of the pressure distribution over a submerged surface. Since hydrostatic pressure varies linearly with depth, the pressure diagram over a vertical rectangular wall is a **right-angled triangle**:
- The base of the triangle is the height $H$ of the wall (in the depth direction).
- The vertical side at the bottom represents the maximum pressure $p_{max} = \rho g H$.
- The horizontal side at the top is zero (at the free surface).

**Key principles:**
1. The **volume** of the pressure prism equals the **total hydrostatic force per unit width** of the wall.
2. The **centroid** of the pressure prism locates the **center of pressure** on the wall.
3. For a triangular prism (vertical wall), the centroid lies at $H/3$ from the base (or $2H/3$ from the free surface).

**For a vertical rectangular wall of width $b$ and height $H$ with top edge at free surface:**
- Volume of prism per unit width = $\dfrac{1}{2} \rho g H^2$
- Total force: $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g H^2 \cdot b$
- Center of pressure depth: $h_p = \dfrac{2H}{3}$ (i.e., two-thirds of the wall height below the free surface)

For a wall whose top is at depth $h_1$ and bottom at depth $h_2$ (i.e., $H = h_2 - h_1$):
- $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g (h_2^2 - h_1^2) \cdot b$
- $h_p = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{h_2^3 - h_1^3}{h_2^2 - h_1^2}$

This method provides a quick visual check for design engineers when sketching the pressure distribution on lock gates, dam faces, and bulkheads.

**(b)** A rectangular sluice gate of width **1.5 m** and height **2 m** is inclined at **60°** to the horizontal. The top edge of the gate is at a depth of **3 m** below the free water surface. Determine the magnitude of the resultant force on the gate and the depth of the center of pressure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Width $b = 1.5$ m, height of gate $= 2$ m
- Area $A = 1.5 \times 2 = 3$ m²
- $\theta = 60°$, so $\sin 60° = 0.8660$
- Depth of top edge $h_{top} = 3$ m

**Step 1 — Locate the Centroid:**
The centroid of the rectangular gate is at its geometric centre, i.e., 1 m below the top edge along the slant. The vertical depth of the top edge is 3 m, so the slant distance from the free-surface line (extended) to the top edge is:

$$
x_{top} = \dfrac{h_{top}}{\sin 60°} = \dfrac{3}{0.8660} = 3.464 \text{ m}
$$

Slant distance to centroid:
$$
\bar{x} = x_{top} + 1 = 4.464 \text{ m}
$$

Vertical depth of centroid:
$$
h_c = \bar{x} \sin 60° = 4.464 \times 0.8660 = 3.866 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Force:**
$$
F = \rho g h_c A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.866 \times 3
$$
$$
F = 113{,}777 \text{ N} \approx 113.78 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Second Moment of Area:**
For a rectangle about its centroidal axis (parallel to width):
$$
I_G = \dfrac{b h^3}{12} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 2^3}{12} = \dfrac{12}{12} = 1 \text{ m}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Depth of Center of Pressure:**
$$
h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \cdot h_c}
$$
$$
h_p = 3.866 + \dfrac{1 \times (0.8660)^2}{3 \times 3.866}
$$
$$
h_p = 3.866 + \dfrac{0.75}{11.598} = 3.866 + 0.0647
$$
$$
h_p = 3.931 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Total Force $F = 113.78$ kN** (acting normal to the gate face)
- **Center of Pressure at depth $h_p = 3.931$ m** (i.e., 0.065 m = 65 mm below the centroid, or 4.931 m along the slant from the free-surface line)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing slant distance with vertical depth:** Many students forget to multiply $\bar{x}$ by $\sin\theta$ when computing $h_c$, leading to an incorrect $F$. Always state: *"vertical depth = slant distance × sin(θ)"*.
> 2. **Using the wrong $I_G$ formula:** For a rectangle, $I_G = bh^3/12$ refers to the axis *perpendicular to the height*. Mixing up $b$ and $h$ costs full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the parallel axis theorem:** A common error is using $I_G$ directly in the moment equation without transferring it to the free-surface axis. The board examiner deducts **2 marks** for this oversight.
> 4. **Unit slip-ups:** Always express force in **N** or **kN**, depth in **m**, and second moment in **m⁴**. Mixing units (e.g., cm and m) is a frequent mark-loser.
> 5. **Omitting the derivation of $h_p$:** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect *both* $F$ and $h_p$ derived and then computed numerically. A derivation without a numerical answer scores only ~6/7 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] Hydrostatic pressure $p = \rho g h$ is **linear in depth** and acts **normal** to the submerged surface.
- [x] Total force on a plane surface: $F = \rho g h_c A$, where $h_c$ is the **vertical** depth of the centroid.
- [x] For inclined surfaces, slant distance $\bar{x}$ relates to depth as $h_c = \bar{x} \sin\theta$.
- [x] Center of pressure (slant): $x_p = \bar{x} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \bar{x}}$.
- [x] Center of pressure (depth): $h_p = h_c + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A h_c}$ — **always $h_p \geq h_c$**.
- [x] Parallel axis theorem: $I_o = I_G + A \bar{x}^2$ — connect $I$ about $C_p$ to $I$ about $G$.
- [x] Pressure prism: triangular volume for vertical walls; its centroid gives the location of $C_p$.
- [x] Standard $I_G$ values: Rectangle $\dfrac{bh^3}{12}$, Triangle $\dfrac{bh^3}{36}$, Circle $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$.
- [x] KTU high-weightage: A 14-mark question combining *derivation + numerical problem* on an inclined gate is a near-certainty every year.
- [x] Common mistake to avoid: confusing slant and vertical distances, and forgetting $I_G$ vs $I_o$.
- [x] Always verify units (N, m, m⁴) before finalising the numerical answer.
- [x] For **vertical surfaces**, $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$, slant distance = vertical depth — formulas simplify elegantly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centre of Pressure, Buoyancy and Metacentre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Centre of Pressure, Buoyancy and Metacentre # Centre of Pressure, Buoyancy and Metacentre — KTU Premier Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Centre of Pressure (CoP) **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** The *Centre of Pressure* on a submerged surface is the point on that surface through which the *resultant hydrostatic pressure force* is assumed to act. It is the geometric location where the entire distributed fluid pressure can be replaced by a single equivalent point load without altering the external moment effect on the surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:** > *"Centre of pressure is the point of application of the resultant pressure force acting on a submerged surface."* **Conceptual Analogy — The "Push the Door" Analogy:** Imagine pushing open a heavy, fully submerged rectangular gate at the bottom of a swimming pool. The pressure is *not* uniform across the gate — the water near the bottom pushes much harder than the water near the top. If you tried to balance the gate with a single finger, you would naturally place that finger **below the geometric centre** of the gate. That single balance point is the **Centre of Pressure**. - For a **horizontal** submerged surface, the pressure is uniform, so CoP coincides exactly with the **Centroid (G)**. - For a **vertical or inclined** surface, CoP lies *below* the centroid. ### 1.2 Buoyancy **Formal Definition:** *Buoyancy* is the upward force exerted by a fluid on a body fully or partially immersed in it. Its magnitude, by **Archimedes' Principle**, equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. $$F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$ > [!NOTE] > The point through which this buoyant force acts is called the **Centre of Buoyancy (B)**. It is, by definition, the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume**. **Intuition — Why ships float:** A 1000-tonne steel ship does not sink because it is not a solid block of steel. Its hull is a hollow, air-filled vessel. The volume of water *displaced* by the giant hull weighs more than 1000 tonnes, so the upward buoyant force easily supports the ship's weight. ### 1.3 Metacentre **Formal Definition:** The **Metacentre (M)** is the point at which the line of action of the new buoyant force (acting through the shifted Centre of Buoyancy) intersects the *original vertical axis of symmetry* of the floating body, when the body is given a small angular tilt. **Analogy — The Tippy-Cup Test:** Place a tall, narrow glass (high G) on the table. Push it slightly. It topples over. Now place a wide, low bowl (low G) on the table. Push it slightly. It rights itself. The reason is *where the Metacentre sits* relative to the centre of gravity. In the cup, M lies *below* G → unstable. In the bowl, M lies *above* G → stable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability Rule (KTU High-Yield):** > A floating body is in **STABLE EQUILIBRIUM** if and only if the **Metacentre (M) lies ABOVE the Centre of Gravity (G)**, i.e., **GM > 0** and **M is above G**. > > Standard relationship: $$\boxed{GM = BM - BG}$$ (when G lies above B, the typical case for ships). ]<]minimax[>[ ### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters (Engineering Utility) | Real-World Application | Concept Used | |---|---| | Design of dam gates & sluice gates | Centre of Pressure | | Ship hull design & naval architecture | Metacentre & GM | | Submarine depth control (ballast tanks) | Buoyancy | | Design of pontoons, barges, floating cranes | All three concepts together | | Hydrometer & submarine operation | Buoyancy + stability | ]<]minimax[>[ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Centre of Pressure — Step-by-Step Logic Consider a flat plane surface of area **A**, fully immersed in a static liquid of density **ρ**. The plane is *inclined* at angle **θ** with the horizontal free surface. **Setup:** Take the **X-axis** along the intersection of the inclined plane with the free liquid surface. Take the **Y-axis** down the inclined plane (perpendicular to X). **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Elemental Strip:** Consider a thin strip of area `dA = b·dy` at perpendicular distance *y* from the X-axis. 2. **Depth of Strip:** Vertical depth below free surface = `h = y sin θ`. 3. **Pressure on Strip:** `p = ρ g h = ρ g y sin θ`. 4. **Force on Strip:** `dF = p · dA = ρ g y sin θ · dA`. 5. **Total Force (by integration):** `F = ∫ dF = ρ g sin θ ∫ y dA = ρ g sin θ · ȳ · A`, where `ȳ` is the distance of the centroid from the X-axis along the inclined plane. Equivalently, `F = ρ g h̄ A`, where `h̄ = ȳ sin θ` is the **depth of the centroid**. 6. **Moment about X-axis:** `M_X = ∫ y · dF = ρ g sin θ ∫ y² dA = ρ g sin θ · I_X`, where `I_X` is the *second moment of area* of the surface about the X-axis. 7. **Use Parallel Axis Theorem:** `I_X = I_G + A ȳ²`, where `I_G` is the second moment about the centroidal axis (parallel to X). 8. **Locate the Centre of Pressure** at perpendicular distance `y*` from X-axis: `y* = M_X / F = (I_G + A ȳ²) / (A ȳ) = ȳ + I_G / (A ȳ)`. 9. **Convert to vertical depth:** `h* = y* sin θ = h̄ + (I_G · sin θ) / (A · ȳ)`. 10. Since `ȳ = h̄ / sin θ`, this becomes the celebrated formula: `h* = h̄ + (I_G · sin²θ) / (A · h̄)`. ### 2.2 Buoyancy — Logic Steps 1. Consider an arbitrary submerged body. The fluid pressure acts *normal* to every point on the body's surface. 2. Resolve and integrate these pressures over the entire closed surface. 3. The vertical components of the pressure forces, when integrated over the body, yield exactly the weight of the displaced fluid — directed **upward**. 4. The horizontal components cancel out by symmetry. 5. The line of action of this net upward force passes through the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume** — this point is the **Centre of Buoyancy, B**. ### 2.3 Metacentre — Logic Steps 1. Body floats in equilibrium → Weight `W` (acting down through **G**) = Buoyancy `F_B` (acting up through **B**) along the same vertical line. 2. Body is given a small angular tilt **dθ** about some longitudinal axis through the centroid of the waterline area. 3. The submerged volume geometry changes; the new centre of buoyancy shifts from **B** to **B′**. 4. The new buoyant force `F_B′` (still vertical, magnitude unchanged for small tilts) now acts along a different vertical line through `B′`. 5. Extend this new vertical line upward until it meets the **original vertical axis of symmetry** of the body. The intersection point is the **Metacentre, M**. 6. By definition, the distance from the new buoyancy point to this intersection, along the original axis, is **BM = I / V_d**, where: - `I` = Second moment of area of the **waterline plane** about the axis of tilting - `V_d` = Displaced (submerged) volume ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This table contains the **must-know formulas** for any KTU exam question on this topic. Memorise the units alongside each formula. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total pressure force | $F = \rho \, g \, \bar{h} \, A$ | $\rho$=density, $g$=9.81, $\bar{h}$=depth of centroid, $A$=area | N (Newton) | | 2 | Centre of Pressure depth | $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_{G}\sin^{2}\theta}{A\,\bar{h}}$ | $h^{*}$=depth of CoP, $I_{G}$=2nd moment about centroidal axis | m | | 3 | Distance CoP below centroid | $h^{*} - \bar{h} = \dfrac{I_{G}\sin^{2}\theta}{A\,\bar{h}}$ | — | m | | 4 | CoP along inclined plane | $y^{*} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_{G}}{A\,\bar{y}}$ | $y^{*}$=perpendicular distance from free-surface line | m | | 5 | Buoyant force | $F_{B} = \rho_{fluid}\,g\,V_{displaced}$ | $V_{displaced}$=volume of fluid displaced | N | | 6 | Archimedes' Weight | $W_{fluid,disp} = \gamma \, V_{disp}$ | $\gamma = \rho g$ = specific weight | N | | 7 | Depth of flotation | $V_{disp} = A_{plan} \times h_{sub}$ | $h_{sub}$=submerged depth | m³ | | 8 | Distance B to M | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}}$ | $I$=2nd moment of waterline area about tilt axis | m | | 9 | Metacentric height | $GM = BM - BG$ (if G above B) | $BG$=vertical distance between B and G | m | | 10 | Stability condition | $GM > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;$ Stable | Metacentre above G | m | ### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Naval Architecture:** Ship designers compute `GM` to ensure a vessel does not capsize. Passenger ships typically require `GM \geq 0.15 m` for comfortable, non-rolly motion. - **Dam Engineering:** Hydrostatic force on a sluice gate is computed via `F = \rho g \bar{h} A`. The hinge of the gate must be placed *above* the CoP so the gate closes automatically under its own weight when water is released. - **Submarine Design:** Ballast tanks are flooded/emptied to vary average density, controlling buoyancy and depth. - **Storage Tanks:** Side walls of large tanks experience non-uniform pressure; CoP is needed to design reinforcement. ]<]minimax[>[ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Centre of Pressure on an Inclined Submerged Surface **Given:** A plane surface of area *A*, inclined at angle **θ** with the horizontal free surface of a liquid of density **ρ**. The centroid *G* of the area lies at vertical depth $\bar{h}$ below the free surface. **To find:** The vertical depth $h^{*}$ of the Centre of Pressure below the free surface. **Derivation:** Consider a small elemental strip of the surface, parallel to the free surface intersection line (X-axis), at a perpendicular distance *y* from the X-axis. The strip has width *b* (measured along X-axis) and thickness *dy*. The area of the strip is: $$dA = b \, dy$$ The vertical depth of this strip below the free surface is: $$h = y \sin\theta$$ The hydrostatic pressure acting on this strip is given by: $$p = \rho \, g \, h = \rho \, g \, y \, \sin\theta$$ Therefore, the hydrostatic force on the elemental strip is: $$dF = p \, dA = \rho \, g \, y \, \sin\theta \, b \, dy$$ **Total force on the entire surface** (integrating across the area): $$F = \int_{A} dF = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \int_{A} y \, dA$$ By the definition of the centroid (first moment of area), we know that: $$\int_{A} y \, dA = \bar{y} \cdot A$$ Therefore, the total hydrostatic force becomes: $$F = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \cdot \bar{y} \cdot A$$ Since the depth of the centroid is $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$, we can also write: $$F = \rho \, g \, \bar{h} \cdot A$$ **Moment of the pressure forces about the X-axis:** $$M_X = \int_{A} y \, dF = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \int_{A} y^{2} \, dA$$ By definition, $\int_{A} y^{2} \, dA = I_X$, where $I_X$ is the **second moment of area** of the surface about the X-axis (the line where the inclined plane meets the free liquid surface). Therefore: $$M_X = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \cdot I_X$$ **Apply the Parallel Axis Theorem:** The second moment about the X-axis can be expressed in terms of the second moment about the centroidal axis (parallel to X): $$I_X = I_G + A \, \bar{y}^{\,2}$$ Substituting back: $$M_X = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \left(I_G + A \, \bar{y}^{\,2}\right)$$ **Locate the Centre of Pressure:** By definition, the Centre of Pressure is at perpendicular distance $y^{*}$ from the X-axis such that: $$M_X = F \cdot y^{*}$$ Solving for $y^{*}$: $$y^{*} = \dfrac{M_X}{F} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, \sin\theta \left(I_G + A \, \bar{y}^{\,2}\right)}{\rho \, g \, \sin\theta \cdot \bar{y} \cdot A}$$ Simplify: $$y^{*} = \dfrac{I_G + A \, \bar{y}^{\,2}}{A \, \bar{y}} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \, \bar{y}}$$ **Convert perpendicular distance to vertical depth:** The vertical depth of the Centre of Pressure below the free surface is: $$h^{*} = y^{*} \sin\theta = \left(\bar{y} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \, \bar{y}}\right) \sin\theta$$ Since $\bar{h} = \bar{y} \sin\theta$ and $\bar{y} = \bar{h} / \sin\theta$: $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin\theta}{A \cdot (\bar{h} / \sin\theta)} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}$$ **Final Result (KTU Board Standard Form):** $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}\,}$$ The **shift of the Centre of Pressure below the Centroid** is therefore: $$\boxed{\,h^{*} - \bar{h} = \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Limit Case Sanity Check:** As $\theta \to 0$ (horizontal surface), $\sin^{2}\theta \to 0$, so $h^{*} \to \bar{h}$: the Centre of Pressure coincides with the centroid. ✓ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Metacentric Height (BM = I / V) **Given:** A floating body of weight *W*, displaced volume $V_d$, with its waterline plane having second moment of area *I* about the axis of tilting. **Scenario:** The body is tilted by a small angle *dθ* about a longitudinal axis passing through the centroid *O* of the waterline area. **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Identify the Geometry.** - Original Centre of Buoyancy: *B* (centroid of original submerged volume). - Original Weight line of action: vertical through *G*. - In equilibrium, *B* and *G* lie on the same vertical line. **Step 2 — Tilt the Body by Small Angle dθ.** - The submerged wedge-shaped volume on one side **increases** by $\delta V$. - An equal volume on the other side is **submerged** less. - The **new Centre of Buoyancy** shifts to a new position $B'$. - The shift $BB'$ is horizontal (to first order in $d\theta$). **Step 3 — Compute the Shift BB'.** The shift of the Centre of Buoyancy equals the moment of the wedge volume about the axis of tilting, divided by the total submerged volume: $$BB' = \dfrac{\int (\text{elemental wedge moment})}{V_d}$$ Consider a small elemental strip at distance *x* from the axis of rotation. The change in submerged depth at this strip is $x \cdot d\theta$. So the change in elemental volume is: $$d(\delta V) = x \, d\theta \cdot dA$$ The moment of this elemental volume about the rotation axis is: $$dM = x \cdot d(\delta V) = x^{2} \, d\theta \cdot dA$$ Total moment of all wedge volumes: $$\int x^{2} \, d\theta \cdot dA = d\theta \int x^{2} \, dA = d\theta \cdot I$$ Therefore, the shift of the Centre of Buoyancy is: $$BB' = \dfrac{I \, d\theta}{V_d}$$ **Step 4 — Locate the Metacentre.** The new line of action of buoyancy is vertical through $B'$. Extend this line upward. It intersects the original vertical axis (the line *BG*) at the Metacentre *M*. From the geometry of similar triangles in the tilt configuration: $$\dfrac{BM \cdot d\theta}{1} = BB'$$ Wait — more precisely, since *B* moves to $B'$ by horizontal distance $BB'$, and $B'$ is directly below *M* on the original vertical axis, the geometry gives: $$BM = \dfrac{BB'}{d\theta} = \dfrac{I \, d\theta / V_d}{d\theta} = \dfrac{I}{V_d}$$ **Final Result (KTU Board Standard Form):** $$\boxed{\,BM = \dfrac{I}{V_d}\,}$$ And the metacentric height is: $$\boxed{\,GM = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG\,}$$ (when the Centre of Gravity *G* lies above the Centre of Buoyancy *B*). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Shapes & Their Second Moments > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently ask questions involving different shapes (circular, rectangular, triangular plates). Memorise this table — it saves marks in numericals. | Shape | Area *A* | Centroid $\bar{y}$ from base | $I_G$ about centroidal axis | |---|---|---|---| | Rectangle (b × d) | $b \cdot d$ | $d/2$ | $\dfrac{b \, d^{3}}{12}$ | | Circle (radius R) | $\pi R^{2}$ | $R$ (from diameter) | $\dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{4}$ | | Triangle (base b, height h) | $\dfrac{1}{2} b \, h$ | $h/3$ from base | $\dfrac{b \, h^{3}}{36}$ | | Trapezium | $\dfrac{(a+b)}{2} \cdot h$ | $\dfrac{h(2a+b)}{3(a+b)}$ from base | $\dfrac{h^{3}(a^{2}+4ab+b^{2})}{36(a+b)}$ | | Semi-circle (radius R) | $\dfrac{\pi R^{2}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{4R}{3\pi}$ from diameter | $0.1098 R^{4}$ ≈ $\dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{8} \cdot 0.28$ | ]<]minimax[>[ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Inclined Submerged Surface — Functional Flow The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical flow of forces, depths, and reference axes used in the Centre of Pressure derivation: ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Free Liquid Surface
Pressure equals zero"] node2["Inclined Submerged Plane
Area equals A
Angle theta with horizontal"] node3["Reference X axis
At free surface intersection line"] node4["Centroid G of Area A
Located at depth h-bar"] node5["Centre of Pressure P
Located at depth h-star"] node6["Elemental Strip
at distance y from X axis
Width equals b dy"] node7["Pressure p equals rho g y sin theta"] node8["Force dF equals p dA
equals rho g y sin theta dA"] node9["Total Force F equals rho g h-bar A"] node10["Moment M-X equals rho g sin theta I-X"] node11["Parallel Axis Theorem
I-X equals I-G plus A y-bar squared"] node12["CoP Depth h-star
equals h-bar plus I-G sin squared theta over A h-bar"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node2 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node3 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node8 --> node10 node10 --> node11 node9 --> node12 node10 --> node12 node11 --> node12 ``` ### 4.2 Metacentre & Body Stability — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Floating Body
Weight W acts through G"] nodeB["Centre of Buoyancy B
Centroid of displaced volume"] nodeC["Original Vertical Axis
Passing through G and B"] nodeD["Body Tilted by Small Angle d-theta"] nodeE["New Centre of Buoyancy B-prime
Shifted horizontally by BB-prime"] nodeF["New Buoyancy Line
Vertical through B-prime"] nodeG["Metacentre M
Intersection of new buoyancy line with original vertical axis"] nodeH["Compute BM equals I divided by V-disp"] nodeI["Compute GM equals BM minus BG"] nodeJ{"Is GM greater than zero
and M above G?"} nodeK["STABLE EQUILIBRIUM
Body returns to upright"] nodeL["UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM
Body capsizes"] nodeM["NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM
GM equals zero
Body stays tilted"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeL nodeJ --> nodeM ``` ### 4.3 CoP Visual Position Reference ``` | ← FREE SURFACE (P = 0) | | ←─── X-axis (reference line) | ____________ | / /| |/ / | ← Inclined Plane (angle θ) | ─── G ─── | G = Centroid (depth h̄) | | P = Centre of Pressure (depth h*) | | h* > h̄ (CoP always below Centroid) | P | | __________| | / | / |/ ``` ### 4.4 Metacentre Stability Schematic ``` M ← Metacentre (above G → STABLE) | | dθ (small tilt) | ↗ | G ← Centre of Gravity | ↕ BG | B ← Centre of Buoyancy (original) | | ← Waterline plane ~~~~~|~~~|~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ | | | | ← Submerged volume | | |___| Body Tilted View (after small rotation): M ←─── Same Metacentre on original axis | | ↗ New buoyancy line through B' | G | B ─ ─ ─ B' (B' shifted horizontally; lies below M on the new vertical) ``` ]<]minimax[>[ ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Centre of Pressure. Show that for a horizontal submerged surface, the Centre of Pressure coincides with the Centroid.** **Model Answer:** > **Centre of Pressure:** It is the point on a submerged surface through which the resultant hydrostatic pressure force is assumed to act. **Proof:** For a horizontal submerged surface, the angle of inclination $\theta = 0°$ with the free surface. From the CoP formula: $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}$$ Substituting $\theta = 0°$, $\sin^{2}(0°) = 0$: $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + 0 = \bar{h}$$ Since $h^{*} = \bar{h}$, the Centre of Pressure and Centroid lie at the same depth. **Hence proved.** ∎ > **Key Point to State:** The pressure on a horizontal surface is uniform, so the resultant must pass through the centroid. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is a Metacentre? When is a floating body said to be in stable equilibrium?** **Model Answer:** > **Metacentre (M):** It is the point at which the line of action of the new buoyant force (after a small angular displacement) intersects the original vertical axis of symmetry of a floating body. **Stable Equilibrium Condition:** A floating body is in **stable equilibrium** when the Metacentre *M* lies **above** the Centre of Gravity *G*, i.e., the **metacentric height GM > 0**. Mathematically: $$GM = BM - BG > 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{I}{V_d} > BG$$ If GM = 0 → Neutral equilibrium (body stays at the tilted position). If GM < 0 (M below G) → Unstable equilibrium (body capsizes). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ## **Question A (14 Marks)** ### **Q.A (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]** **Derive an expression for the depth of the Centre of Pressure on a submerged inclined plane surface.** **Model Solution:** The detailed derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** above. The expected board solution is summarised here step-by-step: **Setup:** - Plane area *A* inclined at angle *θ* to free surface. - X-axis along intersection line of inclined plane with free surface. - Y-axis along the inclined plane (perpendicular to X-axis). **Step 1: Elemental Force.** $$dF = \rho \, g \, y \, \sin\theta \, dA$$ **Step 2: Total Force.** $$F = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \int_A y \, dA = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \cdot \bar{y} \cdot A = \rho \, g \, \bar{h} \cdot A$$ **Step 3: Moment about X-axis.** $$M_X = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \int_A y^{2} \, dA = \rho \, g \, \sin\theta \cdot I_X$$ **Step 4: Parallel Axis Theorem.** $$I_X = I_G + A \bar{y}^{\,2}$$ **Step 5: Centre of Pressure location.** $$y^{*} = \dfrac{M_X}{F} = \dfrac{I_G + A \bar{y}^{\,2}}{A \, \bar{y}} = \bar{y} + \dfrac{I_G}{A \, \bar{y}}$$ **Step 6: Vertical depth.** $$\boxed{\,h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}\,}$$ **Conclusion:** The Centre of Pressure lies at a depth $h^{*}$ below the free surface, which is always greater than the centroidal depth $\bar{h}$ for any inclined or vertical surface. ∎ --- ### **Q.A (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **A circular plate of diameter 1.5 m is immersed in water with its plane inclined at 45° to the free water surface. The centre of the plate is at a depth of 3 m below the free surface. Find:** **(i) the total pressure force on the plate,** **(ii) the position of the Centre of Pressure.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Diameter $D = 1.5$ m → Radius $R = 0.75$ m - Angle $\theta = 45°$ - Centroid depth $\bar{h} = 3$ m - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Geometric Properties of Circular Plate.** $$A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times (0.75)^{2} = 1.7671 \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$I_G = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.75)^{4}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.31641}{4} = 0.24850 \text{ m}^{4}$$ **Step 2: Total Pressure Force.** $$F = \rho \, g \, \bar{h} \, A$$ $$F = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3 \times 1.7671$$ $$\boxed{F = 51{,}994 \text{ N} \approx 51.99 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 3: Position of Centre of Pressure (depth).** $$h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}$$ $$\sin^{2}(45°) = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} = 0.5$$ $$h^{*} = 3 + \dfrac{0.24850 \times 0.5}{1.7671 \times 3}$$ $$h^{*} = 3 + \dfrac{0.12425}{5.3014}$$ $$h^{*} = 3 + 0.02344 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{h^{*} = 3.0234 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 4: Shift of CoP below Centroid.** $$h^{*} - \bar{h} = 0.0234 \text{ m} = 23.44 \text{ mm}$$ **Conclusion:** The total hydrostatic force is **51.99 kN**, acting at a depth of **3.0234 m**, which is **23.44 mm below the centroid** of the plate. ∎ --- ## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** ### **Q.B (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Buoyancy and Centre of Buoyancy. Derive the expression for metacentric height of a floating body.** **Model Solution:** **Definitions:** > **Buoyancy:** It is the upward force exerted by a fluid on a body immersed (fully or partially) in it. By **Archimedes' Principle**, this force equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the body: > $$F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$ > **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** It is the point through which the buoyant force is assumed to act. By definition, it is the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume**. **Derivation of Metacentric Height (Section 3.2 Recap):** **Step 1:** When the floating body is tilted by a small angle *dθ* about an axis through the centroid of the waterline area, the centre of buoyancy shifts from *B* to *B′*. **Step 2:** Considering a strip at distance *x* from the rotation axis, the change in submerged depth is $x \, d\theta$. The elemental volume change is: $$dV = x \, d\theta \cdot dA$$ **Step 3:** Moment of the changed wedge volume about the axis: $$dM = x \cdot dV = x^{2} \, d\theta \cdot dA$$ **Step 4:** Total moment of all such wedge elements: $$M = d\theta \int x^{2} \, dA = d\theta \cdot I$$ **Step 5:** Shift of Centre of Buoyancy: $$BB' = \dfrac{M}{V_d} = \dfrac{I \, d\theta}{V_d}$$ **Step 6:** From the geometry of the tilt: $$BM = \dfrac{BB'}{d\theta} = \dfrac{I}{V_d}$$ **Step 7:** Metacentric Height: $$GM = BM - BG = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG$$ $$\boxed{\,GM = \dfrac{I}{V_d} - BG\,}$$ provided G lies above B. ∎ --- ### **Q.B (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **A floating body has a weight of 250 kN and displaces 25 m³ of water when in equilibrium. The Centre of Gravity is 1.5 m above the Centre of Buoyancy. The second moment of area of the waterline plane about the longitudinal axis is 30 m⁴. Determine the metacentric height and state whether the body is in stable equilibrium.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Weight of body, $W = 250$ kN $= 250{,}000$ N - Displaced volume, $V_d = 25$ m³ - $BG = 1.5$ m (G above B) - Second moment of area of waterline, $I = 30$ m⁴ **Step 1: Calculate BM.** $$BM = \dfrac{I}{V_d} = \dfrac{30}{25} = 1.2 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Calculate GM.** $$GM = BM - BG = 1.2 - 1.5$$ $$\boxed{GM = -0.3 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 3: Stability Assessment.** Since $GM$ is **negative**, the Metacentre *M* lies **below** the Centre of Gravity *G*. The body is in **UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM** and will **capsize** if tilted. **Conclusion:** $GM = -0.3$ m → Body is **UNSTABLE**. ∎ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Direct from KTU Valuation Key Patterns):** > > 1. **Wrong angle usage in CoP formula:** Students often write $\sin\theta$ instead of $\sin^{2}\theta$ in the final depth expression. The correct formula is $h^{*} = \bar{h} + (I_G \sin^{2}\theta)/(A \bar{h})$. **−1 mark** penalty is common. > > 2. **Forgetting the second moment for the SHAPE:** In numericals, students write the *area* formula correctly but forget to look up $I_G$ for the same shape. A circular plate has $I_G = \pi R^{4}/4$, NOT $\pi R^{2}/4$. **−2 marks** typical loss. > > 3. **Sign convention in GM:** When G is *below* B, $BG$ is treated as negative. Many students blindly use $GM = BM - BG$ and lose **1–2 marks** for missing the case analysis. > > 4. **Units inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert kN to N, or using $g = 9.8$ instead of 9.81. KTU strictly expects **SI units** throughout. > > 5. **Confusing "depth" with "perpendicular distance":** In inclined problems, always state the final answer as **vertical depth** below the free surface. Writing $y^{*}$ instead of $h^{*}$ loses **1 mark**. > > 6. **Stability verdict:** Always explicitly state **"Stable"**, **"Unstable"**, or **"Neutral"** at the end of a metacentric height problem. A correct $GM$ value without a verdict loses **1 mark**. > > 7. **Drawing a free-body diagram:** KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for a labelled FBD in metacentric height problems.
