Always draw the body, mark *G*, *B*, *M*, and the rotation axis. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **CoP Formula (most-tested):** $h^{*} = \bar{h} + \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}$ - [ ] **CoP Shift:** $h^{*} - \bar{h} = \dfrac{I_G \sin^{2}\theta}{A \, \bar{h}}$ - [ ] **Horizontal surface check:** $\theta = 0°$ → CoP = Centroid (verify by substitution). - [ ] **Total Pressure Force:** $F = \rho g \bar{h} A$ - [ ] **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ - [ ] **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** Always the centroid of the *displaced* fluid volume. - [ ] **Metacentric Radius:** $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_d}$ - [ ] **Metacentric Height:** $GM = BM - BG$ (when G above B) - [ ] **Stability Rule:** $GM > 0$ → Stable; $GM = 0$ → Neutral; $GM < 0$ → Unstable. - [ ] **Standard $I_G$ values** for rectangle, circle, triangle (must memorise). - [ ] **Always use SI units:** Force in N, length in m, pressure in Pa. - [ ] **Always draw a labelled diagram** in Part B questions. - [ ] **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless the problem specifies otherwise. - [ ] **Final answer must include a stability verdict** in metacentric height problems. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** > **"PIG-DIG"** → **P**ressure = $\rho g \bar{h} A$, **I**nertia $I_G$, **G**overned by shape; **D**istance $h^* - \bar{h}$; **I**nclination factor $\sin^{2}\theta$; **G**ravity (BG) versus **M**etacentre (BM) — Stability. ]<]minimax[>[

topics.title: Stability of Immersed and Floating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stability of Immersed and Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of a Floating/Immersed Body:** A body is said to be in **stable equilibrium** if, when slightly displaced (tilted or translated), it returns to its original position due to the restoring moments/couples developed by the fluid forces. If it moves further away, it is **unstable**; if it remains in the new position, it is **neutral**.

In fluid statics, every body floating or fully submerged in a liquid possesses three fundamental points:

| Point | Symbol | Definition |
|-------|--------|------------|
| **Centre of Gravity** | **G** | Point where the entire weight **W** of the body acts vertically downwards |
| **Centre of Buoyancy** | **B** | Point where the resultant hydrostatic pressure force (Buoyant force **F_B**) acts vertically upwards |
| **Metacentre** | **M** | Point where the line of action of the new buoyant force (after small angular displacement) intersects the original vertical axis through G and B |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ball and the Bowl"

Imagine three scenarios with a ball:
- **Ball inside a bowl** → The ball is pushed slightly and rolls back. This is **Stable Equilibrium** (Restoring force/moment exists).
- **Ball balanced on top of an inverted dome** → The ball is pushed slightly and rolls away. This is **Unstable Equilibrium** (Destabilising moment exists).
- **Ball on a perfectly flat horizontal table** → The ball is pushed slightly and stays at the new location. This is **Neutral Equilibrium** (Zero net moment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a ship sailing in rough seas, designers ensure the vessel is in **stable equilibrium** so that even after being tilted by waves, it rights itself automatically. This is quantified using the **Metacentric Height (GM)**.

## 1.3 The Three Types of Equilibrium

For a body floating on or immersed in a fluid, the equilibrium is classified based on the relative positions of the **Metacentre (M)**, **Centre of Gravity (G)**, and **Centre of Buoyancy (B)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition 1: } & M \text{ lies ABOVE } G \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{STABLE equilibrium} \\
\text{Condition 2: } & M \text{ lies BELOW } G \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{UNSTABLE equilibrium} \\
\text{Condition 3: } & M \text{ COINCIDES with } G \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{NEUTRAL equilibrium}
\end{aligned}
$$

The vertical distance **GM** is universally known as the **Metacentric Height** and serves as the single quantitative indicator of a floating body's rotational stability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stability of Fully Submerged (Immersed) Bodies

A body that is **completely submerged** in a liquid has its centre of buoyancy **B** located at the **centroid of the displaced volume**. When the body is tilted slightly, since the displaced volume geometry remains essentially the same, the centre of buoyancy **does NOT shift**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Rule for Immersed Bodies:** A fully submerged body is in **STABLE** equilibrium if its **Centre of Gravity (G) lies BELOW the Centre of Buoyancy (B)**, i.e., the body must be **heavier at the bottom**.

**Logical Reasoning (Why?):**
- Weight **W** acts downwards through **G**.
- Buoyant force **F_B** acts upwards through **B**.
- If the body tilts, since **B** does not move, a **couple** is formed between **W** and **F_B**.
- If **G is below B**, this couple tends to **restore** the body to its original vertical orientation.
- If **G is above B**, the couple **overturns** the body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exception — Suspended Bodies:** A body suspended freely in a fluid (neither floating nor resting) is in stable equilibrium when its **Centre of Gravity is vertically below the Centre of Buoyancy**. This applies to balloons and underwater pendulums.

## 2.2 Stability of Floating Bodies — The Metacentre Concept

Unlike submerged bodies, when a **floating body** is tilted, the **shape of the submerged portion changes**. Therefore, the centre of buoyancy **shifts** from **B** to a new position **B′**.

**Step-by-step logical flow of tilting a floating body:**

1. Initially, the body floats vertically with **W** at **G** and **F_B** at **B**, both acting on the same vertical line (equilibrium).
2. The body is tilted by a **small angle θ** about a horizontal axis through some point on the body's axis.
3. Due to the change in the geometry of the submerged volume, the new centre of buoyancy shifts to **B′**.
4. The weight **W** still acts through the *same* point **G** (mass distribution unchanged).
5. The new buoyant force **F_B′** acts vertically upwards through **B′**.
6. The vertical line through **B′** intersects the original axis of symmetry (line GB) at a point called the **Metacentre (M)**.
7. The distance from **G** to **M** along this vertical axis is the **Metacentric Height (GM)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notation |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Buoyant Force | $F_B \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | $N$ (where $\nabla$ = displaced volume) |
| 2 | **Metacentric Radius** | $BM \;=\; \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}}$ | **m** |
| 3 | **Metacentric Height** | $GM \;=\; BM \;\pm\; BG$ | **m** |
| 4 | Moment of Inertia (Rectangular Waterplane $L \times B$) | $I \;=\; \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12}$ | $m^{4}$ |
| 5 | Moment of Inertia (Circular Waterplane, diameter $D$) | $I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{4}}{64}$ | $m^{4}$ |
| 6 | **Time Period of Oscillation** (Rolling) | $T \;=\; 2\pi \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k^{2}}{g \cdot GM}}$ | **s** |
| 7 | **Radius of Gyration** | $k \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{I_G}{M}}$ | **m** |
| 8 | **Restoring Couple** | $C_R \;=\; W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | $N \cdot m$ |
| 9 | Submerged Body Stability | $G$ must be **below** $B$ | — |
| 10 | Floating Body Stability | $M$ must be **above** $G$ (i.e., $GM > 0$) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention for GM:**
> - $GM \;=\; BM \;-\; BG$  → when **G is BELOW B** (common in floating ships)
> - $GM \;=\; BM \;+\; BG$  → when **G is ABOVE B** (less common, use this carefully)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** Every commercial ship, submarine, and aircraft carrier is designed with a target $GM$ value (typically **0.15 m to 1.5 m**) to ensure safe rolling behaviour. Too low → capsizes easily; too high → uncomfortable jerky rolling.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Semi-submersible rigs and FPSOs use metacentric height calculations for seakeeping analysis.
- **Hydraulic Float Valves & Pontoons:** Stability calculations ensure uniform water-level sensing.
- **Aerostat Balloons:** Suspended-body stability governs their hovering altitude control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Metacentric Radius (BM = I / V_disp)

**Setup:** Consider a floating body with original waterplane area $A$ at the free surface. When tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about the longitudinal axis through the centroid **O** of the waterplane, the wedge-like volumes on the two sides of the original vertical axis change.

**Step 1 — Identify the wedge volume:**
On one side, a wedge of fluid is **submerged** (added buoyancy), and on the opposite side, a wedge of equal cross-section is **emerged** (lost buoyancy). Let $\delta V$ be the volume of one wedge.

For an elementary strip of length $dy$ at distance $y$ from the axis of rotation:

$$
dV \;=\; y \cdot \tan\theta \cdot dy \approx y \cdot \theta \cdot dy \quad (\text{for small } \theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Shift in Centre of Buoyancy (Horizontal distance BB'):**
The shift occurs because volume $\delta V$ is effectively transferred from one side to the other. Taking moments about the original vertical axis:

$$
B B^{\prime} \cdot V_{disp} \;=\; \int y \cdot dV \;=\; \int y \cdot (y \cdot \theta) \cdot dy \;=\; \theta \int y^{2} \cdot dy
$$

But $\int y^{2} \cdot dA \;=\; I$ is the **second moment of area (moment of inertia)** of the waterplane about the axis of rotation.

**Step 3 — Solve for horizontal shift BB':**

$$
B B^{\prime} \cdot V_{disp} \;=\; \theta \cdot I \quad \Longrightarrow \quad B B^{\prime} \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot \theta}{V_{disp}}
$$

**Step 4 — From geometry, find BM:**
In the tilted position, considering triangle $B M B'$ where $\angle B M B'$ equals $\theta$ (alternate angles from parallel vertical lines):

$$
\tan\theta \;\approx\; \theta \;=\; \dfrac{B B^{\prime}}{B M}
$$

Therefore:

$$
BM \;=\; \dfrac{B B^{\prime}}{\theta} \;=\; \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}}
$$

This is the **famous Metacentric Radius formula**, valid for small angles of tilt.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time Period of Rolling

When a floating body is given a small angular tilt $\theta$, the **restoring couple** generated is:

$$
C_R \;=\; W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \;\approx\; W \cdot GM \cdot \theta \quad (\text{for small } \theta)
$$

This couple acts as the restoring torque analogous to a torsional spring. Equating this to the **mass moment of inertia** equation for angular motion:

$$
I_G \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} \;=\; -W \cdot GM \cdot \theta
$$

Substituting $W = M \cdot g$ and $I_G = M \cdot k^{2}$:

$$
M \cdot k^{2} \cdot \ddot{\theta} \;=\; -M \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta
$$

Rearranging into the standard SHM form $\ddot{\theta} + \omega^{2} \theta = 0$:

$$
\ddot{\theta} + \left(\dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^{2}}\right) \theta \;=\; 0
$$

The angular frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^{2}}}$, and therefore the **time period of rolling** is:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^{2}}{g \cdot GM}}
$$

This explains why **larger GM → faster (shorter) rolling period** (stiffer ship) and vice versa.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** A rectangular pontoon of dimensions **6 m long × 3 m wide × 1.5 m deep** floats in water with a draft of **1.0 m**. The centre of gravity of the pontoon is **0.6 m above the base**. Find:
> (a) The metacentric height
> (b) Whether the pontoon is stable
> (c) The time period of rolling, given radius of gyration $k = 1.2$ m.

**Given Data:**
- $L = 6$ m, $B = 3$ m, Depth $= 1.5$ m
- Draft (submerged depth) $d = 1.0$ m
- Height of G above base, $\overline{OG} = 0.6$ m
- Centre of buoyancy B is at the centroid of submerged volume: $\overline{OB} = d/2 = 0.5$ m
- $k = 1.2$ m

**Step 1 — Calculate BG (with G above B):**
Since G (0.6 m) is above B (0.5 m):

$$
BG \;=\; \overline{OG} - \overline{OB} \;=\; 0.6 - 0.5 \;=\; 0.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Moment of Inertia of Waterplane:**

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{6 \times 3^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{6 \times 27}{12} \;=\; 13.5 \text{ m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Displaced Volume:**

$$
V_{disp} \;=\; L \cdot B \cdot d \;=\; 6 \times 3 \times 1.0 \;=\; 18 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Metacentric Radius:**

$$
BM \;=\; \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}} \;=\; \dfrac{13.5}{18} \;=\; 0.75 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Metacentric Height GM:**
Since G is above B:

$$
GM \;=\; BM - BG \;=\; 0.75 - 0.1 \;=\; 0.65 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Stability Check:**
Since $GM = 0.65 \text{ m} > 0$, the metacentre M lies above G → **The pontoon is STABLE.** ✓

**Step 7 — Time Period of Rolling:**

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^{2}}{g \cdot GM}} \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{(1.2)^{2}}{9.81 \times 0.65}}
$$

$$
T \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1.44}{6.3765}} \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{0.2259} \;=\; 2\pi \times 0.4753 \;\approx\; 2.987 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $GM = 0.65$ m (Stable), and $T \approx 2.99$ seconds per oscillation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Metacentre Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Floating Body in Equilibrium] --> stepB[Body at Rest - W and FB act along same vertical line]
    stepB --> stepC[Apply Small Angular Tilt Theta]
    stepC --> stepD[Submerged Wedge Volume Changes on Both Sides]
    stepD --> stepE[Centre of Buoyancy Shifts from B to B prime]
    stepE --> stepF[Vertical Line through B prime intersects Original Axis at Point M]
    stepF --> stepG[Define BM as Metacentric Radius]
    stepG --> stepH[Define GM as Metacentric Height]
    stepH --> stepI{Metacentre M above G?}
    stepI -->|Yes GM greater than zero| stepJ[STABLE EQUILIBRIUM - Body Returns to Original Position]
    stepI -->|No GM less than zero| stepK[UNSTABLE EQUILIBRIUM - Body Capsizes]
    stepI -->|Coincides M equals G| stepL[NEUTRAL EQUILIBRIUM - Body Stays in New Position]
```

## 4.2 Stability Analysis Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input Body Geometry L B Depth] --> nodeB[Compute Displaced Volume Vdisp]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Centroid OB]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Waterplane Moment of Inertia I]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute BM equals I divided by Vdisp]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Locate Centre of Gravity G]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute BG distance]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute GM equals BM minus BG]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Stability Check}
    nodeI -->|GM Positive| nodeJ[Output STABLE]
    nodeI -->|GM Negative| nodeK[Output UNSTABLE]
    nodeI -->|GM Zero| nodeL[Output NEUTRAL]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Stability Diagram (Text-Based Schematic)

```
        BEFORE TILT                  AFTER SMALL TILT (θ)
                                    
            | W↓                         | W↓ (still at G)
            |                            |
            G                            G  ← Weight acts here
            |                            |\
            |                            | \
            | FB↑                        |  \  FB↑ (new line through B')
            |                            |   \
            B                            B---B'  (B' is new centre of buoyancy)
            |                            
            |                            ↕ angle θ
           ---  (Waterline)             ---  (Tilted waterline)
                                    
                                     M = intersection of new FB line 
                                         with original vertical axis
                                         M is ABOVE G → STABLE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In the tilted configuration, **W** and **F_B** are no longer collinear, forming a **restoring couple** that tries to rotate the body back. The distance **GM** measures the "lever arm" of this restoring couple.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the terms "Metacentre" and "Metacentric Height" with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Metacentre (M)** of a floating body is defined as the point at which the line of action of the new buoyant force (after a small angular displacement) intersects the original vertical axis passing through the centre of gravity and the original centre of buoyancy.

The **Metacentric Height (GM)** is the vertical distance between the centre of gravity (G) of the floating body and the metacentre (M). It is the measure of the **static stability** of the floating body. A positive value of GM (i.e., M above G) indicates stable equilibrium.

Mathematically:

$$
GM \;=\; BM - BG
$$

where $BM = I / V_{disp}$ is the metacentric radius and $BG$ is the distance between the centre of gravity and the original centre of buoyancy.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between the stability of a fully submerged body and a floating body.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fully Submerged Body | Floating Body |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|
| Centre of Buoyancy (B) position | Centroid of entire body volume | Centroid of submerged volume only |
| Effect of tilting on B | B does **NOT** shift | B **shifts** to a new position B′ |
| Stability condition | **G must lie below B** | **M must lie above G** (i.e., $GM > 0$) |
| Stability measure | Distance BG | Metacentric height GM |
| Metacentre concept | **Not applicable** | **Applicable and essential** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** A rectangular barge of dimensions **8 m long × 4 m wide** floats in seawater (specific gravity = 1.025) with a draft of **1.8 m**. The centre of gravity of the barge is located **1.2 m above the base of the barge**. Determine:
- (i) The metacentric radius (BM)
- (ii) The metacentric height (GM)
- (iii) Comment on the stability of the barge. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **time period of oscillation** (rolling) of a floating body in terms of metacentric height and radius of gyration. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution for Q.A(a):

**Given:** $L = 8$ m, $B = 4$ m, Draft $d = 1.8$ m, $\overline{OG} = 1.2$ m, $\rho_{sw}/\rho_w = 1.025$

**Step 1 — Displaced Volume:**

$$
V_{disp} \;=\; L \cdot B \cdot d \;=\; 8 \times 4 \times 1.8 \;=\; 57.6 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia of Waterplane:**

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{8 \times 4^{3}}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{8 \times 64}{12} \;=\; 42.667 \text{ m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Metacentric Radius:**

$$
BM \;=\; \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}} \;=\; \dfrac{42.667}{57.6} \;=\; 0.7407 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Distance BG:**
Centroid of submerged volume (B) is at half the draft: $\overline{OB} = d/2 = 0.9$ m

$$
BG \;=\; \overline{OG} - \overline{OB} \;=\; 1.2 - 0.9 \;=\; 0.3 \text{ m} \quad (G \text{ is above } B)
$$

**Step 5 — Metacentric Height:**

$$
GM \;=\; BM - BG \;=\; 0.7407 - 0.3 \;=\; 0.4407 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Stability Comment:**
Since $GM = +0.4407$ m $> 0$, the metacentre lies **above** the centre of gravity → **The barge is STABLE.** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the specific gravity of seawater (1.025) does not affect the metacentric height calculation for a fully floating body, since $V_{disp}$ depends on the geometry and draft only. It becomes relevant only when calculating weight/displacement or buoyancy in Newtons.

---

#### Solution for Q.A(b):

When a floating body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$ from its equilibrium position, the **restoring couple** acting on it is given by:

$$
C_R \;=\; W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta \;\approx\; W \cdot GM \cdot \theta \quad (\text{for small angles})
$$

For the **dynamic** rolling motion, applying Newton's second law for rotation about the centre of gravity G:

$$
I_G \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} \;=\; -C_R \;=\; -W \cdot GM \cdot \theta
$$

Substituting $W = M \cdot g$ (weight = mass × gravity) and $I_G = M \cdot k^{2}$ (mass moment of inertia in terms of radius of gyration):

$$
M \cdot k^{2} \cdot \ddot{\theta} \;=\; -M \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta
$$

Dividing both sides by $M \cdot k^{2}$:

$$
\ddot{\theta} + \left(\dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^{2}}\right) \theta \;=\; 0
$$

This is the standard **Simple Harmonic Motion** equation of the form $\ddot{\theta} + \omega_n^{2} \theta = 0$, where:

$$
\omega_n \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{g \cdot GM}{k^{2}}}
$$

Therefore, the **time period of rolling oscillation** is:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} \;=\; 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^{2}}{g \cdot GM}}
$$

This derivation shows that:
- A **larger metacentric height** produces a **shorter time period** (stiffer, faster rolling).
- A **larger radius of gyration** produces a **longer time period** (sluggish rolling).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Define and explain with a sketch the **three types of equilibrium** for a floating body. State the conditions for stability in each case. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A cylindrical buoy of diameter **2 m** and height **2 m** weighs **20 kN**. Calculate:
- (i) The depth to which it will sink in seawater (specific gravity = 1.025)
- (ii) Whether the buoy is stable in this position, given that its centre of gravity is at its geometric centre. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution for Q.B(a):

The three types of equilibrium for a floating body depend on the relative positions of the **Metacentre (M)**, **Centre of Gravity (G)**, and **Centre of Buoyancy (B)**.

**1. Stable Equilibrium:**
When the body is tilted by a small angle, the metacentre M lies **above** the centre of gravity G. The buoyant force F_B′ acting through B′ and the weight W acting through G form a **restoring couple** that brings the body back to its original vertical position.

**Condition:** $GM > 0$ (Metacentre above G)

**2. Unstable Equilibrium:**
When tilted, the metacentre M lies **below** G. The couple formed between W and F_B′ acts as an **overturning couple**, causing the body to tilt further and capsize.

**Condition:** $GM < 0$ (Metacentre below G)

**3. Neutral Equilibrium:**
When M coincides **exactly** with G, the couple between W and F_B′ has **zero moment arm**. The body remains stable at any tilted position without any restoring or overturning tendency.

**Condition:** $GM = 0$ (M coincides with G)

```
      STABLE              UNSTABLE              NEUTRAL
                                         
        | W↓                 | W↓                  | W↓
        |                    |                     |
        G                    G                     G = M
        |                    |                     |
        |  FB↑               |  FB↑                |  FB↑
        |                    |                     |
        M ← above G          G ← above M           B
```

---

#### Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:** Diameter $D = 2$ m, Height $H = 2$ m, Weight $W = 20$ kN $= 20000$ N, Specific gravity of seawater $S = 1.025$, G at geometric centre.

**Step 1 — Calculate Buoyant Force at Full Submersion:**

$$
F_B \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{cyl} \;=\; 1025 \times 9.81 \times \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 2^{2} \times 2\right)
$$

$$
F_B \;=\; 1025 \times 9.81 \times (\pi \times 2) \;=\; 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.2832 \;\approx\; 63170 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 63.17 \text{ kN}
$$

Since $W = 20$ kN $< F_B = 63.17$ kN, the buoy **floats** (does not fully submerge). ✓

**Step 2 — Equating W = F_B for equilibrium:**
Let submerged depth be $h$:

$$
W \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h
$$

$$
20000 \;=\; 1025 \times 9.81 \times \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 2^{2}\right) \times h
$$

$$
20000 \;=\; 1025 \times 9.81 \times \pi \times h \;=\; 31591.5 \cdot h
$$

$$
h \;=\; \dfrac{20000}{31591.5} \;\approx\; 0.633 \text{ m}
$$

**The buoy sinks to a depth of 0.633 m in seawater.**

**Step 3 — Locate Centre of Buoyancy B (centroid of submerged cylindrical segment):**

$$
\overline{OB} \;=\; \dfrac{h}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.633}{2} \;=\; 0.3165 \text{ m (above base)}
$$

**Step 4 — Locate Centre of Gravity G (geometric centre of full cylinder):**

$$
\overline{OG} \;=\; \dfrac{H}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2}{2} \;=\; 1.0 \text{ m (above base)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute BG:**

$$
BG \;=\; \overline{OG} - \overline{OB} \;=\; 1.0 - 0.3165 \;=\; 0.6835 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute BM (using circular waterplane area):**

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 2^{4}}{64} \;=\; \dfrac{16\pi}{64} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \;\approx\; 0.7854 \text{ m}^{4}
$$

$$
V_{disp} \;=\; A \cdot h \;=\; \pi \times 0.633 \;\approx\; 1.988 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
BM \;=\; \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.7854}{1.988} \;\approx\; 0.3951 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute GM (G is above B):**

$$
GM \;=\; BM - BG \;=\; 0.3951 - 0.6835 \;=\; -0.2884 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 8 — Stability Conclusion:**
Since $GM = -0.2884$ m $< 0$, the metacentre M lies **below** G → **The buoy is UNSTABLE** and will capsize. To make it stable, weight must be added at the bottom or the centre of gravity must be lowered. ✗

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in BG Calculation:** Students frequently compute BG as $|\overline{OB} - \overline{OG}|$ without noting whether G is above or below B. Always **state** the position of G relative to B explicitly before plugging into the GM formula.
>
> 2. **Using $BM = I/V$ for Submerged Bodies:** The formula $BM = I/V_{disp}$ is valid **ONLY for floating bodies** with a free water surface. It is **not** applicable to fully submerged bodies.
>
> 3. **Confusing $I$ with $I_G$:** In the time-period formula, the $k$ used is the **radius of gyration about G**, not about any other axis. If a value of $k$ is given about a different axis, use the **parallel axis theorem** first.
>
> 4. **Draft vs. Depth Confusion:** Draft is the *submerged* depth, not the total depth of the body. Using the full depth of the body instead of the draft will give a wildly wrong answer.
>
> 5. **Units in $I$:** Moment of inertia of the waterplane area has units of **m⁴**, and $V_{disp}$ has units of **m³**, so $BM$ correctly comes out in **meters**. Students often forget to convert cm to m, leading to a 100× or 10000× error.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Specify Stability:** The final answer MUST include a clear statement like *"Since GM > 0, the body is STABLE"* to earn the concluding 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three Types of Equilibrium:** Stable (M above G), Unstable (M below G), Neutral (M = G).
- ✅ **Submerged Body Stability Rule:** Centre of Gravity **G must be below** Centre of Buoyancy **B** for stability.
- ✅ **Floating Body Stability Rule:** Metacentric Height **GM must be positive** ($GM > 0$).
- ✅ **Metacentric Radius Formula:** $BM = I / V_{disp}$ (only for floating bodies, valid for **small** tilts).
- ✅ **Metacentric Height:** $GM = BM \pm BG$ (minus if G is above B; plus if G is below B).
- ✅ **Waterplane Moment of Inertia:**
  - Rectangle ($L \times B$): $I = LB^{3}/12$
  - Circle (diameter $D$): $I = \pi D^{4}/64$
- ✅ **Time Period of Rolling:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{k^{2}/(g \cdot GM)}$.
- ✅ **Restoring Couple:** $C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$.
- ✅ **Draft** = submerged depth of a floating body (used in $V_{disp}$ calculation, NOT the full body depth).
- ✅ **Centre of Buoyancy B** = centroid of the *displaced volume* (submerged portion).
- ✅ **Small angle assumption** ($\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$) is critical for the validity of the BM formula.
- ✅ **Higher GM** → stiffer, faster-rolling ship; **Lower GM** → softer, slower-rolling ship (but dangerously close to capsizing if too low).
- ✅ **Specific gravity of fluid** affects the **weight and buoyancy forces** but **does NOT** directly change the metacentric height formula (only the equilibrium draft).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Description of Fluid Motion - Types of Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Description of Fluid Motion - Types of Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Description of Fluid Motion — Fundamental Philosophy

Fluid motion can be described in fundamentally **two ways**, as per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Fluid Mechanics:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lagrangian Description** — Tracks the **history (path, velocity, acceleration)** of *individual fluid particles* as they move through space and time, similar to tagging a specific person walking through a crowded market.
>
> **Eulerian Description** — Observes the fluid motion at **fixed points in space** through which the fluid flows, similar to a CCTV camera watching a highway.

### Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

> **Lagrangian Method:** A method of describing fluid flow where the observer follows an individual fluid particle along its trajectory, tracking all its kinematic properties (position, velocity, acceleration) as a function of time.

> **Eulerian Method:** A method of describing fluid flow where the observer remains stationary at a fixed point in space and records the kinematic properties of the fluid as it passes through that point.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Camera" Method

Imagine you want to study traffic on **NH-66, Kerala**:

- **Lagrangian approach** → You stick a GPS tracker on **one specific Innova car** and follow its journey from Kasaragod to Thiruvananthapuram. You record its speed at every instant.
- **Eulerian approach** → You install **CCTV cameras at fixed locations** (e.g., every 50 km) and record the speed of *whatever car* passes that point.

For **engineering problems**, the Eulerian method is overwhelmingly preferred because:
1. The math is much simpler (we deal with **fields** rather than individual particles).
2. Real-world instruments (Pitot tubes, anemometers, pressure probes) all measure at **fixed points**.

---

## 1.2 The Universal Fluid Flow Field

The complete state of a moving fluid is described by a **flow field** — a mathematical function of position and time:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$$

$$p = p(x, y, z, t)$$

$$\rho = \rho(x, y, z, t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **velocity field** $\vec{V}$ is the most important descriptor of fluid motion. It is a **vector field** with three components: $u$ (x-direction), $v$ (y-direction), and $w$ (z-direction).
>
> $$\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$$

---

## 1.3 Three Kinematic Lines of Fluid Motion (Board-Favorite)

| Line Type | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Pathline** | The actual trajectory traced by a **single fluid particle** over time | "Where has this water molecule been?" |
| **Streakline** | The locus of all particles that have **previously passed through a given point** | "The colored dye line" in a flow visualization experiment |
| **Streamline** | An **imaginary curve** whose tangent at any point is in the direction of the velocity vector at that instant | "Instantaneous flow map" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Result:** For a **steady flow**, the three lines (pathline, streakline, streamline) are **identical**. For **unsteady flow**, they are all **different**.

---

## 1.4 Acceleration Field (The Critical Bridge)

In the **Eulerian description**, the acceleration of a fluid particle is found by applying the chain rule because the particle's position changes with time:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \left(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\right)\vec{V}$$

Where:
- $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ = **Local acceleration** (unsteadiness)
- $\left(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\right)\vec{V}$ = **Convective acceleration** (spatial variation)

> [!NOTE]
> For a **steady flow**, the local acceleration $\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$, but the convective term can still be non-zero (e.g., flow through a nozzle).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Fluid Flows

The KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to classify fluid flows into **eight primary categories** based on different physical criteria:

### 2.1.1 Based on Time Dependence (Steady vs. Unsteady)

**Steady Flow:** All flow parameters (velocity, pressure, density) at any point in the flow field are **independent of time**.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Steady flow **does not** mean the velocity is the same at all points — it means the velocity at a *given point* does not change with time. Example: Flow through a long straight pipe at constant flow rate.

**Unsteady Flow:** Flow parameters **vary with time** at any point. Example: Pulsating flow from a human heart (cardiac cycle), or opening a tap gradually.

---

### 2.1.2 Based on Spatial Variation (Uniform vs. Non-Uniform)

**Uniform Flow:** The velocity (in magnitude and direction) is **constant at every instant** along the direction of flow.

$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0$$

> Example: Flow through a straight pipe of constant diameter at constant rate.

**Non-Uniform Flow:** Velocity changes with position along the flow direction.
> Example: Flow through a **converging nozzle** (velocity increases as area decreases).

---

### 2.1.3 Based on Dimensionality (1D, 2D, 3D)

- **1-D Flow:** Flow properties are functions of one spatial coordinate only. Example: Flow through a long pipe.
- **2-D Flow:** Flow properties are functions of two spatial coordinates. Example: Flow over a long cylinder (infinite span).
- **3-D Flow:** General flow — all three coordinates matter. Example: Flow around a finite body.

---

### 2.1.4 Based on Rotation (Rotational vs. Irrotational)

**Irrotational Flow:** Fluid elements **do not rotate** about their own axes as they move. The angular velocity of each fluid element is zero.

$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0 \quad \text{(Vorticity} = 0)$$

> Example: Ideal flow around a corner, potential flow.

**Rotational Flow:** Fluid elements **rotate** as they move. Vorticity is non-zero.
> Example: Flow inside a rotating tank, flow with viscous effects near a solid boundary.

---

### 2.1.5 Based on Compressibility (Compressible vs. Incompressible)

- **Incompressible:** Density is constant, $\rho = $ const. Liquid water and air at low speeds (M < 0.3).
- **Compressible:** Density varies significantly. High-speed gas flows (M > 0.3).

---

### 2.1.6 Based on Viscosity (Viscous vs. Inviscid)

- **Viscous Flow:** Internal friction is significant. Example: Flow in the **boundary layer**.
- **Inviscid Flow:** Friction is negligible. Example: Flow outside the boundary layer (ideal flow).

---

### 2.1.7 Based on Flow Pattern (Laminar vs. Turbulent)

- **Laminar Flow:** Smooth, layered flow with parallel streamlines. Reynolds number Re < 2000 (pipe flow).
- **Turbulent Flow:** Chaotic, mixing flow with eddies. Re > 4000.

---

### 2.1.8 Based on Boundaries (Internal vs. External)

- **Internal Flow:** Fluid is completely bounded by solid surfaces. Example: Pipe flow, duct flow.
- **External Flow:** Fluid flows over an external surface with an unbounded region. Example: Flow over an aircraft wing, river flow around a bridge pier.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Concept | Governing Equation / Condition | SI Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lagrangian position vector | $\vec{r} = \vec{r}(\vec{r_0}, t)$ | m | Tracking individual particles |
| 2 | Lagrangian velocity | $\vec{V}_L = \dfrac{\partial \vec{r}}{\partial t}\bigg\vert_{\vec{r_0}}$ | m/s | Particle speed in tracking |
| 3 | Eulerian velocity field | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ | m/s | Field-based analysis (CFD) |
| 4 | Material derivative (acceleration) | $\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | m/s² | Newton's 2nd law for fluids |
| 5 | Convective acceleration (1D) | $a_{conv} = V \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s}$ | m/s² | Flow through varying cross-section |
| 6 | Steady flow condition | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ | — | Reduces material derivative |
| 7 | Uniform flow condition | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0$ | — | Constant cross-section pipe |
| 8 | Irrotational flow (vorticity) | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ | 1/s | Velocity potential exists |
| 9 | Streamline equation (2D) | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ | — | Visualizing flow patterns |
| 10 | Incompressibility | $\rho = \text{const}, \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | kg/m³ | Liquid flows, low-speed air |
| 11 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime identification |
| 12 | Vorticity vector | $\vec{\omega} = 2\vec{\Omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | rad/s | Rotation of fluid element |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Identifying irrotational regions outside the boundary layer for lift calculations.
- **Civil/Hydraulic Engineering:** Using Reynolds number to determine whether pipe flow is laminar or turbulent (Kerala Water Authority pipe design).
- **Mechanical Engineering (Turbomachinery):** Describing steady vs. unsteady flow in pumps and turbines.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Lagrangian tracking of red blood cells; Eulerian Doppler ultrasound measurement of blood velocity.
- **Weather Prediction:** Eulerian field description of atmospheric flow (used by IMD, Kerala).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Streamline Equation

A streamline is defined as a curve whose tangent at every point is parallel to the velocity vector $\vec{V}$ at that instant. Let $d\vec{r} = dx\hat{i} + dy\hat{j} + dz\hat{k}$ be an infinitesimal vector along the streamline.

**Step 1:** By definition of a streamline, $d\vec{r}$ must be parallel to $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$.

$$d\vec{r} \times \vec{V} = 0$$

**Step 2:** This cross-product gives us the proportionality:

$$dx : dy : dz = u : v : w$$

**Step 3:** Converting to equal ratios (the standard form):

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

This is the **streamline equation in 3-D Cartesian coordinates** — a frequently asked KTU derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 2-D flow** (u, v as functions of x, y), the streamline equation reduces to:
> 
> $$\frac{dx}{u(x,y)} = \frac{dy}{v(x,y)} \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u}$$
> 
> This is a **first-order ODE** whose solution is the **stream function** $\psi(x,y) = $ constant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Material (Total) Acceleration in Eulerian Frame

This is the **most important derivation in Module 2** of PCMET303.

**Step 1:** In the Eulerian description, velocity is a function of both position and time:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$$

**Step 2:** A fluid particle moves, so its position changes with time:

$$x = x(t), \quad y = y(t), \quad z = z(t)$$

**Step 3:** The acceleration is the time rate of change of velocity **following the particle**. Apply the **chain rule** for a function of multiple variables:

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = u$, $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = v$, $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = w$ are the velocity components:

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the second group as the **convective derivative** in operator form:

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

> This is the **material derivative** or **substantial derivative** — central to the Navier-Stokes equations.

**Step 6 (Special Case — 1-D Convective Acceleration):** If the flow is one-dimensional and $\vec{V} = V(x)\hat{i}$:

$$a_{conv} = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

For purely 1-D flow, $v = w = 0$:

$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Vorticity for Rotational Classification

The **angular velocity** of a fluid element about the z-axis is defined as the average rotation of two perpendicular line elements. Consider an element at point P with sides $dx$ and $dy$.

**Step 1:** Rotation rate of the side $dx$ (about z-axis):
- The line rotates from horizontal due to gradient $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$.
- Rotation rate = $\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$ (counterclockwise positive).

**Step 2:** Rotation rate of the side $dy$ (about z-axis):
- The line rotates from vertical due to gradient $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$.
- Rotation rate = $-\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ (clockwise is negative).

**Step 3:** Total angular velocity $\Omega_z$ is the average:

$$\Omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)$$

**Step 4:** The **vorticity** is defined as $\omega_z = 2\Omega_z$:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General 3-D Vorticity Vector** (KTU 2024 expected form):
> 
> $$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix}$$
> 
> **Irrotational flow condition:** $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ (velocity potential exists).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Lagrangian vs. Eulerian

| Aspect | Lagrangian | Eulerian |
|---|---|---|
| **Observer** | Moves with the fluid particle | Stationary in space |
| **Independent variables** | $\vec{r_0}$ (initial position), $t$ | $x, y, z, t$ |
| **Velocity representation** | $\vec{V}_L = \dfrac{\partial \vec{r}}{\partial t}\bigg\vert_{\vec{r_0}}$ | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ |
| **Acceleration** | $\vec{a}_L = \dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{r}}{\partial t^2}\bigg\vert_{\vec{r_0}}$ | $\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt}$ |
| **Typical use** | Tracking droplets/pollutants | CFD, experimental instruments |
| **Mathematical complexity** | High (coupled ODEs) | Low (PDEs in field) |
| **KTU Example** | Tag a single raindrop | Measure wind speed at Kochi airport |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 2-D velocity field is given by $\vec{V} = (3x + 2y)\hat{i} + (2x - y)\hat{j}$ m/s. Determine:
(i) The streamline passing through point (1, 2).
(ii) The vorticity at the same point.
(iii) Classify the flow as rotational or irrotational.

**Solution:**

**(i) Streamline Equation:**

Using $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$:

$$\frac{dx}{3x + 2y} = \frac{dy}{2x - y}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$(2x - y)\,dx - (3x + 2y)\,dy = 0$$

$$(2x\,dx - 3x\,dy) - (y\,dx + 2y\,dy) = 0$$

$$2x\,dx - 3x\,dy - y\,dx - 2y\,dy = 0$$

$$d(x^2) - 3x\,dy - d\left(\frac{y^2}{2}\right) - y\,dx = 0$$

This is exact, integrating:

$$x^2 - \frac{y^2}{2} - 3xy = C$$

Substituting (1, 2):

$$1 - \frac{4}{2} - 3(1)(2) = C \implies C = 1 - 2 - 6 = -7$$

$$\boxed{x^2 - 3xy - \frac{y^2}{2} = -7}$$

**(ii) Vorticity:**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(2x - y) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x + 2y)$$

$$\omega_z = 2 - 2 = 0$$

**(iii) Classification:** Since $\omega_z = 0$ at the point and in fact everywhere for this flow, the flow is **IRROTATIONAL**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Master Classification of Fluid Flows

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Flow] --> B{Time Dependent?}
    A --> C{Spatial Variation?}
    A --> D{Dimensionality?}
    A --> E{Rotation?}
    A --> F{Compressibility?}
    A --> G{Viscosity?}
    A --> H{Boundary?}
    A --> I{Flow Pattern?}

    B -->|No| B1[Steady Flow]
    B -->|Yes| B2[Unsteady Flow]

    C -->|No| C1[Uniform Flow]
    C -->|Yes| C2[Non-Uniform Flow]

    D -->|1 coord| D1[1-D Flow]
    D -->|2 coords| D2[2-D Flow]
    D -->|3 coords| D3[3-D Flow]

    E -->|Vorticity = 0| E1[Irrotational Flow]
    E -->|Vorticity nonzero| E2[Rotational Flow]

    F -->|rho constant| F1[Incompressible Flow]
    F -->|rho varies| F2[Compressible Flow]

    G -->|Friction matters| G1[Viscous Flow]
    G -->|Frictionless| G2[Inviscid Flow]

    H -->|Bounded| H1[Internal Flow e.g. Pipe]
    H -->|Unbounded| H2[External Flow e.g. Airfoil]

    I -->|Re less than 2000| I1[Laminar Flow]
    I -->|Re greater than 4000| I2[Turbulent Flow]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Lagrangian vs. Eulerian — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lagrangian["LAGRANGIAN FRAME"]
        L1[Initial particle position r0] --> L2[Track single fluid particle]
        L2 --> L3[Record position r of t]
        L3 --> L4[Compute velocity = dr of dt]
        L4 --> L5[Compute acceleration = d2r of dt2]
    end

    subgraph Eulerian["EULERIAN FRAME"]
        E1[Fixed observation point xyz] --> E2[Measure passing fluid properties]
        E2 --> E3[Field quantities Vxyz t]
        E3 --> E4[Apply material derivative]
        E4 --> E5[Total acceleration = DV of Dt]
    end

    L5 -->|Compare with| E5
    Lagrangian -.->|Time integration bridge| Eulerian
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Pathline, Streakline, and Streamline in Unsteady Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PL[Pathline: Trajectory of ONE particle over time] --> R1[Particle at t=0]
    PL --> R2[Particle at t=1s]
    PL --> R3[Particle at t=2s]
    PL --> R4[Particle at t=3s]
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4

    SL[Streakline: Locus of ALL particles that passed Point P] --> S1[Particle A at t=0]
    SL --> S2[Particle B at t=1s]
    SL --> S3[Particle C at t=2s]
    SL --> S4[Particle D at t=3s]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4

    ST[Streamline: Instantaneous curve tangent to V] --> T1[Tangent at P1]
    ST --> T2[Tangent at P2]
    ST --> T3[Tangent at P3]

    R4 -.->|All three DIFFERENT in unsteady flow| Result[In STEADY flow all 3 are IDENTICAL]
    S4 -.-> Result
    T3 -.-> Result
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Components of the Material Derivative

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MD[Material Derivative DV of Dt] --> P1[Local Term dV of dt]
    MD --> P2[Convective Term V dot grad V]
    P2 --> P3[Streamwise acceleration V dV of ds]
    P2 --> P4[Cross-stream effects]
    P1 --> P5[Unsteadiness contribution]
    P3 --> P6[Nozzle of diffuser effect]
    Result[Steady Flow: Local term = 0]
    Result --> R1[Only convective term survives]
    Result --> R2[Example: Flow in a tap being opened slowly is UNSTEADY]
    Result --> R3[Example: Constant flow in a converging nozzle is STEADY]
```
