topics.title: Stream Function and Velocity Potential Function, Flow Net
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Stream Function and Velocity Potential Function, Flow Net ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Stream Function (ψ — "psi") **Formal Academic Definition:** The **Stream Function** $\psi(x, y, t)$ is a scalar function of position (and time, for unsteady flow) in a two-dimensional incompressible flow field such that its partial derivatives yield the velocity components. By the standard KTU 2024 convention: $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad ; \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$ The very *existence* of a single-valued stream function is guaranteed whenever the **continuity equation** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$ is satisfied. Hence, for a 2D incompressible (continuous) flow, the stream function is **automatically defined**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Identity:** $\psi = \text{constant}$ defines a **streamline**. Therefore, the stream function is a mathematical label that *paints* the family of streamlines in a flow field. **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Topographic Map:** Imagine a hilly landscape. A stream function $\psi$ is like the **contour map of a hill**. The peak of the hill corresponds to a "source," the valley to a "sink," and the concentric rings (contours) drawn on the map represent lines of *equal elevation* — except in fluid mechanics, we call them streamlines, and the "elevation" $\psi$ measures the *cumulative volumetric flow rate* passing between two streamlines. Just as contour lines never cross (or you'd be in two altitudes at once), streamlines never intersect — a physical impossibility in steady flow. --- ### 1.2 The Velocity Potential Function (φ — "phi") **Formal Academic Definition:** The **Velocity Potential Function** $\phi(x, y, t)$ is a scalar function whose gradient gives the velocity vector: $$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \quad ; \quad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$ Unlike the stream function, $\phi$ does **not** exist for all flows. It exists **only when the flow is irrotational**, i.e., when the vorticity $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Identity:** $\phi = \text{constant}$ defines an **equipotential line** (a line of equal velocity potential). No work is done by pressure forces on a fluid particle as it moves *along* an equipotential. **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Electric Field:** Think of $\phi$ like the **electric potential** in electrostatics. The electric field lines (analogous to streamlines) are always perpendicular to the equipotential surfaces. In fluid flow, the velocity vector at any point is "downhill" on the $\phi$ surface — fluid flows from high $\phi$ to low $\phi$, just as current flows from high voltage to low voltage. A stagnation point is the "peak" or "valley" of the $\phi$ hill. --- ### 1.3 The Flow Net **Formal Academic Definition:** A **Flow Net** is a graphical network of two mutually orthogonal families of curves — **streamlines** ($\psi = $ const) and **equipotential lines** ($\phi = $ const) — that together describe the 2D pattern of an incompressible, irrotational flow through a bounded region (typically porous media such as soil under a dam, or flow through a channel constriction). **Intuitive Analogy — A Fish Net in a River:** Picture a fisherman holding a square-meshed net submerged in a river. The horizontal ropes are the *streamlines* (the path the water follows), and the vertical ropes are the *equipotential lines* (the lines along which the "pressure potential" is constant). Where the ropes cross at perfect right angles and form approximate **squares**, the net gives us a beautiful picture of how the water is sneaking under the fisherman's feet. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favourite:** Flow nets are heavily tested for **seepage problems** (e.g., discharge under a dam sheet pile). Mastering the *properties of a flow net* is non-negotiable. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Does Each Function Exist? | Function | Exists If… | Physical Meaning of Level Curves | Velocity Recovered By | |----------|------------|----------------------------------|----------------------| | $\psi$ (Stream Function) | Continuity holds ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$) | Curves tangent to velocity vector | $u = \partial_y \psi,\ v = -\partial_x \psi$ | | $\phi$ (Velocity Potential) | Irrotationality holds ($\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$) | Curves perpendicular to velocity vector | $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$ | ### 2.2 Properties of the Stream Function 1. **Streamlines:** Curves $\psi(x,y) = C$ are streamlines of the flow. 2. **Volumetric Flow Rate:** The volume flow rate per unit depth between any two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is $$q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$ This is independent of the path chosen — a powerful KTU result. 3. **Automatic Continuity:** Any differentiable $\psi$ *guarantees* a divergence-free (incompressible) 2D velocity field. 4. **Differentiability:** For $\psi$ to be single-valued, the flow must be irrotational OR the circulation around any closed contour must be zero. ### 2.3 Properties of the Velocity Potential Function 1. **Equipotential Lines:** Curves $\phi(x,y) = C$ are equipotential lines. 2. **Irrotationality is Mandatory:** $\phi$ exists **only** if $\omega_z = 0$. 3. **Laplace's Equation:** Combining irrotationality + continuity yields $$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$ 4. **No Net Circulation:** The line integral $\oint \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{s} = \oint d\phi = 0$ around any closed path (since $\phi$ is single-valued). ### 2.4 Cauchy–Riemann Equations (The Bridge Between ψ and φ) When *both* functions exist (i.e., the flow is **inviscid, incompressible, and irrotational**), the two are conjugate harmonic functions: $$\boxed{\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}}$$ These are mathematically identical to the **Cauchy–Riemann equations** of complex variable theory — the basis for the hodograph method. ### 2.5 Orthogonality — The Most Tested Result in KTU The angle $\theta$ between a streamline and an equipotential is given by: $$\tan\theta = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right)} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\big/\left(-\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right)\right)} = -1$$ Therefore $\theta = 90^\circ$. **Streamlines and equipotential lines always intersect orthogonally.** This is the cornerstone property that makes the **flow net** possible. ### 2.6 Flow Net — Engineering Properties > [!IMPORTANT] > A properly drawn flow net has these FOUR properties — examiners often test them as 3-mark short questions. 1. **Orthogonality:** Streamlines $\perp$ equipotential lines at every intersection. 2. **Square Cells:** Curvilinear "squares" form between adjacent streamlines and equipotentials (i.e., the inscribed circle touches all four sides). 3. **Constant Flow per Channel:** Volume flow between any two adjacent streamlines is the same. 4. **Constant Head Drop:** The head loss (potential drop) between any two adjacent equipotentials is the same. ### 2.7 Seepage Discharge Formula (Vital for Part-B Questions) For seepage through a porous medium (e.g., beneath a sheet pile or dam), Darcy's law combined with the flow net gives: $$\boxed{q = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_f}{N_d}}$$ where: - $q$ = seepage discharge per unit length (m³/s/m) - $k$ = coefficient of permeability of soil (m/s) - $H$ = head difference driving the flow (m) - $N_f$ = number of flow channels (counted between streamlines) - $N_d$ = number of equipotential drops (counted between equipotentials) > A **flow channel** is the region between two adjacent streamlines. An **equipotential drop** is the region between two adjacent equipotential lines. ### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Tag | |---|---------|---------|---------| | 1 | $u = \partial \psi / \partial y,\ v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ | Velocity from stream function | Continuity satisfied | | 2 | $u = \partial \phi / \partial x,\ v = \partial \phi / \partial y$ | Velocity from potential | Irrotational required | | 3 | $\omega_z = \partial v / \partial x - \partial u / \partial y$ | Vorticity (rotation) | Zero for $\phi$ to exist | | 4 | $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ | Continuity (2D) | Existence of $\psi$ | | 5 | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ | Laplace (continuity + irrotational) | Harmonic | | 6 | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | Laplace (irrotational + incompressible) | Conjugate harmonic | | 7 | $\partial \phi/\partial x = \partial \psi/\partial y$ | Cauchy–Riemann (1) | Conjugate pair | | 8 | $\partial \phi/\partial y = -\partial \psi/\partial x$ | Cauchy–Riemann (2) | Conjugate pair | | 9 | $q = k H (N_f/N_d)$ | Seepage discharge per unit length | Flow net | | 10 | $V = k \cdot i = k \cdot (H/L)$ | Darcy's law | Porous flow | ### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hydraulic Engineering:** Designing earth dams, coffer dams, sheet pile structures — computing uplift pressure and seepage loss. - **Aerospace:** Computing lift and pressure distribution around an airfoil using potential flow superposition. - **Hydrogeology:** Tracing groundwater flow paths and contaminant migration. - **Turbomachinery (relevant to PCMET303 Module 2!):** Potential flow theory underpins cascade design in axial turbines. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity Components from Stream Function (and Vice Versa) **Starting Point:** The 2D continuity equation for an incompressible fluid. $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$ This equation has the exact form of an **exact differential**. We introduce a scalar $\psi$ such that: $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Substitute into continuity:** $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$ $$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$ $$0 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Identity satisfied for any } \psi)$$ > **Conclusion:** Any differentiable $\psi$ automatically yields a divergence-free velocity field. The stream function is *not unique* — it is determined up to an arbitrary additive constant. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Existence of Velocity Potential for Irrotational Flow **Starting Point:** Irrotationality condition (zero vorticity in 2D). $$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$ This is again an exact differential. We define a scalar $\phi$ such that: $$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ $$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ **Substitute into the irrotationality condition:** $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$ $$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$ $$0 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ > **Conclusion:** Any irrotational 2D flow admits a velocity potential $\phi$, unique up to an arbitrary constant. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Laplace Equation for φ (Continuity + Irrotational) **Combine (iii) and (iv) with the 2D continuity equation:** $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$ Substitute the potential relations: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \phi = 0}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Laplace Equation for ψ (Same Conditions) **Combine (i) and (ii) with the 2D irrotationality condition:** $$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$ Substitute the stream-function relations: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$ $$-\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \psi = 0}$$ > **Key Insight:** *Both* $\phi$ and $\psi$ are **harmonic functions** (satisfy Laplace's equation) when the flow is simultaneously incompressible *and* irrotational. This dual-harmonicity is the foundation of the hodograph and conformal-mapping methods. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Cauchy–Riemann Equations (When BOTH Functions Exist) **Given** that $\phi$ exists (irrotational) and $\psi$ exists (incompressible), we equate the velocity components from both representations: $$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$$ $$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$ These are the **Cauchy–Riemann equations** of complex analysis. They guarantee that $w(z) = \phi + i\psi$ (where $z = x + iy$) is an **analytic function** of $z$. This is the foundation of potential flow superposition. --- ### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Orthogonality of Streamlines and Equipotentials **Goal:** Show that the curves $\psi = $ const and $\phi = $ const intersect at $90^\circ$. **Step 1:** The slope of an equipotential line $\phi(x,y) = $ const is found by total differentiation: $$d\phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} dy = 0$$ $$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi = \text{const}} = -\frac{\partial \phi/\partial x}{\partial \phi/\partial y} = -\frac{u}{v}$$ **Step 2:** The slope of a streamline $\psi(x,y) = $ const: $$d\psi = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} dy = 0$$ $$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi = \text{const}} = -\frac{\partial \psi/\partial x}{\partial \psi/\partial y} = -\frac{-v}{u} = \frac{v}{u}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply the two slopes: $$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} \cdot \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} = \left(-\frac{u}{v}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = -1$$ > **Conclusion:** The product of slopes equals $-1$, confirming **perpendicularity at every intersection**. This is the geometric heart of the flow net. --- ### 3.7 Derivation 7 — Volumetric Flow Rate Between Two Streamlines Consider two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ separated by a distance $dn$ measured normal to the flow. The local velocity is $V$ at that point. The flow rate per unit depth through an element $dn$ is: $$dq = V \, dn$$ But $V$ is tangent to the streamline, and $dn$ is normal to it. From the directional derivative: $$V = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial n}$$ Therefore: $$dq = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial n} dn = d\psi$$ Integrating between the two streamlines: $$\boxed{q = \int_{\psi_1}^{\psi_2} d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1}$$ > **Engineering Meaning:** The flow rate between two streamlines is *numerically equal* to the difference of their $\psi$-values. Streamlines can thus be **labeled in units of flow rate** (e.g., m²/s), which is the basis of the flow-channel counting method. --- ### 3.8 Derivation 8 — Seepage Discharge from a Flow Net Consider a rectangular flow-net cell of width $a$ (along streamline) and length $b$ (along equipotential). Within the cell: - **Hydraulic gradient** $i = \Delta h / b$ (where $\Delta h$ is the head loss across the cell) - **Discharge per unit area** $v = k \cdot i = k \cdot \Delta h / b$ (Darcy's law) - **Cross-sectional area per unit depth** $A = a \cdot 1 = a$ Cell discharge: $$\Delta q = v \cdot a = k \cdot \frac{\Delta h}{b} \cdot a = k \cdot \Delta h \cdot \frac{a}{b}$$ For a **square cell** ($a = b$): $\Delta q = k \cdot \Delta h$. Summing over $N_f$ flow channels and $N_d$ equipotential drops, with total head $H$: $$q = k \cdot \frac{H}{N_d} \cdot N_f = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_f}{N_d}$$ This is the **seepage formula** given in §2.7. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of 2D Flow Description ```mermaid flowchart TD A[2D Incompressible Flow Field] --> B{Continuity
Satisfied?} A --> C{Irrotational
Zero Vorticity?} B -- Yes --> D[Stream Function psi exists] B -- No --> E[No single-valued psi] C -- Yes --> F[Velocity Potential phi exists] C -- No --> G[No phi — flow is rotational] D --> H[u = dpsi/dy, v = -dpsi/dx] D --> I[psi = const are Streamlines] H --> J[Laplace Equation for psi] I --> J F --> K[u = dphi/dx, v = dphi/dy] F --> L[phi = const are Equipotentials] K --> M[Laplace Equation for phi] L --> M D --> N[Combined psi and phi] F --> N N --> O[Cauchy Riemann Equations] N --> P[Orthogonal Streamlines and Equipotentials] N --> Q[Flow Net Construction] O --> Q P --> Q Q --> R[Engineering Application:
Seepage Discharge q = kH times Nf by Nd] Q --> S[Engineering Application:
Uplift Pressure Distribution] Q --> T[Engineering Application:
Potential Flow around Bodies] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Flow Net Properties Checklist ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Valid Flow Net] --> B[Property 1: Orthogonality
psi perp phi] A --> C[Property 2: Square Cells
a equals b approx] A --> D[Property 3: Equal Flow per Channel
Delta q constant] A --> E[Property 4: Equal Head Drop per Strip
Delta h constant] B --> F[Verify by inspection] C --> F D --> G[Count flow channels Nf] E --> H[Count equipotential drops Nd] G --> I[Seepage Discharge q] H --> I ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Typical Flow Net Under a Sheet Pile ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UPSTREAM["Upstream Water Level H_up"] W1[Water Surface] end subgraph SOIL["Porous Soil Region"] SP[Sheet Pile Wall
Impervious Boundary] S1[Streamline 1 - psi 1] S2[Streamline 2 - psi 2] S3[Streamline 3 - psi 3] E1[Equipotential 1 - phi 1] E2[Equipotential 2 - phi 2] E3[Equipotential 3 - phi 3] E4[Equipotential 4 - phi 4] end subgraph DOWNSTREAM["Downstream Tail Level H_down"] W2[Water Surface] end W1 --> SOIL SOIL --> W2 SP -.Blocks flow.-> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 E1 --> E2 E2 --> E3 E3 --> E4 ``` ### 4.4 Schematic — Orthogonal Intersection of Streamline and Equipotential ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Streamline
tangent to V] --> X((Intersection Point P)) X --> B[Equipotential
normal to V] A -. psi constant .-> X B -. phi constant .-> X X -. Right angle 90 deg .-> A X -. Right angle 90 deg .-> B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reader's Tip:** Mermaid cannot draw smooth curved streamlines physically. In actual KTU exam answer scripts, the examiner expects hand-drawn flow nets with smooth curves. The block diagrams above map the *logical* relationships that your hand-drawn net must respect. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Define stream function and velocity potential function. State the conditions for their existence.** **Model Answer:** The **stream function $\psi$** is a scalar function of space and time defined by $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$. It exists for any 2D **incompressible** (continuity-satisfying) flow. The **velocity potential $\phi$** is a scalar function defined by $u = \partial \phi/\partial x$ and $v = \partial \phi/\partial y$. It exists only for 2D **irrotational** flow (zero vorticity). > **Self-Explanation:** Stream function is about *mass conservation*; velocity potential is about *zero rotation*. Both can exist simultaneously when the flow is incompressible **and** irrotational — this is the regime of *potential flow theory*. **Examiner Note:** Most students lose the **3rd mark** by not mentioning the *condition* (incompressibility for $\psi$, irrotationality for $\phi$). Always state BOTH definition and condition. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Show that streamlines and equipotential lines are mutually orthogonal.** **Model Answer:** Let $\phi(x,y) = $ const be an equipotential and $\psi(x,y) = $ const be a streamline. The slopes are: $$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} = -\frac{\partial \phi/\partial x}{\partial \phi/\partial y} = -\frac{u}{v}$$ $$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} = -\frac{\partial \psi/\partial x}{\partial \psi/\partial y} = -\frac{-v}{u} = \frac{v}{u}$$ Product of slopes: $$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} \cdot \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} = \left(-\frac{u}{v}\right)\left(\frac{v}{u}\right) = -1$$ Hence the curves are **perpendicular** at every intersection. ∎ --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]** **For a 2D incompressible flow, the velocity field is given by:** $$u = x^2 - y^2 \quad ; \quad v = -2xy$$ **(a) [7 Marks]** Show that continuity is satisfied, determine the stream function, check whether the flow is irrotational, and if so, derive the velocity potential function. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the streamlines are labeled such that $\psi_1 = 0$ is the $x$-axis, compute the volumetric flow rate (per unit depth) between the streamlines $\psi = 0$ and $\psi = 2$ m²/s. State the engineering significance. --- **Model Solution to A(a):** **Step 1 — Verify Continuity:** $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2x + (-2x) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ Continuity is satisfied. Hence stream function exists. **Step 2 — Determine $\psi$:** We have $u = \partial \psi/\partial y = x^2 - y^2$. Integrating with respect to $y$: $$\psi = \int (x^2 - y^2)\, dy = x^2 y - \frac{y^3}{3} + f(x) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ Now $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x = -2xy$ implies $\partial \psi/\partial x = 2xy$. Differentiating (i) with respect to $x$: $$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 2xy + f'(x) = 2xy \Rightarrow f'(x) = 0 \Rightarrow f(x) = C$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\psi = x^2 y - \frac{y^3}{3} + C}$$ **Step 3 — Check Irrotationality:** $$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -2y - (-2y) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ Flow is irrotational. Therefore velocity potential $\phi$ exists. **Step 4 — Determine $\phi$:** $u = \partial \phi/\partial x = x^2 - y^2$. Integrating with respect to $x$: $$\phi = \int (x^2 - y^2)\, dx = \frac{x^3}{3} - xy^2 + g(y) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ $v = \partial \phi/\partial y = -2xy$. Differentiating (ii) with respect to $y$: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -2xy + g'(y) = -2xy \Rightarrow g'(y) = 0 \Rightarrow g(y) = K$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\phi = \frac{x^3}{3} - xy^2 + K}$$ > This is the **classical doublet flow** combined with a uniform stream — a famous potential flow solution used in classical aerodynamics. --- **Model Solution to A(b):** The volumetric flow rate per unit depth between two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is: $$q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$ (derived in §3.7). Given $\psi_1 = 0$ and $\psi_2 = 2$ m²/s: $$\boxed{q = 2 - 0 = 2 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \text{ (per unit depth)}}$$ **Engineering Significance:** Streamlines act as natural *boundaries* of the flow. The flow rate between any two of them is invariant along the stream — useful in (1) computing discharge in open channels, (2) computing seepage through porous media (via flow nets), and (3) verifying 2D CFD simulations. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style — Internal Choice]** **A sheet pile wall driven into a homogeneous porous soil retains water with a head difference of $H = 6$ m between upstream and downstream sides. The coefficient of permeability of the soil is $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. A flow net drawn for the seepage under the wall shows $N_f = 4$ flow channels and $N_d = 12$ equipotential drops.** **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the seepage discharge per unit length of the wall. Also find the head loss and uplift pressure at the point located 4 equipotential drops downstream of the upstream soil surface. **(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove the four essential properties of a flow net. Justify why these properties must hold for a valid flow net. --- **Model Solution to B(a):** **Step 1 — Seepage Discharge per unit length:** $$q = k \cdot H \cdot \frac{N_f}{N_d} = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 6 \cdot \frac{4}{12}$$ $$q = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 6 \cdot 0.3333 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s/m}$$ $$\boxed{q = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s per metre length of wall}}$$ **Step 2 — Head Loss per Equipotential Drop:** Each equipotential drop corresponds to a head loss of: $$\Delta h = \frac{H}{N_d} = \frac{6}{12} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Uplift Pressure at the 4th Drop:** After 4 drops downstream of the upstream face, the total head loss is: $$h_{\text{lost}} = 4 \times \Delta h = 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ m}$$ The remaining **residual head** (uplift pressure head) is: $$h_{\text{uplift}} = H - h_{\text{lost}} = 6 - 2.0 = 4.0 \text{ m}$$ If $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³, the uplift pressure intensity is: $$p_{\text{uplift}} = \gamma_w \cdot h_{\text{uplift}} = 9.81 \times 4.0 = 39.24 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{p_{\text{uplift}} = 39.24 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ acting upward}}$$ --- **Model Solution to B(b):** **The Four Properties of a Flow Net:** 1. **Orthogonality:** Streamlines and equipotential lines intersect at right angles (proved in §3.6 using $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$). *Why?* Because $\nabla \psi \perp \nabla \phi$ — the gradient of $\psi$ is along the velocity direction, the gradient of $\phi$ is also along the velocity direction, and the level curves are perpendicular to their respective gradients. Thus the two families of level curves are perpendicular to each other. 2. **Square Cells:** Between any two adjacent streamlines and any two adjacent equipotentials, the curvilinear cell is a "square" (inscribed circle touches all four sides). *Why?* Darcy's law for a cell gives flow $\Delta q = k \cdot (a/b) \cdot \Delta h$. For $\Delta q$ and $\Delta h$ to be the *same* in every cell (properties 3 and 4), we require $a/b = $ const. Normalizing gives $a = b$, i.e., a square. 3. **Equal Flow per Channel:** The flow rate $\Delta q$ between any two adjacent streamlines is the same throughout the net. *Why?* From continuity, the flow entering a streamline channel from one end must exit from the other end (no sources/sinks inside the flow domain). 4. **Equal Head Drop per Strip:** The head loss $\Delta h$ between any two adjacent equipotentials is constant. *Why?* Total head is dissipated uniformly along the seepage path; by linearity of Darcy's law, equal strips consume equal head. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Lose up to 4–5 marks each):** > 1. **Sign Errors in Stream Function:** Using $v = +\partial \psi/\partial x$ instead of $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$ — yields the *wrong* stream function (transpose-equivalent). KTU strictly follows the $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$ convention. > 2. **Forgetting Constant of Integration:** When integrating for $\psi$ or $\phi$, the function $f(x)$ or $g(y)$ MUST be resolved. Skipping this step is a 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Confusing "Streamline" with "Pathline":** In *steady* flow they coincide; in unsteady flow they differ. KTU Part-A questions often test this. > 4. **Seepage Formula Misuse:** Using $N_f$ and $N_d$ swapped (numerator-denominator) — yields wrong order of magnitude. Recheck dimensionally: $q$ must be in m³/s/m. > 5. **Assuming φ exists for Rotational Flow:** The biggest conceptual error. If $\omega_z \neq 0$, write "Velocity potential does not exist" and stop — do NOT attempt to find $\phi$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Stream function $\psi$** exists wherever 2D continuity is satisfied; constant-$\psi$ curves = **streamlines**. - [x] **Velocity potential $\phi$** exists wherever 2D flow is irrotational; constant-$\phi$ curves = **equipotential lines**. - [x] Velocity recovery: - $u = \partial \psi/\partial y,\ v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$ - $u = \partial \phi/\partial x,\ v = \partial \phi/\partial y$ - [x] Both $\psi$ and $\phi$ are **harmonic** ($\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ and $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$) when flow is incompressible AND irrotational. - [x] **Cauchy–Riemann equations** link $\psi$ and $\phi$: $\partial \phi/\partial x = \partial \psi/\partial y$ and $\partial \phi/\partial y = -\partial \psi/\partial x$. - [x] Streamlines and equipotentials are **always orthogonal** (slopes multiply to $-1$). - [x] Flow rate between streamlines = $\psi_2 - \psi_1$ (per unit depth). - [x] **Flow net** = orthogonal mesh of $\psi$ = const and $\phi$ = const curves. - [x] **Flow net properties:** orthogonality, square cells, equal flow per channel, equal head drop per strip. - [x] **Seepage discharge:** $q = k H (N_f/N_d)$ — must be applied for dam/seepage questions. - [x] $\phi$ does **not** exist for rotational flow — always check $\omega_z$ first.

topics.title: Fluid Dynamics: Continuity Equation, Euler's and Bernoulli's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fluid Dynamics: Continuity Equation, Euler's and Bernoulli's Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fluid Dynamics — The Science of Flowing Fluids

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Fluid Dynamics* is the branch of fluid mechanics that deals with the motion of fluids (liquids and gases) and the forces acting on them while in motion. It establishes fundamental conservation laws—**Conservation of Mass (Continuity)**, **Conservation of Momentum (Euler's equation)**, and **Conservation of Energy (Bernoulli's equation)**—that govern fluid flow.

### The Three Governing Pillars of Fluid Dynamics

| # | Law | Physical Meaning | Governing Equation |
|---|-----|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Continuity Equation** | Mass can neither be created nor destroyed | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) + \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ |
| 2 | **Euler's Equation** | Newton's 2nd law applied to an inviscid fluid element | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$ |
| 3 | **Bernoulli's Equation** | Mechanical energy is conserved along a streamline | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In Module 2, students are expected to (i) derive the continuity equation for 3D and 1D flows, (ii) derive Euler's equation of motion along a streamline, and (iii) integrate Euler's equation to obtain Bernoulli's equation with its assumptions clearly stated. Application problems include venturimeters, orifices, and pitot tubes.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "River-City" Picture

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy: Water Flowing Through a Pipe Network**

Imagine a city water distribution system. The total amount of water entering a junction **must equal** the total amount leaving it (no water magically appears or vanishes) — this is the **Continuity Principle**. Now, as water rushes through a narrow section of pipe, it speeds up (like a river squeezing through a gorge), and simultaneously the pressure drops — this exchange between velocity and pressure energy is the essence of **Bernoulli's Effect**. The water's *acceleration* is caused by the *net pressure and gravity forces* on a fluid parcel — exactly what **Euler's Equation** describes.

In simple words:
- **Continuity Equation** = "What goes in must come out" (mass accounting)
- **Euler's Equation** = "Force equals mass times acceleration" (Newton's law for fluids)
- **Bernoulli's Equation** = "Energy trades between pressure, velocity, and height" (energy accounting)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Atmospheric Metric Used Throughout This Module:**
> The **acceleration due to gravity** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and the **specific weight of water** $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ are the default constants assumed in all KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

---

## 1.3 Types of Flow — Classification Cheat Sheet

| Flow Type | Definition | Governing Equation Form |
|-----------|------------|------------------------|
| **Steady Flow** | Flow parameters do not change with time at a fixed point | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ |
| **Unsteady Flow** | Flow parameters vary with time | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} \neq 0$ |
| **Uniform Flow** | Velocity is the same at every point at a given instant | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0$ |
| **Non-Uniform Flow** | Velocity varies along the flow direction | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} \neq 0$ |
| **Laminar Flow** | Fluid particles move in parallel layers without intermixing | $Re < 2000$ |
| **Turbulent Flow** | Fluid particles move in irregular, chaotic paths | $Re > 4000$ |
| **Compressible Flow** | Fluid density $\rho$ changes significantly | Variable $\rho$ |
| **Incompressible Flow** | Fluid density $\rho$ is constant | $\rho = \text{constant}$ |
| **Rotational Flow** | Fluid particles rotate about their own axes | $\nabla \times \vec{V} \neq 0$ |
| **Irrotational Flow** | Fluid particles do not rotate about their own axes | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Assumption:** The derivations of the standard forms of the Continuity, Euler's, and Bernoulli's equations in this module assume **steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless), and irrotational** flow along a **streamline**.

---

## 1.4 Concept of a Streamline, Streakline, and Pathline

- **Streamline:** An imaginary curve whose tangent at every point is in the direction of the velocity vector at that instant. Two streamlines never intersect.
- **Pathline:** The actual trajectory traced by a single fluid particle over time.
- **Streakline:** The locus of all particles that have previously passed through a given point.

For **steady flow**, all three lines are identical. For Bernoulli's equation, the integration path is a **streamline**.

---

## 1.5 Prerequisites Checklist (Revise Before Proceeding)

- Newton's Second Law of Motion: $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$
- Concept of pressure as force per unit area: $p = F/A$
- Differentiation along a streamline: $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + V\dfrac{\partial}{\partial s}$
- Vector and scalar field concepts (gradient, divergence)
- Line integral and conservative force fields
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuity Equation — Derivation Logic Steps

### 2.1.1 General 3D Continuity Equation (Vector Form)

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume:** Consider an infinitesimal fluid element of dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ at point $(x, y, z)$ with centre coordinates.

**Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Mass:** The net mass flux out of the control surface equals the rate of decrease of mass within the element.

**Step 3 — Mass Flux Along x-direction:**
- Mass entering through the left face (at $x$): $\rho u \, dy \, dz$
- Mass leaving through the right face (at $x + dx$): $\left[ \rho u + \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \right] dy \, dz$
- **Net x-outflow:** $\dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$

**Step 4 — Repeat for y and z Directions:** Apply the same logic to obtain the net mass outflows in the y and z directions.

**Step 5 — Total Net Outflow:** Sum the three directional contributions.

**Step 6 — Rate of Mass Change Inside the Element:** $-\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz$

**Step 7 — Equate and Simplify:** Dividing by the elemental volume $dx\,dy\,dz$ yields the **general continuity equation**.

### 2.1.2 Simplified Forms of the Continuity Equation

For **incompressible flow** ($\rho = \text{constant}$), the equation reduces dramatically:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is the famous **incompressible continuity equation**, which states that the **divergence of the velocity field is zero** (i.e., velocity field is solenoidal).

For **1-D steady incompressible flow** through a pipe of varying cross-section:

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \text{ (constant)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Continuity Principle:** Mass is a conserved quantity. For a control volume, the algebraic sum of mass entering, mass leaving, and mass accumulated must equal zero. The continuity equation is simply a mathematical statement of this physical fact.

---

## 2.2 Euler's Equation of Motion — Derivation Logic Steps

### 2.2.1 Physical Setup

Consider an **inviscid (frictionless) fluid element** of size $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ moving along a streamline. The forces acting on it are:
1. **Pressure forces** on the six faces of the element.
2. **Body force** (gravity) acting on the mass of the element.

### 2.2.2 Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Identify Pressure Forces:**
- On face perpendicular to x: net force $= -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$
- Similarly for y and z directions.

**Step 2 — Identify Body Force (Gravity):**
- For gravity acting in the negative z-direction: $\rho g_x dx\,dy\,dz$, $\rho g_y dx\,dy\,dz$, $\rho g_z dx\,dy\,dz$ where $\vec{g} = (g_x, g_y, g_z)$.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law:** $\sum F_x = m a_x$, and analogous for y and z.

**Step 4 — Divide by Elemental Mass $\rho dx\,dy\,dz$** to obtain acceleration components.

**Step 5 — Write the Material Derivative:** The acceleration of a fluid particle is the **material (substantial) derivative**:
$$a_x = \dfrac{Du}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the Vector Form:** Combine all three components to get Euler's equation in vector form.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How" Behind Euler's Equation:** It is simply **Newton's second law** rewritten for a fluid particle. The left-hand side captures the inertia (mass × acceleration) of the fluid parcel, while the right-hand side captures all surface forces (pressure gradient) and body forces (gravity) acting on it.

---

## 2.3 Bernoulli's Equation — From Euler to Energy

### 2.3.1 Integration of Euler's Equation Along a Streamline

**Step 1 — Take the Dot Product of Euler's Equation with the Velocity Vector $\vec{V}$.** This projects the equation along the flow direction.

**Step 2 — Identify Key Identities:**
- $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = V\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s} = \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right)$
- $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p = \dfrac{dp}{ds}$ (since pressure is constant across a streamline in steady flow)
- $\vec{V} \cdot \vec{g} = -g \dfrac{dz}{ds}$ (if z is the vertical coordinate, upward positive)

**Step 3 — Integrate Along the Streamline** from point 1 to point 2.

**Step 4 — Apply Steady Flow Assumption:** $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$, which makes the material derivative reduce to the convective term.

**Step 5 — Apply Incompressibility:** $\rho = \text{constant}$ allows it to be taken out of the integration.

**Step 6 — The Result:** Bernoulli's equation in its standard form emerges.

### 2.3.2 Assumptions of Bernoulli's Equation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Marks are frequently lost when students *forget to state the assumptions*. Always enumerate them in your answer.

1. Flow is **steady**.
2. Flow is **incompressible** ($\rho = \text{constant}$).
3. Flow is **inviscid** (frictionless, $\mu = 0$).
4. Flow is along a **streamline** (or the flow is irrotational).
5. The fluid is **ideal** (no heat transfer, no chemical reaction).
6. The equation applies between **two points on the same streamline**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in 80% of KTU Module 2 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Name | Units (SI) | Applicability |
|---|---------|------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$ | General Continuity Equation | kg/(m³·s) | All flows |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Incompressible Continuity | 1/s | Incompressible flow |
| 3 | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$ | Euler's Equation (Vector) | N/m³ | Inviscid flow |
| 4 | $\rho \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \rho g_x$ | Euler's Equation (x-component) | N/m³ | Inviscid flow |
| 5 | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ | Bernoulli's Equation (Head form) | m | Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along streamline |
| 6 | $\dfrac{p}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz = \text{constant}$ | Bernoulli's Equation (Energy form) | J/kg | Same as above |
| 7 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | Bernoulli's Equation (Between 2 points) | m | Same as above |
| 8 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ | 1-D Continuity (Incompressible) | m³/s | Steady, incompressible pipe flow |
| 9 | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}$ | 1-D Continuity (Compressible) | kg/s | Steady compressible flow |
| 10 | $\dfrac{p}{\gamma} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = H \text{ (Total Head)}$ | Total Energy Head | m | Steady, incompressible, inviscid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility of Bernoulli's Principle:**
> - **Aerofoil lift generation** in aircraft wings
> - **Venturimeter** flow measurement in pipes
> - **Pitot-static tube** for aircraft airspeed measurement
> - **Carburettor** fuel-air mixing in older automobiles
> - **Atomizers and spray guns**
> - **Blood flow measurement** in biomedical engineering
> - **Sailing** — Bernoulli effect on sail curvature

---

## 2.5 Energy, Pressure, and Velocity Heads — Visual Interpretation

Each term in Bernoulli's equation can be visualized as a "head" (energy per unit weight of fluid):

- **Pressure Head** $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ — Energy stored as fluid pressure (measured in m of fluid column).
- **Velocity Head** $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ — Kinetic energy per unit weight (measured in m of fluid column).
- **Elevation Head** $z$ — Potential energy per unit weight (measured in m of fluid column).

The sum of these three heads is the **Total Head** $H$, which is **constant** along a streamline for an ideal fluid.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember Bernoulli's Equation:** "**P**ressure + **V**elocity + **E**levation = **E**ternal **C**onstant"
> Or simpler: **PVC = PVC** (P, V, E are constant) — *but actually, it is the SUM that is constant!*
> Better mnemonic: **"Per Van Es Constant"** — *P/rho·g + V²/2g + z = Constant*

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the 3-D Continuity Equation

### Setup

Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element (control volume) of dimensions $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ located at point $P(x, y, z)$ in a fluid flow field. Let the velocity components at $P$ be $u$, $v$, $w$ along the x, y, z directions respectively. Let the density at $P$ be $\rho$.

### Step 1: Mass Flow Rate Through the Left Face (Face at $x$)

The left face has an area of $dy \cdot dz$ and is perpendicular to the x-axis. The mass flow rate entering through this face is:

$$\dot{m}_{x, \text{in}} = (\rho u) \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 2: Mass Flow Rate Through the Right Face (Face at $x + dx$)

By Taylor's series expansion, the value of $\rho u$ at the right face is:

$$(\rho u)_{x+dx} = (\rho u)_x + \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx$$

Therefore, the mass flow rate leaving through the right face is:

$$\dot{m}_{x, \text{out}} = \left[ \rho u + \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \right] dy \, dz$$

### Step 3: Net Mass Outflow in the x-Direction

Subtracting inflow from outflow:

$$\dot{m}_{x, \text{net out}} = \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 4: Analogous Expressions for y and z Directions

By the same logic applied to the y and z faces:

$$\dot{m}_{y, \text{net out}} = \dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

$$\dot{m}_{z, \text{net out}} = \dfrac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 5: Total Net Mass Outflow from the Element

Summing all three contributions:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{total, net out}} = \left[ \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 6: Rate of Change of Mass Inside the Element

The mass inside the element at time $t$ is $\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$. Its rate of change is:

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz) = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 7: Apply Conservation of Mass

The net outflow of mass per unit time must equal the rate of decrease of mass within the element:

$$\left[ \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] dx \, dy \, dz = -\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 8: Simplify by Dividing by the Elemental Volume

Dividing both sides by $dx \, dy \, dz$ and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

### Final Result — General 3-D Continuity Equation

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$

> **Explanation Note:** This is the most general form. For steady flow, $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$, giving $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$. For incompressible flow, $\rho = \text{constant}$, giving $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, i.e., the velocity field is divergence-free (solenoidal).

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of Euler's Equation of Motion

### Setup

Consider an inviscid fluid element of size $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ moving along a streamline. The pressure at the centre $P(x, y, z)$ is $p$. The element's mass is $\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz$. The body force per unit mass is $\vec{g} = (g_x, g_y, g_z)$.

### Step 1: Net Pressure Force in the x-Direction

The pressure on the left face (at $x$) is $p$ (force in +x direction since pressure pushes inward from the left). The pressure on the right face (at $x + dx$) is $p + \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} dx$ (force in -x direction).

Net pressure force in x-direction:

$$F_{x, \text{pressure}} = p \, dy \, dz - \left( p + \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} dx \right) dy \, dz = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 2: Body Force in the x-Direction

$$F_{x, \text{body}} = \rho \, g_x \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 3: Total Force in the x-Direction

$$F_x = \left( -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \rho g_x \right) dx \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law

$$F_x = m \cdot a_x = (\rho \, dx \, dy \, dz) \cdot a_x$$

where $a_x$ is the x-component of acceleration of the fluid particle.

### Step 5: Express Acceleration Using the Material Derivative

The acceleration of a fluid particle is the **material (substantial) derivative** of velocity:

$$a_x = \dfrac{Du}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

### Step 6: Combine and Simplify

$$\rho \dfrac{Du}{Dt} = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \rho g_x$$

Dividing by $\rho$:

$$\dfrac{Du}{Dt} = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + g_x$$

### Step 7: Write Analogous Equations for y and z Components

By the same process:

$$\rho \dfrac{Dv}{Dt} = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial y} + \rho g_y$$

$$\rho \dfrac{Dw}{Dt} = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \rho g_z$$

### Step 8: Assemble the Vector Form

$$\boxed{\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}}$$

> **Explanation Note:** The left-hand side represents the inertia of the fluid element (mass × acceleration). The right-hand side represents all the forces acting on it: the surface (pressure) force $-\nabla p$ and the body (gravity) force $\rho \vec{g}$. The **material derivative** is the proper way to express acceleration in a moving fluid because it accounts for both the local time variation and the convective (spatial) variation as the fluid particle moves through the field.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

### Step 1: Start with Euler's Equation

$$\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Steady Flow Assumption

For steady flow, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0$. The material derivative reduces to:

$$\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$$

So Euler's equation becomes:

$$\rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$$

### Step 3: Use the Vector Identity

A useful vector identity is:

$$(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) - \vec{V} \times (\nabla \times \vec{V})$$

For **irrotational flow**, $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$, so:

$$(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right)$$

Alternatively, for flow along a streamline, we can use:

$$\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = V \dfrac{\partial}{\partial s}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = V \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right)$$

### Step 4: Take the Dot Product of Euler's Equation with $\vec{V}$

$$\rho \vec{V} \cdot \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = -\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p + \rho \vec{V} \cdot \vec{g}$$

### Step 5: Express Right-Hand Side Terms

For steady flow, the pressure $p$ at a point is constant in time, so its gradient projected along the streamline gives:

$$\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p = V \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial s} = V \dfrac{dp}{ds}$$

For gravity, if we choose the z-axis vertically upward:

$$\rho \vec{V} \cdot \vec{g} = -\rho g \dfrac{dz}{ds} V$$

(assuming $g_z = -g$, since gravity points in the $-z$ direction)

### Step 6: Substitute and Rearrange

$$\rho V \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = -V \dfrac{dp}{ds} - \rho g V \dfrac{dz}{ds}$$

Dividing through by $\rho V$ (assuming $V \neq 0$):

$$\dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{dp}{ds} - g \dfrac{dz}{ds}$$

### Step 7: Rearrange as a Total Derivative

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{dp}{ds} + \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) + g \dfrac{dz}{ds} = 0$$

$$\dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{p}{\rho}\right) + \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) + g \dfrac{dz}{ds} = 0$$

(using $\rho = \text{constant}$ for incompressible flow to factor it out of the derivative)

### Step 8: Integrate Along the Streamline

Integrating from point 1 to point 2 along the streamline:

$$\int_1^2 \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{p}{\rho}\right) ds + \int_1^2 \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) ds + \int_1^2 g \dfrac{dz}{ds} ds = 0$$

$$\left[ \dfrac{p}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz \right]_1^2 = 0$$

### Step 9: Final Result — Bernoulli's Equation

$$\boxed{\dfrac{p_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 = \text{constant}}$$

### Alternative Form (Head Form) — Dividing by $g$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant (Total Head, in metres of fluid)}}$$

> **Explanation Note:** The integration was along a streamline because the term $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p$ was replaced by $V \, dp/ds$, which is only valid along a streamline. For *irrotational* flow, the equation holds between *any two points in the flow field*, not just on the same streamline.

---

## 3.4 Special Case — Bernoulli's Equation for a Horizontal Pipe

For a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

This shows the famous inverse relationship: **higher velocity → lower pressure** (and vice versa). This is the principle behind venturimeters and pitot tubes.

---

## 3.5 Summary of "What Was Derived, From What, Under What Assumptions"

| Derivation | Starting Point | Assumption(s) | Resulting Equation |
|------------|---------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Continuity (General) | Conservation of mass in elemental volume | None (general) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ |
| Continuity (Incompressible) | General continuity | $\rho = \text{constant}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ |
| Continuity (1-D) | Incompressible continuity | Steady, incompressible, uniform velocity profile | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ |
| Euler's Equation | Newton's 2nd law on fluid element | Inviscid, $\mu = 0$ | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$ |
| Bernoulli's Equation | Euler's equation integrated along a streamline | Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{const}$ |
