## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Sequence of Integration: From Euler to Bernoulli

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Start with Euler Equation rho DV over Dt = minus grad p + rho g] --> S2[Step 2: Apply Steady Flow: d V over dt equals V dot grad V]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Take dot product with V: V dot grad V squared by 2 minus V cross curl V]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Assume Irrotational: curl V equals 0]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Result V dot grad V squared by 2]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: V dot grad p = V dp by ds along streamline]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: V dot g = minus g dz by ds]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Substitute into integrated form]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Apply Incompressibility: rho constant]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Integrate from 1 to 2]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Final Bernoulli Equation p by rho + V squared by 2 + gz = constant]

    style S1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S11 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are short-answer conceptual questions. They test definitions, understanding of assumptions, and ability to explain the physical meaning of equations. Each carries **3 marks**.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the continuity equation for a 1-D steady incompressible flow. Mention its significance in fluid mechanics.**

**Model Answer:**

The continuity equation for a 1-D steady incompressible flow states that the **mass flow rate** of a fluid is constant across any cross-section of a pipe or duct.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \text{ (volumetric flow rate, m}^3/\text{s)}$$

**Derivation Logic:** Starting from the general 3-D continuity equation $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$, applying the steady flow assumption ($\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$) and the incompressibility assumption ($\rho = \text{constant}$), we obtain $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$. For 1-D flow where the velocity is uniform across the cross-section, this reduces to $AV = \text{constant}$.

**Physical Significance:**
1. It embodies **conservation of mass** in fluid flow.
2. It shows that **velocity is inversely proportional to cross-sectional area**: $V \propto 1/A$.
3. It is essential for **sizing pipes, ducts, and nozzles** in engineering design.
4. It forms the basis for flow measurement devices (venturimeter, orifice meter).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List the assumptions made in the derivation of Bernoulli's equation. Why is the equation valid only along a streamline?**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions of Bernoulli's Equation:**

| # | Assumption | Justification |
|---|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Steady flow** | Time derivatives vanish; simplifies the material derivative |
| 2 | **Incompressible flow** | Density is constant and can be taken out of the integration |
| 3 | **Inviscid flow** | Viscous (friction) terms are absent from Euler's equation |
| 4 | **Flow along a streamline** | Required for the substitution $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p = V \cdot dp/ds$ |
| 5 | **No heat transfer or shaft work** | Energy is purely mechanical; no thermal or shaft energy exchanges |
| 6 | **Ideal fluid** | No chemical reactions or phase changes |

**Why Along a Streamline?**

During the integration, we used the identity $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p = V \cdot \partial p / \partial s$, where $s$ is the distance **along the streamline**. This identity is valid because the pressure gradient in a direction perpendicular to the streamline does not contribute to changes in pressure experienced by a fluid particle moving along the streamline. Therefore, the equation can only relate properties at **two points on the same streamline**. For *irrotational flow*, this restriction is relaxed, and the equation holds between *any two points* in the flow field.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions are long-answer type with internal choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total: **14 marks per question**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the continuity equation in three-dimensional Cartesian coordinates for a compressible fluid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Water flows steadily through a horizontal pipe that contracts from a diameter of 20 cm at section 1 to 10 cm at section 2. The pressure at section 1 is 100 kPa and the velocity at section 1 is 1.5 m/s. Calculate:
- (i) The velocity at section 2.
- (ii) The pressure at section 2.
- (iii) The mass flow rate of water.

Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Derivation of 3-D Continuity Equation for Compressible Flow:**

Refer to the **detailed derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The step-by-step logic is:

1. Consider an infinitesimal fluid element $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ at point $P(x, y, z)$.
2. Compute the net mass outflow in the x-direction: $\dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} \, dx \, dy \, dz$
3. Similarly for y and z directions.
4. Sum the three contributions to get the total net mass outflow.
5. Equate to the rate of mass decrease inside the element: $-\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz$
6. Divide by the elemental volume and simplify.

**Final Result:**

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

In vector notation:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Pipe Contraction Problem:**

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$, so $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$
- $D_2 = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$, so $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.00785 \text{ m}^2$
- $p_1 = 100 \text{ kPa} = 100{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $V_1 = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$
- $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal pipe)
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Part (i) — Velocity at Section 2:**

Using the **1-D continuity equation**:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

$$V_2 = \dfrac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \dfrac{0.0314 \times 1.5}{0.00785}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 6.0 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part (ii) — Pressure at Section 2:**

Using **Bernoulli's equation** (horizontal pipe, so $z_1 = z_2$):

$$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Rearranging:

$$p_2 = p_1 + \dfrac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

Substituting values:

$$p_2 = 100{,}000 + \dfrac{1000}{2}\left((1.5)^2 - (6.0)^2\right)$$

$$p_2 = 100{,}000 + 500 \times (2.25 - 36.0)$$

$$p_2 = 100{,}000 + 500 \times (-33.75)$$

$$p_2 = 100{,}000 - 16{,}875$$

$$\boxed{p_2 = 83{,}125 \text{ Pa} = 83.125 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Part (iii) — Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_1 V_1 = 1000 \times 0.0314 \times 1.5$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 47.1 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Verification using section 2:** $\dot{m} = 1000 \times 0.00785 \times 6.0 = 47.1 \text{ kg/s}$ ✓ (matches!)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from Euler's equation of motion, derive Bernoulli's equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. The stagnation pressure measured is 150 kPa and the static pressure is 120 kPa. Calculate the velocity of water at the measurement point. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation:**

Refer to the **detailed derivation in Section 3.3** of these notes. The logical flow is:

1. Start with Euler's equation: $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$
2. Apply **steady flow assumption**: $\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$
3. Take the **dot product with $\vec{V}$**: $\rho \vec{V} \cdot \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = -\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p + \rho \vec{V} \cdot \vec{g}$
4. Recognize that $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = V \dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right)$, $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla p = V \dfrac{dp}{ds}$, $\vec{V} \cdot \vec{g} = -g \dfrac{dz}{ds}$
5. Divide by $\rho V$: $\dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{dp}{ds} - g\dfrac{dz}{ds}$
6. Apply **incompressibility** ($\rho = \text{constant}$): $\dfrac{d}{ds}\left(\dfrac{p}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz\right) = 0$
7. **Integrate along the streamline**: $\dfrac{p}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz = \text{constant}$

**Final Result (Bernoulli's Equation in Energy Form):**

$$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2$$

**Assumptions Made:**
1. Steady flow
2. Incompressible flow
3. Inviscid (frictionless) flow
4. Flow along a streamline
5. No heat transfer or shaft work

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Pitot-Static Tube Velocity Calculation:**

**Concept Recap:** A pitot-static tube measures both the **stagnation pressure** $p_0$ (at the opening facing the flow) and the **static pressure** $p$ (at the side opening perpendicular to flow). The point where the velocity becomes zero is the **stagnation point**.

**Given Data:**
- Stagnation pressure: $p_0 = 150 \text{ kPa} = 150{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- Static pressure: $p = 120 \text{ kPa} = 120{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between the Free Stream and Stagnation Point:**

At the free stream: pressure = $p$, velocity = $V$
At the stagnation point: pressure = $p_0$, velocity = $0$
Both points are at the same elevation.

$$\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \dfrac{p_0}{\rho g} + \dfrac{0^2}{2g} + z$$

Simplifying (since $z$ is the same):

$$\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_0}{\rho g}$$

$$\dfrac{V^2}{2g} = \dfrac{p_0 - p}{\rho g}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(p_0 - p)}{\rho}}$$

**Substituting Values:**

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(150{,}000 - 120{,}000)}{1000}}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 30{,}000}{1000}}$$

$$V = \sqrt{60}$$

$$\boxed{V = 7.746 \text{ m/s} \approx 7.75 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Bernoulli + Continuity Problems:**

1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently.** Diameter in cm must be converted to m; pressure in kPa must be converted to Pa. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.

2. **Confusing stagnation pressure and static pressure in pitot tube problems.** The pitot tube measures the *stagnation* pressure; the static tap measures the *static* pressure. The difference gives the dynamic pressure. Marks lost: **2–3 marks**.

3. **Not stating assumptions before using Bernoulli's equation.** Always list: steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline. Marks lost: **1 mark**.

4. **Using the wrong continuity equation form.** For compressible flow, use $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$; for incompressible, use $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. Marks lost: **2 marks**.

5. **Sign errors in pressure calculations.** When using $p_2 = p_1 + \dfrac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$, be careful with the sign of $(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$. Marks lost: **1 mark**.

6. **Forgetting to verify the result.** Always check that $\dot{m}$ is the same at both sections. Marks lost: **1 mark** (if your calculation is wrong but the verification would have caught it).

7. **Not writing units in the final answer.** Always write units (m/s, Pa, kg/s, etc.). Marks lost: **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Continuity Equation** = Conservation of Mass. General form has the time derivative of density and divergence of $\rho \vec{V}$. Incompressible form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$. 1-D form: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.

- [ ] **Euler's Equation** = Newton's 2nd Law for an **inviscid** fluid. The material derivative $D\vec{V}/Dt$ captures the fluid particle's acceleration. The surface force is $-\nabla p$, the body force is $\rho \vec{g}$.

- [ ] **Bernoulli's Equation** = Energy conservation along a streamline. Sum of pressure head + velocity head + elevation head = constant.

- [ ] **The Three Energy Heads:**
  - Pressure head: $\dfrac{p}{\rho g}$
  - Velocity head (kinetic head): $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$
  - Elevation head (potential head): $z$
  - **Total head:** $H = \dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$

- [ ] **Assumptions for Bernoulli's Equation** (must be stated in every answer):
  1. Steady flow
  2. Incompressible flow
  3. Inviscid (frictionless) flow
  4. Along a streamline
  5. No heat transfer / shaft work

- [ ] **Physical Interpretation:** Higher velocity → Lower pressure (in horizontal flow). This is why chimneys, venturimeters, and aerofoils work.

- [ ] **The Material Derivative** $D/Dt$ is the key concept that distinguishes fluid mechanics from solid mechanics. It includes both the local change (with time) and the convective change (with position).

- [ ] **Units to remember:**
  - Pressure $p$: Pa or N/m²
  - Density $\rho$: kg/m³
  - Specific weight $\gamma = \rho g$: N/m³
  - Kinematic viscosity $\nu$: m²/s
  - Dynamic viscosity $\mu$: Pa·s
  - Velocity $V$: m/s
  - Discharge $Q$: m³/s

- [ ] **Standard Constants for KTU Numerical Problems:**
  - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
  - $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
  - $\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
  - $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at sea level)
  - $\rho_{mercury} = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$

- [ ] **Common Applications of Bernoulli's Principle** (for 2-mark short questions):
  - Venturimeter — flow rate measurement
  - Pitot tube — velocity measurement
  - Orifice meter — flow rate measurement
  - Aerofoil lift — aircraft wings
  - Carburetor — fuel-air mixing
  - Atomizer / sprayer
  - Bunsen burner
  - Chimney draft

- [ ] **Continuity equation in cylindrical/polar coordinates** (sometimes asked in KTU):
  $$\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

- [ ] **Bernoulli's Equation in Rotating Reference Frames** (advanced): an extra term $\int \omega^2 r \, dr$ appears. Not required for KTU Module 2, but good to know.

- [ ] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do NOT confuse the **static, dynamic, and stagnation pressures**.
  - Static pressure $p$ = actual pressure of the fluid
  - Dynamic pressure $\dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ = pressure due to fluid motion
  - Stagnation pressure $p_0 = p + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ = pressure when fluid is brought to rest isentropically

---

## 5.5 Final Quick-Reference Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Sacred Equations of Module 2:**

1. **Continuity:** $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$
2. **Euler:** $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$
3. **Bernoulli:** $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$

These three equations, together with appropriate boundary conditions, form the foundation of **classical (inviscid) fluid dynamics** and are the prerequisites for all advanced topics in fluid mechanics, aerodynamics, and hydraulic machinery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring Instruments: Pitot Tube, Orificemeter, Venturimeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measuring Instruments: Pitot Tube, Orificemeter, Venturimeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In fluid mechanics and hydraulics, **flow measuring instruments** are devices used to quantify the **discharge (Q)** or **velocity (V)** of a flowing fluid by converting the kinetic energy of flow into a measurable pressure difference. The three classical instruments discussed in this module are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Venturimeter**: A converging-diverging pipe section with a carefully shaped throat (typically a 21° converging cone followed by a 5°–7° diverging cone) used to measure the discharge of an incompressible fluid in a closed conduit by applying the **Bernoulli principle** and **continuity equation** across the inlet and throat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orificemeter**: A flat, thin, sharp-edged circular plate with a concentric circular hole (orifice) installed between flanges in a pipeline. Fluid contracts downstream of the orifice to form a **vena contracta**, and the resulting pressure difference is used to compute discharge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitot Tube**: A simple L-shaped open-ended tube inserted into a flow stream such that its open end faces the flow. It measures the **stagnation (impact) pressure head**, which when compared with the **static pressure head** at the same point yields the flow velocity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Venturimeter Analogy — The Car on a Hill:**
Imagine a wide, slow-moving river narrowing into a gorge. As the water squeezes through the narrow gorge, its velocity increases dramatically (you can hear the roar). At the wide section the water is calm; at the throat it rushes. Engineers exploit this natural acceleration — the wider section has higher pressure, the narrower throat has lower pressure — and measure that pressure drop to figure out how much water is flowing. A venturimeter does exactly this inside a pipe.

**Orificemeter Analogy — The Faucet Aerator:**
When you partially block a garden hose with your thumb, water sprays out faster on the other side. The pressure drops at the constriction and the jet narrows further downstream (this is the vena contracta). An orificemeter formalises this — it's like a permanent, precise "thumb" inside a pipe.

**Pitot Tube Analogy — Your Hand Out of a Moving Car:**
Stick your hand out of a moving car window. If you angle it flat to the wind, it slips through easily (static pressure only). If you face it forward like a cup, the air pushes hard into your palm — that extra push is the stagnation pressure. The pitot tube is a calibrated "cup" facing the flow that catches the full kinetic energy of the fluid.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water at standard conditions: **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**
- Specific weight of water: **γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³**
- Typical coefficient of discharge for Venturimeter: **C_d ≈ 0.95 to 0.98**
- Typical coefficient of discharge for Orificemeter: **C_d ≈ 0.60 to 0.65**
- Typical coefficient of velocity for Pitot tube: **C_v ≈ 0.98 to 1.00**

> [!NOTE]
> All three instruments rely on the same physical foundation — **Bernoulli's equation** coupled with the **continuity equation** — but differ in geometry, accuracy, cost, and head loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Each Instrument

#### A. Pitot Tube — Velocity Measurement by Stagnation

1. The tube has an opening facing directly upstream. Fluid decelerates to zero velocity at this opening (the **stagnation point**).
2. The fluid column inside the tube rises to a height equal to the **stagnation pressure head** $\frac{P_0}{\rho g}$.
3. A separate piezometer (or static tap) measures the **static pressure head** $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ at the same depth in the flow.
4. The difference between the two readings equals the **velocity head** $\frac{V^2}{2g}$.
5. This is essentially Bernoulli's equation applied between a free-stream point and the stagnation point on the same streamline.

#### B. Venturimeter — Discharge Measurement by Pressure Drop

1. Fluid enters at the **inlet section (1)** with area $A_1$ and velocity $V_1$.
2. The cross-section contracts smoothly to the **throat (2)** with area $A_2 < A_1$, so velocity increases to $V_2$.
3. According to Bernoulli, pressure must drop as velocity rises: $P_1 > P_2$.
4. This pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ (measured by a differential manometer as head $h$) is related to velocity at the throat.
5. Continuity gives $V_1 = V_2 \frac{A_2}{A_1}$, which is substituted to eliminate $V_1$.
6. Actual discharge = theoretical discharge × coefficient of discharge $C_d$.

#### C. Orificemeter — Same Principle, Sharp-Edged Plate

1. A **sharp-edged orifice plate** is clamped between flanges. The orifice diameter $d_2$ is smaller than pipe diameter $d_1$.
2. The flow contracts sharply just downstream of the plate, reaching minimum cross-section $A_c$ at the **vena contracta** (typically located 0.5 $d_2$ downstream of the plate).
3. Beyond the vena contracta, the jet expands to fill the pipe again — but this sudden expansion causes significant **head loss** (much higher than in a venturimeter).
4. Pressure is tapped **upstream** of the plate and at the **vena contracta**, and the difference gives the discharge.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every formula you need for solving KTU university exam problems on this topic. Memorise these.

| Quantity | Pitot Tube | Venturimeter | Orificemeter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Governing principle | Bernoulli (free-stream ↔ stagnation) | Bernoulli + Continuity | Bernoulli + Continuity + Vena Contracta |
| Theoretical velocity | $V = \sqrt{2gh}$ | $V_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | $V_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ |
| Theoretical discharge | Not applicable | $Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | $Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ |
| Actual discharge | $V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$ | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ |
| Differential manometer head $h$ | $h = \dfrac{P_0 - P}{\rho g}$ | $h = x \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)$ | $h = x \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)$ |
| Typical $C_d$ range | $C_v \approx 0.98 - 1.00$ | $C_d \approx 0.95 - 0.98$ | $C_d \approx 0.60 - 0.65$ |
| Head loss | Negligible | Very low ($\approx$ 10–15% of $h$) | High ($\approx$ 40–60% of $h$) |
| Best application | Point velocity measurement | Large pipelines, permanent installations | Compact installations, low cost |

In the above table:
- $h$ = differential head across the instrument (in metres of flowing fluid)
- $x$ = differential manometer deflection reading (in metres of manometer fluid)
- $\rho$ = density of flowing fluid; $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid (mercury ≈ 13600 kg/m³, or CCl₄ ≈ 1595 kg/m³)
- $A_1, A_2$ = cross-sectional areas at inlet and throat (orifice), respectively

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Venturimeters** are the **standard discharge meter** in water treatment plants, large pumping stations, and hydroelectric installations due to their high accuracy (within ±1%) and minimal head loss.
- **Orificemeters** are ubiquitous in oil & gas pipelines, chemical processing, and HVAC systems where **cost, simplicity, and compactness** outweigh accuracy concerns.
- **Pitot tubes** are used in **aircraft airspeed indicators**, river flow gauging, chimney draft measurement, and in combination with **static pressure taps** to form **Pitot-static probes** for aerodynamic testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pitot Tube Equation

Let point **A** be a free-stream point in the undisturbed flow with velocity $V$, static pressure $P$. Let point **B** be the stagnation point at the mouth of the Pitot tube where velocity is forced to zero and pressure rises to stagnation pressure $P_0$. Both points lie on the same horizontal streamline (assuming a horizontal pitot tube; the elevation difference can be neglected for small tubes).

Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between A and B:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z_A = \frac{P_0}{\rho g} + \frac{0^2}{2g} + z_B
$$

Since $z_A = z_B$ (horizontal streamline), the elevation terms cancel:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0}{\rho g}
$$

Rearranging for $V^2$:

$$
\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0 - P}{\rho g}
$$

Define the **piezometric head difference** $h = \dfrac{P_0 - P}{\rho g}$, which is read directly from a differential manometer or twin-tube manometer connected between the stagnation tube and a static tap:

$$
V^2 = 2gh
$$

Therefore the theoretical velocity is:

$$
\boxed{V_{th} = \sqrt{2gh}}
$$

In practice, a **coefficient of velocity** $C_v$ is introduced to account for minor viscous and turbulence effects near the stagnation opening, so:

$$
\boxed{V_{actual} = C_v \sqrt{2gh}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a well-designed pitot tube, $C_v \approx 0.98$. The pitot tube is a **velocity-measuring device**, not a discharge-measuring device — to get discharge you would need to integrate the velocity profile across the entire cross-section, which is why it is rarely used for total flow in closed conduits.

### 3.2 Derivation of Venturimeter Equation

Consider a horizontal venturimeter with an **inlet section (1)** of area $A_1$ and a **throat section (2)** of area $A_2$, where $A_2 < A_1$. The fluid has pressure $P_1$, velocity $V_1$ at section 1 and pressure $P_2$, velocity $V_2$ at section 2.

**Step 1 — Apply Continuity Equation** (incompressible, steady flow):

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

Solving for $V_1$:

$$
V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation** between sections 1 and 2 (horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2$):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the velocity difference**:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

Define the **piezometric head difference** $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$, which is what a differential manometer reads:

$$
h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_1$ from continuity**:

$$
h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left( \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 V_2^2}{2g}
$$

Factor $V_2^2$ out:

$$
h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_2$ (throat velocity)**:

$$
V_2^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical discharge** through the throat:

$$
Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = A_2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Actual discharge** is obtained by multiplying by the coefficient of discharge $C_d$:

$$
\boxed{Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}}
$$

This is the **master equation** for the venturimeter.

### 3.3 Derivation of Orificemeter Equation

The orificemeter follows **exactly the same mathematical derivation** as the venturimeter because Bernoulli's and continuity equations still govern the flow between the upstream tap (section 1) and the vena contracta (section 2'). The only conceptual difference is:

- At section 2' (vena contracta), the area is $A_c = C_c \cdot A_o$, where $C_c$ is the **coefficient of contraction** and $A_o$ is the geometric orifice area.
- Because $A_c$ is smaller than $A_o$, the velocity there is higher than what $A_o$ alone would suggest.

If we denote the orifice geometric area as $A_o = A_2$, and apply the same Bernoulli + Continuity derivation, the formula for theoretical discharge through the orifice is:

$$
Q_{th} = A_o \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_c/A_1)^2}}
$$

But in practice we combine $C_c$ and $C_v$ into a single **coefficient of discharge** $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$, and approximate using $A_o$ instead of $A_c$:

$$
\boxed{Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_o \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_o/A_1)^2}}}
$$

For a sharp-edged circular orifice, $C_d \approx 0.62$, which makes the orificemeter far less accurate than the venturimeter.

### 3.4 Differential Manometer Conversion (Mercury Manometer)

If the differential pressure is measured using a U-tube mercury manometer with deflection $x$ metres:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho) g x
$$

Dividing by $\rho g$ to get the head $h$ in metres of the flowing fluid:

$$
\boxed{h = x \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right)}
$$

For mercury ($\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$) and water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$
h = x \left( \frac{13600}{1000} - 1 \right) = 12.6 \, x
$$

So a 1 cm mercury deflection represents 12.6 cm of water head — this conversion frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Pitot Tube | Venturimeter | Orificemeter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Measures | Local velocity $V$ | Total discharge $Q$ | Total discharge $Q$ |
| Pressure taps | 1 stagnation + 1 static | 2 (inlet & throat) | 2 (upstream & vena contracta) |
| Permanent head loss | Almost zero | Small ($\approx 0.1 V_2^2/2g$) | Large ($\approx 0.5 V_2^2/2g$) |
| Construction cost | Very low | High (precision machining) | Low (flat plate) |
| Length of device | Tiny (cm) | Long (≈ $10 D$) | Short ($\approx 0.5 D$) |
| Accuracy | High for point velocity | Very high (±1%) | Moderate (±2–5%) |
| Suitable for | Open channel + closed | Closed conduits only | Closed conduits only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Venturimeter (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inlet1["INLET SECTION 1"] --> conv["CONVERGING CONE 21 DEG"]
    conv --> throat2["THROAT SECTION 2 AREA A2"]
    throat2 --> div["DIVERGING CONE 5 TO 7 DEG"]
    div --> outlet1["OUTLET SECTION"]
    tap1["PRESSURE TAP 1 P1"] -.->|"Static pressure upstream"| man["DIFFERENTIAL MANOMETER"]
    tap2["PRESSURE TAP 2 P2"] -.->|"Static pressure at throat"| man
    man -->|"Reads head h = P1-P2 over rho g"| calc["DISCHARGE EQUATION"]
    calc -->|"Q = Cd A2 sqrt 2gh over sqrt 1 - A2 by A1 squared"| output["FLOW RATE Q IN M3 PER S"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of an Orificemeter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    upstream["UPSTREAM PIPE SECTION AREA A1"] --> flange1["FLANGE 1"]
    flange1 --> plate["SHARP EDGED ORIFICE PLATE AREA A0"]
    plate --> tapup["PRESSURE TAP 1 P1 UPSTREAM"]
    plate --> vena["VENA CONTRACTA AREA AC = Cc A0"]
    vena --> tapvena["PRESSURE TAP 2 P2 AT VENA CONTRACTA"]
    vena --> recovery["FLOW RECOVERY REGION"]
    recovery --> downstream["DOWNSTREAM PIPE SECTION"]
    tapup --> man["DIFFERENTIAL MANOMMER DEFLECTION X"]
    tapvena --> man
    man --> calc["HEAD H EQUALS X TIMES RHOM OVER RHO MINUS 1"]
    calc --> qfinal["DISCHARGE Q EQUALS Cd A0 SQRT 2GH OVER SQRT 1 - A0 BY A1 SQUARED"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Pitot Tube Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    freestream["FREE STREAM FLOW VELOCITY V STATIC PRESSURE P"] --> decel["DECELERATION AT TUBE OPENING"]
    decel --> stag["STAGNATION POINT VELOCITY ZERO STAGNATION PRESSURE P0"]
    stag --> risecol["RISE OF FLUID COLUMN INSIDE PITOT TUBE"]
    statictap["STATIC PRESSURE TAP PIEZOMETER"] --> rise2["RISE OF FLUID COLUMN TO STATIC HEAD P"]
    risecol --> manometer["DIFFERENTIAL MANOMETER READS DELTA H EQUALS P0 MINUS P OVER RHO G"]
    rise2 --> manometer
    manometer --> velocity["VELOCITY V EQUALS Cv SQRT 2GH"]
```

### 4.4 Side-by-Side Schematic Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PITOT["PITOT TUBE: MEASURES VELOCITY V"]
        P1["Open end faces flow"] --> P2["Stagnation pressure P0 captured"]
        P3["Static tap reads P"] --> P4["Differential h gives V = Cv sqrt 2gh"]
    end

    subgraph VENT["VENTURIMETER: MEASURES DISCHARGE Q"]
        V1["Inlet area A1"] --> V2["Smooth contraction to throat A2"]
        V2 --> V3["Bernoulli + Continuity applied"]
        V3 --> V4["Manometer reads h"]
        V4 --> V5["Q = Cd A2 sqrt 2gh over sqrt 1 - A2 by A1 squared"]
    end

    subgraph ORIF["ORIFICEMETER: MEASURES DISCHARGE Q"]
        O1["Pipe diameter D1"] --> O2["Sharp edged plate with hole D2"]
        O2 --> O3["Vena contracta forms"]
        O3 --> O4["Sudden expansion causes head loss"]
        O4 --> O5["Q = Cd A0 sqrt 2gh over sqrt 1 - A0 by A1 squared"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between a venturimeter and an orificemeter. List any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Venturimeter | Orificemeter |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gradually converging-diverging shape (21° cone at inlet, 5°–7° at outlet) | Flat sharp-edged plate with a concentric hole |
| 2 | Coefficient of discharge is high ($C_d \approx 0.95$ to $0.98$) | Coefficient of discharge is low ($C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$) |
| 3 | Head loss is small (about 10–15% of measured differential head) | Head loss is large (about 40–60% of differential head) |
| 4 | More expensive due to precision machining of cones | Cheap, simple, compact |
| 5 | Suitable for high-accuracy permanent installations | Suitable for cost-sensitive applications |

**(3 marks: 1 mark each for any three valid distinguishing points.)**

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a pitot tube? How is it used to measure the velocity of flow?**

**Model Answer:**

A **pitot tube** is an L-shaped open-ended tube inserted into a flowing fluid such that its open end faces directly upstream. At the open mouth, the fluid is brought to rest (stagnation), and the fluid column inside the tube rises to a height corresponding to the **stagnation pressure head** $P_0/\rho g$.

A second piezometer tap nearby measures the **static pressure head** $P/\rho g$ at the same point in the flow. The difference in heights gives the **velocity head**:

$$
h = \frac{P_0 - P}{\rho g}
$$

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the free stream and the stagnation point:

$$
V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}
$$

where $C_v$ is the coefficient of velocity (≈ 0.98 for a well-designed pitot tube).

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark principle, 1 mark final equation.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a venturimeter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal venturimeter with inlet diameter 30 cm and throat diameter 15 cm is used to measure the flow of water. The differential mercury manometer shows a deflection of 20 cm. Assuming $C_d = 0.98$, calculate: (i) the velocity at the throat, (ii) the discharge through the venturimeter in litres per second. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Consider a horizontal venturimeter with inlet section (1) of area $A_1$, pressure $P_1$, velocity $V_1$, and throat section (2) of area $A_2$, pressure $P_2$, velocity $V_2$.

**Continuity equation:**

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2
$$

**Bernoulli's equation (horizontal flow):**

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

Let $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$:

$$
h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

Substituting $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$:

$$
h = \frac{V_2^2 - (A_2/A_1)^2 V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]
$$

Solving:

$$
V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}
$$

Theoretical discharge:

$$
Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}
$$

Actual discharge:

$$
\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}}
$$

**(7 marks: 1 mark for continuity, 2 marks for Bernoulli, 2 marks for algebraic simplification, 1 mark for $V_2$, 1 mark for final $Q$.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**

- Inlet diameter $d_1 = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}$
- Throat diameter $d_2 = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Manometer deflection $x = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- $C_d = 0.98$
- $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (mercury), $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (water)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute areas:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Convert mercury deflection to water head:**

$$
h = x \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right) = 0.20 \times \left( \frac{13600}{1000} - 1 \right) = 0.20 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute area ratio squared:**

$$
\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{0.15}{0.30}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4 = 0.0625
$$

**Step 4 — (i) Velocity at throat:**

$$
V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} = \frac{\sqrt{49.44}}{\sqrt{0.9375}} = \frac{7.031}{0.9682} = 7.262 \text{ m/s}
$$

**$\boxed{V_2 \approx 7.26 \text{ m/s}}$**

**Step 5 — (ii) Discharge:**

$$
Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = 0.98 \times 0.01767 \times 7.262
$$

$$
Q = 0.98 \times 0.01767 \times 7.262 = 0.1258 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**$\boxed{Q \approx 0.1258 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 125.8 \text{ L/s}}$**

**(7 marks: 1 mark for area, 2 marks for manometer conversion, 1 mark for area ratio, 1 mark for velocity, 2 marks for discharge.)**

---

#### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a pitot tube. How is it used to measure the velocity of flow? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pitot tube is placed at the centre of a pipe of diameter 20 cm carrying water. The stagnation head is 7 cm of water and the static head is 3 cm of water. If the coefficient of velocity is 0.98, find: (i) the velocity of flow at the centre of the pipe, (ii) the discharge through the pipe assuming the velocity profile follows the relation $V_r = V_c \left(1 - \dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{1/n}$ with $n = 7$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Explanation:**

Refer to the Pitot Tube diagram in Section 4.3. A pitot tube is an L-shaped open tube inserted into the flow stream such that its open end faces directly upstream. At the open mouth, the fluid decelerates to zero velocity and the pressure rises to the **stagnation pressure** $P_0$. The fluid column inside the tube rises to a height $\dfrac{P_0}{\rho g}$.

A separate static pressure tap (piezometer) measures the **static pressure head** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ at the same point in the flow.

The difference $h = \dfrac{P_0 - P}{\rho g}$ is the velocity head. Applying Bernoulli's equation between the free-stream point and the stagnation point on the same horizontal streamline:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0}{\rho g} + 0
$$

Hence:

$$
V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}
$$

This is the velocity of flow at the point where the pitot tube is placed.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for principle, 1 mark for Bernoulli application, 2 marks for final formula.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**

- Pipe diameter $D = 20 \text{ cm}$, so $R = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- Stagnation head $= 7 \text{ cm}$, static head $= 3 \text{ cm}$
- Differential head $h = 7 - 3 = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $C_v = 0.98$
- Velocity profile: $V_r = V_c \left(1 - \dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{1/n}$ with $n = 7$

**Step 1 — (i) Centre-line velocity:**

$$
V_c = C_v \sqrt{2gh} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.04}
$$

$$
V_c = 0.98 \times \sqrt{0.7848} = 0.98 \times 0.8859 = 0.868 \text{ m/s}
$$

**$\boxed{V_c \approx 0.868 \text{ m/s}}$**

**Step 2 — Average velocity from velocity profile:**

For a profile of the form $V_r = V_c \left(1 - \dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{1/n}$, the ratio of average to centre-line velocity is given by:

$$
\frac{V_{avg}}{V_c} = \frac{n}{(n+1)(2n+1)} \cdot \text{(compatibility factor)}
$$

The general formula for the average velocity ratio for power-law profiles is:

$$
\frac{V_{avg}}{V_c} = \frac{2n^2}{(n+1)(2n+1)}
$$

For $n = 7$:

$$
\frac{V_{avg}}{V_c} = \frac{2 \times 7^2}{8 \times 15} = \frac{98}{120} = 0.8167
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{avg} = 0.8167 \times 0.868 = 0.709 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Discharge through the pipe:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q = A \cdot V_{avg} = 0.03142 \times 0.709 = 0.02228 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**$\boxed{Q \approx 22.28 \text{ L/s}}$**

**(7 marks: 2 marks for centre-line velocity, 2 marks for average velocity ratio, 1 mark for substitution, 2 marks for final discharge.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the area ratio term** $\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$ — many students write $V_2 = \sqrt{2gh}$ without it. This costs **2–3 marks immediately**.
> 2. **Wrong manometer conversion** — using $h = x \cdot \rho_m/\rho$ instead of $h = x(\rho_m/\rho - 1)$ loses the differential density term and gives a higher answer.
> 3. **Using diameter ratio squared instead of area ratio squared** — $(d_2/d_1)^2 \neq (A_2/A_1)^2$. You must use $(d_2/d_1)^4$ or compute the areas.
> 4. **Forgetting $C_d$** in the final discharge equation for venturimeter/orificemeter — losing **1 mark**.
> 5. **Mixing up stagnation and static heads** in pitot tube problems — these are **two separate measurements**; their difference gives the velocity head.
> 6. **Incorrect unit conversions** — diameters in cm with areas requiring m²; always convert to SI before substituting.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam**
>
> - **Three instruments, one principle**: All three use Bernoulli + Continuity to relate pressure drop to velocity.
> - **Pitot tube**: $V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$ where $h = $ (stagnation head) $-$ (static head). Measures **local point velocity**, not total discharge.
> - **Venturimeter**: $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$. Highest $C_d$ (≈ 0.98), lowest head loss.
> - **Orificemeter**: Same formula as venturimeter but $C_d \approx 0.62$ due to vena contracta and high head loss.
> - **Manometer conversion**: $h = x\left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$; for Hg–water: $h = 12.6\,x$.
> - **Continuity always first**, then Bernoulli; never the other way round.
> - **Diameter ratio power**: $(A_2/A_1)^2 = (d_2/d_1)^4$.
> - **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ for venturimeter is **higher** than for orificemeter.
> - **Order of head loss**: Orificemeter > Venturimeter > Pitot tube.
> - **Always convert units to SI** (metres, kg/m³, m/s²) before plugging into formulas.
> - **Sketch the instrument** in every long answer — KTU awards 2–3 marks for labelled diagrams.
> - **Final answer must include units** — write m³/s or L/s explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectangular and Triangular Notches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rectangular and Triangular Notches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Notch?

A **notch** is a small opening of specified shape cut in the vertical side wall of a tank or reservoir, fitted with a sharp-edged metallic plate, through which a liquid (usually water) is allowed to flow. The depth of liquid above the **sill** (bottom edge of the notch) is called the **head (H)**. The quantity of liquid flowing over the notch in unit time is determined by measuring this head.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notch vs. Weir — The Key Distinction (KTU Syllabus Highlight)**
> A **weir** is essentially a large-sized notch, often built in an open channel with a broad crest, and is used both as a measuring device and a flow-regulating barrier. A **notch** is small, sharp-edged, and always cut in a vertical wall. Weirs are used for large flows (e.g., rivers, spillways), notches for laboratory and small-channel measurements.

| Feature | Notch | Weir |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Small | Large |
| Edge | Sharp-edged metallic plate | Broad or sharp crest |
| Location | Vertical side of tank | Open channel bed |
| Head range | Small (mm to a few cm) | Large (cm to meters) |
| Use | Lab calibration | River/spillway flow |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bathroom Tap" Analogy

Imagine a bathtub with a horizontal slit cut into its vertical wall. The higher the water level above the slit, the faster the water shoots out. This is exactly the principle behind a **rectangular notch**. Now imagine replacing the slit with a triangular (V-shaped) opening — a small trickle of water creates a measurable jet, but a large flow lets a wide column out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physics**: The exit velocity of a fluid particle through an orifice (or notch) is governed by **Torricelli's Theorem**:
>
> $$v \;=\; \sqrt{2gh}$$
>
> where **g = 9.81 m/s²** is the acceleration due to gravity and **h** is the depth of the particle below the free liquid surface. The velocity head **h** drives the entire discharge calculation.

## 1.3 Types of Notches (Based on Shape)

1. **Rectangular Notch** — width *b* is constant, depth *H* is variable.
2. **Triangular (V-Notch)** — sides form a "V" of total angle **2θ**.
3. **Trapezoidal Notch** — combination of rectangular + triangular.
4. **Stepped Notch** — used in special applications.

The first two are the focus of KTU Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Principle

For *any* notch, the discharge is computed by treating the flowing sheet of water as a stack of infinitesimally thin **horizontal strips**. Each strip is treated as a small **orifice** whose theoretical velocity is found from **Torricelli's theorem**, and then corrected using a **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cd < 1?**
> The actual discharge is always less than the theoretical because of:
> 1. **Vena Contracta** — the jet contracts just after the notch, reducing the effective area.
> 2. **Surface tension and friction** along the sharp edge.
> 3. **Velocity of approach** being neglected.
>
> Typical values:
> - **Cd ≈ 0.61 to 0.66** (Rectangular Notch, with 0.62 most common)
> - **Cd ≈ 0.58 to 0.62** (Triangular Notch, with 0.60 most common)

## 2.2 Logic of the Discharging Sheet

For a notch of total head **H** above the sill:

1. Consider a horizontal strip at depth **h** below the free surface, of thickness **dh**.
2. The strip's area = (width at that depth) × dh.
3. Theoretical velocity through strip = **√(2gh)**.
4. Theoretical discharge through strip = (width) × dh × √(2gh).
5. Actual discharge = Cd × theoretical discharge.
6. **Integrate from h = 0 to h = H** to obtain the total discharge.

## 2.3 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise these two expressions — they account for **80 % of KTU problems** on this topic.

| S.No | Notch Type | Discharge Equation | Exponent of H |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rectangular Notch** | $$Q \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$ | 3/2 |
| 2 | **Triangular (V-Notch) – General** | $$Q \;=\; \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$ | 5/2 |
| 3 | **V-Notch, 2θ = 90°** (special) | $$Q \;=\; \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$ | 5/2 |
| 4 | **V-Notch, 2θ = 60°** (special) | $$Q \;=\; \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan 30° \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$ | 5/2 |

Where the symbols denote:

- **Q** = Actual discharge (m³/s)
- **Cd** = Coefficient of discharge (dimensionless)
- **b** = Width of rectangular notch (m)
- **H** = Head of water above the sill/vertex (m)
- **g** = 9.81 m/s² (acceleration due to gravity)
- **2θ** = Total angle of V-notch (degrees/radians)
- **θ** = Half-angle of V-notch

## 2.4 Comparison: Rectangular vs. Triangular Notches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite** — This comparison is asked as a 7-mark "Discuss" or "Compare" question almost every year.

| Property | Rectangular Notch | Triangular Notch |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Straight horizontal sill | V-shaped sill |
| Formula | Q ∝ H^(3/2) | Q ∝ H^(5/2) |
| Accuracy for **small heads** | Poor (low Q, errors magnified) | **Excellent** |
| Accuracy for **large heads** | Good | Lower precision at very high H |
| Range of discharge | Wide, for larger flows | Narrow, for smaller flows |
| Construction | Simpler | Slightly more involved |
| Sensitivity to H | Lower | **Higher** (H to the 5/2 power) |
| Typical Cd | 0.61 – 0.66 | 0.58 – 0.62 |
| Common application | Tanks, channels | Laboratory & precise flow metering |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Wastewater treatment plants** — rectangular notches measure flow from settling tanks.
- **Hydraulic laboratories** — V-notches give high precision for small laboratory flows.
- **Irrigation channels** — rectangular weirs (large notches) meter supply to fields.
- **Spillways of dams** — broad-crested weirs (a variant of notch theory) estimate flood discharge.
- **Chemical dosing tanks** — V-notch ensures precise volumetric flow of expensive reagents.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Discharge Over a Rectangular Notch

### Assumptions
1. The notch has a **sharp horizontal sill** at its lower edge.
2. The plate is **thin** so the jet springs clear immediately.
3. The **head H** is measured at a point sufficiently upstream where the surface is undisturbed.
4. The **velocity of approach** is negligible.
5. There are **no end contractions** (or they are accounted for in Cd).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup**

Consider a rectangular notch of width **b** cut in the vertical wall of a tank. Let the free liquid surface be at a head **H** above the sill of the notch.

**Step 2 — Consider an Elementary Horizontal Strip**

Take a thin horizontal strip of water of thickness **dh** at a depth **h** below the free surface.

Because the notch is rectangular, the width of this strip equals the notch width:

$$\text{Width of strip} \;=\; b$$

**Step 3 — Area of the Strip**

$$dA \;=\; \text{width} \times \text{thickness} \;=\; b \, dh$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Velocity (Torricelli's Theorem)**

A water particle at depth **h** below the free surface possesses a velocity:

$$v \;=\; \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Discharge Through the Strip**

$$dQ_{th} \;=\; v \cdot dA \;=\; \sqrt{2gh} \cdot b \, dh \;=\; b \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Apply Coefficient of Discharge**

The actual discharge is reduced by a factor **Cd**:

$$dQ \;=\; C_d \cdot dQ_{th} \;=\; C_d \cdot b \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 7 — Integrate Over the Full Head H**

The total discharge equals the sum (integral) of elemental discharges from the sill (h = 0) to the free surface (h = H):

$$Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{H} C_d \cdot b \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh$$

Pulling out the constants:

$$Q \;=\; C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the Integral**

Using the power rule $\int h^{1/2} \, dh = \dfrac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} = \dfrac{2}{3} h^{3/2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \;&=\; C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[\frac{2}{3} h^{3/2}\right]_{0}^{H} \\[6pt]
\;&=\; C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2} \\[6pt]
\;&=\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Rectangular Notch**
>
> $$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\, C_d\, b\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Discharge Over a Triangular (V-Notch)

### Assumptions
1. The notch is a **symmetrical V** of total vertex angle **2θ**.
2. The vertex of the V points **downward**.
3. The plate is **thin and sharp-edged**.
4. The head **H** is measured vertically above the **vertex**.
5. The **velocity of approach** is negligible.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup**

Let the half-angle of the V be **θ**, so the total angle at the vertex is **2θ**. The free surface of water lies at a head **H** above the vertex.

**Step 2 — Width of the Notch at a Depth (H − h) Above Vertex**

If we travel upward a distance **(H − h)** from the vertex, we are still below the free surface by **h**. The total horizontal width of the notch at this level is:

$$b \;=\; 2(H - h)\,\tan\theta$$

**Step 3 — Consider an Elementary Strip**

A horizontal strip of thickness **dh** at depth **h** below the free surface has area:

$$dA \;=\; b \cdot dh \;=\; 2(H - h)\,\tan\theta \cdot dh$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Velocity**

By Torricelli's theorem:

$$v \;=\; \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Discharge Through the Strip**

$$dQ_{th} \;=\; v \cdot dA \;=\; \sqrt{2gh} \cdot 2(H - h)\,\tan\theta \, dh$$

$$dQ_{th} \;=\; 2\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot (H - h)\,h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Coefficient of Discharge**

$$dQ \;=\; C_d \cdot dQ_{th} \;=\; 2\,C_d\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot (H - h)\,h^{1/2}\,dh$$

**Step 7 — Integrate from h = 0 to h = H**

$$Q \;=\; 2\,C_d\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \int_{0}^{H} (H - h)\,h^{1/2}\,dh$$

**Step 8 — Expand the Integrand**

$$(H - h)\,h^{1/2} \;=\; H\,h^{1/2} - h^{1/2} \cdot h \;=\; H\,h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$$

Therefore:

$$Q \;=\; 2\,C_d\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \int_{0}^{H} \left(H\,h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}\right) dh$$

**Step 9 — Evaluate Both Integrals**

Using $\int h^{1/2} dh = \frac{2}{3} h^{3/2}$ and $\int h^{3/2} dh = \frac{2}{5} h^{5/2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \;&=\; 2\,C_d\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[\, H \cdot \frac{2}{3}\,h^{3/2} \;-\; \frac{2}{5}\,h^{5/2} \,\right]_{0}^{H} \\[6pt]
\;&=\; 2\,C_d\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[\, \frac{2}{3}\,H \cdot H^{3/2} \;-\; \frac{2}{5}\,H^{5/2} \,\right] \\[6pt]
\;&=\; 2\,C_d\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot \left[\, \frac{2}{3} \;-\; \frac{2}{5} \,\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10 — Simplify the Bracket**

$$\frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5} \;=\; \frac{10 - 6}{15} \;=\; \frac{4}{15}$$

**Step 11 — Final Result**

$$Q \;=\; 2\,C_d\,\tan\theta \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot \frac{4}{15}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \frac{8}{15}\, C_d\, \tan\theta\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case — Right-Angled V-Notch (2θ = 90°)**
>
> When **2θ = 90°**, **θ = 45°**, and **tan 45° = 1**, hence:
>
> $$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \frac{8}{15}\, C_d\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (K1-Style Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (Not in exam — for clarity):**
> A **rectangular notch** of width **b = 1.2 m** discharges water under a head of **H = 0.3 m**. Determine the discharge if **Cd = 0.62**.

**Step 1 — Write the formula**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.3)^{3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute piece by piece**

- $\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294$ m^(1/2)/s
- $H^{3/2} = (0.3)^{1.5} = 0.3 \times \sqrt{0.3} = 0.3 \times 0.5477 = 0.1643$ m^(3/2)
- $\frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 = 0.4133$

**Step 4 — Multiply**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \;&=\; 0.4133 \times 1.2 \times 4.4294 \times 0.1643 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 0.4133 \times 1.2 \times 0.7277 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 0.4133 \times 0.8733 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 0.3610 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer: Q ≈ 0.361 m³/s ≈ 361 L/s**

---

## 3.4 Master Summary Table — KTU High-Yield Equations

| Equation Form | Use It For |
|---|---|
| $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Velocity of a particle at depth h |
| $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$ | Rectangular notch |
| $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan\theta \sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}$ | Triangular (V-) notch, general |
| $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}$ | Right-angled (90°) V-notch |
| $H = \left[\dfrac{15\,Q}{8\,C_d\,\tan\theta\,\sqrt{2g}}\right]^{2/5}$ | Head from given Q (V-notch) |
| $H = \left[\dfrac{3\,Q}{2\,C_d\,b\,\sqrt{2g}}\right]^{2/3}$ | Head from given Q (Rectangular) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Notch Discharge Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the analytical workflow used to compute discharge for any notch — a high-yield "thought-process" diagram that mirrors what a KTU topper writes on the answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Notch Type"] --> B{"Rectangular or Triangular?"}
    B -- Rectangular --> C["Read Width b and Head H"]
    B -- Triangular --> D["Read Angle 2 theta and Head H"]
    C --> E["Apply Q equals 2 by 3 times Cd times b times root 2g times H to the 3 by 2"]
    D --> F["Apply Q equals 8 by 15 times Cd times tan theta times root 2g times H to the 5 by 2"]
    E --> G["Use Cd equals 0.62 for rectangular"]
    F --> H["Use Cd equals 0.60 for triangular"]
    G --> I["Substitute g equals 9.81 m per s squared"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Compute Final Q in m cubed per s"]
    J --> K["Convert to Litres per second if required"]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic — Rectangular Notch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tank["Reservoir or Tank"]
        direction TB
        FS["Free Water Surface"]
        NOTCH["Rectangular Notch Plate"]
        SILL["Sill at the Bottom of Notch"]
        FLOW["Flowing Sheet of Water"]
    end
    H1["Head H above sill"] --> NOTCH
    B1["Width b of notch"] --> NOTCH
    STRIP["Elementary strip at depth h of thickness dh"]
    FS --> STRIP
    STRIP --> FLOW
    FLOW --> JET["Free discharging jet"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The free water surface lies at the top; the notch is cut in the side wall; the sill is at the bottom of the notch. The head **H** is the vertical distance from the sill to the free surface, and **b** is the (constant) horizontal width of the notch.

## 4.3 Geometric Schematic — Triangular (V-Notch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph VNotch["Triangular V-Notch Geometry"]
        direction TB
        TOP["Free Water Surface at Head H above vertex"]
        WIDTH["Width at Free Surface equals 2 H tan theta"]
        STRIP2["Elementary strip at depth h thickness dh"]
        WIDTH2["Width of this strip equals 2 times H minus h times tan theta"]
        VERTEX["Vertex of V notch at the bottom"]
    end
    TOP --> STRIP2
    WIDTH2 --> STRIP2
    STRIP2 --> VERTEX
    VERTEX --> JET2["Converging V shaped jet discharges"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Choosing Rectangular vs Triangular

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Need to choose a notch for flow measurement"] --> Q1{"What is the expected range of discharge?"}
    Q1 -- Large Q greater than 50 L per s --> R["Use RECTANGULAR notch"]
    Q1 -- Small Q less than 50 L per s --> T["Use TRIANGULAR V notch"]
    Q1 -- Precise measurement required --> T
    R --> RNOTE["Simple construction Cd equals 0.62 Q proportional to H to the 3 by 2"]
    T --> TNOTE["Higher accuracy at low heads Cd equals 0.60 Q proportional to H to the 5 by 2"]
```

---
