# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Define a notch. Differentiate between a notch and a weir.**

**Model Answer:**

A **notch** is a small opening of definite geometric shape cut in the vertical side of a tank, fitted with a sharp-edged metallic plate, used to measure the discharge of a liquid. The head of water above the sill is measured to compute the flow rate.

| Notch | Weir |
|---|---|
| Smaller in size | Larger in size (notch scaled up) |
| Sharp-edged metallic plate | May have a broad crest |
| Cut in a vertical side wall | Built in an open channel bed |
| Used for small flows in labs | Used for large flows in channels and rivers |
| Head range: mm to a few cm | Head range: cm to metres |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. State the formula for discharge over a rectangular notch. What is the role of the coefficient of discharge Cd?**

**Model Answer:**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\, C_d\, b\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$$

where **Q** = discharge (m³/s), **Cd** = coefficient of discharge, **b** = width of notch (m), **g** = 9.81 m/s², **H** = head over the sill (m).

**Role of Cd:** The actual discharge is always less than the theoretical discharge computed by Torricelli's theorem, because the jet contracts just past the notch (vena contracta) and suffers frictional losses at the sharp edge. The coefficient **Cd** (≈ 0.62 for rectangular notch) accounts for this reduction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting to convert units**: Head H must be in **metres** before substitution. Many students substitute H in mm and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Mixing up θ and 2θ**: In V-notch derivations, the term is **tan θ** (half-angle). Using tan 2θ is a very common error.
> - **Using g = 10 instead of 9.81**: The KTU board deducts 1 mark for this unless the question explicitly says "take g = 10 m/s²".
> - **Skipping the integration step**: Always show the integral $\int_0^H h^{1/2}\,dh = \frac{2}{3}H^{3/2}$. Half the marks of a derivation question are for the integration work.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — RECTANGULAR NOTCH

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge of water flowing over a **rectangular notch**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A rectangular notch 1.5 m wide is fixed in the side of a tank. The head of water over the notch is 300 mm. Calculate the discharge in litres per second. Take **Cd = 0.62**. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

*(Provide the full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes. Each of the following steps should appear on the answer script:)*

1. Define the geometry — width *b*, head *H*, thin strip at depth *h*, thickness *dh*.
2. Strip area: $dA = b\,dh$.
3. Theoretical velocity: $v = \sqrt{2gh}$.
4. Theoretical elemental discharge: $dQ_{th} = b\sqrt{2g}\,h^{1/2}\,dh$.
5. Actual elemental discharge: $dQ = C_d \cdot dQ_{th}$.
6. Integrate: $Q = C_d\,b\sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2}\,dh$.
7. Final result: $Q = \frac{2}{3}\,C_d\,b\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Given data**

- b = 1.5 m
- H = 300 mm = 0.3 m
- Cd = 0.62
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 2 — Formula**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{2}{3}\, C_d\, b\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting values**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.5 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.3)^{3/2}$$

**Step 4 — Piece-wise computation**

- $\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294$ m^(1/2)/s
- $H^{3/2} = 0.3 \times \sqrt{0.3} = 0.3 \times 0.5477 = 0.1643$ m^(3/2)
- $\frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 = 0.4133$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q \;&=\; 0.4133 \times 1.5 \times 4.4294 \times 0.1643 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 0.4511 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres per second**

$$Q \;=\; 0.4511 \times 1000 \;=\; 451.1 \; \text{L/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer (Q5.2A): Q ≈ 0.451 m³/s ≈ 451.1 L/s**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — TRIANGULAR (V-NOTCH)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge of water flowing over a **triangular (V-) notch**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 90° V-notch is used to measure a discharge of **25 litres per second**. Find the head of water over the notch. Take **Cd = 0.60**. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

*(Provide the full derivation as in Section 3.2 of these notes. Each of the following steps should appear on the answer script:)*

1. Define the geometry — total vertex angle **2θ**, head **H** above vertex, thin strip at depth *h*, thickness *dh*.
2. Strip width: $b = 2(H - h)\tan\theta$.
3. Strip area: $dA = 2(H - h)\tan\theta\,dh$.
4. Theoretical velocity: $v = \sqrt{2gh}$.
5. Theoretical elemental discharge: $dQ_{th} = 2\tan\theta\,\sqrt{2g}\,(H-h)\,h^{1/2}\,dh$.
6. Apply Cd and integrate from 0 to H: $Q = 2\,C_d\tan\theta\sqrt{2g}\int_0^H(H\,h^{1/2} - h^{3/2})\,dh$.
7. Evaluate the integrals: $\int_0^H H\,h^{1/2}dh = \frac{2}{3}H^{5/2}$ and $\int_0^H h^{3/2}dh = \frac{2}{5}H^{5/2}$.
8. Simplify: $Q = 2\,C_d\tan\theta\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}\left(\frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5}\right) = \frac{8}{15}\,C_d\tan\theta\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Given data**

- 2θ = 90° → θ = 45° → tan 45° = 1
- Q = 25 L/s = 0.025 m³/s
- Cd = 0.60
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 2 — Formula for a 90° V-notch**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{8}{15}\, C_d\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values**

$$0.025 \;=\; \frac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times \sqrt{19.62} \times H^{5/2}$$

$$0.025 \;=\; \frac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times 4.4294 \times H^{5/2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constant**

$$\frac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times 4.4294 \;=\; 0.5333 \times 4.4294 \;=\; 2.3625$$

**Step 5 — Solve for H^(5/2)**

$$H^{5/2} \;=\; \frac{0.025}{2.3625} \;=\; 0.01058$$

**Step 6 — Take the 2/5 power**

$$H \;=\; (0.01058)^{2/5} \;=\; (0.01058)^{0.4}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(0.01058) = -4.548$, so $0.4 \times (-4.548) = -1.8192$, and $e^{-1.8192} = 0.1622$ m.

**Step 7 — Convert to millimetres**

$$H \;=\; 0.1622 \times 1000 \;\approx\; 162.2 \; \text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer (Q5.2B): H ≈ 0.162 m ≈ 162.2 mm**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it to your wall a day before the exam.**

- ✅ **Notch** = small sharp-edged opening in a **vertical** wall; **Weir** = large version in an **open channel**.
- ✅ Always begin a notch problem by drawing a **neat labelled sketch** — this alone can earn you 1 mark in a derivation.
- ✅ **Rectangular notch formula**: $Q = \frac{2}{3}\,C_d\,b\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$, with **Cd ≈ 0.62**.
- ✅ **Triangular notch formula**: $Q = \frac{8}{15}\,C_d\tan\theta\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$, with **Cd ≈ 0.60**.
- ✅ For a **90° V-notch**, the formula reduces to $Q = \frac{8}{15}\,C_d\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ because tan 45° = 1.
- ✅ **Always** use **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless the question specifies otherwise.
- ✅ **Always** convert H to **metres** before substitution.
- ✅ The **vena contracta** phenomenon is the principal reason Cd < 1.
- ✅ **Triangular notches are preferred for small flows**; rectangular for large flows.
- ✅ The exponent **3/2** for rectangular and **5/2** for triangular is the key distinction — get this right even if you forget the coefficients.
- ✅ To find **H from Q**, raise both sides to the power **2/3** (rectangular) or **2/5** (triangular).
- ✅ The integration $\int_0^H h^{1/2}dh = \frac{2}{3}H^{3/2}$ and $\int_0^H h^{3/2}dh = \frac{2}{5}H^{5/2}$ must be shown in every derivation answer.
- ✅ In **velocity of approach problems** (rare in KTU Module 2 but possible), add a head correction: $H_a = H + \frac{v_a^2}{2g}$ where $v_a$ is the approach velocity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Qualitative Comparison between Laminar and Turbulent Boundary Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Qualitative Comparison between Laminar and Turbulent Boundary Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Boundary Layer?

The **boundary layer** is the thin region of fluid in the immediate vicinity of a solid surface where the effects of fluid viscosity (friction) are significant. The fluid velocity varies from zero at the solid surface (no-slip condition) to the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$ at the outer edge of the boundary layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prandtl's Boundary Layer Concept (1904):** For a viscous fluid flowing past a solid surface, the flow field can be broadly divided into two regions:
> 1. A **thin boundary layer** near the surface where viscous effects and velocity gradients are dominant.
> 2. A **free-stream region** outside this layer where the fluid behaves essentially as an inviscid (frictionless) flow.

The boundary layer thickness is conventionally denoted by **$\delta$**, defined as the distance from the solid surface where the local velocity $u$ reaches **$99\%$ of the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$**, i.e., $u = 0.99\,U_\infty$.

## 1.2 What is a Laminar Boundary Layer?

A **laminar boundary layer** is a boundary layer in which the fluid particles move in smooth, parallel, and orderly layers (laminae) with no macroscopic mixing between adjacent fluid layers. The flow is characterized by:

- **Low fluid velocity** relative to the free stream.
- **Low Reynolds number** ($\text{Re}_x < 5 \times 10^5$ for a flat plate).
- **Steady, predictable streamlines**.
- Molecular-level (viscous) momentum transfer dominates.

## 1.3 What is a Turbulent Boundary Layer?

A **turbulent boundary layer** is a boundary layer in which fluid particles move in chaotic, random, three-dimensional eddies and vortices. There is vigorous macroscopic mixing between fluid layers. The flow is characterized by:

- **High fluid velocity** relative to the free stream.
- **High Reynolds number** ($\text{Re}_x > 5 \times 10^5$ for a flat plate).
- **Fluctuating velocity components** superimposed on the mean flow.
- Momentum transfer is dominated by **Reynolds stresses** (turbulent shear), which are typically orders of magnitude larger than viscous shear.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍯

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy – Honey vs. Stirred Water:**
> Imagine slowly pouring a thin stream of **honey** down a tilted glass plate. The honey flows in smooth, glassy layers that barely mix — this is a **laminar** boundary layer. Now, replace the honey with **fast-flowing water** in a river over a rough rock. The water near the rock surface churns, swirls, and eddies violently — this is a **turbulent** boundary layer. The same principle applies to air flowing over an aircraft wing: at the leading edge the boundary layer is **laminar**, but as it grows downstream, it transitions to **turbulent**.

**Key take-away:** The **Reynolds number** is the master "switch" between laminar and turbulent behavior. Low $\text{Re}$ → orderly (laminar); high $\text{Re}$ → chaotic (turbulent).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physics of the Two Boundary Layer Regimes

### 🔹 Laminar Boundary Layer Characteristics

- Fluid particles glide in **smooth, parallel layers** (laminae).
- Fluid motion is **2-D, steady, and deterministic** at the macroscopic level.
- **Viscous (molecular) shear stress** $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ is the dominant momentum transfer mechanism.
- The **velocity profile** is smooth, well-ordered, and typically follows the **Blasius exact solution** (similar in shape to an error function).
- The boundary layer **grows slowly** along the surface — proportional to $\sqrt{x}$.
- **Skin friction drag is lower** than in turbulent flow at the same $\text{Re}_L$.
- Susceptible to **boundary layer separation** (because the near-wall velocity gradient can become zero or negative under adverse pressure gradients).

### 🔹 Turbulent Boundary Layer Characteristics

- Fluid particles exhibit **chaotic, random, 3-D motion** with eddies of many sizes.
- Fluid motion has **strong fluctuating components** $u'$, $v'$, $w'$ on top of the mean flow.
- **Reynolds (turbulent) shear stress** $\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$ dominates; viscous shear is negligible by comparison.
- The **velocity profile is fuller and flatter** — well described empirically by the **1/7th Power Law**.
- The boundary layer **grows much faster** — proportional to $x^{4/5}$.
- **Skin friction drag is significantly higher** (typically 3–5× the laminar value at the same $\text{Re}_L$).
- **More resistant to separation** under adverse pressure gradients due to higher near-wall momentum.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Laminar Boundary Layer | Turbulent Boundary Layer |
|---|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Reynolds Number Criterion | $\text{Re}_x < \text{Re}_{x,\text{crit}} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ | $\text{Re}_x > \text{Re}_{x,\text{crit}} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ |
| 2 | Boundary Layer Thickness $\delta$ | $\delta = \dfrac{5.0\,x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$ (approx.) | $\delta = \dfrac{0.37\,x}{(\text{Re}_x)^{1/5}}$ |
| 3 | Growth Law | $\delta \propto x^{1/2}$ | $\delta \propto x^{4/5}$ |
| 4 | Velocity Profile (empirical) | Blasius exact / $u/U_\infty = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$ for low Re | $\dfrac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\dfrac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$ (1/7th Power Law) |
| 5 | Local Skin Friction Coeff. $C_f$ | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.664}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$ | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{0.0592}{(\text{Re}_x)^{1/5}}$ |
| 6 | Average Skin Friction $C_D$ | $C_D = \dfrac{1.328}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}}$ | $C_D = \dfrac{0.074}{(\text{Re}_L)^{1/5}}$ |
| 7 | Wall Shear Stress $\tau_w$ | $\tau_w = 0.332\,\mu U_\infty / x \cdot \sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$ | $\tau_w = 0.0288\,\rho U_\infty^{2} / (\text{Re}_x)^{1/5}$ |
| 8 | Dominant Momentum Transfer | Molecular Viscosity ($\mu$) | Reynolds Stresses ($-\rho \overline{u'v'}$) |
| 9 | Energy Dissipation | Low | Very High |
| 10 | Separation Tendency | High (under adverse $\,dP/dx$) | Low (resists separation) |
| 11 | Shape of Velocity Profile | Sharp gradient near wall, thin layer | Flatter, "fuller" profile |
| 12 | Thickness of Lamina Sub-layer | Not defined (no turbulence) | $y^+ < 5$ (viscous sublayer) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (SI):** $x, y, \delta$ in m; $U_\infty$ in m/s; $\mu$ in N·s/m²; $\rho$ in kg/m³; $\tau_w$ in N/m²; $C_f$ is dimensionless.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Aerospace:** Designers *deliberately trip* the boundary layer to turbulent (using vortex generators) on aircraft wings to delay stall, even though drag increases.
> - **Automotive:** Car bodies are shaped to maintain laminar flow as long as possible to reduce drag and improve fuel efficiency.
> - **Pipelines:** Flow inside pipes starts laminar; engineers size pumps accounting for transition to turbulent regime.
> - **Wind turbines & Turbomachinery:** Blade surfaces are designed with boundary layer control to manage lift/drag trade-offs.
> - **Heat Exchangers:** Turbulent boundary layers enhance convective heat transfer (used in cooling systems).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boundary Layer Thickness for Laminar Flow (Blasius Solution)

Starting from the **boundary layer equation** for steady, 2-D, incompressible flow over a flat plate:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

For a flat plate, the pressure gradient in the free stream is zero ($\partial p / \partial x = 0$). The equation reduces to:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

Blasius introduced the **similarity variable** $\eta$ to obtain a self-similar solution:

$$\eta = y \sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}}$$

After substantial algebraic manipulation (solving the Blasius ODE numerically), the dimensionless velocity profile $f'(\eta) = u / U_\infty$ is obtained. The boundary layer thickness is defined by the conventional criterion $u = 0.99\,U_\infty$, which yields $\eta \approx 5.0$. Hence:

$$y = \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta = \delta \sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}} \approx 5.0$$

Solving for $\delta$:

$$\delta \sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}} = 5.0$$

$$\delta = 5.0 \sqrt{\frac{\nu x}{U_\infty}}$$

Substituting $\text{Re}_x = U_\infty x / \nu$:

$$\delta = \frac{5.0\,x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The laminar boundary layer grows as $\sqrt{x}$ — i.e., it thickens slowly with downstream distance because viscous effects diffuse momentum only weakly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Boundary Layer Thickness for Turbulent Flow (1/7th Power Law)

Assume the velocity profile follows the **empirical 1/7th power law**:

$$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

Substitute this into the **von Kármán momentum integral equation** for a zero pressure gradient:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^{2}} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

where $\theta$ is the **momentum thickness**, defined as:

$$\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta} \frac{u}{U_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$

### Step 1: Evaluate the momentum thickness

Substitute $u/U_\infty = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$:

$$\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta} \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7} \left[1 - \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}\right] dy$$

Let $\xi = y/\delta \;\Rightarrow\; dy = \delta\,d\xi$:

$$\theta = \delta \int_{0}^{1} \xi^{1/7} \left(1 - \xi^{1/7}\right) d\xi$$

$$\theta = \delta \int_{0}^{1} \left(\xi^{1/7} - \xi^{2/7}\right) d\xi$$

$$\theta = \delta \left[\frac{\xi^{8/7}}{8/7} - \frac{\xi^{9/7}}{9/7}\right]_{0}^{1}$$

$$\theta = \delta \left[\frac{7}{8} - \frac{7}{9}\right] = \delta \left[\frac{63 - 56}{72}\right] = \frac{7\,\delta}{72}$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the wall shear stress

For a turbulent flow, the wall shear stress is modeled as:

$$\tau_w = 0.0225\,\rho U_\infty^{2} \left(\frac{\nu}{U_\infty \delta}\right)^{1/4}$$

(This is the **Prandtl mixing-length based expression** with constants from the 1/7th power law assumption.)

### Step 3: Substitute into the momentum integral equation

$$0.0225\,\rho U_\infty^{2} \left(\frac{\nu}{U_\infty \delta}\right)^{1/4} = \rho U_\infty^{2} \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

Substitute $\theta = 7\delta/72$:

$$0.0225 \left(\frac{\nu}{U_\infty \delta}\right)^{1/4} = \frac{7}{72} \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

Rearrange:

$$\delta^{1/4}\,d\delta = \frac{0.0225 \times 72}{7} \left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{1/4} dx$$

$$\delta^{1/4}\,d\delta = 0.2314 \left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{1/4} dx$$

Integrate from $0$ to $x$ (assuming $\delta = 0$ at $x = 0$):

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} \delta^{1/4}\,d\delta = 0.2314 \left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{1/4} \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

$$\frac{\delta^{5/4}}{5/4} = 0.2314 \left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{1/4} x$$

$$\delta^{5/4} = 0.2893 \left(\frac{U_\infty x}{\nu}\right)^{1/4} x$$

$$\delta^{5/4} = 0.2893\, x^{5/4} \left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{1/4}$$

Raise both sides to the power $4/5$:

$$\delta = 0.2893^{4/5}\, x \left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{1/5}$$

$$\delta = 0.37\, x \left(\frac{\nu}{U_\infty x}\right)^{1/5}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{0.37\,x}{(\text{Re}_x)^{1/5}}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The turbulent boundary layer grows as $x^{4/5}$ — much faster than the laminar case ($\sqrt{x}$). This rapid growth is due to vigorous eddy mixing that spreads momentum outward aggressively.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Quantitative)

| Property | Laminar | Turbulent | Ratio (Turb/Lam) at $\text{Re}_L = 10^{7}$ |
|----------|---------|-----------|----------------------------------------------|
| Boundary layer thickness $\delta$ | $\delta_L = 5.0\,L / \sqrt{\text{Re}_L}$ | $\delta_T = 0.37\,L / (\text{Re}_L)^{1/5}$ | $\delta_T / \delta_L \approx 1.48$ |
| Avg. skin friction coeff. $C_D$ | $C_{D,L} = 1.328 / \sqrt{\text{Re}_L}$ | $C_{D,T} = 0.074 / (\text{Re}_L)^{1/5}$ | $C_{D,T} / C_{D,L} \approx 3.3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from the matrix:** A turbulent boundary layer is **thicker** AND produces **3–5× more drag** than a laminar one at the same Reynolds number. This is why engineers strive to maintain laminar flow on streamlined bodies.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Side-by-Side Boundary Layer Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FLAT PLATE Leading Edge] --> B[Bypass Air Stream U_inf]
    A --> C[LAMINAR BOUNDARY LAYER]
    A --> D[TRANSITION POINT Re_x_crit approx 5e5]
    D --> E[TURBULENT BOUNDARY LAYER]

    subgraph lam[CHARACTERISTICS LAMINAR]
        C1[Velocity Profile: Smooth and Parabolic]
        C2[Growth: delta proportional to sqrt_x]
        C3[Dominant Force: Molecular Viscosity]
        C4[Drag: Lower]
        C5[Separation: Highly Prone]
    end

    subgraph tur[CHARACTERISTICS TURBULENT]
        D1[Velocity Profile: Flatter 1 by 7th Power Law]
        D2[Growth: delta proportional to x power 4 by 5]
        D3[Dominant Force: Reynolds Stresses]
        D4[Drag: Higher 3 to 5 times]
        D5[Separation: Resists Separation]
    end

    C --> lam
    E --> tur

    lam --> OUT[ENGINEERING OUTCOME]
    tur --> OUT
    OUT[Low drag efficient flow but separation risk] --> G1[Aircraft Wings early sections Submarines]
    OUT[High drag intense mixing late sections] --> G2[Heat exchangers Turbine blades]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Boundary Layer Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Stream U_inf] -->|Impinges on plate| B[Viscous Region near wall]
    B --> C{Laminar or Turbulent ?}
    C -->|Re_x less than 5e5| D1[Laminar BL]
    C -->|Re_x greater than 5e5| D2[Turbulent BL]

    D1 --> E1[Thin delta proportional to sqrt_x]
    D1 --> E2[u varies smoothly]
    D1 --> E3[tau_w mu du_by_dy]

    D2 --> F1[Thick delta proportional to x power 4 by 5]
    D2 --> F2[u varies steeply near wall]
    D2 --> F3[tau_w contains rho u_prime v_prime bar]

    E1 --> G[SKIN FRICTION DRAG]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G

    G --> H[Fluid Resistance on the surface]
```

U_inf ────────────────          U_inf ────────────────
        |       |                         |       |

        |       |                 __/     |       |     \__
        |    ___|              __/        |       |        \__
        |  /                   /          |       |          
        |/                    /           |       |          
   ─────|─── surface         /____________|       |__________
                            ↑                       ↑
                     y = δ_L (thin)         y = δ_T (thick, fuller)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the terms: (i) Boundary layer thickness, (ii) Laminar boundary layer, (iii) Turbulent boundary layer.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Boundary layer thickness ($\delta$):** The distance from the solid surface measured in the $y$-direction at which the local velocity $u$ reaches $99\%$ of the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$. It marks the outer edge of the region where viscous effects are significant.

(ii) **Laminar boundary layer:** A boundary layer in which the fluid particles move in smooth, orderly, parallel layers (laminae) with no macroscopic mixing. Momentum transfer is governed by molecular viscosity alone, and $\text{Re}_x < 5 \times 10^{5}$ (typical critical value on a flat plate).

(iii) **Turbulent boundary layer:** A boundary layer characterized by chaotic, random, 3-D eddies and intense macroscopic mixing. Momentum transfer is dominated by **Reynolds stresses** ($-\rho\,\overline{u'v'}$), and $\text{Re}_x > 5 \times 10^{5}$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four differences between laminar and turbulent boundary layers.*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Feature | Laminar | Turbulent |
|-------|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Velocity profile | Smooth, parabolic | Flatter, 1/7th power law |
| 2 | Growth rate | $\delta \propto \sqrt{x}$ | $\delta \propto x^{4/5}$ |
| 3 | Skin friction drag | Lower | Higher (3–5×) |
| 4 | Separation tendency | High (under adverse $\,dP/dx$) | Low (resists separation) |
| 5 | Dominant shear | Molecular viscosity | Reynolds stresses |
| 6 | Heat/mass transfer | Low | High |

*(Any four valid differences earn full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice – KTU ESE Pattern)

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** *With the help of neat sketches, explain the development of laminar and turbulent boundary layers over a flat plate. Clearly indicate the laminar, transition, and turbulent regions. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Air at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ flows over a smooth flat plate with a free-stream velocity of $15\,\text{m/s}$. The plate length is $2\,\text{m}$. Calculate:*
*(i) The laminar boundary layer thickness at the trailing edge of the plate.*
*(ii) The local skin friction coefficient at the trailing edge.*
*(iii) The average drag coefficient for the entire plate (assuming fully laminar flow). (7 marks)*

**Take:** $\nu_{\text{air}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A flat plate aligned with a uniform free-stream flow $U_\infty$ experiences the following along its length $L$:

- **At the leading edge** ($x = 0$): the boundary layer thickness is infinitesimally small; the flow is intrinsically laminar.
- **Laminar Region** ($0 < x < x_c$): The BL grows as $\delta \propto \sqrt{x}$. Streamlines are smooth and parallel.
- **Transition Region** ($x_c < x < x_t$): Disturbances amplify; the flow becomes unstable.
- **Turbulent Region** ($x > x_t$): The BL grows rapidly as $\delta \propto x^{4/5}$ with violent mixing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sketch should clearly show: the velocity vectors approaching $U_\infty$, the BL thickness $\delta$ growing with $x$, and the velocity profiles at different $x$-stations (parabolic for laminar, fuller for turbulent).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $U_\infty = 15\,\text{m/s}$, $L = 2\,\text{m}$, $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$.

**Step 1: Compute $\text{Re}_L$ to verify laminar assumption.**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{15 \times 2}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2 \times 10^{6}$$

Since $\text{Re}_L = 2 \times 10^{6} > 5 \times 10^{5}$, the flow actually transitions to turbulent. However, the question specifies **assume fully laminar** — so we proceed with laminar formulas.

**Step 2: (i) Boundary layer thickness at $x = L$**

Using the Blasius result:

$$\delta = \frac{5.0\,L}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}} = \frac{5.0 \times 2}{\sqrt{2 \times 10^{6}}} = \frac{10}{1414.21}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 7.07 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 7.07\,\text{mm}}$$

**Step 3: (ii) Local skin friction coefficient at $x = L$**

$$C_{f,L} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{2 \times 10^{6}}} = \frac{0.664}{1414.21}$$

$$\boxed{C_{f,L} \approx 4.69 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 4: (iii) Average drag coefficient for the plate**

$$C_D = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}} = \frac{1.328}{1414.21}$$

$$\boxed{C_D \approx 9.39 \times 10^{-4}}$$

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the boundary layer thickness over a flat plate for a turbulent boundary layer using the 1/7th power law velocity profile. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A ship is $80\,\text{m}$ long and moves through seawater at $8\,\text{m/s}$. Assuming turbulent boundary layer from the leading edge, calculate:*
*(i) The turbulent boundary layer thickness at the stern.*
*(ii) The total skin friction drag on the hull (assume wetted area $A = 1200\,\text{m}^{2}$ and $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$). (7 marks)*

**Take:** $\nu_{\text{seawater}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

(Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.2** above. Step-by-step:)

1. Assume $u/U_\infty = (y/\delta)^{1/7}$.
2. Compute momentum thickness: $\theta = 7\delta/72$.
3. Use von Kármán integral equation: $\tau_w / \rho U_\infty^2 = d\theta / dx$.
4. Use Prandtl's wall shear expression: $\tau_w = 0.0225\,\rho U_\infty^2 (\nu / U_\infty \delta)^{1/4}$.
5. Solve the resulting ODE to obtain:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{0.37\,x}{(\text{Re}_x)^{1/5}}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $L = 80\,\text{m}$, $U_\infty = 8\,\text{m/s}$, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$, $A = 1200\,\text{m}^{2}$, $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$.

**Step 1: Compute $\text{Re}_L$**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{8 \times 80}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.4 \times 10^{8}$$

This is well into the turbulent regime ✓

**Step 2: (i) Boundary layer thickness at stern**

$$\delta = \frac{0.37\,L}{(\text{Re}_L)^{1/5}} = \frac{0.37 \times 80}{(6.4 \times 10^{8})^{1/5}}$$

Compute $(6.4 \times 10^{8})^{1/5}$:

$$\log_{10}(6.4 \times 10^{8}) = 8.806 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{value} = 10^{8.806/5} = 10^{1.7612} \approx 57.74$$

$$\delta = \frac{29.6}{57.74} \approx 0.513\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.513\,\text{m} = 51.3\,\text{cm}}$$

**Step 3: Average skin friction coefficient**

$$C_D = \frac{0.074}{(\text{Re}_L)^{1/5}} = \frac{0.074}{57.74}$$

$$\boxed{C_D \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4: Total skin friction drag**

$$F_D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,U_\infty^{2}\,A$$

$$F_D = (1.28 \times 10^{-3}) \times \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (8)^{2} \times 1200$$

$$F_D = (1.28 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.5 \times 1025 \times 64 \times 1200$$

$$F_D = (1.28 \times 10^{-3}) \times 39{,}360{,}000$$

$$\boxed{F_D \approx 50{,}380\,\text{N} \approx 50.4\,\text{kN}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up formulas:** A common mistake is using the laminar $\delta$ formula for a turbulent BL. Always check the **Re first**!
> 2. **Forgetting the "from leading edge" assumption:** The given formulas are valid only when the boundary layer starts as laminar (or turbulent) **at the leading edge**. For mixed BL, use a blended formula.
> 3. **Using $\nu$ in $\text{cm}^{2}\text{/s}$:** Always convert kinematic viscosity to **SI units** ($\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$). $\nu_{\text{water}} \approx 10^{-6}$, $\nu_{\text{air}} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$.
> 4. **Sketching errors:** In Part B(a) sketches, students often draw the velocity profile for laminar as triangular — it should be **curved (parabolic-like)**.
> 5. **Unit mismatch in drag:** Always express drag in **N** (or kN), not in kgf.
> 6. **Sign convention:** Reynolds stress $-\rho \overline{u'v'}$ has a **negative sign**; students often drop it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Boundary Layer** = thin region near a surface where viscosity matters ($\delta$: where $u = 0.99\,U_\infty$).
- ⭐ **Laminar BL** → smooth layers, $\delta \propto \sqrt{x}$, $\tau_w$ from molecular viscosity.
- ⭐ **Turbulent BL** → chaotic eddies, $\delta \propto x^{4/5}$, $\tau_w$ from Reynolds stresses.
- ⭐ **Critical $\text{Re}_x$** on flat plate $\approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ (transition point).
- ⭐ **Laminar formulas:** $\delta = 5x/\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$, $C_f = 0.664/\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$, $C_D = 1.328/\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}$.
- ⭐ **Turbulent formulas (1/7th Power Law):** $\delta = 0.37x/(\text{Re}_x)^{1/5}$, $C_f = 0.0592/(\text{Re}_x)^{1/5}$, $C_D = 0.074/(\text{Re}_L)^{1/5}$.
- ⭐ **Velocity profile:** Blasius (laminar) vs 1/7th Power Law (turbulent).
- ⭐ **Drag comparison:** Turbulent skin friction is **3–5× higher** than laminar at same $\text{Re}$.
- ⭐ **Separation:** Laminar BL separates easily; turbulent BL resists separation.
- ⭐ **Engineering implication:** Maintain laminar flow on streamlined bodies; induce turbulence where heat transfer or delay of separation is desired.
- ⭐ **Remember the sign of Reynolds stress** $-\rho\,\overline{u'v'}$ (it's negative because $\overline{u'v'} < 0$).
- ⭐ **Units check:** $\text{Re}$ is dimensionless; $\delta$ in m; $C_f$ dimensionless; $F_D$ in N.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Layer Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boundary Layer Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Boundary Layer Separation** is a fundamental fluid dynamics phenomenon in which the flow within the boundary layer detaches or "separates" from the solid surface over which it is flowing, instead of continuing to flow along the contour of the surface. Mathematically, separation is defined as the point where the velocity gradient at the wall becomes zero, i.e.,

$$\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

Beyond this point, the velocity gradient becomes negative, causing a reverse flow near the wall that results in the formation of eddies, wake, and significantly increased drag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Boundary layer separation occurs only under conditions of **adverse pressure gradient** (pressure increasing in the direction of flow), which decelerates the fluid particles within the boundary layer to the point where they cannot overcome the rising pressure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a cyclist riding up a steadily inclining hill (an adverse pressure gradient). As the hill gets steeper, the cyclist slows down. At a critical point, the cyclist comes to a stop and begins rolling backward — they have "separated" from the forward motion. Similarly, fluid particles in a boundary layer lose kinetic energy when pushed against a rising pressure. When their momentum is insufficient to climb the "pressure hill," they stop and reverse direction, causing the entire boundary layer to lift off the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Highlighted in Bold:**
> - **Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_{cr}$):** Typically $5 \times 10^5$ for flow over a flat plate (transition from laminar to turbulent).
> - **Wall Shear Stress ($\tau_w$):** Becomes **zero** at the exact point of separation.
> - **Displacement Thickness ($\delta^*$):** Increases sharply downstream of separation due to wake formation.
> - **Shape Factor ($H$):** The ratio $\delta^*/\theta$ exceeds **2.6** in separated flows, compared to about **2.0** for attached laminar flow.

## 1.3 Prerequisites to Understand Separation

Before diving deeper, ensure clarity on the following foundational concepts:

- **Boundary Layer:** The thin region adjacent to a solid surface where viscous effects dominate and velocity varies from zero (at the wall, no-slip condition) to the free-stream value.
- **Pressure Gradient:** The rate of change of pressure along the direction of flow, $\frac{dp}{dx}$.
- **Favorable Pressure Gradient:** $dp/dx < 0$ — accelerates the flow, suppresses separation.
- **Adverse Pressure Gradient:** $dp/dx > 0$ — decelerates the flow, promotes separation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Mechanism of Separation

The phenomenon of boundary layer separation is governed by an interplay between inertial forces, pressure forces, and viscous forces. Below is the structured logical breakdown of the process:

- **Step 1: Inception of Boundary Layer Flow**
  Fluid approaches a solid surface, and a boundary layer begins to develop from the leading edge. The fluid within this layer possesses kinetic energy proportional to $\frac{1}{2}\rho u^2$.

- **Step 2: Encounter with Adverse Pressure Gradient**
  When the body geometry causes the cross-sectional flow area to diverge (e.g., diffuser, rear half of an airfoil), the free-stream pressure rises in the direction of flow. This creates a positive pressure gradient, $dp/dx > 0$.

- **Step 3: Deceleration of Boundary Layer Particles**
  Fluid particles near the wall are already slower than the free-stream due to viscous retardation. The adverse pressure gradient imposes a retarding force, $F_p = -\frac{dp}{dx}$, that further decelerates these near-wall particles.

- **Step 4: Velocity Profile Inflection**
  The velocity profile progressively flattens near the wall. The gradient $\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ at the wall decreases and eventually reaches zero. This is the **separation point**.

- **Step 5: Reverse Flow and Vortex Formation**
  Beyond separation, the near-wall fluid reverses direction, creating recirculation eddies and a turbulent wake. The wall shear stress becomes zero and then negative, indicating reversed flow.

- **Step 6: Wake Development**
  Downstream of separation, the wake grows thicker, causing significant form drag (pressure drag) and energy loss due to viscous dissipation.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Separation:** The boundary layer has finite momentum. When external conditions (geometry-induced pressure rise) demand more energy than the boundary layer can supply, the layer can no longer remain attached and physically lifts off.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$ | Condition for boundary layer separation at the wall | $s^{-1}$ |
| $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$ | Adverse pressure gradient condition | $Pa/m$ |
| $\frac{dp}{dx} < 0$ | Favorable pressure gradient (no separation) | $Pa/m$ |
| $\tau_w = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0}$ | Newton's law of viscosity for wall shear stress | $Pa$ or $N/m^2$ |
| $\tau_w = 0$ | Wall shear stress at the point of separation | $Pa$ |
| $H = \frac{\delta^*}{\theta}$ | Shape factor (H increases sharply with separation) | dimensionless |
| $C_D = C_{D,\text{pressure}} + C_{D,\text{friction}}$ | Total drag coefficient decomposition | dimensionless |
| $C_p = 1 - \left( \frac{u}{U_\infty} \right)^2$ | Pressure coefficient (Bernoulli-based) | dimensionless |
| $\delta \approx \frac{5x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Turbulent boundary layer thickness | $m$ |
| $\delta \approx \frac{4.91x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Laminar boundary layer thickness (Blasius) | $m$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In markdown tables, all absolute values, divisions, and conditional separators are written using LaTeX commands like `\vert`, `\mid`, `\frac{}{}` to preserve the markdown table structure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Boundary layer separation is a central concern in many engineering domains:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Designers shape airfoils to delay or eliminate separation to maximize lift and minimize drag. Trailing-edge flaps and slats manipulate the pressure gradient.
- **Automotive Industry:** Car bodies are streamlined to control the location of separation and reduce aerodynamic drag, improving fuel efficiency.
- **Turbomachinery:** Diffusers in centrifugal pumps and compressors suffer from separation if the divergence angle exceeds roughly $7^\circ$ for walls and $11^\circ$ for conical diffusers.
- **Civil Engineering (Wind Loading):** Tall buildings and bridges must be designed to mitigate vortex-induced vibrations caused by separated flow.
- **Marine Engineering:** Ship hulls are shaped to control separation and reduce form drag, lowering fuel consumption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mathematical Condition for Separation

The starting point is the boundary layer momentum equation (von Kármán integral form) for steady, two-dimensional, incompressible flow:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dp}{dx} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

**Step 1:** At the wall, $y = 0$ and the no-slip condition gives $u = 0$, $v = 0$. The convective acceleration terms vanish, simplifying the equation to:

$$0 = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dp}{dx} + \nu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0}$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging to isolate the velocity curvature at the wall:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the adverse pressure gradient condition, $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$. Therefore:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0} > 0$$

This means the velocity profile is **concave upward** at the wall, indicating a velocity profile that is flattening as it approaches the wall.

**Step 4:** As $dp/dx$ increases further, the profile flattens completely, and the velocity gradient at the wall becomes zero. This is the **separation point**:

$$\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Using Newton's law of viscosity, the wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

This confirms the physical criterion: separation occurs where the wall shear stress is zero, transitioning to negative values downstream.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Gradient Required to Cause Separation

Consider a linear adverse pressure gradient along a flat surface. For a self-similar velocity profile in the form:

$$u(y) = U_\infty \sin\left( \frac{\pi y}{2\delta} \right)$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate with respect to $y$ to find the velocity gradient:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\pi U_\infty}{2\delta} \cos\left( \frac{\pi y}{2\delta} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate at the wall, $y = 0$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = \frac{\pi U_\infty}{2\delta}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the separation condition, set this to zero. Since $U_\infty$ and $\delta$ are non-zero, the only way to satisfy the condition is if the function or its evaluation allows it. At $y = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$, so the velocity gradient is positive. To make it zero, the boundary layer thickness must grow, or the pressure gradient must increase until the profile is linear.

**Step 4:** Setting up a balance: at the separation point, the velocity profile near the wall is linear:

$$u(y) \approx \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} y = 0 \quad \text{at separation}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the x-momentum equation at the wall to find the critical pressure gradient:

$$\frac{dp}{dx}\bigg|_{cr} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

This is the limiting case. The pressure gradient just beyond separation is positive and growing, leading to reverse flow.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Conditions Favoring or Suppressing Separation

| Condition | Effect on Separation | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Adverse pressure gradient $dp/dx > 0$ | **Promotes** separation | Diffusers, rear of bluff bodies |
| Favorable pressure gradient $dp/dx < 0$ | **Suppresses** separation | Nozzles, front of streamlined bodies |
| Increased free-stream velocity $U_\infty$ | **Delays** separation (more momentum) | Higher Reynolds number operation |
| Increased viscosity $\mu$ | **Promotes** separation (more retardation) | Highly viscous fluids (oil) |
| Turbulent boundary layer | **Delays** separation (better momentum mixing) | Use of turbulators, vortex generators |
| Smooth, gradual divergence angle | **Delays** separation | Optimal diffuser design |
| Sharp geometric discontinuity | **Triggers** separation | Sudden expansion, abrupt bends |

## 3.4 Methods to Control or Prevent Boundary Layer Separation

The following techniques are widely used in engineering practice to manipulate boundary layer behavior:

- **Streamlining the Body Shape:** Gradual curvature reduces the magnitude of the adverse pressure gradient. Streamlined bodies like airfoils, fish, and birds exemplify this principle.
- **Use of Vortex Generators:** Small fins placed on the surface trip the boundary layer into turbulence, increasing its momentum and energy, thereby delaying separation.
- **Boundary Layer Suction:** Removing slow-moving fluid near the wall through porous surfaces re-energizes the boundary layer.
- **Boundary Layer Blowing:** Injecting high-velocity fluid tangentially along the wall adds momentum to the near-wall region.
- **Diffuser Angle Optimization:** Limiting divergence angles to less than $7^\circ$ for parallel-walled diffusers and less than $11^\circ$ for conical diffusers to maintain attached flow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Separation Mechanism

The following diagram maps the cause-and-effect chain of boundary layer separation in a structured flow chart, making the abstract process visually concrete.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID APPROACHES SURFACE] --> B[BOUNDARY LAYER DEVELOPS]
    B --> C[GEOMETRY INDUCES ADVERSE PRESSURE GRADIENT]
    C --> D{IS dp BY dx GREATER THAN 0}
    D -- NO --> E[FLOW ACCELERATES OR REMAINS ATTACHED]
    D -- YES --> F[NEAR WALL VELOCITY DECREASES]
    F --> G[VELOCITY GRADIENT AT WALL DECREASES]
    G --> H{GRADIENT REACHES ZERO}
    H -- NO --> I[BOUNDARY LAYER STILL ATTACHED]
    H -- YES --> J[SEPARATION POINT OCCURS]
    J --> K[REVERSE FLOW NEAR WALL]
    K --> L[EDDY AND WAKE FORMATION]
    L --> M[INCREASED FORM DRAG AND ENERGY LOSS]
    M --> N[ENGINEERING MITIGATION NEEDED]
    N --> O[STREAMLINING OR VORTEX GENERATORS OR SUCTION OR BLOWING]
    O --> P[SEPARATION DELAYED OR ELIMINATED]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: Velocity Profile Evolution

The following diagram illustrates the evolution of the velocity profile from a fully attached state to a fully separated state, with three critical milestones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FULLY ATTACHED PROFILE] -- PRESSURE RISES --> B[INFLECTION POINT FORMS]
    B -- GRADIENT APPROACHES ZERO --> C[SEPARATION POINT]
    C -- REVERSE FLOW --> D[FULLY SEPARATED WAKE PROFILE]
    A -.-> A1[du BY dy AT WALL IS POSITIVE]
    B -.-> B1[VELOCITY PROFILE NEAR WALL IS FLATTENING]
    C -.-> C1[du BY dy AT WALL IS ZERO]
    D -.-> D1[NEGATIVE NEAR WALL VELOCITY GRADIENT]
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow: Separation Control Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SEPARATION IDENTIFIED] --> B[SELECT CONTROL METHOD]
    B --> C{GEOMETRY BASED}
    B --> D{ACTIVE CONTROL}
    C --> E[STREAMLINING THE SHAPE]
    C --> F[GRADUAL DIFFUSER ANGLES]
    D --> G[BOUNDARY LAYER SUCTION]
    D --> H[BOUNDARY LAYER BLOWING]
    E --> I[SEPARATION DELAYED]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[IMPROVED AERODYNAMIC EFFICIENCY]
    I --> K[REDUCED FORM DRAG]
    I --> L[ENHANCED LIFT OR THRUST]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Use Block Diagrams Here?** A Mermaid physical drawing of streamline curvature and wake formation is extremely complex to render as a textual diagram. The block-level functional architecture above provides a clearer mental model of cause and effect for exam preparation and conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define boundary layer separation. State the necessary condition for it. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Boundary layer separation is the phenomenon in which the fluid flow within the boundary layer detaches from a solid surface, instead of continuing to flow along the contour of the surface. This typically occurs when the velocity gradient at the wall becomes zero due to an adverse pressure gradient.

The necessary condition is the presence of an adverse pressure gradient, expressed as:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$$

At the exact point of separation, the wall shear stress becomes zero:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

This loss of wall friction causes the near-wall fluid to reverse direction, forming a wake and eddies downstream.

---

### Question 2: List any four methods to control boundary layer separation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The following four methods are commonly used in engineering practice to delay or prevent boundary layer separation:

- **Streamlining the Body:** Shaping the body so that the adverse pressure gradient develops gradually, avoiding steep pressure rises.
- **Vortex Generators:** Small fins placed on the surface to trip the boundary layer into turbulence, increasing its resistance to separation.
- **Boundary Layer Suction:** Removing slow-moving fluid through porous surfaces to re-energize the boundary layer.
- **Boundary Layer Blowing:** Injecting high-velocity fluid tangentially along the wall to add momentum to the near-wall region.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to state both the gradient condition AND the pressure condition**. Always mention $\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$ **and** $dp/dx > 0$ for full marks.
> - Drawing velocity profile diagrams is **mandatory** in KTU board exams. A 14-mark question with no diagram typically loses **2 to 3 marks**.
> - Avoid confusing "favorable" with "adverse" pressure gradient. Use the phrase **"pressure increasing in the direction of flow"** if uncertain.
> - Mention **wall shear stress becoming zero** as an alternate but equivalent criterion. Examiners award bonus credit for multiple valid criteria.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a): Explain the phenomenon of boundary layer separation with neat velocity profile diagrams. State and derive the mathematical conditions under which separation occurs. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The boundary layer is a thin fluid region adjacent to a solid surface where the velocity varies from zero at the wall (no-slip condition) to the free-stream value $U_\infty$. When this layer encounters an adverse pressure gradient, the near-wall fluid decelerates and may eventually reverse direction, causing the layer to lift off the surface. This is known as boundary layer separation.

**Velocity Profile Diagrams (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

- **Before Separation:** Velocity gradient at the wall is positive, profile is concave near the wall, shear stress is positive.
- **At Separation Point:** Velocity gradient at the wall is zero, profile is linear near the wall, shear stress is zero.
- **After Separation:** Velocity gradient at the wall is negative, reverse flow exists near the wall, profile has inflection.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Starting from the boundary layer x-momentum equation:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dp}{dx} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

At the wall ($y = 0$), the no-slip condition gives $u = 0$ and $v = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dp}{dx} + \nu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0}$$

Rearranging:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

For an adverse pressure gradient, $dp/dx > 0$, hence the velocity profile is concave upward at the wall. As the adverse gradient intensifies, the velocity gradient at the wall approaches zero. The separation condition is:

$$\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

Equivalent statement in terms of wall shear stress:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

**Part (b): A diffuser has a divergence half-angle of $5^\circ$. The free-stream velocity is $20 \, m/s$ and the pressure gradient is $dp/dx = 250 \, Pa/m$. The density and viscosity of air are $1.2 \, kg/m^3$ and $1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s$ respectively. Determine whether separation will occur. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

- Divergence half-angle $\theta = 5^\circ$
- Free-stream velocity $U_\infty = 20 \, m/s$
- Pressure gradient $dp/dx = 250 \, Pa/m$
- Density $\rho = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s$

**Step 1:** Apply the separation criterion equation. The condition derived earlier is:

$$\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the right-hand side:

$$\frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{250}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.389 \times 10^{7} \, s^{-2} / m$$

**Step 3:** Since $dp/dx > 0$, the curvature of the velocity profile at the wall is positive. The profile is flattening at the wall.

**Step 4:** Check the divergence angle. For a parallel-walled diffuser, the safe divergence angle to avoid separation is approximately $7^\circ$. Since the given half-angle is $5^\circ$, well within the safe limit.

**Step 5:** For a more precise check, we use the relation for the critical pressure gradient in a flat plate boundary layer approximation. The local wall shear stress for turbulent flow can be estimated as:

$$\tau_w \approx 0.0288 \, \rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_x^{-0.2}$$

For the critical value at separation, $\tau_w = 0$, so this gives an indication that the flow can tolerate some adverse gradient.

**Step 6:** Given the moderate pressure gradient and small divergence angle, **separation will NOT occur** in this diffuser. The flow remains attached, and the diffuser operates efficiently.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a): With the help of a sketch, discuss the mechanism of boundary layer separation over a curved surface. Explain how the phenomenon of separation leads to the formation of a wake. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Separation over a Curved Surface:**

When fluid flows over a curved surface, the geometry of the body influences the pressure distribution along the flow direction. Consider a circular cylinder or an airfoil:

- **Front (Favorable Region):** The cross-section of the flow is converging, so by continuity, the free-stream velocity increases. By Bernoulli's principle, the pressure decreases. The boundary layer is thin, energetic, and firmly attached to the surface.

- **Rear (Adverse Region):** The flow cross-section diverges, the free-stream velocity decreases, and the pressure rises. This is the **adverse pressure gradient region**.

**Sketch Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

A circular cylinder with the following key features:
- **Stagnation point** at the front (velocity zero, pressure maximum).
- **Attached boundary layer** on the front half with streamlines following the contour.
- **Separation point** on the rear half where the boundary layer detaches.
- **Wake region** downstream of the separation point with recirculating eddies.
- **Free-stream streamline** that diverges away from the surface beyond the separation point.

**Wake Formation Mechanism:**

Once separation occurs, the boundary layer can no longer follow the curved surface. The near-wall fluid reverses and rolls up into vortices that detach into the wake. The wake is a low-pressure, low-velocity, highly turbulent region that:

- Creates a **pressure differential** between the front and rear of the body, contributing to **form drag**.
- Consumes kinetic energy through **viscous dissipation**.
- Generates **vortex shedding** at characteristic Strouhal numbers, causing periodic forces on the body.

**Mathematical Justification:**

The pressure differential between the front stagnation point and the rear wake is:

$$\Delta p = p_{\text{front}} - p_{\text{wake}} > 0$$

This net pressure imbalance, integrated over the body's projected area, gives the form drag:

$$F_D = \int_A \Delta p \, dA$$

**Part (b): An airfoil has a free-stream velocity of $50 \, m/s$ in air of density $1.225 \, kg/m^3$. The maximum velocity over the airfoil is $65 \, m/s$. Calculate the pressure coefficient at the point of maximum velocity. If separation occurs at a location where the velocity gradient is just zero, what is the local wall shear stress? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

- Free-stream velocity $U_\infty = 50 \, m/s$
- Maximum velocity $u_{\max} = 65 \, m/s$
- Air density $\rho = 1.225 \, kg/m^3$

**Step 1:** Calculate the pressure coefficient at the point of maximum velocity using the Bernoulli-based formula:

$$C_p = 1 - \left( \frac{u}{U_\infty} \right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$C_p = 1 - \left( \frac{65}{50} \right)^2 = 1 - \left( 1.3 \right)^2 = 1 - 1.69 = -0.69$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. A negative pressure coefficient indicates a **suction effect** at the upper surface of the airfoil, which is responsible for generating lift. The value $-0.69$ is typical of well-designed airfoils at moderate angles of attack.

**Step 4:** Wall shear stress at the point of separation. By the separation condition:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$$

Since the velocity gradient at the wall is zero at the separation point, the wall shear stress is **exactly zero**.

**Step 5:** Practical implication. Beyond this point, the wall shear stress becomes negative, indicating reverse flow. This causes the boundary layer to thicken dramatically, form eddies, and contribute to stall in the airfoil.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**

- **Definition:** Boundary layer separation is the detachment of the boundary layer from a solid surface due to an adverse pressure gradient.
- **Primary Condition:** $dp/dx > 0$ (adverse pressure gradient) is necessary for separation.
- **Mathematical Condition at Wall:** $\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0$ at the separation point.
- **Wall Shear Stress:** $\tau_w = 0$ at the separation point; $\tau_w < 0$ beyond separation.
- **Curvature Condition:** $\left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right)_{y=0} = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dp}{dx}$; for adverse gradient, profile is concave upward.
- **Favorable Gradient:** $dp/dx < 0$ — flow accelerates, no separation.
- **Turbulent Boundary Layer:** Delays separation due to enhanced momentum exchange.
- **Control Methods:** Streamlining, vortex generators, suction, blowing, optimal diffuser angles.
- **Diffuser Limit:** Half-angle $\leq 7^\circ$ for parallel-walled diffusers; $\leq 11^\circ$ for conical diffusers.
- **Engineering Impact:** Causes increased form drag, energy loss, stall in airfoils, performance loss in diffusers.
- **Shape Factor $H$:** Exceeds $2.6$ in separated flows; equals $2.0$ for attached laminar flow.
- **Reynolds Number:** Higher $Re$ delays separation due to greater boundary layer momentum.
- **High-Yield Keywords for Theory:** Adverse pressure gradient, wall shear stress, velocity gradient, vortex shedding, wake, form drag, momentum thickness, displacement thickness.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing boundary layer separation with boundary layer transition — they are entirely different phenomena. Separation is geometric/pressure-driven; transition is viscous/flow-stability-driven.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Jet on Fixed and Moving Vertical Flat Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact of Jet on Fixed and Moving Vertical Flat Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Impact of Jets** refers to the dynamic force exerted by a fluid jet when it strikes a surface (stationary or moving) and undergoes a change in momentum. This phenomenon is governed by **Newton's Second Law of Motion**, which states that the net force acting on a body equals the rate of change of its linear momentum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** "When a jet of fluid strikes a plate, the force exerted by the jet on the plate is equal to the rate of change of momentum of the jet in the direction of the plate."

The mathematical basis relies on the **Impulse-Momentum Theorem**:

$$F = \dot{m} \times \Delta V = \rho A V (V_{initial} - V_{final})$$

Where the symbols have their standard fluid mechanics meaning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing on a beach holding a high-pressure water hose pointed directly at a large, flat sandcastle wall:

- If the wall is **immovable (fixed)**, the water slaps the wall and splashes sideways. You feel the hose "kick back" with full force. The wall absorbs 100% of the jet's perpendicular momentum.
- If the wall is mounted on **frictionless wheels and rolling away** from the hose at speed *u*, the water strikes the wall *less hard* (relative velocity is *V – u*). Some of the jet's energy is now transferred to the wall as **useful mechanical work** (the wall accelerates and moves).

This is the exact principle that drives **Pelton Wheel turbines** in hydroelectric power stations — the jet strikes curved buckets (moving plates) and transfers energy to rotate the turbine.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Density of water (ρ) | **1000 kg/m³** (SI Standard) |
| Density of water (ρ) | **9810 N/m³** (when using weight as force) |
| Acceleration due to gravity (g) | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Standard jet velocity range | **10 m/s to 100 m/s** (typical in Pelton wheels) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Vertical Flat Plates specifically?** KTU examiners emphasize *vertical* plates because the jet strikes **perpendicular** (90°) to the plate surface. This guarantees **maximum momentum change** along the axis of the jet — a configuration with the highest achievable force and cleanest mathematical derivation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### Case 1: Fixed Vertical Flat Plate

1. A jet of water with cross-sectional area *A* and velocity *V* travels in a straight horizontal line.
2. It strikes a **stationary** vertical plate placed perpendicular to its path.
3. The plate is rigid; it does not move. So the water cannot push the plate forward.
4. After striking, the water splashes along the surface of the plate — its velocity **perpendicular to the jet** becomes **zero**.
5. Only the **momentum in the direction of the jet** is destroyed.
6. By Newton's Second Law, the destructive force on the water (and equal-and-opposite reaction force on the plate) equals the rate of change of momentum.

### Case 2: Moving Vertical Flat Plate (Moving in the Direction of the Jet)

1. The plate is now mounted on frictionless rails and is moving away from the nozzle with velocity *u* (*u < V*).
2. The jet still travels at absolute velocity *V*, but the *relative* velocity at which it strikes the plate is **(V – u)**.
3. The mass of water that *actually* hits the plate per second is reduced: $\dot{m} = \rho A (V - u)$ — because some water "misses" the plate as the plate recedes.
4. The water still comes to rest relative to the plate (splashes sideways).
5. The force is now less (because relative velocity is less), but this force is sustained over a distance as the plate moves — generating **work**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Force (F) | Work Done / sec (W) | Efficiency (η) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fixed Vertical Flat Plate | $\rho A V^{2}$ | **0** (no displacement) | **0 %** |
| 2 | Moving Vertical Flat Plate (in jet direction) | $\rho A (V-u)^{2}$ | $\rho A (V-u)^{2} \cdot u$ | $\dfrac{2u(V-u)^{2}}{V^{3}}$ |
| 3 | Maximum Efficiency Condition | $u = \dfrac{V}{3}$ | — | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{8}{27} \approx 29.63\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder for KTU Exams:** Use **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** and force in **Newtons (N)**, velocity in **m/s**, area in **m²**. Mixing units (e.g., using ρ = 9810 N/m³ in momentum equations) is a common mistake — *do not* use weight density in the momentum equation; it is strictly mass density.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pelton Wheel Turbines (Hydropower):** Curved buckets act as moving plates; impact of jet extracts rotational energy.
- **Firefighting Nozzles:** Reaction force of the jet on the firefighter is the recoil (a safety design parameter).
- **Rocket Propulsion:** Thrust = mass flow × exhaust velocity — the exact opposite reaction principle.
- **Ship Propeller Design:** Blade angle is optimized using the moving-plate impact theory to maximize thrust efficiency.
- **Water Jet Cutting Machines:** Force on the workpiece must be known for precision cutting pressures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force on a Fixed Vertical Flat Plate

**Given:**
- Jet velocity = $V$ m/s
- Jet cross-sectional area = $A$ m²
- Fluid density = $\rho$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Mass of fluid striking the plate per second:**

The volume of fluid striking per second is equal to the jet's cross-sectional area multiplied by its velocity (volume swept per unit time):

$$\dot{V} = A \times V$$

Therefore, the mass striking per second is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times \dot{V} = \rho A V$$

**Step 2 — Initial and final velocities of the jet in the direction of motion:**

- Initial velocity (in direction of jet) = $V$ m/s
- Final velocity (in direction of jet) = $0$ m/s (water deflects sideways along the plate)

**Step 3 — Change in velocity:**

$$\Delta V = V_{initial} - V_{final} = V - 0 = V$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law (Force = rate of change of momentum):**

$$F = \dot{m} \times \Delta V$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$F = (\rho A V) \times (V)$$

$$\boxed{F_{fixed} = \rho A V^{2} \;\; \text{Newtons}}$$

This is the **normal force exerted by the jet on the fixed vertical plate** along the axis of the jet. By Newton's Third Law, the jet exerts this same force on the plate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Force on a Moving Vertical Flat Plate

**Given:**
- Jet velocity = $V$ m/s (absolute)
- Plate velocity = $u$ m/s (in the same direction as jet, with $u < V$)
- Jet cross-sectional area = $A$ m²
- Fluid density = $\rho$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Relative velocity of the jet with respect to the plate:**

Since the plate is moving *away* from the nozzle at velocity *u*, the relative velocity at which the jet strikes the plate is:

$$V_{rel} = V - u$$

**Step 2 — Mass of fluid actually striking the plate per second:**

Because the plate is receding, only a fraction of the original jet stream catches the plate. The effective volume rate of striking water is $A(V-u)$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A (V - u)$$

**Step 3 — Change in velocity of water (relative to plate):**

The water comes to rest relative to the plate (splashes sideways along the plate's surface). So:

$$\Delta V_{rel} = (V - u) - 0 = (V - u)$$

**Step 4 — Force exerted on the moving plate:**

$$F = \dot{m} \times \Delta V_{rel} = \rho A (V - u) \times (V - u)$$

$$\boxed{F_{moving} = \rho A (V - u)^{2} \;\; \text{Newtons}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Done per Second by the Moving Plate

**Step 1 — The plate moves with velocity *u* against the force *F*.**

Work done per second (Power) is:

$$W = F \times u$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expression for *F*:**

$$W = \rho A (V - u)^{2} \times u$$

$$\boxed{W = \rho A u (V - u)^{2} \;\; \text{Watts}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Efficiency of the Moving Plate

**Step 1 — Define efficiency as the ratio of useful work output to the kinetic energy supplied by the jet per second.**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Work done per second on plate}}{\text{Kinetic energy of jet per second}}$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy of the jet per second:**

$$KE_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} (\rho A V) V^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute both expressions:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\rho A u (V - u)^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{2 u (V - u)^{2}}{V^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — For maximum efficiency, differentiate η with respect to u and set to zero.**

Let $x = \dfrac{u}{V}$, so $u = xV$ and $(V-u) = V(1-x)$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{2 (xV) [V(1-x)]^{2}}{V^{3}} = 2x(1-x)^{2}$$

Differentiate with respect to *x*:

$$\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 2(1-x)^{2} + 2x \cdot 2(1-x)(-1) = 2(1-x)[(1-x) - 2x] = 2(1-x)(1-3x)$$

Setting $\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$:

$$2(1-x)(1-3x) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 1 \;\; \text{or} \;\; x = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

Discarding $x = 1$ (which would mean the plate escapes the jet, so no work is done):

$$\boxed{u = \dfrac{V}{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum efficiency value:**

$$\eta_{max} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{4}{9} = \dfrac{8}{27}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 0.2963 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 29.63\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This maximum efficiency of **29.63%** is a *theoretical upper limit* for a single flat plate. Theoretically, curved/scoop plates can achieve **~100%** efficiency, which is why **Pelton wheel buckets are double-cup shaped**, not flat.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
