# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: A jet of water of diameter 75 mm strikes a fixed vertical plate with a velocity of 20 m/s. Calculate the force exerted by the jet on the plate. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³.**

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- Diameter, d = 75 mm = 0.075 m
- Velocity, V = 20 m/s
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³

Area of jet:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^{2} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

Force on fixed plate:

$$F = \rho A V^{2} = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (20)^{2}$$

$$F = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 400 = 1767.1 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 1767.15 \text{ N} \;\; (\text{or } 1.767 \text{ kN})}$$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: State the condition for maximum efficiency of a jet striking a moving vertical flat plate. What is the value of this maximum efficiency?**

**Model Answer:**

For a moving vertical flat plate, the efficiency is given by:

$$\eta = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^{2}}{V^{3}}$$

Differentiating with respect to *u* and equating to zero gives the condition for maximum efficiency:

$$\boxed{u = \dfrac{V}{3}}$$

i.e., the plate must move at **one-third** of the jet velocity.

Substituting $u = V/3$ back into the efficiency expression:

$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (V/3) \cdot (V - V/3)^{2}}{V^{3}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (1/3) \cdot (2/3)^{2}}{1} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{4}{9} = \dfrac{8}{27}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \dfrac{8}{27} = 0.2963 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 29.63\%}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the force exerted by a jet of water on a fixed vertical flat plate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A jet of water 50 mm in diameter moving with a velocity of 30 m/s strikes a vertical plate moving away from the jet with a velocity of 12 m/s. Find: (i) Force exerted on the plate, (ii) Work done per second, (iii) Efficiency of the plate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. Outline the derivation:

1. Define mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A V$
2. Identify initial velocity (V) and final velocity (0 in jet direction)
3. Apply Newton's second law: $F = \dot{m} \times \Delta V$
4. Arrive at $F = \rho A V^{2}$

Mention the assumptions: jet is steady, incompressible, plate is smooth, water splashes tangentially (perpendicular component = 0).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- d = 50 mm = 0.05 m, so $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^{2} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- V = 30 m/s, u = 12 m/s
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³

**(i) Force on moving plate:**

$$F = \rho A (V - u)^{2} = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times (30 - 12)^{2}$$

$$F = 1000 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 324 = 636.17 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 636.17 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Work done per second:**

$$W = F \times u = 636.17 \times 12 = 7634.07 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 7.634 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Efficiency of the plate:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^{2}}{V^{3}} = \dfrac{2 \times 12 \times (18)^{2}}{(30)^{3}}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{24 \times 324}{27000} = \dfrac{7776}{27000} = 0.288$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 28.8\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students use weight density (9810 N/m³) instead of mass density (1000 kg/m³) in the momentum equation. This gives a **dimensional error** and zero marks for the formula. *Always use ρ = 1000 kg/m³ for momentum-based force problems.*
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to subtract the plate velocity *u* from the jet velocity *V*. Using V instead of (V−u) in the moving plate formula is the single most frequent error — penalize **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Computing efficiency as (W / V²) instead of (W / KE of jet). Always divide by $\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** A jet of water having a velocity of 25 m/s strikes a moving vertical plate at the center. The plate is moving in the direction of the jet with a velocity of 8 m/s. The jet diameter is 60 mm. Determine: (i) The force exerted by the jet on the plate, (ii) The power developed, (iii) The efficiency of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive an expression for the work done per second on a moving vertical flat plate by a water jet. Also derive the condition for maximum efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:**
- V = 25 m/s, u = 8 m/s, d = 60 mm = 0.06 m

Area of jet:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.06)^{2} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**(i) Force on plate:**

$$F = \rho A (V - u)^{2} = 1000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times (25 - 8)^{2}$$

$$F = 1000 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 289 = 817.05 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 817.05 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Power developed:**

$$W = F \times u = 817.05 \times 8 = 6536.4 \text{ W} \approx 6.536 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^{2}}{V^{3}} = \dfrac{2 \times 8 \times (17)^{2}}{(25)^{3}} = \dfrac{16 \times 289}{15625} = \dfrac{4624}{15625} = 0.2960$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 29.60\%}$$

**Note:** This is very close to the theoretical maximum (29.63%) because *u/V* = 8/25 = 0.32, which is just slightly above the optimum 1/3 = 0.333.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Refer to **Section 3.3 and 3.4** of these notes. The examiner expects:

1. A clear sketch showing the jet, plate, and velocity vectors.
2. Identification of relative velocity $(V - u)$.
3. Mass of water striking per second = $\rho A (V - u)$.
4. Force = $\rho A (V - u)^{2}$.
5. Work per second = $F \times u = \rho A u (V - u)^{2}$.
6. KE of jet per second = $\frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$.
7. Efficiency $\eta = \frac{2u(V-u)^{2}}{V^{3}}$.
8. Setting $\frac{d\eta}{du} = 0 \Rightarrow u = V/3$ and $\eta_{max} = 8/27 \approx 29.63\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** In the derivation part (b), students often write $F = \rho A V (V-u)$ instead of $F = \rho A (V-u)^{2}$. The correct expression comes from **mass × relative velocity** = $\rho A(V-u) \times (V-u)$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In efficiency derivation, students forget that differentiation is with respect to *u* (not *V*, which is constant for a given jet). Marks lost on this step: **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to mention that at $u = V/3$, the maximum efficiency is $8/27$. Just writing $u = V/3$ without computing $\eta_{max}$ loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- **Force on fixed plate:** $F = \rho A V^{2}$
- **Force on moving plate:** $F = \rho A (V - u)^{2}$
- **Work done per second on moving plate:** $W = \rho A u (V - u)^{2}$
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^{2}}{V^{3}}$
- **Condition for max efficiency:** $u = V/3$
- **Maximum efficiency value:** $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{8}{27} = 29.63\%$

### Critical Assumptions
- Jet is **steady, incompressible, and continuous**
- Plate is **smooth and rigid**
- Water **splashes tangentially** along the plate (no rebound back into the jet)
- Friction losses are **negligible**
- Plate motion is **in the same direction as the jet**

### Common Numerical Values
- ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³** (NOT 9810 N/m³ in momentum equations)
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- η_max = 29.63% (memorize this number — it appears in nearly every KTU question paper)

### Frequently Confused Points
- **Fixed plate** → Force exists, work = 0, efficiency = 0
- **Moving plate at u = V/3** → Force exists, work exists, efficiency = MAX (29.63%)
- **Moving plate at u = V** → Plate escapes jet, force = 0, work = 0, efficiency = 0
- **Moving plate at u = 0** → Reduces to fixed plate case

### KTU Exam Tip
In part (a) derivations, always start with the **mass flow rate equation** and the **momentum change equation** separately, then substitute. This earns full marks even if the final step has a minor arithmetic slip. Avoid writing directly $F = \rho A V^2$ without derivation — KTU awards marks for **method**, not just the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Jet on Curved Vanes - Fixed and Moving, Velocity Triangles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact of Jet on Curved Vanes - Fixed and Moving, Velocity Triangles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impact of Jet:** The impact of a fluid jet refers to the dynamic force exerted by a high-velocity fluid stream (typically water) when it strikes a stationary or moving solid surface (vane). The force is a direct consequence of the **change in momentum** of the fluid jet as per Newton's Second Law of Motion.

**Curved Vane:** A curved (non-planar) guiding surface shaped to gradually deflect the fluid jet through an angle, thereby extracting maximum kinetic energy from the jet while minimizing shock losses and turbulence.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is foundational for understanding **hydraulic turbines** (Pelton wheel) and **hydraulic pumps** (centrifugal pump), where curved vanes are used to convert kinetic energy into rotational mechanical energy.

**Classification of Impact Problems:**

| Class | Description | Tangential Motion |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| Fixed Single Vane | One stationary vane | $u = 0$ |
| Fixed Series of Vanes | Multiple stationary vanes on ring | $u = 0$ |
| Moving Single Vane | One vane in translational motion | $u \neq 0$ |
| Moving Series of Vanes | Multiple vanes rotating as a wheel | $u \neq 0$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Wind-Blown Windmill:**
> Imagine standing still and feeling the force of wind hitting a curved metal blade (like a stationary wind scoop). The wind changes direction, and you feel a push — that push is the **impact force** of a jet on a **fixed curved vane**. Now imagine that blade is mounted on a rotating shaft of a windmill. The wind hits it, changes direction along the curve, and in doing so, **transfers energy**, spinning the shaft. This is the impact of a jet on a **moving curved vane** — exactly the principle behind a **Pelton Wheel** used in hydroelectric power plants in Kerala's Idukki district.

**Physical Interpretation:**
- When water issues from a nozzle as a high-velocity jet, it carries **linear momentum** = mass flow rate × velocity = $\rho a V^2$ (where $a$ is jet area).
- When this jet is abruptly turned by a vane, the **direction of velocity changes**, but the magnitude may remain nearly constant (if frictionless).
- A change in velocity = change in momentum = **Force × time**.
- Therefore, the **rate of change of momentum** gives the **force** exerted on the vane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Curved" and not "Flat"?**
> A flat plate causes an abrupt 90° reversal, producing massive turbulence and shock loss (kinetic energy wasted as noise/heat). A **smoothly curved vane** (typically a circular arc) deflects the jet gradually through an angle $\theta$, so the **outlet velocity is directed away from the inlet path**, and most kinetic energy can be harnessed as useful work.

---

## 1.3 Assumptions Standard in KTU Problems

The following assumptions are used unless explicitly stated otherwise in the problem statement:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. Jet issues from a **circular nozzle** — area $a = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$.
> 2. **Frictionless** flow along the vane surface (no energy loss between inlet and outlet).
> 3. **Atmospheric pressure** exists everywhere on the vane surface (gauge pressure = 0).
> 4. Jet velocity $V$ at inlet equals outlet velocity relative to vane (magnitude).
> 5. Flow is **steady**, **incompressible**, and **one-dimensional** at inlet/outlet sections.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Velocity Triangles — The Heart of Moving Vane Analysis

The **velocity triangle** is a graphical vector decomposition that resolves the absolute, relative, and tangential velocities of the jet with respect to the moving vane. This is the single most important tool in turbine and pump analysis.

### The Three Velocity Vectors

Let:
- $V$ = Absolute velocity of jet (velocity of jet w.r.t. ground/earth)
- $u$ = Velocity of vane (tangential velocity of vane at the point of impact)
- $V_r$ = Relative velocity of jet w.r.t. vane (velocity of jet as seen by an observer moving with the vane)
- $\alpha$ = Angle made by absolute velocity $V$ with the direction of vane motion $u$
- $\beta$ = Angle made by relative velocity $V_r$ with the direction of vane motion $u$ (in opposite sense)
- $\theta$ = Blade angle (angle subtended by the vane at its center of curvature)

### Inlet Velocity Triangle

At the **inlet**, the jet approaches the moving vane. The vector relationship is:

$$\vec{V_1} = \vec{u} + \vec{V_{r1}}$$

For a typical KTU problem where the jet is directed **tangentially** to the wheel (Pelton-type), $\alpha_1 \approx 0°$ for a single jet striking the vane tip. However, for a more general scenario:

**Magnitude of relative velocity at inlet:**
$$V_{r1}^2 = V_1^2 + u_1^2 - 2 V_1 u_1 \cos\alpha_1$$

### Outlet Velocity Triangle

At the **outlet**, the jet leaves the vane after being deflected. The relative velocity $V_{r2}$ makes an angle $\beta_2$ with the direction of $u$:

$$\vec{V_2} = \vec{u} + \vec{V_{r2}}$$

**Magnitude of absolute velocity at outlet:**
$$V_2^2 = u^2 + V_{r2}^2 - 2 u V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - Velocity component in **direction of vane motion** is taken as **positive**.
> - Velocity component **opposite** to vane motion is taken as **negative**.
> - For a jet that has been turned backward (typical Pelton wheel), $V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$ acts opposite to $u$, hence negative in the tangential momentum equation.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam mandatory** for PCMET303. Memorize these before any numerical problem.

### A. Fixed Single Curved Vane

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Mass flow rate of jet | $\dot{m} = \rho a V = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2 \cdot V$ | kg/s |
| 2 | Force in direction of jet (axial) | $F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ | N |
| 3 | Force perpendicular to jet | $F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$ | N |
| 4 | Resultant force on vane | $F_R = \rho a V^2 \sqrt{(1-\cos\theta)^2 + \sin^2\theta} = 2\rho a V^2 \sin(\theta/2)$ | N |
| 5 | Work done | $W = 0$ (vane is fixed) | J/s |

> **Derivation Hint for $F_R$:** Using identity $(1-\cos\theta)^2 + \sin^2\theta = 2 - 2\cos\theta = 4\sin^2(\theta/2)$, so $\sqrt{\cdot} = 2\sin(\theta/2)$.

### B. Fixed Series of Vanes (Continuous Power Generation)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Force on each vane | $F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ | N |
| 2 | Mass striking per second | $\dot{m} = \rho a V$ | kg/s |
| 3 | Work done per second per vane | $W = 0$ | J/s |
| 4 | Use case | Stationary deflectors, splitter vanes | — |

### C. Moving Single Vane

For a vane moving with velocity $u$ in the **same direction** as the jet:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Mass striking vane per sec | $\dot{m} = \rho a (V - u)$ | kg/s |
| 2 | Force on vane in direction of motion | $F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ | N |
| 3 | Work done per second | $P = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V-u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ | W |
| 4 | Work done per unit weight | $W = \dfrac{u(V-u)^2}{g}$ (for $\theta = 180°$, $W = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^2}{g}$) | m |

### D. Moving Series of Vanes (Pelton Wheel Principle)

For a wheel with multiple vanes rotating with tangential velocity $u$:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Mass flow rate striking | $\dot{m} = \rho a V$ (jet total) | kg/s |
| 2 | Mass striking per second per vane | $\dot{m}_{vane} = \rho a (V - u)$ | kg/s |
| 3 | Force on each vane in direction of $u$ | $F_x = \rho a (V-u) [V - u - V_{r2}\cos\phi]$ where $\phi = 180° - \beta_2$ | N |
| 4 | Work done per second by jet on wheel | $P = \rho a u (V-u) [V - u + V_{r2}\cos\beta_2]$ | W |
| 5 | Maximum work condition | $u = \dfrac{V}{2}$ (when $\beta_2 = 0$, frictionless) | m/s |
| 6 | Maximum efficiency | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{2u(V-u)^2}{V^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{g}$, peak at $u = V/3$ for max efficiency | — |

### E. Efficiency Definitions

| Efficiency | Formula | Conditions |
|------------|---------|------------|
| Hydraulic Efficiency $\eta_h$ | $\dfrac{2u(V-u)}{V^2}$ | For $\theta = 180°$, $V_{r2} = V - u$ |
| Mechanical Efficiency $\eta_m$ | $\dfrac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\text{Water Power}}$ | Includes bearing/friction losses |
| Overall Efficiency $\eta_o$ | $\eta_h \times \eta_m$ | Overall turbine efficiency |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these concepts are applied in industry:**
> 1. **Pelton Wheel Turbines** — Idukki & Mullaperiyar hydroelectric plants, Kerala.
> 2. **Centrifugal Pumps** — Impeller design for irrigation, water supply (KWA projects).
> 3. **Hydraulic Ram Pumps** — Rural water lifting without electricity.
> 4. **Ship Propeller Design** — Curved blades for thrust generation.
> 5. **Wind Turbine Blades** — Same momentum-change principle for air.
> 6. **Jet Engine Compressors** — Stator and rotor blade interactions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force on a Fixed Single Curved Vane

### Setup

A jet of water with velocity $V$ and area $a$ strikes a **fixed** curved vane that deflects the jet through an angle $\theta$. The jet enters along the x-axis and leaves at angle $\theta$ from the x-axis (measured from inlet direction).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply momentum equation in x-direction.**

The momentum flux at inlet (x-direction) = $\dot{m} V_1 = \rho a V^2$
The momentum flux at outlet (x-direction) = $\dot{m} V_2 \cos\theta = \rho a V^2 \cos\theta$

> The **frictionless assumption** implies $V_1 = V_2 = V$ (Bernoulli along streamline).

Force exerted **by jet on vane** in x-direction (Newton's 2nd Law for control volume):

$$F_x = \dot{m}(V_{1x} - V_{2x})$$

$$F_x = \rho a V^2 - \rho a V^2 \cos\theta = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

This force $F_x$ acts on the vane in the **direction of jet motion** (positive x).

**Step 2: Apply momentum equation in y-direction.**

Initial momentum flux in y = 0 (jet moves along x)
Final momentum flux in y = $\dot{m} V_2 \sin\theta = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$

$$F_y = \dot{m}(V_{1y} - V_{2y})$$

$$F_y = 0 - \rho a V^2 \sin\theta = -\rho a V^2 \sin\theta$$

The negative sign means the force on the vane in the y-direction is **opposite** to the jet's deflection. Magnitude:

$$\vert F_y \vert = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$$

**Step 3: Resultant force on the vane.**

$$F_R = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{[\rho a V^2 (1-\cos\theta)]^2 + [\rho a V^2 \sin\theta]^2}$$

$$F_R = \rho a V^2 \sqrt{(1-\cos\theta)^2 + \sin^2\theta}$$

Expanding inside the square root:

$$(1-\cos\theta)^2 + \sin^2\theta = 1 - 2\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 2 - 2\cos\theta$$

Using identity $1 - \cos\theta = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$:

$$2 - 2\cos\theta = 4\sin^2(\theta/2)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{F_R = 2 \rho a V^2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

> This is the **master formula** for fixed single curved vane. For $\theta = 90°$: $F_R = \sqrt{2}\rho a V^2$. For $\theta = 180°$: $F_R = 2\rho a V^2$ (maximum, jet reversed).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Force on a Moving Single Vane (Translational Motion)

### Setup

A single vane moves in the **same direction** as the jet with velocity $u$. An observer **on the vane** sees the jet approaching with **relative velocity** $(V - u)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Mass flow rate striking the vane (in ground frame).**

Since the vane moves away from the jet at speed $u$, the effective jet speed hitting the vane is $(V - u)$. The mass flow rate actually striking the vane per second:

$$\dot{m}_{strike} = \rho \cdot a \cdot (V - u)$$

> Note: This is **different** from the total jet mass flow $\rho a V$ because the vane intercepts only a fraction of it.

**Step 2: Force on vane in direction of motion (in vane's reference frame).**

Using the fixed vane result with relative velocity $(V - u)$:

$$F_x' = \rho a (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3: Convert to ground frame — does the frame change the force?**

By the **Galilean invariance of forces** in non-relativistic mechanics, the force on the vane is the **same in both frames** (since the vane's acceleration is zero in both frames for steady motion):

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)}$$

**Step 4: Work done per second by jet on vane.**

Power transmitted = Force × Velocity of vane:

$$P = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$$

For the special case $\theta = 180°$ (jet completely reversed — Pelton bucket):

$$\boxed{P = 2 \rho a u (V - u)^2}$$

**Step 5: Work done per unit weight of water striking.**

Work per second = $P$
Weight of water striking per second = $\dot{m}_{strike} \cdot g = \rho a g (V - u)$

$$\frac{\text{Work done}}{\text{Weight of water}} = \frac{P}{\rho a g (V - u)} = \frac{u (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)}{g}$$

For $\theta = 180°$:

$$\boxed{W = \frac{2u(V-u)^2}{g} \quad \text{[metres]}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Done and Efficiency for Moving Series of Vanes (Pelton Wheel)

### Setup

A series of vanes moves past the jet at velocity $u$. Each vane is a double-cup bucket (Pelton type) with deflection angle $\theta = 180°$ ideally. The jet of velocity $V$ has cross-section $a$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Inlet velocity triangle (at point of entry to bucket).**

For an ideal Pelton wheel, the jet is directed **tangentially** to the wheel, so $\alpha_1 = 0°$. This means the absolute velocity $V$ is parallel to the vane velocity $u$.

The relative velocity of jet w.r.t. vane at inlet:

$$V_{r1} = V - u$$

The relative velocity is directed **along the bucket at entry**, so $\beta_1 = 0°$.

**Step 2: Outlet velocity triangle (at point of exit from bucket).**

After the jet is deflected by angle $\theta$ inside the bucket, the **relative velocity** $V_{r2}$ leaves the bucket. The magnitude of $V_{r2}$:

$$V_{r2} = K_v \cdot V_{r1} = K_v (V - u)$$

where $K_v$ is the **velocity ratio** (loss factor); $K_v = 1$ for frictionless, $K_v < 1$ for real buckets.

The relative velocity $V_{r2}$ makes an angle $\phi$ with the direction of $u$ (where $\phi = 180° - \beta_2$). For a Pelton bucket with $\theta = 180°$, ideally $\phi = 180°$ (jet reverses completely relative to vane).

**Step 3: Force on vane in direction of motion.**

The change in momentum of the jet in the direction of $u$ per unit time:

$$F_x = \dot{m}_{strike} \cdot [V_{1x} - V_{2x}]$$

Where $V_{1x}$ = velocity of jet in direction of $u$ at inlet = $V$ (parallel)
And $V_{2x}$ = velocity of jet in direction of $u$ at outlet = $u + V_{r2}\cos(180°) = u - V_{r2}$

$$F_x = \rho a (V - u) \cdot [V - (u - V_{r2})]$$

$$\boxed{F_x = \rho a (V - u)(V - u + V_{r2})}$$

**Step 4: Work done per second.**

$$P = F_x \cdot u = \rho a u (V - u)(V - u + V_{r2})$$

For **frictionless** bucket ($V_{r2} = V_{r1} = V - u$):

$$\boxed{P = 2 \rho a u (V - u)^2}$$

**Step 5: Maximum work condition.**

Differentiate $P$ with respect to $u$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{du} = 2\rho a (V - u)^2 + 2\rho a u \cdot 2(V - u)(-1) = 0$$

$$2\rho a (V - u)[(V - u) - 2u] = 0$$

Either $u = V$ (trivial, no power) or $V - u = 2u$, giving:

$$\boxed{u = \frac{V}{2}}$$

At this condition, $V - u = V/2$, so:

$$P_{max} = 2\rho a \cdot \frac{V}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\rho a V^3}{4}$$

**Step 6: Hydraulic efficiency.**

$$\eta_h = \frac{\text{Kinetic energy utilized per second}}{\text{Kinetic energy supplied per second}}$$

$$= \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2} \dot{m}_{total} V^2} = \frac{2\rho a u (V - u)^2}{\frac{1}{2} \rho a V \cdot V^2} = \frac{4u(V-u)^2}{V^3}$$

For maximum efficiency, differentiate $\eta_h$ w.r.t. $u$ and set to zero. Let $k = u/V$:

$$\eta_h = 4k(1-k)^2$$

$$\frac{d\eta_h}{dk} = 4[(1-k)^2 + k \cdot 2(1-k)(-1)] = 4(1-k)[1-k - 2k] = 4(1-k)(1 - 3k)$$

Setting to zero (excluding trivial $k = 1$): $1 - 3k = 0$, so:

$$\boxed{u = \frac{V}{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{h,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} = 0.593 = 59.3\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU takeaway:** Maximum **work** is at $u = V/2$, but maximum **efficiency** is at $u = V/3$. Designers choose based on whether power or efficiency is the priority.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Given Information | Find | Approach |
|-------------------|------|----------|
| Fixed vane, $\theta$ known | $F_x, F_y, F_R$ | Use Section 3.1 formulas directly |
| Moving vane, $u, V, \theta$ | Force, work/sec | Convert to relative velocity $(V-u)$, use Section 3.2 |
| Multiple moving vanes, $V_{r2}$ given | Force, power, efficiency | Use Section 3.3 with velocity triangles |
| Find max work / max efficiency | Optimal $u$ | $u = V/2$ for max work, $u = V/3$ for max efficiency |
| Bucket with $\theta \neq 180°$ | Force | Use $F_x = \rho a (V-u)^2(1-\cos\theta)$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "impact of jet". State the assumptions made while analyzing the impact of jet on a fixed curved vane.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Impact of Jet:** When a fluid jet issuing from a nozzle strikes a solid surface (vane), there is a change in the momentum of the fluid. According to Newton's Second Law, this rate of change of momentum produces a dynamic force on the surface, which is termed as the **impact of jet**.

**Assumptions:**
1. The jet is **incompressible, steady, and one-dimensional**.
2. Friction between the jet and vane surface is **negligible** ($V_1 = V_2 = V$).
3. **Atmospheric pressure** prevails over the entire vane surface (pressure forces cancel out).
4. The jet cross-section remains **constant** ($a_1 = a_2 = a$) due to negligible gravitational effect over short vane length.
5. The flow is along a single streamline at inlet and outlet.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between "fixed" and "moving" curved vanes. Why are curved vanes preferred over flat plates in hydraulic machines?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fixed Vane | Moving Vane |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Vane velocity $u$ | $u = 0$ | $u > 0$ |
| Mass flow striking | $\rho a V$ | $\rho a (V - u)$ |
| Work done per second | **Zero** (no displacement) | $F \cdot u > 0$ |
| Application | Deflectors, splitter vanes | Turbine blades, pump impellers |

**Why curved vanes are preferred:**

1. **Smooth gradual deflection** — avoids sudden 90° reversal, reducing shock loss and turbulence.
2. **Higher force component in useful direction** — force is directed along the vane motion, maximizing work extraction.
3. **Higher efficiency** — kinetic energy is gradually converted to mechanical work with minimal dissipation.
4. **Better guidance of fluid** — flow follows the contour, avoiding separation and cavitation.
5. **Reduced noise and vibration** — gradual deflection produces less acoustic and mechanical disturbance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

> **(a)** Derive an expression for the **force exerted by a jet of water on a fixed curved vane** at the exit angle $\theta$. Hence obtain an expression for the resultant force.
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A jet of water 75 mm diameter strikes a fixed curved vane with a velocity of 25 m/s. The vane is curved through an angle of 135°. Find:
> - (i) The force exerted by the jet on the vane in the direction of the jet.
> - (ii) The force exerted by the jet on the vane perpendicular to the direction of the jet.
> - (iii) The resultant force on the vane.
> - (iv) The power lost in terms of head.
> **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the full derivation. Key results:

$$F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\theta), \quad F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_R = 2\rho a V^2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

**Part (b):** Given:
- Diameter $d = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m, so $a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $V = 25$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\theta = 135°$

**Compute $\rho a V^2$:**
$$\rho a V^2 = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 625 = 2761.25 \text{ N}$$

**(i) Force in direction of jet:**
$$F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos 135°) = 2761.25 \times (1 - (-0.7071))$$
$$F_x = 2761.25 \times 1.7071 = \boxed{4713.6 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Force perpendicular to jet:**
$$F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin 135° = 2761.25 \times 0.7071$$
$$F_y = \boxed{1952.7 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Resultant force:**
$$F_R = 2\rho a V^2 \sin(135°/2) = 2 \times 2761.25 \times \sin 67.5°$$
$$\sin 67.5° = 0.9239$$
$$F_R = 5522.5 \times 0.9239 = \boxed{5101.2 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification:** $F_R = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{4713.6^2 + 1952.7^2} = \sqrt{2.222 \times 10^7 + 3.813 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{2.603 \times 10^7} = 5102$ N ✓

**(iv) Power lost (for fixed vane):**

The kinetic energy leaving the vane is still $\frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3$ (since $V_1 = V_2 = V$), but the energy that **could have been** utilized had the jet been stopped equals the kinetic energy at inlet. In fixed vane case, the entire KE is lost (no work extraction).

Power lost = $\frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 25^3$
$$= 0.5 \times 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 15625 = 34516 \text{ W} = 34.52 \text{ kW}$$

Head equivalent: $H = \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{625}{2 \times 9.81} = \boxed{31.86 \text{ m}}$ of head is dissipated.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` (Internal Choice)

> **(a)** With the help of velocity triangles at inlet and outlet, derive an expression for the **work done per second by a jet of water on a series of moving curved vanes**. State the condition for maximum work done.
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A jet of water of diameter 100 mm moving with a velocity of 30 m/s strikes a series of curved vanes moving with a velocity of 15 m/s. The vanes deflect the jet through an angle of 165°. The relative velocity at outlet is 0.92 of that at inlet. Calculate:
> - (i) Force exerted by the jet on the vanes in the direction of motion.
> - (ii) Work done per second.
> - (iii) Efficiency of the series of vanes.
> **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a):** Refer to **Section 3.3** above for derivation. Key results:

$$P = \rho a u (V - u)(V - u + V_{r2}\cos\beta_2)$$

For frictionless case ($V_{r2} = V - u$, $\beta_2 = 0°$):
$$P = 2 \rho a u (V - u)^2$$

Maximum work at $u = V/2$, giving $P_{max} = \rho a V^3 / 4$.

**Part (b):** Given:
- $d = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m $\Rightarrow a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $V = 30$ m/s, $u = 15$ m/s
- $\theta = 165°$, $V_{r2}/V_{r1} = K_v = 0.92$

**Step 1: Compute relative velocity at inlet.**

Since $\alpha_1 = 0°$ (tangential jet), $V_{r1} = V - u = 30 - 15 = 15$ m/s.

**Step 2: Compute relative velocity at outlet.**

$$V_{r2} = K_v \cdot V_{r1} = 0.92 \times 15 = 13.8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Find outlet angle $\beta_2$ from deflection.**

The jet is deflected through $\theta = 165°$ inside the vane. The angle of relative velocity at outlet with the direction of motion:

$$\beta_2 = 180° - \theta = 180° - 165° = 15°$$

**Step 4: Force in direction of motion.**

$$F_x = \rho a (V - u)(V - u + V_{r2}\cos\beta_2)$$

$$\cos 15° = 0.9659$$

$$V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 = 13.8 \times 0.9659 = 13.33 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V - u + V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 = 15 + 13.33 = 28.33 \text{ m/s}$$

$$F_x = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 \times 28.33$$

$$F_x = 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 425 = \boxed{3338 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5: Work done per second.**

$$P = F_x \cdot u = 3338 \times 15 = \boxed{50,070 \text{ W} = 50.07 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency.**

$$\eta_h = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho a V^3} = \frac{50070}{\frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 30^3}$$

Denominator = $0.5 \times 1000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 27000 = 106,029$ W

$$\eta_h = \frac{50070}{106029} = 0.4722 = \boxed{47.22\%}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $F_R = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ with $F_x$:** Many students write the resultant force as just $F_x$. The correct resultant is $F_R = 2\rho a V^2 \sin(\theta/2)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $(V - u)$ in mass flow for moving vanes:** The mass flow rate **striking the vane** is $\rho a (V - u)$, NOT $\rho a V$. The total jet mass is $\rho a V$, but only a fraction strikes.
>
> 3. **Sign error in $V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$:** For Pelton-type buckets, the relative velocity at exit points **opposite** to $u$. So the tangential component of $V_{r2}$ must be **subtracted** correctly: $V_{2x} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$.
>
> 4. **Mixing up maximum power and maximum efficiency:** Max power at $u = V/2$, max efficiency at $u = V/3$. These are **different** operating points.
>
> 5. **Unit conversion errors:** Diameter must be in **metres** before computing area. Velocity in m/s. Density = 1000 kg/m³ for water (or $\rho_w = 9810$ N/m³ for specific weight).
>
> 6. **Not drawing velocity triangles:** In KTU valuation, **diagrams carry marks** (typically 1-2 marks). Always draw clean inlet and outlet velocity triangles with proper labeling of $V$, $u$, $V_r$, $\alpha$, $\beta$.
>
> 7. **Using $V$ instead of $(V - u)$ for the moving vane force equation:** Substituting $V$ in place of $(V - u)$ in the formula $F = \rho a V^2(1-\cos\theta)$ is the most common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before Exam:**
>
> **Foundational Concepts:**
> - [ ] Impact of jet arises from **Newton's Second Law** applied to a control volume.
> - [ ] Force = **rate of change of momentum** of the jet.
> - [ ] Curved vanes deflect the jet **gradually** through angle $\theta$ to minimize losses.
> - [ ] Frictionless vane: $V_1 = V_2 = V$ (magnitude conserved, direction changes).
>
> **Fixed Vane Master Equations:**
> - [ ] $F_x = \rho a V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$
> - [ ] $F_y = \rho a V^2 \sin\theta$
> - [ ] $F_R = 2\rho a V^2 \sin(\theta/2)$
> - [ ] Work done = 0 (no displacement)
>
> **Moving Vane Master Equations:**
> - [ ] Mass striking per second = $\rho a (V - u)$
> - [ ] $F_x = \rho a (V - u)^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ for single vane
> - [ ] $F_x = \rho a (V - u)(V - u + V_{r2}\cos\beta_2)$ for series of vanes
> - [ ] Power $P = F_x \cdot u$
>
> **Velocity Triangles:**
> - [ ] $\vec{V} = \vec{u} + \vec{V_r}$ at both inlet and outlet
> - [ ] Inlet: $V_{r1} = V - u$ (when $\alpha_1 = 0°$)
> - [ ] Outlet: $\beta_2 = 180° - \theta$
> - [ ] For Pelton: jet is **tangential**, $\alpha_1 = 0°$, $V_{r2}$ leaves backward
>
> **Optimization Conditions (Memorize!):**
> - [ ] Maximum **work** per second: $u = V/2$, $P_{max} = \rho a V^3 / 4$
> - [ ] Maximum **efficiency**: $u = V/3$, $\eta_{max} = 16/27 = 59.3\%$
>
> **Practical Design Values (for Pelton Wheel):**
> - [ ] $u = 0.45V$ to $0.50V$ (typical operating range)
> - [ ] Bucket angle $\theta = 165°$ to $170°$ (avoid 180° for clean exit)
> - [ ] Velocity ratio $K_v = V_{r2}/V_{r1} = 0.90$ to $0.95$ in practice
>
> **Unit Conversions:**
> - [ ] Specific weight $\rho g = 9810$ N/m³ for water
> - [ ] $1$ HP $= 745.7$ W, $1$ kW $= 1000$ W
> - [ ] Head $H = V^2/(2g)$ when all PE converts to KE

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Turbines and Pumps - Comparison and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Turbines and Pumps - Comparison and Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — CLASSIFICATION OF TURBINES AND PUMPS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Hydraulic Turbines** are rotary mechanical devices that convert the **kinetic energy** and **pressure energy** of a flowing fluid (usually water) into useful mechanical work (shaft rotation). They are classified based on the energy conversion mechanism (impulse vs. reaction), direction of flow through the runner, head available, and specific speed.
>
> **Hydraulic Pumps** are machines that convert mechanical energy (from a prime mover) into hydraulic energy, raising the pressure of a fluid to enable transport against head/pressure. They are broadly classified as **rotodynamic (centrifugal)** and **positive displacement (reciprocating)** pumps.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel vs. The Bicycle Pump

Imagine a small village with a stream flowing down a hill:

- **The Turbine Analogy (Pelton Wheel):** Picture a **rotating water wheel** placed below a waterfall. The high-speed water jet strikes cup-shaped blades fixed on the wheel, **pushing** them around. The wheel does not need to be submerged; only the *velocity* (kinetic energy) of the jet matters. This is the essence of an **impulse turbine** — energy conversion happens due to *impact* in the *atmosphere*.

- **The Pump Analogy (Centrifugal Pump):** Now imagine a **fanning blade spinning inside a sealed drum filled with water**. As the blade spins, it flings water outward to the rim, creating low pressure at the centre (inlet) — atmospheric pressure pushes more water in, and the water exits at high pressure at the rim. This is a **centrifugal pump** — it imparts *kinetic* energy to the fluid, which is then converted to *pressure* in the volute casing.

- **The Pump Analogy (Reciprocating Pump):** Think of a **bicycle pump** in action. A piston moves back and forth inside a cylinder. On the forward stroke, water is pushed out through a delivery valve; on the return stroke, a suction valve opens and draws more water in. This is a **positive displacement pump** — fluid is *displaced* by physical volume change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Energy Direction:**
> - **Turbines:** Fluid Energy → Mechanical Energy (produce work)
> - **Pumps:** Mechanical Energy → Fluid Energy (consume work)
> The two are essentially **reverse thermodynamic cousins** — their governing equations (Euler's Turbomachinery Equation) are mirror images.

### 1.1.2 Primary Classification Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4, CO4):**
> A KTU examiner expects you to know **two classification tables** cold:
> 1. **Classification of Hydraulic Turbines** (based on head, flow direction, energy conversion).
> 2. **Classification of Hydraulic Pumps** (centrifugal vs. reciprocating, with sub-categories).

---

## 1.2 Detailed Classification of Turbines

### A. Based on the Type of Energy at Inlet to the Runner

| Type | Energy at Inlet | Energy Conversion | Pressure Change Across Runner |
|------|----------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Impulse Turbine** | Pure **Kinetic Energy** | Kinetic → Mechanical (at atmospheric pressure) | **Zero** pressure change |
| **Reaction Turbine** | **Kinetic + Pressure** Energy | Both pressure and kinetic energy are converted | **Significant** pressure drop across runner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pelton Wheel** = Impulse (high head, low flow).
> **Francis & Kaplan** = Reaction (medium and low head, respectively).

### B. Based on Direction of Flow Through the Runner

| Class | Flow Path | Example |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| **Tangential Flow** | Water enters tangentially in the plane of the wheel | Pelton Wheel |
| **Radial Flow** | Water flows from outer radius to inner radius (or vice-versa) | Old Francis Turbine (radial inward) |
| **Axial Flow** | Water flows parallel to the axis of rotation | Kaplan Turbine, Propeller Turbine |
| **Mixed Flow** | Combination of radial and axial flow | Modern Francis Turbine |

### C. Based on Specific Speed (Nₛ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Speed (Nₛ)** is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would run to develop **1 unit power (1 kW)** under a **1 unit head (1 m)**. It is a *dimensionless index* used to **select** the appropriate turbine for a given site condition.

| Specific Speed Range | Suitable Turbine | Application |
|---------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Low Nₛ** (10 – 35 for metric, single jet) | **Pelton Wheel** | Very high head, low discharge (e.g., 300 m+ head) |
| **Medium Nₛ** (60 – 400) | **Francis Turbine** | Medium head (30 m – 300 m), medium flow |
| **High Nₛ** (300 – 1000) | **Kaplan / Propeller** | Low head (< 30 m), high discharge |

### D. Based on Head Available

| Head Category | Head Range | Turbine Used |
|---------------|-----------|--------------|
| **High Head** | > 250 m | Pelton Wheel (single or multi-jet) |
| **Medium Head** | 30 m – 250 m | Francis Turbine |
| **Low Head** | < 30 m | Kaplan or Propeller Turbine |

---

## 1.3 Detailed Classification of Pumps

### A. Broad Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pumps are classified under two main heads: **Centrifugal (Rotodynamic)** and **Reciprocating (Positive Displacement)**. KTU frequently asks the **differences** between these two as a 3-mark or 7-mark question.

**1. Centrifugal Pumps (Rotodynamic Pumps)**
   - Based on flow direction:
     - **Radial Flow** (Francis vane type, low specific speed)
     - **Mixed Flow** (medium specific speed)
     - **Axial Flow** (Propeller type, high specific speed)
   - Based on number of impellers:
     - **Single-stage**
     - **Multi-stage**
   - Based on suction:
     - **Single-suction**
     - **Double-suction**

**2. Reciprocating Pumps (Positive Displacement Pumps)**
   - Based on number of cylinders:
     - **Single-acting** (delivery on one stroke)
     - **Double-acting** (delivery on both strokes)
   - Based on number of cylinders:
     - **Single-cylinder**
     - **Double-cylinder / Duplex**
   - Based on liquid handled:
     - **Piston pump** (high pressure, viscous fluids)
     - **Plunger pump** (very high pressure)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Comparative Analysis: Turbines vs. Pumps

The two are conceptually opposite, yet share the same fundamental fluid mechanics. The **Euler Turbomachinery Equation** is the unifying principle:

$$
\frac{V_{w1} u_1 - V_{w2} u_2}{g} = H \quad \text{(per unit weight basis)}
$$

- For a **turbine**, the fluid loses energy: $V_{w1} u_1 > V_{w2} u_2$, so $H > 0$ (work extracted).
- For a **pump**, the fluid gains energy: $V_{w2} u_2 > V_{w1} u_1$, so $H$ is work done *on* the fluid.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Module 4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-defining cheat sheet** for this classification topic. Master these for the 14-mark derivations and 3-mark definitions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $N_{s,t} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Specific speed of **turbine** | Selects turbine type from site head and power |
| 2 | $N_{s,p} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed of **pump** | Selects pump type from head and discharge |
| 3 | $P = \rho \, Q \, g \, H$ | Hydraulic power (Watts) | Power available in fluid |
| 4 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Shaft power required | Sizing the prime mover |
| 5 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{h} \times \eta_{m} \times \eta_{v}$ | Efficiency decomposition | Total = hydraulic × mechanical × volumetric |
| 6 | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Peripheral velocity of blade | Velocity triangle construction |
| 7 | $V_{f} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Flow velocity (axial/radial) | Velocity triangle construction |
| 8 | $H_{manometric} = H_s + h_d + \dfrac{V_d^2}{2g}$ | Manometric head of pump | Pump performance testing |
| 9 | $Slip = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{th}} \times 100\%$ | Slip of reciprocating pump | Loss in positive displacement pumps |
| 10 | $H_{theoretical} = \dfrac{w}{g} \cdot \dfrac{A}{a} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ (single acting) | Theoretical head of reciprocating pump | Head developed by piston pump |
| 11 | $\text{Co-efficient of discharge} \; C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Discharge coefficient of pump | Reciprocating pump performance |
| 12 | $\dfrac{H}{N^2 D^2} = \text{constant}$ | Head coefficient (dimensionless) | For geometrically similar machines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** In the table, $H$ = head in metres, $Q$ = discharge in m³/s, $N$ = rpm, $P$ = power in kW, $D$ = diameter, $u$ = blade speed, $V_f$ = flow velocity, $V_w$ = whirl velocity, $V_d$ = velocity at delivery.

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Why Two Broad Pump Families?

### 2.3.1 Rotodynamic (Centrifugal) Pumps

- **Mechanism:** An **impeller** rotating at high speed imparts kinetic energy to the fluid, which is then converted to pressure in the **volute casing** (diffuser).
- **Energy transfer:** Continuous — no physical displacement of fluid.
- **Why preferred for high flow, moderate head:** Smooth flow, low maintenance, handles dirty fluids, high speeds.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot deliver fluid against a *fixed* pressure without losing flow (i.e., discharge falls as head rises).

### 2.3.2 Positive Displacement (Reciprocating) Pumps

- **Mechanism:** A **piston/plunger** physically displaces a fixed volume of fluid per stroke.
- **Energy transfer:** Periodic — fixed volume delivered per cycle.
- **Why preferred for high head, low flow:** Discharge is *almost independent* of head; suitable for high-pressure applications (boiler feed, hydraulic presses).
- **Disadvantage:** Pulsating flow, requires air vessel, more maintenance, cannot handle dirty/abrasive fluids.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydroelectric Power Stations:** Pelton (Himalayan high-head plants in India — e.g., Nathpa Jhakri, 1500 m head), Francis (most of India's medium-head stations — e.g., Bhakra Nangal), Kaplan (low-head run-of-river projects).
- **Water Supply Schemes:** Centrifugal pumps dominate municipal water distribution.
- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Reciprocating plunger pumps for mud pumping and well-head injection.
- **Agriculture:** Axial flow / propeller pumps for irrigation from rivers.
- **Boiler Feed:** Multistage centrifugal or duplex reciprocating pumps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Speed of a Turbine

**Objective:** Show rigorously that for geometrically similar turbines, the dimensionless index $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ uniquely identifies the geometric class (Pelton, Francis, Kaplan).

### Step 1 — Define Geometric Similarity

For two geometrically similar turbines running under similar conditions, the **Head Coefficient** $\psi$ and **Power Coefficient** $\lambda$ must be constant:

$$
\psi = \frac{gH}{N^2 D^2} = \text{constant} \quad \ldots (1)
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5} = \text{constant} \quad \ldots (2)
$$

### Step 2 — Eliminate Diameter $D$ from Both Equations

From (1):

$$
D^2 = \frac{gH}{\psi N^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \left( \frac{gH}{\psi N^2} \right)^{1/2}
$$

### Step 3 — Raise to the 5th Power and Substitute into (2)

$$
D^5 = \left( \frac{gH}{\psi N^2} \right)^{5/2}
$$

Substitute into (2):

$$
\lambda = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 \left(\dfrac{gH}{\psi N^2}\right)^{5/2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Expression

$$
\lambda = \frac{P}{\rho \, N^3} \cdot \left( \frac{\psi N^2}{gH} \right)^{5/2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{P}{\rho} \cdot \frac{\psi^{5/2} \cdot N^5}{g^{5/2} \, H^{5/2} \, N^3}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{P \, \psi^{5/2}}{\rho \, g^{5/2} \, H^{5/2} \, N^{-2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange to Isolate $N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$

$$
N^2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \left( \frac{\lambda \, \rho \, g^{5/2}}{\psi^{5/2}} \right)^{1/2}
$$

Since the RHS depends only on constants for geometrically similar machines, the LHS is the **specific speed**:

$$
N_{s,t} = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad \text{(in SI, with } P \text{ in kW, } H \text{ in m, } N \text{ in rpm)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** $N_s$ is the speed in rpm at which a *unit* (geometrically similar) turbine would develop **1 kW** of power under **1 m** of head. It is **not** the actual running speed. It is a *class index* used for **selection**, not a direct operating parameter.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Specific Speed of a Pump

**Objective:** Prove that for pumps, the analogous index is $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$.

### Step 1 — Use the Discharge Coefficient

For geometrically similar pumps:

$$
\phi = \frac{Q}{N D^3} = \text{constant} \quad \ldots (3)
$$

From (1) and (3), eliminate $D$:

$$
D^3 = \left( \frac{Q}{\phi N} \right) \quad \text{and} \quad D^2 = \left( \frac{gH}{\psi N^2} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Form the Ratio $D^3 / D^{2}$ to Eliminate One Power

$$
\frac{D^3}{D^2} = D = \frac{Q \, \psi N^2}{\phi N \, gH} = \frac{Q \psi N}{\phi g H}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Specific Speed

Rearranging to isolate $N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$:

$$
N \sqrt{Q} = D^{1/2} \cdot \frac{\phi g H}{\psi}
$$

Combining with $D^2 = gH/(\psi N^2)$:

$$
N_{s,p} = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} = \text{constant for the class}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Turbine Sub-Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **comparative matrix** is a high-yield tool for KTU 14-mark comparison questions (e.g., "Compare Pelton Wheel and Francis Turbine").

| Parameter | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Turbine Type** | Impulse | Reaction | Reaction |
| **Specific Speed (Nₛ)** | 10 – 35 (single jet) | 60 – 400 | 300 – 1000 |
| **Head Range** | High (250 m – 1800 m) | Medium (30 m – 250 m) | Low (< 30 m) |
| **Discharge** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Runner Shape** | Wheel with cup-shaped buckets | Spiral casing with guide vanes + runner blades | Axial-flow propeller-like runner |
| **Flow Direction** | Tangential | Radial / Mixed | Axial |
| **Pressure Variation** | Atmospheric throughout | Pressure drop across runner | Pressure drop across runner |
| **Casing** | Not essential (no draft tube needed in basic) | Spiral casing + draft tube | Scroll casing + draft tube |
| **Speed Regulation** | Easy (using needle valve) | Complex (guide vanes) | Complex (adjustable blades) |
| **Setting** | Horizontal shaft common | Vertical shaft common | Vertical shaft (bulb) |
| **Number of Jets** | 1, 2, 4, or 6 | — | — |
| **Examples in India** | Nathpa Jhakri (1500 m) | Bhakra Nangal (128 m) | Many small hydro (< 30 m head) |
| **Construction** | Simple | More complex | Most complex (bulb turbine) |
| **Part Load Efficiency** | Poor (single jet) | Good | Excellent (adjustable blades) |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Centrifugal vs. Reciprocating Pump

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Frequency Question:** "Compare centrifugal and reciprocating pumps" (commonly 7 marks). Use the following matrix as the answer skeleton.

| Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Working Principle** | Kinetic → Pressure energy | Displacement of fixed volume |
| **Type** | Rotodynamic | Positive Displacement |
| **Discharge vs. Head** | Discharge falls as head rises (non-linear) | Discharge nearly constant (independent of head) |
| **Suction Lift** | Limited (~7 m theoretical) | Higher, but limited by NPSH |
| **Flow Type** | Smooth, continuous | Pulsating (needs air vessel) |
| **Pressure** | Low to moderate head (single stage) | High pressure possible |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High (valves, pistons) |
| **Priming** | Required (cannot self-prime easily) | Self-priming |
| **Fluid Suitability** | Clean to slightly dirty fluids | Clean, non-abrasive fluids |
| **Efficiency at Varying Load** | High efficiency near design point | High efficiency over wide range |
| **Speed (rpm)** | High (1500 – 3000) | Low (60 – 200) |
| **Cost per kW** | Low | High |
| **Best Suited For** | Large Q, moderate H | Small Q, very high H |
| **Example Application** | Water supply, firefighting | Boiler feed, hydraulic press |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Identifying Turbine Type from Site Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A site has an available head of **80 m** and expected power output of **5000 kW**. The proposed turbine speed is **300 rpm**. Identify the type of turbine.

**Step 1 — Compute Specific Speed**

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{300 \times \sqrt{5000}}{80^{5/4}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{5000} \approx 70.71
$$

$$
80^{5/4} = 80 \times 80^{1/4} = 80 \times 2.99 \approx 239.4
$$

$$
N_s = \frac{300 \times 70.71}{239.4} \approx 88.6
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Standard Ranges**

- Pelton: 10 – 35
- Francis: 60 – 400
- Kaplan: 300 – 1000

**Step 3 — Conclusion**

$$
N_s \approx 88.6 \in (60,\ 400) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Francis Turbine is suitable.}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📒 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — Turbines

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HYDRAULIC TURBINES]:::main
    A --> B[Impulse Type]:::sub
    A --> C[Reaction Type]:::sub

    B --> B1[Pelton Wheel]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Turgo Turbine]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Cross Flow Turbine]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Francis Turbine]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Kaplan Turbine]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Bulb Turbine]:::leaf
    C --> C4[Propeller Turbine]:::leaf

    C1 --> C1a[Radial Inward]:::subleaf
    C1 --> C1b[Mixed Flow]:::subleaf
    C1 --> C1c[Axial Flow Francis]:::subleaf

    C2 --> C2a[Adjustable Blade]:::subleaf
    C2 --> C2b[Fixed Blade Propeller]:::subleaf

    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef sub fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef leaf fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef subleaf fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **root** is `HYDRAULIC TURBINES` (a single big box).
> - The **first split** is by energy conversion mechanism: Impulse vs. Reaction.
> - **Impulse branch** contains the three main impulse machines used in practice.
> - **Reaction branch** is further split into modern reaction turbine variants.

## 4.2 Master Classification Flowchart — Pumps

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[HYDRAULIC PUMPS]:::main
    P --> Q[Rotodynamic Pumps]:::sub
    P --> R[Positive Displacement Pumps]:::sub

    Q --> Q1[Centrifugal Pump]:::leaf
    Q --> Q2[Axial Flow Pump]:::leaf
    Q --> Q3[Mixed Flow Pump]:::leaf
    Q --> Q4[Special Pumps]:::leaf

    Q1 --> Q1a[Radial Vane]:::subleaf
    Q1 --> Q1b[Backward Curved]:::subleaf
    Q1 --> Q1c[Forward Curved]:::subleaf
    Q1 --> Q1d[Open Impeller]:::subleaf

    Q4 --> Q4a[Submersible]:::subleaf
    Q4 --> Q4b[Self Priming]:::subleaf

    R --> R1[Reciprocating Pump]:::leaf
    R --> R2[Rotary Pump]:::leaf

    R1 --> R1a[Single Acting]:::subleaf
    R1 --> R1b[Double Acting]:::subleaf
    R1 --> R1c[Piston Type]:::subleaf
    R1 --> R1d[Plunger Type]:::subleaf

    R2 --> R2a[Gear Pump]:::subleaf
    R2 --> R2b[Vane Pump]:::subleaf
    R2 --> R2c[Screw Pump]:::subleaf

    classDef main fill:#7f6000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef sub fill:#bf9000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef leaf fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef subleaf fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The root is `HYDRAULIC PUMPS`.
> - **Rotodynamic pumps** are the *workhorses* of industry (most common).
> - **Positive displacement pumps** are used where *precise volume* or *high pressure* is required.
> - Rotary pumps are a third branch shown for completeness — common in **automotive and chemical** industries.

## 4.3 Selection Logic — Which Turbine/Pump for Which Site?

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[START: Given Site Head H and Power P]:::start
    S --> T1{Calculate Specific Speed Ns}:::decision
    T1 --> T2{Ns less than 35}:::decision
    T1 --> T3{Ns between 60 and 400}:::decision
    T1 --> T4{Ns greater than 300}:::decision
    T2 --> A1[SELECT PELTON WHEEL]:::final
    T3 --> A2[SELECT FRANCIS TURBINE]:::final
    T4 --> A3[SELECT KAPLAN TURBINE]:::final

    classDef start fill:#385723,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef decision fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef final fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000,font-weight:bold
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use of this Decision Tree:** This is the **field procedure** used by design engineers to select a turbine for a hydro-electric project. A KTU question may ask you to identify the *selection criteria* — this flowchart is the answer.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pump–Motor Coupled System

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1[Electrical Power Supply]:::a
    IN2[Switchgear and Starter]:::b
    IN3[Electric Motor]:::c
    IN4[Shaft Coupling]:::d
    IN5[Suction Pipe with Strainer]:::e
    IN6[Suction Reservoir]:::f

    P1[Centrifugal Pump Impeller]:::p
    P2[Volute Casing Diffuser]:::p
    P3[Delivery Valve]:::p
    P4[Delivery Pipe]:::p
    P5[Pressure Gauge]:::p
    P6[Delivery Reservoir]:::p

    OUT1[High Pressure Water to Mains]:::o
    OUT2[Noise and Vibration Monitoring]:::o

    IN1 --> IN2 --> IN3 --> IN4 --> P1
    IN6 --> IN5 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> OUT1
    P2 --> OUT2

    classDef a fill:#1f3864,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef b fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef c fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef d fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef e fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef f fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef p fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef o fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:**
> - **Black-blue boxes** (left): energy input chain (electrical → mechanical).
> - **Red boxes** (centre): the pump itself — impeller, volute, delivery.
> - **Green boxes** (right): useful output and monitoring.
> - The fluid path enters from the **suction reservoir** (bottom-left) and exits to the **delivery reservoir** (right).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📕 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Differentiate between impulse and reaction turbines.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Impulse Turbine:**
> - Fluid energy is converted to mechanical work due to *change in kinetic energy only*.
> - Fluid pressure remains **atmospheric** before, during, and after the runner.
> - Example: **Pelton Wheel**.
> - Suitable for **high head, low discharge** applications.

> **Reaction Turbine:**
> - Fluid energy is converted to mechanical work due to *change in both pressure and kinetic energy*.
> - Fluid pressure **drops** across the runner.
> - Example: **Francis Turbine** and **Kaplan Turbine**.
> - Suitable for **medium to low head** applications.

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by specific speed of a turbine? Why is it an important selection parameter?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **specific speed ($N_s$)** of a turbine is defined as the speed at which a *geometrically similar* turbine would run to develop a *unit power (1 kW)* under a *unit head (1 m)*. It is mathematically given by:
>
> $$
> N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
> $$
>
> **Why it is important:**
> 1. It is a **unique identifier of the turbine class** — Pelton, Francis, or Kaplan.
> 2. It allows engineers to **select the optimal turbine** for a given site head and power.
> 3. It ensures the chosen turbine operates at **high efficiency** for the available site conditions.
> 4. It guides the **preliminary design** of the runner, casing, and draft tube geometry.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Model)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

> **(a) Classify hydraulic turbines based on:**
> **(i) Type of energy at the inlet of the runner.**
> **(ii) Direction of flow through the runner.**
> **(iii) Specific speed.**
> **Explain each classification with suitable examples and neat sketches.** `[7 Marks]`

#### **Model Solution:**

**(i) Based on Type of Energy at Inlet:**

The energy of a fluid in motion is of two types: **pressure energy** and **kinetic energy**. Hydraulic turbines are classified as:

- **Impulse Turbine:** At the inlet of the runner, the fluid possesses **pure kinetic energy**. Pressure energy is converted to kinetic energy in the **nozzle** upstream of the runner. The runner operates at *atmospheric pressure* throughout. Example: **Pelton Wheel** — water jet from a nozzle strikes cup-shaped buckets mounted on a wheel.

- **Reaction Turbine:** At the inlet of the runner, the fluid possesses **both pressure and kinetic energy**. Part of the pressure energy is converted to kinetic energy *as the fluid flows through the runner*. The runner operates under a *pressure difference* between inlet and outlet. Example: **Francis Turbine** — fluid flows under pressure through a spiral casing, guide vanes, and then through the runner where pressure energy is partially converted to kinetic and then to mechanical work.

**(ii) Based on Direction of Flow Through the Runner:**

- **Tangential Flow:** Fluid enters the runner in the *plane of rotation*, tangentially. Example: **Pelton Wheel** — the jet strikes the buckets tangentially.
- **Radial Flow:** Fluid flows *perpendicular to the axis of rotation* — radially inward or outward. Example: **Old Francis Turbine** (radial inward).
- **Axial Flow:** Fluid flows *parallel to the axis of rotation*. Example: **Kaplan Turbine** — water flows axially through the runner.
- **Mixed Flow:** A combination of radial and axial. Example: **Modern Francis Turbine**.

**(iii) Based on Specific Speed ($N_s$):**

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad \text{(in SI: kW–m–rpm units)}
$$

| $N_s$ Range | Turbine Class | Example |
|-------------|--------------|---------|
| 10 – 35 | Pelton (Single Jet) | High head (300 m+) |
| 35 – 60 | Pelton (Multi-Jet) | Very high head, low flow |
| 60 – 300 | Francis | Medium head (30 – 250 m) |
| 300 – 1000 | Kaplan / Propeller | Low head (< 30 m) |

> **Sketch (Velocity Triangle of Pelton):** Show inlet and outlet velocity triangles with $V_1$, $V_{r1}$, $u$, bucket angle $\beta$, etc.

---

> **(b) Compare the working principle and construction of a Pelton Wheel and a Francis Turbine. List the typical head range, specific speed, and two real-world applications for each.** `[7 Marks]`

#### **Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Energy Conversion** | Impulse: K.E. → Mechanical | Reaction: P.E. + K.E. → Mechanical |
| **Head Range** | High (250 – 1800 m) | Medium (30 – 250 m) |
| **Specific Speed** | 10 – 35 (single jet) | 60 – 300 |
| **Runner Shape** | Disc with cup-shaped buckets | Curved blades on a wheel |
| **Casing** | No casing (operates in air) | Spiral casing with guide vanes |
| **Pressure on Runner** | Atmospheric (constant) | Drops across the runner |
| **Water Regulation** | Needle valve + deflector | Guide vanes (wicket gates) |
| **Setting** | Horizontal shaft common | Vertical shaft common |
| **Real-world Application 1** | Nathpa Jhakri HEP (1500 m, Sutlej River) | Bhakra Nangal Dam (128 m) |
| **Real-world Application 2** | Underground high-head stations in Himalayas | Sharavathy Generating Station (Karnataka) |
| **Construction Complexity** | Simple, no draft tube needed | Complex (draft tube for suction recovery) |
| **Part-Load Efficiency** | Poor for single jet | Good |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Moderate to high |

> **Working Principle Summary:**
> - **Pelton Wheel:** A high-velocity water jet from a nozzle strikes the *splitter ridge* of the double-cup bucket. The jet is deflected by ~165°–170°, reversing its direction. The change in momentum of the jet imparts an impulse to the bucket, causing rotation. The runner operates at *atmospheric pressure*.
> - **Francis Turbine:** Water enters the *spiral casing* under pressure, passes through the *guide vanes* (which regulate flow and impart swirl), and then enters the *runner* radially inward. Inside the runner, both pressure and kinetic energy are progressively converted to mechanical work. The water then exits axially into the *draft tube*, which recovers kinetic energy and converts it to suction head, increasing overall efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly **confuse** the **specific speed formula** between turbine and pump. The turbine formula has $H^{5/4}$ in the denominator and $N\sqrt{P}$ in the numerator, while the **pump** formula has $H^{3/4}$ in the denominator and $N\sqrt{Q}$ in the numerator. Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks** instantly. **Memorize the difference clearly.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a) Classify pumps in detail. Compare the working of centrifugal and reciprocating pumps with reference to discharge–head characteristics, priming, applications, and efficiency.** `[7 Marks]`

#### **Model Solution:**

**Classification of Pumps:**

```
HYDRAULIC PUMPS
│
├── ROTODYNAMIC PUMPS (Energy transfer by centrifugal force)
│   ├── Centrifugal Pumps
│   │   ├── Radial Vane
│   │   ├── Mixed Flow
│   │   └── Axial Flow
│   └── Special (Submersible, Self-Priming)
│
└── POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT PUMPS (Fixed volume displaced)
    ├── Reciprocating
    │   ├── Single Acting / Double Acting
    │   ├── Piston Type / Plunger Type
    └── Rotary
        ├── Gear, Vane, Screw
```

**Comparison — Centrifugal vs. Reciprocating Pump:**

| Aspect | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Energy Transfer** | Kinetic → Pressure | Direct displacement (volumetric) |
| **Discharge–Head Curve** | Discharge decreases as head increases (non-linear) | Discharge nearly constant irrespective of head (vertical line) |
| **Priming** | Required before starting (cannot self-prime) | Self-priming (creates vacuum on suction stroke) |
| **Flow Type** | Smooth, continuous | Pulsating (needs air vessel) |
| **Pressure Capability** | Low to moderate (single stage) | Very high pressure possible |
| **Efficiency vs. Load** | High only at design point | High over wide load range |
| **Maintenance** | Low (no valves, no pistons) | High (valves, pistons, packing) |
| **Speed** | High (1500–3000 rpm) | Low (60–200 rpm) |
| **Applications** | Water supply, fire-fighting, irrigation, drainage | Boiler feed, hydraulic press, oil well pumping |
| **Suitability for Dirty Fluids** | Good (open impeller) | Poor (valves clog) |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | High |
| **Suction Lift** | Theoretical limit 7 m (atmospheric) | Higher (up to ~8–9 m practical) |

> **Key Distinction:** A centrifugal pump's discharge is *sensitive* to head — close the delivery valve and discharge falls to zero (the energy is dissipated as heat in the fluid). A reciprocating pump's discharge is *insensitive* to head — close the delivery valve and the pump will simply build up pressure (and may burst the pipe if there is no relief valve). This is why centrifugal pumps can be started with the delivery valve **closed**, but reciprocating pumps require a **relief valve** on the delivery line.

---

> **(b) Define specific speed of a pump. A centrifugal pump is required to deliver $Q = 0.05 \; m^3/s$ against a head of $H = 25 \; m$ at a speed of $N = 1450 \; \text{rpm}$. Calculate its specific speed and identify the type of pump.** `[7 Marks]`

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Formula for Specific Speed of a Pump:**

$$
N_{s,p} = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

where $N$ is in rpm, $Q$ in m³/s, and $H$ in metres.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values:**

$$
N_{s,p} = \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.05}}{(25)^{3/4}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term:**

- $\sqrt{0.05} = 0.2236$
- $25^{3/4} = (25^{1/2})^{3/2} = 5^{1.5} = 5 \times \sqrt{5} = 5 \times 2.236 = 11.18$

**Step 4 — Compute Final Value:**

$$
N_{s,p} = \frac{1450 \times 0.2236}{11.18} = \frac{324.22}{11.18} \approx 29.0
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Pump Type (Standard Ranges):**

| Specific Speed Range | Pump Type |
|---------------------|-----------|
| 10 – 30 | **Low specific speed** — Radial vane centrifugal pump |
| 30 – 80 | **Medium specific speed** — Francis vane centrifugal pump |
| 80 – 300 | **High specific speed** — Mixed flow pump |
| 300 – 1000 | **Very high specific speed** — Axial flow / propeller pump |

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

Since $N_s \approx 29.0$, which falls in the range **10 – 30**, the pump is a **radial vane (low specific speed) centrifugal pump**.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students forget the **3/4 power** on $H$ for the pump formula and use 5/4 (which is the turbine formula). This single mistake produces a wildly wrong $N_s$ and a wrong identification.
> - Another common error: writing $N$ in rps (rev/sec) instead of rpm. Always **check units** before substituting.
> - Always state the **standard range first**, then place the calculated value within it for full marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read this before the exam:**

- ✅ **Turbine vs. Pump** is a **reversal of energy flow** — the same Euler equation applies with signs flipped.
- ✅ **Impulse turbines** (Pelton) operate at *atmospheric pressure* on the runner; **Reaction turbines** (Francis, Kaplan) have a *pressure drop* across the runner.
- ✅ **Specific Speed of Turbine:** $N_s = N\sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ — formula in (kW, m, rpm).
- ✅ **Specific Speed of Pump:** $N_s = N\sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ — formula in (m³/s, m, rpm).
- ✅ **Pelton Wheel** is the only turbine among the three that has **no draft tube**.
- ✅ **Pelton Wheel** uses a **needle valve and deflector** for flow regulation; **Francis** uses **guide vanes**; **Kaplan** uses **adjustable runner blades**.
- ✅ **Centrifugal pump** is *rotodynamic*; **reciprocating pump** is *positive displacement*.
- ✅ Centrifugal pumps have a **falling head–discharge curve**; reciprocating pumps have a **vertical head–discharge curve** (discharge nearly independent of head).
- ✅ Centrifugal pumps need **priming**; reciprocating pumps are **self-priming**.
- ✅ Reciprocating pumps need a **relief valve** on delivery (because they can build up dangerous pressure).
- ✅ Centrifugal pumps can be started with the **delivery valve closed** (energy goes into circulating water).
- ✅ **High Nₛ** → high flow, low head → **Kaplan / Axial**; **Low Nₛ** → low flow, high head → **Pelton**.
- ✅ **Shaft power** = Hydraulic power / overall efficiency.
- ✅ **Draft tube** is used only in reaction turbines to recover kinetic energy at the outlet and improve NPSH.
- ✅ **Specific speed is a *class* indicator**, NOT the actual running speed of the machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **📚 Recommended Self-Study (KTU Module 4, Weeks 9–11):**
> - Revise velocity triangle derivations for Pelton and Francis runner.
> - Practice the **manometric head** and **slip** calculations for reciprocating pumps.
> - Read about **governing mechanisms** of each turbine type (deflector, guide vanes, blade adjustment).
> - Be ready to draw a **block diagram of a hydro-electric power station** showing the turbine-generator-load chain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pelton, Francis and Kaplan Turbines: Principle, Working, Head, Work Done and Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pelton, Francis and Kaplan Turbines: Principle, Working, Head, Work Done and Efficiencies # MODULE 4 — HYDRAULIC TURBINES: PELTON, FRANCIS & KAPLAN ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **hydraulic turbine** is a rotary mechanical device that converts the **kinetic energy** and/or **pressure energy** of a flowing fluid (typically water) into useful mechanical work (shaft rotation). Based on the energy conversion mechanism, hydraulic turbines are classified into two broad families: - **Impulse Turbines (e.g., Pelton Wheel):** The entire pressure head of water is first converted into a high-velocity jet outside the runner, and the kinetic energy of this jet is then impinged on the runner buckets. *Atmospheric pressure acts on the runner throughout.* - **Reaction Turbines (e.g., Francis and Kaplan):** Water under pressure flows through the runner itself, and the pressure drop across the runner (combined with kinetic energy change) produces the driving force. *Pressure drop occurs across the rotating blades.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Focus Point:** A **Pelton Wheel** is a tangential-flow impulse turbine, a **Francis Turbine** is an inward-flow mixed/Francis reaction turbine, and a **Kaplan Turbine** is an axial-flow reaction turbine. These three cover approximately 95% of all hydroelectric installations worldwide. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a child spinning a top by flicking it with their finger (impulse) versus a child holding a top underwater where flowing water pushes the blades (reaction). | Turbine | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | **Pelton Wheel** | A stationary water pistol firing a single solid jet at cup-shaped spoons mounted on a rotating disc — like a water-powered percussion wheel. | | **Francis Turbine** | A shower head with curved vanes mounted on a wheel, where water spiralling inward through guide vanes pushes the wheel while also swirling inside. | | **Kaplan Turbine** | A submarine propeller working in reverse, with adjustable blades that twist as water flows axially through them, much like an aircraft propeller in a water tunnel. | ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Density of water ($\rho$):** $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Specific weight of water ($\gamma = \rho g$):** $9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ - **Power unit (SI):** $1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W}$; $1 \text{ HP} = 745.7 \text{ W}$ > [!NOTE] > **Head ($H$):** The energy per unit weight of fluid, measured in **metres (m)** of water. Velocity head $\frac{V^2}{2g}$, pressure head $\frac{P}{\gamma}$, and elevation head $z$ all combine to give the **Total Head** $H = \frac{P}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z$. ### Classification Tree > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification by Specific Speed ($N_s$):** > - Pelton Wheel: Low $N_s$ ($10$ to $35$, single jet) > - Francis Turbine: Medium $N_s$ ($60$ to $400$) > - Kaplan Turbine: High $N_s$ ($300$ to $1000$) > > The **specific speed** dictates the geometry, head range, and application of the turbine. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 PELTON WHEEL (Tangential-Flow Impulse Turbine) ### Principle of Working Water from a high-head reservoir is conveyed through a long penstock to the nozzle assembly. A **needle valve** inside the nozzle shapes the water into a high-velocity circular jet. This jet strikes the **double-cup (hemispherical) buckets** mounted on the periphery of a runner. The jet is split and deflected through nearly $165°$ to $170°$, reversing its momentum direction. By **Newton's Second Law**, this rate of change of momentum produces an impulsive force that rotates the runner. ### Velocity Diagram for a Single Bucket Let the following velocity notation hold (all velocities in $\text{m/s}$): - $V_1$ = Velocity of the jet at nozzle exit - $V$ = Absolute velocity of the jet (also $V_1$ for Pelton) - $u$ = Tangential (blade) velocity at the mean bucket circle - $V_{r1}$ = Relative velocity of jet with respect to bucket at inlet - $V_{w1}$ = Whirl (tangential component of absolute velocity) of jet at inlet - $\beta$ = Bucket angle (typically $15°$ to $20°$ for max efficiency) - $\alpha$ = Nozzle angle ($0°$ for Pelton, jet is purely axial in tangential plane) - $\theta$ = Deflection angle of the jet at outlet (typical $165°$ to $170°$) ### Key Working Steps 1. **High-pressure water** at the base of the penstock possesses a large pressure head. 2. As the water exits the **nozzle**, pressure energy converts almost completely to **kinetic energy** (atmospheric pressure now prevails at the runner). 3. The jet (with $\alpha = 0$) strikes the moving bucket, so the **relative velocity** $V_{r1}$ makes an angle $\beta$ with the direction of bucket motion. 4. The relative velocity magnitude is conserved (frictionless assumption) but its direction reverses: $V_{r2} = kV_{r1}$ where the bucket friction coefficient $k \approx 0.85$ to $0.98$ (in KTU problems, take $k = 1$ for ideal, $0.9$ for typical). 5. The exiting jet has a **whirl component opposite to runner motion**, producing the net force. ## 2.2 FRANCIS TURBINE (Inward-Flow Reaction Turbine) ### Principle of Working Water from the penstock enters a **scroll casing** (spiral casing), passes through a set of stationary **guide vanes** (wicket gates) that regulate the flow rate, and then flows radially inward over the **runner blades**. The pressure drops substantially as water flows over the runner. The combined effect of **pressure drop and swirl removal** imparts energy to the runner. A **draft tube** below the runner recovers the exit kinetic energy by converting it back to pressure head. ### Velocity Triangles at Inlet and Outlet - $V_1, V_2$ = Absolute velocities at inlet/outlet - $V_{r1}, V_{r2}$ = Relative velocities at inlet/outlet - $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = Whirl components of absolute velocities - $V_{f1}, V_{f2}$ = Flow (radial) components - $u_1, u_2$ = Blade velocities at inlet/outlet - $\beta_1, \beta_2$ = Blade angles at inlet/outlet ## 2.3 KAPLAN TURBINE (Axial-Flow Reaction Turbine) ### Principle of Working Water flows axially through the runner along the shaft axis. The runner has **3 to 6 adjustable blades** (like a propeller). A **hub** of streamlined cross-section accelerates the flow. The runner is preceded by guide vanes (wicket gates) and followed by a **draft tube**.
