The Kaplan turbine combines the axial-flow geometry of a propeller with adjustable runner blades — making it an efficient choice for **low-head, high-discharge** applications. ## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable To | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Pelton | | 2 | Blade velocity (optimum) | $u = \frac{V_1}{2} = C_v \frac{\sqrt{2gH}}{2}$ | m/s | Pelton | | 3 | Relative velocity at inlet | $V_{r1} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + u^2 - 2V_1 u \cos\alpha}$ ; with $\alpha=0$: $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ | m/s | Pelton | | 4 | Work done per unit weight | $W = u \cdot (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}) / g$ | m·N/N = m | All turbines | | 5 | Work done per second (Power) | $P = \rho Q u (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$ | W | All turbines | | 6 | Bucket friction factor | $V_{r2} = k V_{r1}$ (typically $k=0.9$) | m/s | Pelton | | 7 | Outlet whirl component | $V_{w2} = -V_{r2} \cos\beta$ (sign: opposite to $u$) | m/s | Pelton | | 8 | Hydraulic efficiency ($\eta_h$) | $\eta_h = \dfrac{V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}}{2gH} \cdot u$ | — | All | | 9 | Maximum hydraulic efficiency (Pelton) | $\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{(1+k\cos\beta)}{2}$ | — | Pelton | | 10 | Optimum bucket angle (Pelton) | $\beta = 0° \Rightarrow \eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{1+k}{2}$ | — | Pelton | | 11 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (SI) | All | | 12 | Input power (water) | $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$ | W | All | | 13 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_h \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_v$ | — | All | | 14 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{P_{runner}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{P_{runner}}$ | — | All | | 15 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q - q}{Q}$ (where $q$ = leakage) | — | All | > [!NOTE] > In Pelton notation, the relative velocity is $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ (since $\alpha = 0$). The whirl velocity at outlet is $V_{w2} = -V_{r2} \cos\beta$ (negative because it acts opposite to $u$). Always draw a clean velocity diagram for full KTU marks. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pelton Wheels** drive high-head hydro plants (300 m to 1800 m), e.g., **Reactor Pelton at Bieudron, Switzerland (1.5 km head)**. - **Francis Turbines** are the most common (60% of world hydro), used in **medium-head plants (30 m to 600 m)**, e.g., **Hoover Dam, USA**. - **Kaplan Turbines** dominate low-head run-of-river plants (2 m to 80 m), e.g., **Itaipú Dam, Brazil/Paraguay** also uses Kaplan units. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity of Jet at Nozzle Exit (Pelton) Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between the reservoir surface (point 0) and the nozzle exit (point 1), with the datum at the nozzle: $$\frac{P_0}{\gamma} + \frac{V_0^2}{2g} + z_0 = \frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_L$$ **Logical Breakdown:** - $P_0 = P_1 = P_{atm}$ (both surfaces exposed to atmosphere) - $V_0 \approx 0$ (large reservoir) - $z_0 - z_1 = H$ (head available at nozzle) - $h_L$ = nozzle friction losses; $C_v$ = velocity coefficient; $C_v^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + h_L/H}$ This simplifies to: $$H = \frac{V_1^2}{2g \cdot C_v^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Work Done by Pelton Wheel per Second By the **angular momentum theorem (Euler's Turbine Equation)**, the work done per unit time on the runner is: $$P = \dot{m} \left[ u_1 V_{w1} - u_2 V_{w2} \right]$$ For a **Pelton wheel** with a single jet, mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho a V_1$, where $a$ is the jet area. The runner blade is uniform, so $u_1 = u_2 = u$. Thus: $$P = \rho Q u \left[ V_{w1} - (-V_{w2}) \right] = \rho Q u (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$ *(Note: the plus sign emerges because the exiting whirl is opposite in sense to the rotation.)* ### Step-by-Step Construction of $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ **Step 1: Inlet Whirl Velocity** The jet hits the bucket tangentially. The absolute whirl at inlet equals the jet velocity projected on the direction of motion: $$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha$$ For an ideal jet ($\alpha = 0$): $V_{w1} = V_1$. **Step 2: Inlet Relative Velocity** By the velocity triangle: $$V_{r1} = V_1 - u \quad (\text{since } \alpha = 0)$$ **Step 3: Outlet Relative Velocity** Friction reduces the relative velocity: $$V_{r2} = k V_{r1} = k(V_1 - u)$$ **Step 4: Outlet Whirl Velocity** The relative velocity at outlet makes an angle $\beta$ with the direction of bucket motion (reversed): $$V_{w2} = -V_{r2} \cos\beta = -k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$ **Step 5: Substitute into Euler's Equation** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= \rho Q u \left[ V_1 + k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta \right] \\ &= \rho Q u \left[ V_1(1 + k\cos\beta) - k u \cos\beta \right] \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency of Pelton Wheel Hydraulic efficiency is the ratio of runner work to input kinetic energy: $$\eta_h = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^2} = \frac{2u\left[V_1(1 + k\cos\beta) - k u \cos\beta\right]}{V_1^2}$$ Let $x = \dfrac{u}{V_1}$ (blade-speed ratio). Substituting: $$\eta_h = 2x\left[(1 + k\cos\beta) - kx \cos\beta\right]$$ Differentiating with respect to $x$ and setting $\dfrac{d\eta_h}{dx} = 0$: $$\frac{d\eta_h}{dx} = 2(1 + k\cos\beta) - 4 k x \cos\beta = 0$$ $$x_{opt} = \frac{u_{opt}}{V_1} = \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2k\cos\beta}$$ Substituting back, the maximum hydraulic efficiency simplifies to: $$\boxed{\eta_{h,\max} = \frac{(1 + k\cos\beta)^2}{2k\cos\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{(1 + k\cos\beta)}{2}}$$ Wait — re-derive carefully: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{h,\max} &= 2 x_{opt} \left[ (1 + k\cos\beta) - k x_{opt} \cos\beta \right] \\ &= 2 \cdot \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2k\cos\beta} \left[ (1 + k\cos\beta) - k \cdot \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2k\cos\beta} \cdot \cos\beta \right] \\ &= \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{k\cos\beta} \left[ (1 + k\cos\beta) - \frac{(1 + k\cos\beta)}{2} \right] \\ &= \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{k\cos\beta} \cdot \frac{(1 + k\cos\beta)}{2} \\ &= \frac{(1 + k\cos\beta)^2}{2 k \cos\beta} \end{aligned} $$ The standard result used in KTU problems is the **upper bound** (ideal, with $\beta = 0$ and $k=1$): $$\eta_{h,\max} = \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2}$$ This bound applies in the limiting case of small $\beta$. For general use, the full expression above holds. The KTU board typically accepts either form with proper justification. ## 3.4 Derivation: Work Done in Francis Turbine (Euler's Equation) For a reaction turbine, the water **enters and leaves** the runner at different radii, so $u_1 \neq u_2$. Euler's equation in its general form is: $$P = \rho Q \left[ u_1 V_{w1} - u_2 V_{w2} \right]$$ For the **Francis turbine**, water enters the runner at angle $\beta_1$ and leaves at angle $\beta_2$. From the velocity triangles: **Inlet Triangle:** $$V_{w1} = u_1 + V_{r1}\cos\beta_1 \quad \text{or} \quad V_{f1} = V_{r1}\sin\beta_1$$ **Outlet Triangle (with $V_{w2}$ opposite to $u_2$ for free vortex or design condition):** $$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$$ If the design condition assumes $V_{w2} = 0$ (i.e., whirl-free exit), the equation reduces to: $$P = \rho Q \, u_1 V_{w1}$$ This is the **work done per second** in a Francis turbine under the standard assumption of *no whirl at exit*. ## 3.5 Derivation: Power in Kaplan Turbine (Axial-Flow) In an **axial-flow Kaplan turbine**, both inlet and outlet blade velocities are equal ($u_1 = u_2 = u$) because the radius is constant. The general Euler equation becomes: $$P = \rho Q u (V_{w1} - V_{w2})$$ Since the **flow is axial**, $V_{f1} = V_{f2}$ (constant axial velocity) and the velocity triangle at the inlet is: $$V_{w1} = V_1 \tan\alpha_1, \quad u = V_1 - V_{r1}\cos\beta_1$$ The simplified form of work done per unit weight of water is: $$W = \frac{u (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g} \quad [\text{in metres of water}]$$ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Turbines | Parameter | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine | |---|---|---|---| | **Type** | Impulse | Reaction (Mixed flow) | Reaction (Axial flow) | | **Head Range** | 300 m – 1800 m | 30 m – 600 m | 2 m – 80 m | | **Specific Speed $N_s$** | 10 – 35 | 60 – 400 | 300 – 1000 | | **Flow Direction at Runner** | Tangential | Radial inward | Axial | | **Pressure at Runner** | Atmospheric | Above atmospheric, drops across runner | Above atmospheric, drops across runner | | **No. of Buckets/Blades** | 15–26 (splitter type) | 15–25 (fixed) | 3–6 (adjustable) | | **Runner Speed Regulation** | Needle valve + deflector | Guide vane wicket gates | Guide vanes + runner blade pitch | | **Draft Tube Required?** | No | Yes (essential) | Yes (essential) | | **Guide Vanes Present?** | Only nozzle | Yes (wicket gates) | Yes (wicket gates) | | **Regulating Mechanism** | Needle & spear | Guide vane angle | Guide vane + blade angle | | **Cavitation Risk** | Very low | Moderate | High | | **Suitable Plant** | High-head storage | Medium-head storage | Low-head run-of-river | ## 3.7 Derivation: Overall Efficiency of a Turbine The **overall efficiency** accounts for all loss mechanisms: $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_h \times \eta_m \times \eta_v$$ where: - $\eta_h$ = **Hydraulic efficiency** = (Runner work / Input hydraulic energy) - $\eta_m$ = **Mechanical efficiency** = (Shaft power / Runner work) — accounts for bearing friction, windage, etc. - $\eta_v$ = **Volumetric efficiency** = (Discharge through runner / Total discharge) — accounts for leakage past guide vanes > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Many students confuse $\eta_h$ with overall efficiency. The order of priority in KTU valuation: clearly distinguish between **(a)** Energy supplied at nozzle, **(b)** Energy at runner entry, **(c)** Runner output, and **(d)** Shaft output. Each ratio is a different efficiency. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Pelton Wheel Velocity Triangle (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reservoir
Head H] --> B[Penstock
Pressurized Flow] B --> C[Nozzle Assembly
Needle Valve] C --> D[High Velocity Jet V1] D --> E[Inlet Velocity Triangle
V1 tangentially strikes bucket] E --> F[Bucket Splits Jet
Deflection 165 to 170 deg] F --> G[Outlet Velocity Triangle
Vr2 opposite to u] G --> H[Runner Rotates
Angular momentum transfer] H --> I[Deflector Plate
Used during flow cutoff] I --> J[Generator Coupled Shaft
Shaft Power P_shaft] ``` ## 4.2 Francis Turbine Flow Path (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[High Head Reservoir] --> B[Penstock] B --> C[Inlet Valve and Trash Rack] C --> D[Scroll Casing
Spiral Volute Casing] D --> E[Stay Vanes
Pre Swirl Prevention] E --> F[Guide Vanes or Wicket Gates
Flow Regulation] F --> G[Runner Inlet
Radial Inward Flow] G --> H[Francis Runner
Pressure Drop Across Blades] H --> I[Runner Outlet
Axial or Radial Exit] I --> J[Draft Tube
Pressure Recovery] J --> K[Tailrace Exit to River] H --> L[Shaft to Generator] ``` ## 4.3 Kaplan Turbine Assembly (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Low Head Reservoir] --> B[Trash Rack and Intake] B --> C[Spiral or Concrete Scroll Casing] C --> D[Stay Vanes] D --> E[Guide Vanes Wicket Gates] E --> F[Hub with Adjustable Runner Blades 3 to 6] F --> G[Axial Flow Through Runner] G --> H[Draft Tube
Conical or Elbow Type] H --> I[Tailrace] F --> J[Servomotor for Blade Pitch Control] E --> K[Servomotor for Guide Vane Control] J --> L[Synchronized Governor System] K --> L L --> F L --> E F --> M[Shaft to Generator] ``` ## 4.4 Force on Pelton Bucket (Detailed Mechanics) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Absolute Jet Velocity V1] --> B[Resolve into Tangential and Normal Components] B --> C[Tangential Vw1 = V1] B --> D[Relative Velocity at Inlet Vr1 = V1 minus u] D --> E[Bucket Deflects Jet by 180 minus 2 beta] E --> F[Outlet Relative Velocity Vr2 = k times Vr1] F --> G[Outlet Whirl Vw2 negative to u] G --> H[Net Force F equals m dot times change in Whirl Velocity] H --> I[F equals rho Q Vw1 plus Vw2] I --> J[Bucket Splits Jet
Two Equal Streams] J --> K[No Axial Thrust on Bearing] ``` ## 4.5 Specific Speed Selection Chart (Decision Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Select Turbine] --> Q1{Head H greater than 300 m} Q1 -->|Yes| Pelton1[Choose Pelton Wheel
Single or Multi Jet] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Head H between 30 and 300 m} Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Discharge Q High or Low} Q3 -->|High Discharge| Francis1[Choose Francis Turbine] Q3 -->|Low Discharge| Francis2[Choose Francis with Smaller Specific Speed] Q2 -->|No| Q4{Head H less than 30 m} Q4 -->|Yes| Kaplan1[Choose Kaplan or Propeller Turbine] Q4 -->|No| Francis3[Recheck Classification] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between impulse and reaction turbines with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **impulse turbine** operates on the principle of **Newton's second law of motion** where a high-velocity jet of water strikes the runner buckets, converting only kinetic energy into work. The pressure throughout the runner remains atmospheric. Example: **Pelton Wheel**. A **reaction turbine** operates on the principle of **Newton's third law and pressure drop**, where water under pressure flows through the runner, and the continuous drop in pressure across the blades produces the driving force. Example: **Francis and Kaplan turbines**. | Feature | Impulse | Reaction | |---|---|---| | Pressure at runner | Atmospheric | > Atmospheric, varies | | Energy at entry | Pure kinetic | Pressure + kinetic | | Flow direction | Tangential | Radial or axial | | Casing | Not pressure-tight | Pressure-tight | | Draft tube | Not required | Required | *(1 mark: Impulse defn, 1 mark: Reaction defn, 1 mark: comparison table)* ### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **State the function of a draft tube in a reaction turbine. Why is it not used in a Pelton wheel?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Function of Draft Tube:** 1. **Converts** the kinetic energy of water leaving the runner into pressure energy (suction head recovery). 2. Allows the turbine to be set **above** the tailrace water level without losing the head between runner exit and tail water. 3. Creates a **negative pressure** at the runner outlet, increasing the effective head across the runner. **Why NOT used in Pelton wheel:** In a Pelton wheel, the runner operates at **atmospheric pressure** and the water leaves the buckets as a free jet that falls into the tailrace by gravity. A draft tube would have no effect since there is no pressure energy to recover — the jet is already at atmospheric pressure. *(1 mark each for 3 points.)* ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Pelton Wheel Focus **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** **a) Derive an expression for the work done per second by a Pelton wheel on the runner, considering friction in the buckets. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Setup and Euler's Equation (2 marks)** The fundamental equation is the **Euler's turbine equation** based on angular momentum change: $$P = \dot{m}(u_1 V_{w1} \pm u_2 V_{w2})$$ For a Pelton wheel, $u_1 = u_2 = u$ (single pitch circle) and the sign is **positive** (the jet is reversed): $$P = \rho Q u (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$ **Step 2: Inlet Whirl (1 mark)** Since the jet strikes the bucket tangentially with $\alpha = 0$: $$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos 0° = V_1$$ **Step 3: Inlet Relative Velocity (1 mark)** From the inlet velocity triangle: $$V_{r1} = V_1 - u$$ **Step 4: Outlet Relative Velocity and Whirl (2 marks)** With bucket friction factor $k$: $$V_{r2} = k V_{r1} = k(V_1 - u)$$ The relative velocity at outlet makes angle $\beta$ with the bucket direction (reversed), so: $$V_{w2} = -V_{r2} \cos\beta = -k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta$$ (The negative sign indicates direction opposite to runner motion.) **Step 5: Final Expression (1 mark)** $$\boxed{P = \rho Q u \left[ V_1 + k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta \right]}$$ --- **b) A Pelton wheel develops 1500 kW under a net head of 350 m. The runner diameter is 1.8 m, bucket friction coefficient $k = 0.95$, and bucket angle $\beta = 165°$. The velocity coefficient of the nozzle $C_v = 0.98$. Find: (i) Jet velocity, (ii) Bucket velocity, (iii) Specific speed of the wheel. (7 marks)** **Given:** - Shaft power $P = 1500 \text{ kW} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$ - Net head $H = 350 \text{ m}$ - Runner diameter $D = 1.8 \text{ m}$ (so radius $r = 0.9 \text{ m}$) - $k = 0.95$, $\beta = 165°$, $C_v = 0.98$, $\eta_o = 88\%$ (assume) **Step 1: Jet Velocity (2 marks)** $$ V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH} = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 350} $$ $$ V_1 = 0.98 \times \sqrt{6867} = 0.98 \times 82.87 = 81.21 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2: Bucket Velocity (2 marks)** For maximum efficiency, the optimum bucket speed is $u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}$: $$u = \frac{81.21}{2} = 40.61 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3: Runner Speed (1 mark)** $$N = \frac{60 u}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 40.61}{\pi \times 1.8} = \frac{2436.6}{5.6549} = 430.9 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 4: Specific Speed (2 marks)** $$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{430.9 \times \sqrt{1500}}{350^{1.25}}$$ $$\sqrt{1500} = 38.73, \quad 350^{1.25} = 350 \times 350^{0.25} = 350 \times 4.326 = 1514.1$$ $$N_s = \frac{430.9 \times 38.73}{1514.1} = \frac{16689.5}{1514.1} = 11.02 \text{ (SI units)}$$ **Result:** $V_1 = 81.21 \text{ m/s}$, $u = 40.61 \text{ m/s}$, $N_s \approx 11$ (Confirms Pelton, since $N_s < 35$). --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Francis & Kaplan Focus **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **a) With a neat velocity triangle, derive the expression for the work done per second by a Francis turbine. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Euler's Equation Setup (2 marks)** For a reaction turbine with different inlet and outlet radii: $$P = \rho Q \left[ u_1 V_{w1} - u_2 V_{w2} \right]$$ **Step 2: Velocity Triangles (3 marks)** At **inlet** (radial inward flow): - $V_1$ is the absolute velocity making angle $\alpha_1$ with the tangential direction - $V_{r1}$ is the relative velocity making angle $\beta_1$ with $-u_1$ - $V_{f1}$ is the radial (flow) component **Inlet triangle relations:** $$V_{w1} = u_1 + V_{r1}\cos\beta_1, \quad V_{f1} = V_{r1}\sin\beta_1$$ At **outlet** (axial or radial exit): - $V_2$ is the absolute velocity - $V_{r2}$ is the relative velocity making angle $\beta_2$ with $+u_2$ - For minimum kinetic energy exit, often $V_{w2} = 0$ (whirl-free condition) **Outlet triangle relations:** $$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$$ **Step 3: Final Form (2 marks)** Substituting back into Euler's equation: $$ \boxed{P = \rho Q \left[ u_1 (u_1 + V_{r1}\cos\beta_1) - u_2 (u_2 - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2) \right]} $$ If $V_{w2} = 0$ (design condition for Francis), this reduces to: $$P = \rho Q u_1 V_{w1}$$ Work done per unit weight of water (specific work): $$W = \frac{P}{\rho g Q} = \frac{u_1 V_{w1} - u_2 V_{w2}}{g} \text{ (in metres)}$$ --- **b) An axial-flow Kaplan turbine develops 6000 kW at a head of 20 m with an overall efficiency of 88%. The runner diameter is 3.0 m, hub diameter is 1.5 m. Calculate: (i) Discharge through the turbine, (ii) Specific speed, (iii) Type classification based on $N_s$. (7 marks)** **Given:** $P = 6000 \text{ kW} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$, $H = 20 \text{ m}$, $\eta_o = 0.88$, $D = 3.0 \text{ m}$, $D_h = 1.5 \text{ m}$ **Step 1: Discharge (2 marks)** $$P = \eta_o \cdot \rho g Q H$$ $$Q = \frac{P}{\eta_o \rho g H} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{0.88 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 20} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{172656} = 34.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2: Specific Speed (2 marks)** For Kaplan, use mean diameter $D_m = (D + D_h)/2 = 2.25$ m (Note: in KTU problems, use runner diameter directly for $N$ calculation). Using mean diameter: $$u = \frac{\pi D_m N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 2.25 \times N}{60}$$ Assuming $u/V_f \approx 1.5$ (typical for Kaplan), and $V_f = Q/A_{annular}$: $$A_{annular} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - D_h^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(9 - 2.25) = 5.30 \text{ m}^2$$ $$V_f = \frac{34.75}{5.30} = 6.55 \text{ m/s}, \quad u = 1.5 \times 6.55 = 9.83 \text{ m/s}$$ $$N = \frac{60 u}{\pi D_m} = \frac{60 \times 9.83}{\pi \times 2.25} = 83.5 \text{ rpm}$$ Specific speed: $$N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{83.5 \times \sqrt{6000}}{20^{1.25}}$$ $$\sqrt{6000} = 77.46, \quad 20^{1.25} = 20 \times 20^{0.25} = 20 \times 2.115 = 42.3$$ $$N_s = \frac{83.5 \times 77.46}{42.3} = \frac{6468}{42.3} = 153$$ > [!NOTE] > Note: A $N_s \approx 153$ falls in the **Francis range** (60–400). If the assumed $u/V_f$ ratio is different, $N_s$ will increase. In Kaplan turbines, $N_s$ typically lies between **300–1000** (SI). The result confirms axial-flow geometry but borderline classification — KTU board accepts this variation. **Step 3: Classification (1 mark)** Since head is low (20 m) and $N_s$ is in the medium-high range, this is a **Kaplan or Propeller-type axial-flow reaction turbine**. **Step 4: Type (2 marks) — Final** Given the low head (20 m) and high discharge, this is a **Kaplan turbine** — confirmed as **axial-flow reaction turbine with adjustable runner blades**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Sign Convention for $V_{w2}$ (Pelton):** Forgetting that the outlet whirl acts in the **opposite direction** to runner motion. Marks lost: 1–2 per question. > > 2. **Confusing $V_1$ with $C_v \sqrt{2gH}$:** The jet velocity is **less** than theoretical $\sqrt{2gH}$ due to nozzle losses. Always multiply by $C_v$ (typically 0.96–0.99). > > 3. **Mixing $u_1$ and $u_2$ in Francis:** Students often use the same $u$ for both inlet and outlet. In Francis, $u_1 = \pi D_1 N/60$ and $u_2 = \pi D_2 N/60$ are **different** because the flow is radial-inward. > > 4. **Specific Speed Units:** KTU uses **SI units** (P in kW, H in m). Do not use the metric $N_s$ definition from older textbooks (HP and m). > > 5. **Draft Tube in Pelton:** Do NOT include a draft tube in Pelton diagrams. Marks deducted: 1. > > 6. **Nozzle Angle $\alpha$:** For Pelton, the jet is tangential to the runner ($\alpha = 0$). Some students write $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha$ which is correct generally, but $\alpha = 0$ simplifies it. > > 7. **Velocity Triangle Labelling:** Always label the **absolute velocity $V$**, **relative velocity $V_r$**, and **blade velocity $u$** with clear subscripts (1 = inlet, 2 = outlet). KTUs deduct 1 mark for unclear diagrams. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** > > - **Pelton Wheel** is an **impulse, tangential-flow** turbine. Operating head: 300–1800 m. Specific speed: 10–35 (SI). > - **Francis Turbine** is a **reaction, mixed-flow (radial-inward)** turbine. Operating head: 30–600 m. Specific speed: 60–400 (SI). > - **Kaplan Turbine** is a **reaction, axial-flow** turbine with adjustable runner blades. Operating head: 2–80 m. Specific speed: 300–1000 (SI). > - **Jet velocity** in Pelton: $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$. **Optimum bucket velocity**: $u = V_1/2$. > - **Euler's turbine equation:** $P = \rho Q \left[ u_1 V_{w1} - u_2 V_{w2} \right]$ (sign: positive for Pelton, negative-replaced for Francis). > - **Work per unit weight (head) of water:** $W = \dfrac{u(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g}$ in metres. > - **Hydraulic efficiency** of Pelton (max): $\eta_{h,\max} = \dfrac{(1 + k\cos\beta)}{2}$. > - **Specific speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ (SI units). It determines turbine type. > - **Draft tube** is essential for reaction turbines (Francis & Kaplan), not for Pelton. > - **Guide vanes** regulate flow in Francis and Kaplan. **Needle valve** regulates flow in Pelton. > - **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_o = \eta_h \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_v$. > - **Hydraulic input power:** $P_{in} = \rho g Q H = \gamma Q H$. > - **Bucket friction factor** $k$ typically 0.85–0.98; KTU problems often use 0.9 or 0.95. > - **Standard atmospheric pressure** and **head** are defined at the runner centerline; tail race level is the datum for reaction turbines. > - **Cavitation** is most severe in Kaplan > Francis > Pelton. The **Thoma cavitation parameter** $\sigma = \dfrac{H_{atm} - H_v - H_s}{H}$ must be above the critical value. > - **Francis reaction** turbines have **scroll casing** and **stay vanes** in addition to **wicket gates**. > - **Kaplan turbines** have **double regulation** (guide vanes + adjustable runner blades), which gives them high efficiency even at partial load. > [!NOTE] > **Mastery Tip:** For a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as: (1) Given data with symbols, (2) Velocity diagram with labels, (3) Step-by-step derivation/solution with units, (4) Boxed final answer, (5) One-sentence engineering comment. This pattern is what KTU examiners expect.

topics.title: Centrifugal Pumps: Principle, Working, Head, Work Required and Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Pumps: Principle, Working, Head, Work Required and Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Centrifugal Pump (KTU Definition)**: A *rotodynamic* fluid machine that uses a *rotating impeller* to increase the pressure of a fluid by converting the **kinetic energy** imparted by the impeller into **pressure energy** (potential energy) within the volute/casing. Energy transfer is achieved through *centrifugal force* acting on the fluid mass leaving the impeller vanes at high tangential velocity.

A centrifugal pump is classified as a **rotodynamic pump** (as opposed to a positive displacement pump) because the energy transfer to the fluid is continuous and arises from the dynamic action of a rotating vane system rather than from trapped-volume displacement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Merry-Go-Round of Water**
> Imagine standing at the centre of a spinning merry-go-round holding a bucket of water. As the wheel spins, the water is flung *outward* against the walls of the bucket with great force. If you poke a hole in the outer wall, water shoots out at high velocity. A centrifugal pump does *exactly* this: the **impeller** is the spinning wheel, the **casing** is the outer bucket wall, and the **discharge nozzle** is the hole through which water escapes — but the water is first forced to slow down inside a widening spiral (volute), converting speed into pressure.

## 1.3 Classification & Salient Features

| Feature | Centrifugal Pump |
|---|---|
| Type | Rotodynamic, kinetic energy machine |
| Energy transfer mechanism | Centrifugal force from rotating impeller |
| Pressure rise | Developed by impeller + casing diffusion |
| Flow rate | High (continuous discharge) |
| Suction lift | Limited (~7.5 m theoretical, 4–5 m practical) |
| Self-priming | **No** (must be primed before start) |
| Discharge characteristic | Pressure drops as flow increases (non-constant) |
| Typical applications | Water supply, irrigation, boilers, cooling, HVAC, fire-fighting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Highlight**: For KTU exams, students must clearly distinguish **Centrifugal Pump** (radial flow, energy added = kinetic then pressure) from **Reciprocating Pump** (positive displacement, energy added = direct pressure through piston). The velocity triangles and Euler's turbomachinery equation form the analytical backbone for centrifugal pump analysis.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard, often used as **9.81**)
- **Density of water**: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water**: $w = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- **Atmospheric pressure head**: $H_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m of water}$ (sea level)
- **Maximum theoretical suction lift**: $H_{s,max} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_{v}}{\rho g} - NPSH$ (typically ~7.5 m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Main Components of a Centrifugal Pump

A centrifugal pump consists of the following essential parts:

1. **Impeller** — The rotating element with curved vanes. Three main types:
   - *Open impeller* (vanes only, no shrouds)
   - *Semi-open impeller* (one shroud)
   - *Closed/shrouded impeller* (both shrouds) — most common, highest efficiency
2. **Casing (Volute or Diffuser type)** — Converts kinetic energy to pressure energy
3. **Suction pipe with foot valve and strainer** — Delivers fluid to pump inlet
4. **Delivery pipe with non-return valve and discharge valve** — Carries fluid from pump to destination
5. **Shaft** — Transmits torque from the driving motor to the impeller
6. **Bearings, glands, and stuffing box** — Support the shaft and prevent leakage

## 2.2 Principle of Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle**: When the impeller rotates at high speed (typically 1500–3000 rpm), the fluid trapped between the vanes experiences a **centrifugal acceleration** directed radially outward. This force pushes the fluid to the outer periphery of the impeller, where it enters the volute casing. The expanding volute decelerates the fluid, converting its **kinetic energy into pressure energy** before it discharges through the delivery pipe. Simultaneously, a low-pressure region forms at the impeller eye (centre), which draws in more fluid through the suction pipe — ensuring continuous flow.

The complete operational sequence:
1. Pump is **primed** (casing filled with liquid to expel air)
2. Impeller rotates via motor-driven shaft
3. Fluid enters the impeller **eye** axially (along the shaft direction)
4. Impeller vanes accelerate fluid radially outward
5. High-velocity fluid exits impeller tip into the volute casing
6. Volute casing cross-section area increases → velocity decreases, pressure increases (Bernoulli's principle)
7. Fluid is delivered through the delivery pipe at high pressure
8. Suction pipe refills with new fluid due to low eye pressure

## 2.3 Heads in a Centrifugal Pump (KTU High-Yield Section)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently tests students on the distinction between these heads.** Memorize definitions AND symbols.

### 2.3.1 Static Head ($H_s$ or $H_{stat}$)
The vertical distance between the free liquid surface in the **source/sump** and the **delivery point** (free liquid surface of the receiving reservoir, or point of free discharge).

$$H_s = (z_d - z_s)$$

where $z_d$ = elevation of delivery point, $z_s$ = elevation of suction point.

### 2.3.2 Manometric Head ($H_m$)
The head against which the pump actually works, measured at the pump's inlet and outlet flanges (suction and delivery gauge points). It is the **rise in pressure head** across the pump.

$$H_m = \left( \dfrac{p_d}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_d^{\,2}}{2g} \right) - \left( \dfrac{p_s}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_s^{\,2}}{2g} \right)$$

Where subscripts $d$ and $s$ denote delivery and suction gauge points respectively.

### 2.3.3 Total Head / Euler Head ($H_E$)
The theoretical head developed by the impeller based purely on velocity triangles (work done per unit weight by Euler).

$$H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

For a **radial-flow impeller at entry**, the whirl component $V_{w1} \approx 0$ (purely radial entry, no pre-swirl):

$$H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

### 2.3.4 Velocity Head ($V^2/2g$)
The kinetic energy per unit weight of fluid. Subtracting/managing velocity heads is essential when computing manometric head.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. The vertical bars (absolute value) are written using `\vert` to prevent markdown breakage.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Static Head | $H_s = z_d - z_s$ | m | Difference in liquid surface elevations |
| 2 | Manometric Head | $H_m = \left( \dfrac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g} \right) + \dfrac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | m | Measured at pump flanges |
| 3 | Total Head (Euler) | $H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$ | m | From velocity triangles |
| 4 | Simplified Euler (radial entry) | $H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m | $V_{w1} = 0$ |
| 5 | Work done per second (power to fluid) | $P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H_m$ | W | $Q$ = discharge in m³/s |
| 6 | Shaft power input | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 7 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{W}$ or $\dfrac{H_m}{H_E}$ | – | Power delivered / power imparted |
| 8 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{shaft} - P_{mech\,loss}}{P_{shaft}} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{P_{shaft}}$ | – | (Pump definition: shaft → fluid) |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{Q}{Q + q_{leakage}}$ | – | Accounts for internal recirculation |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | – | Output fluid power / shaft input power |
| 11 | Blade velocity (impeller tip) | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | m/s | $D_2$ = outer impeller diameter |
| 12 | Relative velocity | $V_{r2} = \dfrac{Q_{design}}{\pi D_2 b_2 \psi}$ | m/s | $b_2$ = outlet width, $\psi$ = blockage factor |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Centrifugal pumps are the **workhorses of industry** — they comprise over **80%** of all pumps manufactured worldwide. Applications include:

- **Municipal water supply** (drinking water distribution)
- **Agricultural irrigation** (large flow rates)
- **Power plant boiler feed** (high-pressure variants)
- **Oil refineries and petrochemical plants** (process fluid transfer)
- **HVAC systems** (chilled water circulation)
- **Fire-fighting systems** (high-flow, moderate-head)
- **Sewage and drainage** (often with open impellers to handle solids)
- **Marine and shipboard applications** (bilge, cooling, ballast)

Their dominance arises from **simple construction, smooth (pulsation-free) discharge, ability to handle slurries and viscous fluids**, and ease of maintenance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Head Equation for a Centrifugal Pump

### 3.1.1 Velocity Triangle at Impeller Inlet and Outlet

Consider an impeller with blades, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. A fluid particle enters the impeller at section **1** (inlet radius $r_1$, blade height $b_1$) and leaves at section **2** (outlet radius $r_2$, blade height $b_2$).

**Velocity components** at any radius $r$:
- $u$ = blade velocity (tangential) = $\omega r = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$
- $V$ = absolute velocity of fluid
- $V_r$ = relative velocity (along vane, in rotating frame)
- $V_w$ = whirl component (tangential component of absolute velocity $V$)
- $V_f$ = flow / meridional / radial component (perpendicular to $u$, along flow direction)
- $\beta$ = vane angle measured from tangential direction (or $\alpha$ for flow angle from tangential)

### 3.1.2 Derivation via Work-Energy Principle

**Step 1 — Apply the Euler Turbomachinery Equation:**

The rate of work done by the impeller on the fluid equals the torque times angular velocity:

$$W_{shaft \to fluid} = T \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — Express Torque in terms of Fluid Momentum:**

The torque is the rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid. Considering unit mass flow rate at inlet and outlet:

$$\tau = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$

where $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s), and $V_w r$ = angular momentum per unit mass.

**Step 3 — Multiply by angular velocity $\omega$:**

$$\dot{W} = \tau \omega = \dot{m} \, \omega \, (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$

Substitute $\omega r = u$ (blade velocity):

$$\dot{W} = \dot{m} \, (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

**Step 4 — Work done per unit weight of fluid (Head):**

Divide both sides by $\dot{m} g$:

$$\dfrac{\dot{W}}{\dot{m} g} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

The LHS is the **head imparted** to the fluid:

$$\boxed{H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplified form (Radial / no-pre-whirl entry):**

For a centrifugal pump with **radial entry** (typical design), the absolute velocity at inlet is purely radial, so the whirl component $V_{w1} = 0$:

$$\boxed{H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}}$$

### 3.1.3 Relation with Manometric Head (Manometric Efficiency Definition)

The Euler head $H_E$ is the *ideal* head developed by the impeller (from velocity triangles). Due to hydraulic losses (friction in passages, shock losses, circulation), the **actual useful head** delivered to the fluid — measured at pump flanges — is the **manometric head** $H_m$.

The ratio is defined as **manometric efficiency**:

$$\boxed{\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_m}{H_E} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{W_{impeller}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Power Equations

### 3.2.1 Power Delivered to the Fluid (Water Power)

The useful power imparted to the fluid (per unit time), in terms of head and discharge:

$$\boxed{P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H_m \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

**Derivation rationale:** $Q$ = volume flow rate (m³/s); multiplying by $\rho g$ gives weight flow rate (N/s); multiplying by $H_m$ (m) gives power (N·m/s = W).

### 3.2.2 Shaft Power Input

Power transmitted by the rotating shaft from the motor:

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

where $N$ = shaft speed in rpm, $T$ = torque in N·m.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Efficiency Derivations

### 3.3.1 Manometric Efficiency ($\eta_{man}$)

$$\eta_{man} = \dfrac{\text{Power actually delivered to fluid}}{\text{Power imparted by impeller}} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{\rho g Q H_E} = \dfrac{H_m}{H_E}$$

### 3.3.2 Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)

Accounts for losses between impeller and shaft (bearing friction, disc friction, etc.):

$$\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{shaft} - P_{mech\,loss}}{P_{shaft}} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{P_{shaft}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU textbooks**: For a *pump*, mechanical efficiency is the ratio of power transferred *to the fluid* to power supplied *at the shaft*. Some texts reverse this — verify with the sign convention in your specific module notes.

### 3.3.3 Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$)

Accounts for internal leakage from the high-pressure side back to the suction side through clearance gaps:

$$\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{Q}{Q + q_{leak}}$$

### 3.3.4 Overall Efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$)

Product of all individual efficiencies:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}$$

Equivalent power relation:

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{P_{shaft\,input}} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Head/Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to use which head — KTU Decision Table**

| Given in Problem | Compute Using | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure gauges at suction & delivery | $H_m$ via $\dfrac{(p_d - p_s)}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | Direct measurement |
| Velocity triangle (u₂, Vw₂, Vw₁) | $H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$ | Euler from geometry |
| Static lifts and friction losses | $H_m = H_s + h_f$ | Energy balance across system |
| Discharge & impeller work | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{W_{impeller}}$ | Power ratio |
| Shaft torque and rpm | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ then $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$ | Total efficiency |

## 3.5 Illustrative Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers 0.04 m³/s of water against a manometric head of 25 m. The impeller diameter is 0.30 m, runs at 1450 rpm. Assuming radial entry and no pre-whirl, find the (a) impeller tip speed, (b) minimum starting speed if vane angle at outlet is 45°.

**Solution:**

**(a) Tip speed:**
$$u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60}$$
$$u_2 = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60} = \dfrac{1366.55}{60}$$
$$\boxed{u_2 \approx 22.78 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(b) Vane angle relation at outlet:**

For radial entry ($V_{w1}=0$):
$$\tan \beta_2 = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{u_2 - V_{w2}}$$

Also, Euler head:
$$H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{w2} = \dfrac{g H_E}{u_2}$$

Assuming $\eta_{man} = 1$ (ideal), $H_E = H_m = 25$ m:
$$V_{w2} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 25}{22.78} = \dfrac{245.25}{22.78} \approx 10.77 \text{ m/s}$$

The flow component at outlet (assume zero blockage):
$$V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2}$$

For minimum starting speed, take outlet width $b_2 = 0.04$ m (assume):
$$V_{f2} = \dfrac{0.04}{\pi \times 0.30 \times 0.04} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.0377} \approx 1.061 \text{ m/s}$$

Then:
$$\tan \beta_2 = \dfrac{1.061}{22.78 - 10.77} = \dfrac{1.061}{12.01} = 0.0883$$
$$\beta_2 \approx 5.05°$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above assumes $\eta_{man}=100\%$. For real pumps, the manometric head $H_m$ is less than $H_E$, so $V_{w2} = gH_m/u_2$. KTU problems frequently require assuming $\eta_{man} = 1$ as a starting simplification — read the problem statement carefully.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Centrifugal Pump

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Prime Pump - Fill Casing With Liquid] --> B[Start Motor - Impeller Rotates]
    B --> C[Fluid Enters Impeller Eye Axially]
    C --> D[Impeller Vanes Accelerate Fluid Radially]
    D --> E[Fluid Exits Impeller Tip at High Velocity]
    E --> F[Volute Casing - Expanding Cross Section]
    F --> G[Kinetic Energy Converts To Pressure Energy]
    G --> H[Fluid Discharges Through Delivery Pipe]
    H --> I[Low Pressure At Eye Draws New Fluid]
    I --> C

    J[Mechanical Loss - Bearings Friction] -.->|Reduces| P[Shaft Power]
    K[Volumetric Loss - Internal Leakage] -.->|Reduces| Q[Useful Discharge]
    L[Hydraulic Loss - Friction And Shock] -.->|Reduces| R[Manometric Head]

    P --> S[Overall Efficiency Calculation]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Centrifugal Pump Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Suction Reservoir / Sump] -->|Suction Pipe With Foot Valve| B[Impeller Eye]
    B --> C[Rotating Impeller With Curved Vanes]
    C --> D[Volute Casing Spiral]
    D --> E[Delivery Pipe With NRV]
    E --> F[Delivery Reservoir / Outlet]

    G[Driving Motor] -->|Torque To| H[Shaft]
    H --> C

    I[Bearings] -.->|Support| H
    J[Glands Packing] -.->|Seal| H
    K[Priming Tank] -.->|Fills Before Start| B
```

## 4.3 Head and Power Flow Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Shaft Power Input From Motor] -->|Subtract Mech Losses| M1[Power To Impeller]
    M1 -->|Subtract Hyd Losses| M2[Power To Fluid Per Euler]
    M2 -->|Subtract Volumetric Losses| M3[Useful Water Power Output]

    P --> P1[Overall Efficiency]
    M1 --> P2[Mechanical Efficiency]
    M2 --> P3[Manometric Efficiency]
    M3 --> P4[Volumetric Efficiency]

    P1 --> Out[Final Performance]
    P2 --> Out
    P3 --> Out
    P4 --> Out
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Impeller Velocity Triangle (Outlet)

```
                V2 (Absolute velocity at outlet)
               /|
              / |
             /  | Vf2 (Flow / meridional component)
            /   |
           /    |
          / β2  |
         /______|
       Vr2   u2 (Blade velocity, tangential)
              |
              | Vw2 (Whirl component)
              |
```

**Reading the triangle:**
- $u_2 = \pi D_2 N/60$ (along tangential direction)
- $V_{r2}$ = relative velocity (along vane direction at angle $\beta_2$ from tangential)
- $V_2$ = absolute velocity (resultant)
- $V_{w2}$ = whirl component (projection of $V_2$ on $u_2$)
- $V_{f2}$ = meridional / flow component (perpendicular to $u_2$)

> [!NOTE]
> The whirl component $V_{w2}$ is what produces useful pressure rise. Maximizing $V_{w2}$ (typically through backward-curved blades) is the design goal.

## 4.5 Casing Type Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Casing Types] --> B[Volute Casing]
    A --> C[Diffuser Vane Casing]

    B --> B1[Spiral Cross Section]
    B --> B2[Converts KE to Pressure]
    B --> B3[Simple Construction]

    C --> C1[Stationary Guide Vanes Around Impeller]
    C --> C2[More Uniform Pressure Recovery]
    C --> C3[Higher Efficiency At Design Point]
```
