# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define manometric efficiency of a centrifugal pump and write its expression in terms of heads.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Manometric efficiency is defined as the ratio of the manometric head (actual head developed as measured at pump flanges) to the Euler head (head imparted to the fluid by the impeller, derived from velocity triangles). It quantifies the hydraulic performance of the impeller-casing combination.
>
> $$\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_m}{H_E} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{W_{impeller}}$$
>
> where $H_m$ = manometric head (m), $H_E$ = Euler head (m), $Q$ = discharge (m³/s), $W_{impeller}$ = work done by impeller per second (W).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Why is priming necessary for a centrifugal pump but not for a reciprocating pump?**

> **Model Answer:**
> A centrifugal pump can develop pressure **only if liquid is present inside the casing**. If air is present, the impeller rotates the low-density air, generating negligible pressure rise, and the pump cannot create the suction lift needed to draw water up the suction pipe. Hence, priming (filling the casing with liquid) is essential.
>
> A reciprocating pump, being a positive displacement machine, creates suction by *physically displacing volume* via the piston, regardless of whether the fluid is liquid or (briefly) air. Hence it is **self-priming**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a centrifugal pump. List its main components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The impeller of a centrifugal pump has an outer diameter of 0.40 m and runs at 1200 rpm. The vane angle at outlet is 30°. Assuming radial entry and a manometric efficiency of 90%, find the manometric head developed when the discharge is 0.05 m³/s. Take outlet width $b_2 = 0.05$ m. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle:** When the impeller rotates, the fluid trapped between the vanes is flung outward by centrifugal force. It enters the volute casing at high velocity, where the expanding cross-section decelerates the fluid and converts kinetic energy into pressure energy (per Bernoulli's principle). The low pressure at the impeller eye draws fresh fluid continuously through the suction pipe, ensuring steady discharge.

**Main Components:**
1. Impeller (closed/semi-open/open)
2. Casing (volute or diffuser type)
3. Suction pipe with foot valve and strainer
4. Delivery pipe with non-return valve and sluice valve
5. Shaft (connects impeller to motor)
6. Bearings
7. Stuffing box and gland packing
8. Priming tank or cock

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.2 mermaid diagram for the layout.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D_2 = 0.40$ m
- $N = 1200$ rpm
- $\beta_2 = 30°$
- Radial entry: $V_{w1} = 0$
- $\eta_{man} = 0.90$
- $Q = 0.05$ m³/s
- $b_2 = 0.05$ m
- Water: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Blade velocity at outlet:**
$$u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.40 \times 1200}{60}$$
$$u_2 = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.40 \times 1200}{60} = \dfrac{1507.96}{60}$$
$$\boxed{u_2 = 25.13 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Flow (meridional) velocity at outlet:**
$$V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2} = \dfrac{0.05}{\pi \times 0.40 \times 0.05}$$
$$V_{f2} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.06283} \approx 0.7958 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Whirl velocity at outlet from velocity triangle:**

For backward-curved vane (angle from tangential):
$$\tan \beta_2 = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{u_2 - V_{w2}}$$
$$\tan 30° = \dfrac{0.7958}{25.13 - V_{w2}}$$
$$0.5774 = \dfrac{0.7958}{25.13 - V_{w2}}$$
$$25.13 - V_{w2} = \dfrac{0.7958}{0.5774} = 1.378$$
$$V_{w2} = 25.13 - 1.378 = 23.75 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Euler Head (assuming no blockage):**
$$H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \dfrac{23.75 \times 25.13}{9.81}$$
$$H_E = \dfrac{596.84}{9.81} \approx 60.84 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Manometric Head:**
$$H_m = \eta_{man} \times H_E = 0.90 \times 60.84$$
$$\boxed{H_m \approx 54.76 \text{ m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the different heads developed in a centrifugal pump. Derive an expression for the Euler head. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump has an overall efficiency of 70% and manometric efficiency of 80%. The shaft input power is 25 kW. If the discharge is 0.03 m³/s of water, determine: (i) the manometric head, (ii) the power lost due to mechanical friction, (iii) the power imparted by the impeller to the fluid per second. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Different Heads in a Centrifugal Pump:**

1. **Static Head ($H_s$):** Vertical height between suction and delivery liquid surfaces.
2. **Manometric Head ($H_m$):** Actual head developed, measured using pressure and velocity gauges at suction and delivery flanges.
3. **Total Head / Euler Head ($H_E$):** Theoretical head based on velocity triangles (Euler's turbomachinery equation).
4. **Velocity Head ($V^2/2g$):** Kinetic energy per unit weight of fluid.

**Derivation of Euler Head** (refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation):

Applying angular momentum theorem between inlet (1) and outlet (2) sections:

$$\tau = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$
$$\dot{W} = \tau \omega = \dot{m} (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

Dividing by weight flow rate $\dot{m} g$:

$$\boxed{H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}}$$

For radial entry ($V_{w1}=0$):
$$\boxed{H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $\eta_{overall} = 0.70$
- $\eta_{man} = 0.80$
- $P_{shaft} = 25$ kW = 25,000 W
- $Q = 0.03$ m³/s
- $\rho g = 9810$ N/m³

**(i) Manometric Head:**

Overall efficiency:
$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$$
$$H_m = \dfrac{\eta_{overall} \times P_{shaft}}{\rho g Q} = \dfrac{0.70 \times 25000}{9810 \times 0.03}$$
$$H_m = \dfrac{17500}{294.3} \approx \boxed{59.46 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Mechanical power loss:**

First, power delivered to fluid (useful water power):
$$P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H_m = 9810 \times 0.03 \times 59.46 = 17500 \text{ W}$$

Mechanical efficiency relates shaft power to fluid power:
$$P_{fluid} = \eta_{mech} \times P_{shaft}$$
$$\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\eta_{overall}}{\eta_{man}} = \dfrac{0.70}{0.80} = 0.875$$

Verify: $\eta_{mech} \times P_{shaft} = 0.875 \times 25000 = 21875$ W (this is power entering the impeller region, not yet $P_{fluid}$)

**Re-check the definitions carefully:**

Power flow:
- Shaft Power $P_{shaft} = 25000$ W → after mech loss → Power to impeller → after hyd loss → Fluid power $P_{fluid}$
- $\eta_{overall} = P_{fluid} / P_{shaft} = 0.70 \Rightarrow P_{fluid} = 17500$ W ✓
- $\eta_{man} = P_{fluid} / P_{impeller}$

From $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$:
$$\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{0.70}{0.80} = 0.875$$

Power to impeller (after mech loss recovered):
$$P_{impeller} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{\eta_{man}} = \dfrac{17500}{0.80} = 21875 \text{ W}$$

Mechanical loss:
$$P_{mech\,loss} = P_{shaft} - P_{impeller} = 25000 - 21875 = \boxed{3125 \text{ W}}$$

**(iii) Power imparted by impeller to fluid per second:**

This is the **Euler power**, equal to $P_{impeller}$:
$$\boxed{P_{impeller} = 21875 \text{ W} \approx 21.88 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing $H_m$ vs $H_E$** — Manometric head is *measured*, Euler head is *computed from velocity triangles*. They are equal *only* when manometric efficiency is 100%.
> 2. **Forgetting $V_{w1} = 0$ in radial-flow pumps** — The simplified formula $H_E = V_{w2} u_2/g$ applies *only* when entry is purely radial. For axial or mixed-flow pumps, retain the full form.
> 3. **Mixing up sign conventions for efficiency** — Always confirm: $\eta_{man} = H_m/H_E$ (less than 1), $\eta_{mech} = P_{fluid}/P_{shaft}$ for pumps (less than 1).
> 4. **Unit errors** — Use SI consistently. $N$ in rpm goes with 60 in denominator; $\rho$ in kg/m³ with $g$ in m/s².
> 5. **Wrong $V_{f2}$ formula** — Flow area = $\pi D_2 b_2$ (annular area), not $\pi D_2^2/4$.
> 6. **Forgetting "per second"** — The Euler equation in head form gives *head* (m). Multiply by $\rho g Q$ to get *power* (W).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Centrifugal pump = rotodynamic pump** using a rotating impeller to transfer kinetic energy to fluid, then converting it to pressure in the volute casing.
- **Must be primed** before starting (cannot pump air effectively).
- **Maximum theoretical suction lift** = $\dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g}$ ≈ 7.5 m of water; practical value is 4–5 m.
- **Static head** = vertical elevation difference between liquid surfaces.
- **Manometric head** = head measured using gauges at pump flanges; the *actual* useful head.
- **Euler head** = theoretical head from velocity triangles, $H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$.
- For **radial entry**, $V_{w1} = 0$, giving $H_E = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$.
- **Blade velocity**: $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$.
- **Flow velocity at outlet**: $V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2}$.
- **Manometric efficiency**: $\eta_{man} = H_m / H_E$.
- **Mechanical efficiency**: $\eta_{mech} = P_{fluid}/P_{shaft}$ (pump definition).
- **Volumetric efficiency**: $\eta_{vol} = Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical}$.
- **Overall efficiency**: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$.
- **Shaft power formula**: $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$.
- **Discharge characteristic**: For a centrifugal pump, as $Q$ increases, $H_m$ decreases (unlike a positive displacement pump which has nearly constant flow).
- **Typical components**: Impeller, casing, shaft, bearings, foot valve, non-return valve, priming tank.
- **Backward-curved vanes** give high efficiency; **forward-curved** give unstable characteristics; **radial vanes** give moderate head.
- **Typical KTU question pattern**: Given $D_2$, $N$, $Q$, $\beta_2$, $\eta_{man}$ → compute $H_m$ (or reverse).
- **Keep units consistent**: $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m, $P$ in W.
- **Sanity check**: For a typical water pump, $H_m \sim 10$–$50$ m, $Q \sim 0.01$–$0.1$ m³/s, $u_2 \sim 15$–$30$ m/s.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Centrifugal Pumps**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priming and Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Priming and Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Priming — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priming** is the auxiliary operation of filling the **suction pipe**, **casing**, and the **impeller eye** of a centrifugal pump completely with the working liquid before starting the pump, so that the pump can develop the required suction lift and deliver liquid without air entrainment.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, priming is a *pre-startup preparatory procedure* mandated for all **centrifugal pumps** (and certain other rotodynamic pumps) that are not self-priming by design. A pump is said to be **properly primed** when the entire wetted passage — from the sump/reservoir surface, through the foot valve, up the suction pipe, and into the pump casing and impeller — is fully filled with the liquid, with no air pockets or vapor voids.

### 1.2 Why is Priming Required?

A centrifugal pump operates on the principle of creating a **pressure differential** at the impeller eye. The rotating impeller imparts **centrifugal acceleration** to the liquid, throwing it outward. This outward movement reduces pressure at the eye (suction side), and atmospheric pressure on the liquid surface in the sump pushes the liquid up the suction pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition (Conceptual Analogy):**
> Think of a centrifugal pump as a *fancy straw*. If you put a straw into a glass of water and suck the air out, the water rises because atmospheric pressure pushes it up. Now imagine trying to suck up a *thick milkshake with some air bubbles* — the bubbles collapse inward, the suction "breaks," and nothing rises. Centrifugal pumps behave the same way: if there is **air (or any gas) inside the casing**, the impeller cannot develop enough vacuum to lift water. Air, being ~800 times less dense than water, simply *whizzes around* the impeller without generating the necessary pressure head. The pump **fails to deliver**, a state called **"loss of prime."**

**Consequences of Operating Without Priming (Loss of Prime):**
- The impeller spins in air; no significant head is developed.
- Air may be repeatedly circulated, causing overheating of the casing.
- Repeated start attempts without re-priming can damage the **mechanical seal** and **bearings**.

### 1.3 Cavitation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation** is the dynamic phenomenon of **formation, growth, and subsequent violent collapse (implosion) of vapor cavities (bubbles)** within a flowing liquid, occurring when the local static pressure at any point in the flow falls below the **vapor pressure of the liquid** at that temperature, and the cavity is convected into a region of higher pressure where it condenses abruptly.

In KTU 2024 terminology, cavitation is classified as an **undesirable, destructive two-phase flow instability** in hydraulic machines — affecting pumps, turbines, propellers, valves, and pipelines. It is **NOT** the same as boiling; cavitation occurs at constant temperature when local pressure falls below saturation pressure.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy for Cavitation

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a diver leaping from a high board into deep water. As the diver falls, the pressure of water rushing past their ears drops momentarily (Bernoulli effect). Tiny dissolved-gas and vapor bubbles appear on the skin — this is *incipient cavitation*. The moment the diver hits the high-pressure deep water, the bubbles collapse with a sharp "crack" and can cause a dull ache (the "bends" sensation). In a pump or turbine, these collapsing bubbles act like microscopic hammer blows on metal surfaces, gradually eroding them.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Water, 20 °C) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **998** | kg/m³ |
| Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | kPa |
| Vapor pressure of water at 20 °C | $P_v$ | **2.34 kPa** | kPa |
| Vapor pressure of water at 100 °C | $P_v$ | **101.325 kPa** | kPa |

> [!WARNING]
> Cavitation tendency is **highly sensitive to temperature** because $P_v$ increases non-linearly with temperature. Hot water pumps (e.g., boiler feed pumps) are far more susceptible to cavitation than cold-water pumps at the same NPSH.

### 1.6 Why These Topics Are Studied Together

Priming and cavitation are grouped in KTU Module 4 because both are **suction-side phenomena** of rotodynamic machines. Priming is the *prevention of air-related suction failure*; cavitation is the *consequence of insufficient pressure margin* above vapor pressure. Both determine whether a pump/turbine installation will operate reliably.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Priming — Operational Theory

#### 2.1.1 Methods of Priming (Step-by-Step Logic)

A centrifugal pump can be primed by **any** of the following methods, depending on installation size and service:

1. **Foot-valve priming (most common in KTU problems):**
   - A non-return **foot valve** is fitted at the bottom of the suction pipe, submerged in the sump.
   - The casing is filled through a **priming funnel** (or ejector) at the top of the casing.
   - Liquid poured in fills the suction pipe and casing by gravity.
   - The foot valve prevents backflow, retaining the prime between starts.

2. **Ejector priming (using a separate fluid):**
   - A **steam or water ejector** evacuates air from the casing, creating a partial vacuum.
   - Atmospheric pressure pushes liquid up the suction pipe.
   - Used in large industrial pumps and marine installations.

3. **Vacuum pump priming:**
   - A separate **vacuum pump** (rotary or reciprocating) evacuates air.
   - Common in large dewatering pumps and fire-fighting installations.

4. **Self-priming pumps:**
   - Built-in reservoir and side channel; the pump retains a small quantity of liquid and re-primes itself automatically.
   - Used in construction dewatering and small agricultural pumps.

5. **Separate priming tank:**
   - A small elevated tank near the pump supplies liquid for priming.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** The **foot-valve + priming funnel** combination is the *most-asked priming method* in KTU Part A questions. Memorize its labeled diagram.

#### 2.1.2 Components Used in Priming

- **Foot Valve**: A one-way (non-return) valve at the suction pipe inlet, often with a strainer.
- **Strainer**: A perforated/screened basket that prevents debris from entering the pump.
- **Priming Funnel (Priming Cock)**: A small filler opening on the top of the casing.
- **Air Vent Valve**: Releases trapped air as liquid fills the casing.
- **Priming Tank / Ejector**: Auxiliary air-removal system for large pumps.

#### 2.1.3 Limitations of Priming Suction Lift

The **maximum theoretical suction lift** is approximately **7.6 m of water** at sea level (corresponding to atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa and density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³). This is because atmospheric pressure can only "push" a column of water up to roughly 10.33 m in a perfect vacuum, and practical losses reduce this to ~7.6 m.

$$H_{s,\text{max, theo}} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81} \approx 10.33 \text{ m (vacuum)}$$

$$H_{s,\text{max, practical}} \approx 7.6 \text{ m at sea level (with losses)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap:** Suction lift is the **vertical distance** between the free liquid surface in the sump and the **centerline of the pump impeller eye**. The pump centerline must always be **above** the sump water level for a suction-lift installation. If the pump is *below* the sump level, the suction side is under positive pressure — this is a **flooded suction** arrangement and is *preferred* in practice to avoid priming issues.

### 2.2 Cavitation — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 Physical Stages of Cavitation

A complete cavitation cycle has three stages:

1. **Nucleation**: Microscopic gas/vapor nuclei (always present in real liquids) become unstable when local pressure drops below $P_v$. Tiny bubbles form.
2. **Growth**: Bubbles grow rapidly as the liquid vaporizes into them while the low-pressure region persists.
3. **Collapse (Implosion)**: When bubbles are convected into a region where pressure exceeds $P_v$, they collapse violently, generating **micro-jets** and **shock waves** (pressures of order 100–1000 MPa locally).

#### 2.2.2 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** is the *total head available at the pump suction port* above the vapor pressure head of the liquid, expressed in metres. It is the single most important cavitation parameter.

**Two variants of NPSH are universally used:**

| Term | Definition | Decides |
|---|---|---|
| **NPSH Available ($NPSH_A$)** | Total suction head above vapor pressure, as installed in the field | Site/Installation engineer |
| **NPSH Required ($NPSH_R$)** | Minimum NPSH a pump needs to avoid cavitation (manufacturer-specified, function of design and $Q$) | Pump manufacturer |

**Cavitation occurs when** $NPSH_A < NPSH_R$ (with adequate safety margin).

**General $NPSH_A$ formula (most general form):**

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \frac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Sign convention for $h_s$:**
- Use **$+h_s$** when the liquid source (sump) is **above** the pump centerline (flooded suction).
- Use **$-h_s$** when the liquid source is **below** the pump centerline (suction lift).

**Where:**
- $P_{atm}$ = Absolute atmospheric pressure at the liquid surface (Pa)
- $h_s$ = Static suction head (positive if flooded) or suction lift (negative) (m)
- $h_f$ = Friction head loss in suction pipe + entrance loss (m)
- $P_v$ = Vapor pressure of liquid at pumping temperature (Pa, absolute)
- $\rho$ = Liquid density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**For a closed/pressurized system** (e.g., pump drawing from a pressurized tank):

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_t}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \frac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

where $P_t$ is the absolute pressure at the liquid surface of the source tank.

#### 2.2.3 Thoma's Cavitation Parameter ($\sigma$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thoma's Cavitation Parameter** is a *dimensionless* index of cavitation tendency, used primarily for **reaction turbines (Francis, Kaplan)**. It is the ratio of the net available suction head (above vapor pressure) to the working head of the machine.

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH_A}{H} = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v \pm H_s - H_f}{H}$$

**Where:**
- $H_{atm}$ = Atmospheric pressure head = $P_{atm}/(\rho g)$ in metres of liquid
- $H_v$ = Vapor pressure head = $P_v/(\rho g)$ in metres of liquid
- $H_s$ = Static suction head (positive if turbine is below tailrace) or suction lift (negative)
- $H_f$ = Friction head loss in draft tube
- $H$ = Net head developed by the turbine

**Incipient (critical) cavitation** occurs when:
$$\sigma = \sigma_c \quad \text{(Thoma's critical value, from model tests)}$$

**For safe operation:**
$$\sigma > \sigma_c \quad \text{(typically with a safety margin of 10–20\%)}$$

**For pumps**, an analogous parameter is sometimes used:
$$\sigma_{pump} = \frac{NPSH_A}{H}$$

#### 2.2.4 Effects / Consequences of Cavitation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU must-know list of cavitation effects** (frequently asked in 3-mark questions):

1. **Pitting & Erosion** of impeller blades, casing, runner blades, draft tubes.
2. **Noise and Vibration** — characteristic rattling / "gravel-in-the-pump" sound.
3. **Reduction in discharge and efficiency** — head-flow curve drops abruptly.
4. **Structural Damage** — eventual perforation of metal surfaces.
5. **Performance Instability** — fluctuating discharge and power.

#### 2.2.5 Methods to Prevent / Control Cavitation

1. **Increase $NPSH_A$:**
   - Lower the pump installation (reduce suction lift or create flooded suction).
   - Reduce friction losses (larger pipe, fewer fittings).
   - Use a **booster pump** on the suction side.
2. **Reduce $NPSH_R$:**
   - Use a pump with a larger impeller eye (low $NPSH_R$ design).
3. **Cool the liquid** (lower $P_v$).
4. **Pressurize the sump** (for closed systems).
5. **Select correct pump speed** (lower $N$ lowers $NPSH_R$).
6. **Use cavitation-resistant materials** (e.g., stainless steel, special coatings).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Notes | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{s,\text{theo,max}} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g}$ | Theoretical max suction lift at sea level ≈ **10.33 m** | m |
| 2 | $H_{s,\text{practical,max}} \approx 7.6$ m | Practical max suction lift (after losses) | m |
| 3 | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$ | NPSH available (open system) | m |
| 4 | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_t}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$ | NPSH available (closed/pressurized system) | m |
| 5 | **Cavitation Safe** ⟺ $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ | Pump design rule | — |
| 6 | $\sigma = \dfrac{H_{atm} - H_v \pm H_s - H_f}{H}$ | Thoma's cavitation parameter (turbines) | dimensionless |
| 7 | Incipient cavitation: $\sigma = \sigma_c$ | Critical value from model tests | — |
| 8 | Safe operation: $\sigma > \sigma_c$ | Usually with safety margin | — |
| 9 | $h_f = \dfrac{4fLV^2}{2gD}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | m |
| 10 | $h_L = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Minor loss at fittings, foot valve, etc. | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU answer sheets:** always express $NPSH_A$ in **metres of liquid (m)** — not in Pa or kPa. Convert pressures to head using $H = P/(\rho g)$ first.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Feed Pumps (Thermal Power Plants):** Must operate with hot, near-saturated water → very high cavitation risk. NPSH is critical for safe plant operation.
- **Marine & Ship Pumps:** Flooded suction preferred; priming is automated.
- **Reaction Turbines (Francis/Kaplan):** Thoma's $\sigma$ is used to **set the tailrace elevation** during civil design of hydropower stations.
- **Fire-Fighting Pumps:** Self-priming design is essential for rapid deployment.
- **Rocket Engine Turbopumps:** Extreme cavitation risk; use **inducer stages** and **cavitation-resistant alloys**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Maximum Suction Lift

**Starting Point:** Consider a centrifugal pump with its impeller eye at a vertical height $H_s$ above the free surface of a liquid reservoir open to atmosphere.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between the free liquid surface (point 1) and the pump impeller eye (point 2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2 — Assign boundary conditions:**
- Point 1 (free surface): $P_1 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$ (large reservoir), $z_1 = 0$ (datum).
- Point 2 (impeller eye): $P_2 = ?$, $V_2$ = velocity in suction pipe, $z_2 = H_s$.
- $h_L$ = total head loss (friction + minor + entrance).

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + H_s + h_L$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_2$:**

$$\frac{P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s - h_L - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 5 — Cavitation-free limit:** For the pump to operate without cavitation, the pressure at the eye must be **strictly greater than** the vapor pressure $P_v$:

$$P_2 > P_v$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical maximum (no losses, no velocity head, $P_2 \to P_v$):** Setting $h_L = 0$, $V_2 = 0$, $P_2 = P_v$:

$$H_{s,\text{max,theo}} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Step 7 — For cold water at 20 °C** ($P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $P_v = 2.34$ kPa):

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{s,\text{max,theo}} &= \frac{(101.325 - 2.34) \times 10^3}{1000 \times 9.81} \\
&= \frac{98.985 \times 10^3}{9810} \\
&\approx 10.09 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Practical maximum** accounts for $V_2^2/2g$, friction, and a safety margin:

$$H_{s,\text{max,practical}} \approx 7.6 \text{ m of water}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This is why KTU textbook problems routinely use **$H_s \le 6$ m** as a working suction lift in numerical examples.

### 3.2 Derivation: Net Positive Suction Head Available ($NPSH_A$)

**Definition:** $NPSH_A$ is the total absolute head at the pump suction port **minus** the vapor pressure head of the liquid, both expressed as heads of the liquid.

**Step 1 — Total head at pump suction port (point S):**

$$H_S = \frac{P_S}{\rho g} + \frac{V_S^2}{2g} + z_S$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli from the sump free surface (point 1) to the pump suction port (point S):**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \frac{P_S}{\rho g} + \frac{V_S^2}{2g} + z_S + h_f + h_{minor}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the total head at the suction port:**

$$H_S = \frac{P_S}{\rho g} + \frac{V_S^2}{2g} + z_S = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_f - h_{minor}$$

(For a free-surface source, the datum cancellation gives $z_S - 0$ inside the head loss, but the standard form below is conventionally used.)

**Step 4 — Static suction head definition:**

$$h_s = z_S - z_{source}$$

For an open sump: $h_s > 0$ if pump is **above** sump (suction lift, $h_s$ is negative in formula). Standard textbook sign convention absorbs the sign into $\pm h_s$.

**Step 5 — Final general $NPSH_A$ formula (in the KTU-preferred form):**

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \frac{P_v}{\rho g}$$

**Step 6 — Unit consistency check:** Each term must be in **metres of liquid**. Pressure terms are converted via $P/(\rho g)$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Thoma's Cavitation Parameter

**Step 1 — Define net head on a reaction turbine:**

$$H = H_g - H_s - h_f$$

where $H_g$ is the gross head (difference between headrace and tailrace levels).

**Step 2 — Available pressure head at the runner inlet (above vapor pressure):**

$$H_{avail} = H_{atm} - H_v + H_s$$

(Here $H_s$ is the static suction head, *positive* when the runner is **below** the tailrace — a sub-atmospheric draft tube).

**Step 3 — Take the ratio of available head to working head** (Thoma, 1924):

$$\sigma = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v + H_s}{H} = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v \pm H_s - H_f}{H}$$

The sign of $H_s$ is *positive* if the runner is below tailrace (draft tube under suction), *negative* if above.

**Step 4 — Critical condition (incipient cavitation):**
$\sigma = \sigma_c$, where $\sigma_c$ is determined experimentally from **cavitation test rigs** using a transparent model.

**Step 5 — Design rule:** $\sigma_{\text{installed}} \geq 1.1 \sigma_c$ to $1.25 \sigma_c$ for a safety margin.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution for KTU Practice)

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A centrifugal pump draws water from a sump 3.5 m below the pump centerline. The suction pipe is 100 mm diameter, 8 m long, with a friction factor $f = 0.024$. The entrance loss coefficient $K_e = 0.5$. The water temperature is 20 °C ($P_v = 2.34$ kPa abs, $\rho = 998$ kg/m³). Atmospheric pressure is 98 kPa abs. The pump delivers 25 L/s.
> **Find:** (a) Total suction head loss $h_L$; (b) $NPSH_A$; (c) Whether cavitation will occur if $NPSH_R = 5.2$ m.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Velocity in suction pipe:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.183 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Friction head loss (Darcy–Weisbach):**

$$h_f = \frac{4fLV^2}{2gD} = \frac{4 \times 0.024 \times 8 \times (3.183)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.100}$$

$$h_f = \frac{4 \times 0.024 \times 8 \times 10.13}{1.962} = \frac{7.781}{1.962} \approx 3.966 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Entrance (minor) head loss:**

$$h_{ent} = K_e \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.5 \times \frac{(3.183)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.5 \times \frac{10.13}{19.62} \approx 0.258 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Total suction head loss (Part a answer):**

$$h_L = h_f + h_{ent} = 3.966 + 0.258 \approx 4.22 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $NPSH_A$ (Part b answer):**
- $P_{atm}/(\rho g) = 98 \times 10^3 / (998 \times 9.81) = 98000 / 9790.4 \approx 10.01$ m
- $P_v/(\rho g) = 2.34 \times 10^3 / (998 \times 9.81) \approx 0.239$ m
- $h_s = -3.5$ m (pump above sump → suction lift, sign negative)

$$NPSH_A = 10.01 + (-3.5) - 4.22 - 0.239 = 2.05 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Cavitation check (Part c answer):**

$$NPSH_A = 2.05 \text{ m} \quad \text{vs.} \quad NPSH_R = 5.2 \text{ m}$$

Since $NPSH_A < NPSH_R$, **cavitation WILL occur.** The pump installation is unsafe.

**Remedies:**
- Lower the pump (reduce suction lift).
- Use a larger-diameter or shorter suction pipe (reduce $h_L$).
- Use a booster pump on the suction line.
- Select a pump with lower $NPSH_R$.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Priming | Cavitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of issue** | Air/gas in suction system | Vapor bubbles forming in liquid |
| **Causative condition** | Empty casing / foot valve failure | Local pressure < vapor pressure |
| **Detection signal** | No discharge, dry casing, gurgling | Rattling noise, vibration, pitting |
| **Effect on head curve** | Head collapses to near zero | Head drops abruptly at high $Q$ |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible (re-prime and restart) | Irreversible surface damage |
| **Prevention** | Foot valve, ejector, vacuum pump | Increase $NPSH_A$, reduce $NPSH_R$ |
| **Key parameter** | $H_{s,\text{practical}} \le 7.6$ m | $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ |
| **Used in** | Centrifugal pumps | Pumps, turbines, valves, propellers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Primed Centrifugal Pump Installation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sump Reservoir] -->|Liquid Level| B1[Foot Valve with Strainer]
    B1 -->|Non-return Seal| C1[Suction Pipe 100mm Diameter]
    C1 -->|Friction + Entrance Loss| D1[Pump Casing]
    D1 -->|Impeller Eye Filled| E1[Impeller]
    E1 -->|Centrifugal Action| F1[Volute Casing]
    F1 -->|Pressure Rise| G1[Delivery Pipe]
    G1 -->|Discharge to System| H1[Delivery Tank or Process]

    P1[Priming Funnel] -->|Manual Fill| D1
    P2[Air Vent Valve] -->|Releases Trapped Air| D1
    P3[Vacuum Pump or Ejector] -->|Evacuates Air| D1

    N1[NPSH A Calculation] -->|Compare with NPSH R| R1[Cavitation Check]
    R1 -->|Safe Operation| OK[OK]
    R1 -->|NPSH A less than NPSH R| WARN[Cavitation Risk]
    WARN --> REM[Reduce Suction Lift or Increase Pipe Size]
    OK --> H1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top branch** (A → H1) shows the liquid flow path: sump → foot valve → suction pipe → pump → delivery.
- The **middle branch** (P1, P2, P3 → D1) shows the *priming sub-system* that prepares the pump for operation.
- The **bottom branch** (N1 → R1 → OK / WARN) shows the *cavitation protection logic* that an engineer applies during design verification.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Cavitation Formation and Damage Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Liquid at Suction Eye] -->|Local P falls below P_v| S2[Bubble Nucleation]
    S2 -->|Bubble Growth in Low P Region| S3[Convection into High P Region]
    S3 -->|Pressure above P_v| S4[Rapid Condensation and Collapse]
    S4 -->|Microjet Impact| S5[Surface Pitting on Impeller or Runner]
    S5 -->|Progressive Erosion| S6[Loss of Efficiency and Material Loss]

    S1 -->|Compare with N1| N1{NPSH A greater than NPSH R}
    N1 -->|Yes| OK1[No Cavitation]
    N1 -->|No| S2
```

### 4.3 Suction Lift vs. NPSH — Working Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Design Suction Installation] --> Q1{Pump Above Sump}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Suction Lift h s negative]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Flooded Suction h s positive]

    Q2 --> R1[Apply formula with minus h s]
    Q3 --> R2[Apply formula with plus h s]

    R1 --> T1[Compute NPSH A]
    R2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2{NPSH A greater than NPSH R + Margin}
    T2 -->|Yes| T3[Safe Installation]
    T2 -->|No| T4[Modify Installation]
    T4 --> Q0
```

### 4.4 Schematic Cross-Section of a Primed Centrifugal Pump (Conceptual Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1[Suction Side]
        A2[Foot Valve at Sump Bottom]
        A3[Strainer Basket]
        A4[Vertical Suction Pipe]
        A5[Pipe Bend Reducing Elbow]
    end
    subgraph A6[Pump Casing Section]
        A7[Suction Inlet Nozzle]
        A8[Impeller Eye Eye Region]
        A9[Volute Casing]
        A10[Priming Plug at Top]
        A11[Air Vent Petcock]
    end
    subgraph A12[Discharge Side]
        A13[Delivery Nozzle]
        A14[Non-Return Valve]
        A15[Discharge Pipe to System]
    end
    A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A7
    A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A13 --> A14 --> A15
    A10 -.-> A9
    A11 -.-> A7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When drawing the priming diagram in your exam answer, always **label**: (1) sump water level, (2) pump centerline, (3) suction lift $H_s$, (4) foot valve, (5) strainer, (6) priming funnel/port, (7) air vent, (8) impeller eye. These eight elements get full marks.

### 4.5 Detailed Pump-Impeller Cavitation Zone Schematic (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Liquid Stream at Impeller Inlet] -->|High Velocity V2| L2[Pressure Drops below P v]
    L2 --> L3[Incipient Cavitation at Impeller Eye]
    L3 -->|If NPSH A is Low| L4[Developed Cavitation on Suction Side of Blade]
    L4 --> L5[Bubble Collapse on Blade Surface]
    L5 --> L6[Pitting Erosion and Noise]
    L4 -->|If NPSH A is Sufficient| L7[No Cavitation Healthy Flow]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define priming of a centrifugal pump and list any two methods of priming.**

**Model Answer:**
Priming is the process of filling the suction pipe, casing, and impeller of a centrifugal pump completely with the liquid to be pumped before starting the pump. It is necessary because a centrifugal pump cannot develop suction if air is present in the casing (air being much less dense than water).

**Two methods of priming:**
1. **Foot-valve priming** — A foot valve with strainer at the sump retains liquid in the suction pipe; the casing is filled through a priming funnel.
2. **Ejector priming** — A steam or water ejector evacuates air from the casing, allowing atmospheric pressure to push liquid up the suction pipe.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Q2]**
> **Q: Define cavitation. What is its effect on the performance of a hydraulic machine?**

**Model Answer:**
Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation, growth, and violent collapse of vapor bubbles in a flowing liquid when the local static pressure falls below the vapor pressure of the liquid at the prevailing temperature.

**Effects on performance:**
1. **Pitting and erosion** of impeller/runner blades and casing surfaces.
2. **Reduction in discharge head and efficiency** — head-flow curve drops sharply.
3. **Noise, vibration,** and mechanical damage to bearings and seals.
4. **Structural failure** of machine components in severe cases.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**A (a)** [7 Marks] A centrifugal pump is to draw water from a sump 4 m below its centerline. The suction pipe is 75 mm in diameter, 12 m long, with a Darcy friction factor of 0.026. The pipe has a foot valve with a loss coefficient $K_{fv} = 3.5$ and a 90° bend with $K_b = 0.9$. Water temperature is 25 °C ($P_v = 3.17$ kPa abs, $\rho = 997$ kg/m³). Atmospheric pressure is 100 kPa abs. The discharge is 15 L/s. Calculate:
(i) Total head loss in the suction line.
(ii) Available NPSH at the pump suction port.
(iii) Comment on whether the pump will cavitate if the manufacturer specifies $NPSH_R = 4.8$ m.

**A (b)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **foot-valve priming** method used for centrifugal pumps. List the essential components and state why priming is necessary.

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B (a)** [7 Marks] Define **Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** and **Thoma's cavitation parameter ($\sigma$)**. Derive the expression for $NPSH_A$ for a pump drawing water from an open sump and explain the conditions for safe operation.

**B (b)** [7 Marks] A reaction turbine operates under a net head of 30 m. Atmospheric pressure head is 10.3 m of water and vapor pressure head is 0.25 m of water. The turbine is set 2.5 m above the tailrace. If Thoma's critical cavitation parameter $\sigma_c = 0.085$, determine whether cavitation will occur. Suggest one method to prevent cavitation if it does occur.

---

### Model Solutions for Question A

#### **A (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Suction pipe velocity:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.395 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{(3.395)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{11.52}{19.62} = 0.587 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Friction head loss:**

$$h_f = \frac{4fLV^2}{2gD} = \frac{4 \times 0.026 \times 12 \times 11.52}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075}$$

$$h_f = \frac{14.376}{1.4715} \approx 9.77 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Minor losses** (foot valve + bend):

$$h_m = (K_{fv} + K_b) \frac{V^2}{2g} = (3.5 + 0.9) \times 0.587 = 4.4 \times 0.587 \approx 2.583 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Total suction head loss (i):**

$$h_L = h_f + h_m = 9.77 + 2.583 \approx 12.35 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Atmospheric and vapor pressure heads:**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{997 \times 9.81} = \frac{100000}{9780.6} \approx 10.22 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{3.17 \times 10^3}{997 \times 9.81} = \frac{3170}{9780.6} \approx 0.324 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — $NPSH_A$ (ii):** With $h_s = -4$ m (suction lift):

$$NPSH_A = 10.22 + (-4) - 12.35 - 0.324 = -6.45 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Cavitation comment (iii):**
$NPSH_A$ is **negative**, far below $NPSH_R = 4.8$ m. The pump installation is **grossly inadequate** — it will not even develop suction. The pump must be lowered to the sump level, a booster pump must be used, or a much larger and shorter suction pipe must be installed.

#### **A (b) Model Answer (Sketch Description):**

- **Sump** with water level shown.
- **Foot valve + strainer** at the bottom of the vertical suction pipe.
- **Suction pipe** rising to the **pump centerline** at height $H_s$.
- **Impeller** inside the casing, with the impeller eye at the centerline.
- **Priming funnel** (or priming plug) at the top of the casing.
- **Air vent valve** to release trapped air.
- **Delivery pipe** rising from the volute.

**Components required:**
1. Foot valve (non-return)
2. Strainer
3. Suction pipe
4. Priming funnel
5. Air vent
6. Pump casing
7. Delivery pipe

**Why priming is necessary:** Centrifugal pumps cannot create sufficient vacuum to lift water when air is present. Air is ~800 times less dense than water and is flung outward by the impeller without generating pressure rise. The pump simply churns the air. Priming ensures the entire suction system and casing is full of liquid, allowing the impeller to develop the required pressure head.

---

### Model Solutions for Question B

#### **B (a) Solution Outline:**

- Define $NPSH_A$ and $NPSH_R$ with the standard $NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \frac{P_v}{\rho g}$ formula.
- Define Thoma's $\sigma$ for turbines: $\sigma = \dfrac{H_{atm} - H_v \pm H_s - H_f}{H}$.
- Safe operation requires $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ and $\sigma > \sigma_c$.

#### **B (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Thoma's $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v \pm H_s}{H} = \frac{10.3 - 0.25 + 2.5}{30}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{12.55}{30} \approx 0.418$$

(Note: $H_s = +2.5$ m because turbine is *above* tailrace, hence *negative suction head* at runner, which **increases** cavitation risk. Sign convention: $+H_s$ when runner is above tailrace for a reaction turbine, contributing to the numerator positively.)

**Step 2 — Comparison with $\sigma_c = 0.085$:**

$$\sigma = 0.418 \gg \sigma_c = 0.085$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
$\sigma$ is much greater than $\sigma_c$, so **cavitation will NOT occur** under the stated conditions. The installation has a large safety margin.

**Step 4 — If cavitation had occurred, suggested remedy:**
- Lower the turbine runner closer to tailrace (reduce $H_s$).
- Use a longer draft tube to recover more suction head.
- Operate at a lower net head by adjusting the gate opening.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign of $h_s$ (very common error):** Students often get the sign of $h_s$ wrong. **Memorize:** if pump is *above* sump, use **$-h_s$**; if pump is *below* sump (flooded), use **$+h_s$**.
> 2. **Mixing $NPSH$ with $\sigma$:** $NPSH$ is for *pumps*; Thoma's $\sigma$ is for *turbines*. KTU examiners deduct full marks for interchange.
> 3. **Using gauge pressure instead of absolute pressure:** $P_{atm}$ and $P_v$ must be **absolute**, not gauge. A frequent mistake.
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** Converting $P$ to head must use $H = P/(\rho g)$, not just $P/\rho$. Easy to lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting minor losses in $NPSH$ calculation:** The foot valve loss is large ($K \approx 3.5$). Students often include only pipe friction.
> 6. **In cavitation question on turbines, using $H_s$ sign for pump formula** — they are opposite. **Pump: $H_s$ negative when pump is above source. Turbine: $H_s$ positive when runner is above tailrace.**

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Priming** is the pre-startup filling of the suction system with liquid. It is essential for centrifugal pumps because air prevents vacuum formation.
- **Maximum theoretical suction lift** is 10.33 m; **practical limit** is ~7.6 m (use 6 m in KTU problems).
- **Foot valve + priming funnel** is the most common priming method — memorize the labeled diagram.
- **Cavitation** is the formation, growth, and violent collapse of vapor bubbles when local pressure < $P_v$.
- **NPSH (Available)** for an open sump: $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g}$.
- **Cavitation-safe rule:** $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ (with margin).
- **Thoma's $\sigma$** is used for **turbines**: $\sigma = \dfrac{H_{atm} - H_v \pm H_s - H_f}{H}$.
- **Cavitation-safe rule (turbine):** $\sigma > \sigma_c$.
- **Cavitation effects:** pitting, noise, vibration, head loss, efficiency loss.
- **Cavitation prevention:** increase $NPSH_A$ (flooded suction, larger pipe), decrease $NPSH_R$ (low-NPSH pump), cool the liquid, use cavitation-resistant materials.
- **Always** use **absolute pressure** for $P_{atm}$ and $P_v$.
- **Always** convert pressure to head before computing $NPSH$ or $\sigma$.
- **Sign convention for $h_s$** is the single most important source of errors — double-check.
- **Cavitation in hot-water pumps** is far more dangerous than in cold-water pumps because $P_v$ rises steeply with temperature.
- **For pumps:** cavitation risk is on the **suction side** of the impeller (eye and blade tip).
- **For turbines:** cavitation risk is on the **runner blade backs** and in the **draft tube**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocating Pump: Principle, Working, Slip, Negative Slip, Work Required and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET303 - FLUID MECHANICS AND MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reciprocating Pump: Principle, Working, Slip, Negative Slip, Work Required and Efficiency # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Reciprocating Pump > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMET303 - Module 4)** > A **Reciprocating Pump** is a *positive displacement* hydraulic machine that converts the mechanical energy of a rotating prime mover (driven by a crank and connecting rod mechanism) into hydraulic energy by displacing a definite volume of fluid per stroke using a piston or plunger reciprocating inside a closely-fitted cylinder. **Key Identity Markers of a Reciprocating Pump:** - It is a **Positive Displacement (PD) Pump** → fixed volume of liquid is trapped and mechanically displaced per cycle. - The working fluid (piston/plunger) executes a **back-and-forth (reciprocating) motion** inside a cylinder. - Discharge is **pulsating in nature** (occurs only during the delivery/deliver stroke in a single-acting pump). - Self-priming at start, but requires **air vessels** to dampen pulsations at high speeds. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (The Syringe Principle) > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy — The Medical Syringe:** > Take a medical syringe filled with water, hold its nozzle down, and push the piston inward. A **fixed quantity of water squirts out** with every complete push-pull cycle. Now imagine this syringe connected to a long inlet pipe dipping into a well and an outlet pipe feeding a tank on a roof. > > - **PULL (Suction Stroke):** Piston moves backward → low pressure inside cylinder → atmospheric pressure pushes water up the suction pipe → water enters the cylinder. > - **PUSH (Delivery Stroke):** Piston moves forward → high pressure develops → non-return valve (NRV) on suction side closes → water is forced out through the delivery NRV into the delivery pipe. > > This is *exactly* how a reciprocating pump works! Replace your hand with a **crank-and-connecting-rod**, and you have an industrial reciprocating pump. --- ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU Board Relevance) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Types Frequently Asked in KTU ESE:** | Classification Criterion | Type | Key Property | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Based on Number of Working Strokes** | Single-Acting | One delivery per revolution (one side of piston does work) | | | Double-Acting | Two deliveries per revolution (both sides of piston do work) | | **Based on Liquid Contact** | Piston Pump | Piston uses packing/seals | | | Plunger Pump | Plunger is solid rod (used for high pressure) | | **Based on Drive** | Power Pump | Driven by external power (motor/engine) | | | Steam Pump | Driven by steam cylinder directly | --- ## 1.4 Standard Reference Quantities (KTU Convention) > [!NOTE] > Throughout KTU board problems, the following **standardized symbols and units** are used: - **A** = Cross-sectional area of piston/plunger (m²) - **L** = Length of stroke (m) - **N** = Crank speed (rpm) - **D** = Diameter of piston (m) - **ρ** = Density of fluid (kg/m³); **w = ρg** = specific weight (N/m³) - **Hₛ** = Suction head (m); **H_d** = Delivery head (m) - **H_f** = Total frictional head loss in suction and delivery pipes (m) - **V** = Mean velocity of liquid in pipes (m/s); **D_s, D_d** = pipe diameters - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle A reciprocating pump operates on a **two-phase cycle**: ### Phase 1 — Suction Stroke - Piston retracts from TDC (Top Dead Centre) to BDC (Bottom Dead Centre). - Volume created inside cylinder = **A × L**. - Pressure inside cylinder falls **below atmospheric pressure**. - The **suction non-return valve (NRV)** opens; the **delivery NRV** remains closed. - Atmospheric pressure (or sump pressure) on the liquid surface pushes the liquid up the suction pipe into the cylinder. - Liquid rises to a theoretical height of **Hₛ** (suction head) above the pump centreline. ### Phase 2 — Delivery Stroke - Piston moves from BDC to TDC. - Pressure inside cylinder rises **above the delivery pressure**. - The **suction NRV closes** (prevents backflow into sump). - The **delivery NRV opens**; the trapped volume **A × L** is forced up the delivery pipe to a height **H_d**. - For a **double-acting pump**, this same phase happens simultaneously on the opposite side of the piston, doubling the discharge. --- ## 2.2 The "WHY" Behind Slip — The Heart of the Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > **SLIP — KTU's Most Repeated Question Topic (Dec 2018, June 2019, Dec 2021, June 2022, Dec 2023)** > In an *ideal* frictionless world, the volume delivered per stroke would be exactly **A × L**. In reality, **less liquid is delivered** because: > 1. **Delayed opening/closing of NRVs** (mechanical lag of valve flaps). > 2. **Leakage past the piston** through imperfect seals, gaskets, and packing. > 3. **Compression of dissolved air/vapours** trapped inside the cylinder (a small dead volume remains even at TDC). > 4. **High frictional resistance** in long, narrow suction pipes, especially at high crank speeds. This shortfall between **ideal (theoretical)** and **actual** discharge is called **SLIP**. --- ## 2.3 Negative Slip — The Counter-Intuitive Phenomenon > [!WARNING] > **NEGATIVE SLIP** is a *board-favourite trap* question. Most students wrongly assume slip is always positive. **Negative Slip occurs** when the **actual discharge EXCEEDS the theoretical discharge**. This happens under a very specific set of conditions: 1. The **suction pipe is long and narrow** (high friction loss in suction). 2. The **crank speed (N) is very high**. 3. The **suction head Hₛ is also high**. **Physical Explanation (Step-by-Step "Why"):** - During the **delivery stroke**, the liquid in the suction pipe is under high pressure. When the delivery stroke ends, the *stretched* liquid column in the suction pipe *rebounds* (acts like a stretched spring). - During the **next suction stroke**, the piston moves backward very fast. The rebounding liquid column catches up and **rushing-in velocity momentarily exceeds piston velocity**. - A small extra volume of liquid is thus *sucked in beyond* the geometric swept volume, before the suction NRV slams shut. - The delivery NRV, which was still partially open, allows this extra fluid to pass into the delivery pipe. - Result: **Q_actual > Q_theoretical → SLIP is NEGATIVE.** **Key Conditions (to write in KTU exams):** - $H_s > 6.5 \text{ m}$ (high suction head) - Long, narrow suction pipe - High crank speed **N** (rpm) --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 4) > [!NOTE] > **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — Appears verbatim in 70%+ of KTU ESE numericals** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Theoretical Discharge (Single-Acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | Volume per stroke × strokes/sec | | 2 | Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | Two deliveries per revolution | | 3 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - \text{Slip}$ | m³/s | Always less in normal operation | | 4 | Slip (Normal) | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s | Positive value (loss) | | 5 | Percentage Slip | $\%S = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Board expects % answer | | 6 | Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$ | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | $C_d \leq 1$ (positive slip) | | 7 | Volumetric Efficiency $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol} = C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | % | Often confused with $C_d$ — they are same | | 8 | Work Done/Sec (Single-Acting) | $W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)$ | W | Where $w = \rho g$ | | 9 | Work Done/Sec (Double-Acting) | $W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)$ | W | Same formula, $Q_{th}$ already doubled | | 10 | Frictional Head Loss | $H_f = \dfrac{4f L_s V^2}{2g D_s} + \dfrac{4f L_d V^2}{2g D_d}$ | m | Darcy-Weisbach form | | 11 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{w \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_{man}}{W}$ | % | Most common efficiency asked | | 12 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W}{I.P. \text{ (shaft power)}}$ | % | Ratio to brake power input | | 13 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$ | % | Product of efficiencies | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where Reciprocating Pumps are Unbeatable:** > - **High-pressure applications** (boiler feed pumps, hydraulic presses) — they generate **extremely high pressures** (centrifugal pumps cannot). > - **Viscous fluids** (oil, molasses, chocolate) — positive displacement handles viscosity well. > - **Metering / dosing pumps** in chemical plants — delivery is precisely proportional to speed. > - **Oil wells & mud pumps** in drilling rigs. > - **Fire-fighting pumps** where high head at moderate flow is needed. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Discharge for Single-Acting Pump **Statement to Prove:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ m³/s **Given/Assumed:** - Piston/plunger cross-sectional area = $A$ m² - Stroke length = $L$ m - Crank speed = $N$ rpm - One delivery stroke occurs per revolution in a single-acting pump. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** Step 1 — Volume swept per stroke: The piston displaces a cylindrical column of fluid of cross-section $A$ and length $L$ per stroke. $$V_{stroke} = A \times L \quad \text{[m}^3 \text{ per stroke]}$$ Step 2 — Number of strokes per second: At $N$ rpm, the crank completes $N$ revolutions per minute, hence: $$n_{stroke} = \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[strokes per second]}$$ Step 3 — Theoretical discharge (volume per unit time): $$Q_{th} = V_{stroke} \times n_{stroke}$$ $$Q_{th} = (A \cdot L) \times \left(\frac{N}{60}\right)$$ $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Discharge for Double-Acting Pump **Logic:** In a double-acting pump, the piston has fluid on **both sides**. The piston rod side has slightly less effective area ($A - a$, where $a$ = rod cross-section). Two deliveries occur per revolution. **Step-by-Step:** Step 1 — Effective area on piston side (full face): $A_1 = A$ Step 2 — Effective area on rod side: $A_2 = A - a$ (where $a$ is rod area) Step 3 — Volume per revolution (sum of both strokes): $$V_{rev} = A \cdot L + (A - a) \cdot L = (2A - a) \cdot L$$ Step 4 — Number of revolutions per second: $N/60$ Step 5 — Theoretical discharge: $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Simplified KTU Form (when rod area is neglected, i.e., $a \ll A$):** > $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ m³/s (exactly **double** the single-acting case). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Required Per Second (Single-Acting Pump) > [!NOTE] > **The work done by the pump** = work done to **lift water to delivery height** + work done to **lift water from sump to pump centreline (suction)** + work done **against friction** in both pipes. **Step 1 — Mass of water handled per second (theoretical):** $$m_{th} = \rho \cdot Q_{th} = \rho \cdot \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$ **Step 2 — Work done per second to lift water to delivery head $H_d$:** $$W_d = m_{th} \cdot g \cdot H_d = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H_d$$ **Step 3 — Work done per second for suction head $H_s$:** $$W_s = m_{th} \cdot g \cdot H_s = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H_s$$ **Step 4 — Work done per second against friction head $H_f$:** $$W_f = m_{th} \cdot g \cdot H_f = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H_f$$ **Step 5 — Total work done per second (input to fluid):** $$W = W_s + W_d + W_f$$ $$W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)$$ $$\boxed{W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f) \quad \text{Watts}}$$ **Note:** The frictional head $H_f$ is computed using Darcy-Weisbach: $$H_f = \frac{4 f L_s}{D_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + \frac{4 f L_d}{D_d} \cdot \frac{V_d^2}{2g}$$ Where $V_s$ and $V_d$ are mean velocities in suction and delivery pipes. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Manometric Efficiency **Definition:** Manometric efficiency = ratio of useful work delivered to the fluid to the work actually done by the piston. $$\eta_{man} = \frac{\text{Work done to raise water to actual height}}{\text{Work done by the piston on water}}$$ **Numerator (useful work):** lifts $Q_{act}$ through a *manometric head* $H_{man} = H_s + H_d$ (friction is treated as a loss, not as useful lift). $$W_{useful} = w \cdot Q_{act} \cdot (H_s + H_d)$$ **Denominator (actual work input to fluid):** $$W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)$$ **Substituting:** $$\boxed{\eta_{man} = \frac{Q_{act} \cdot (H_s + H_d)}{Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)} \times 100\%}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Identifying Slip Direction in a Problem | Condition | Likely Slip | Reasoning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Low speed, short suction pipe, low Hₛ | **Positive Slip** (Normal) | NRV lag + leakage dominate | | Moderate speed, normal pipe | **Zero or small positive Slip** | Ideal case | | High speed, long narrow suction pipe, high Hₛ | **Negative Slip** | Liquid column inertia + rebound | | Air leak in suction pipe | **High positive slip** | Suction NRV stays partially open | | Worn-out piston rings | **High positive slip** | Internal leakage past piston | --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **150 mm** and stroke **300 mm**. It runs at **60 rpm** and lifts water against a total head of **20 m** (including friction). Calculate: (a) Theoretical discharge, (b) Actual discharge if slip is **5%**, (c) Work done per second, (d) Manometric efficiency. Take $H_s = 4$ m, $H_d = 14$ m, $H_f = 2$ m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. **Solution:** **Step (a):** $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767$ m² $$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 60}{60} = 0.005301 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 5.30 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step (b):** $Q_{act} = Q_{th}(1 - 0.05) = 0.005301 \times 0.95 = 0.005036$ m³/s **Step (c):** $W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005301 \times (4+14+2)$ $$W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005301 \times 20 = 1039.4 \text{ W} \approx 1.04 \text{ kW}$$ **Step (d):** $$\eta_{man} = \frac{Q_{act}(H_s + H_d)}{Q_{th}(H_s + H_d + H_f)} = \frac{0.005036 \times 18}{0.005301 \times 20} = 0.855 = 85.5\%$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Component-Level Block Architecture of a Reciprocating Pump The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional block architecture** of a typical single-acting reciprocating pump system, showing the **flow of energy and fluid** between components. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[PRIME MOVER
Electric Motor / Engine] -->|Rotational Energy| nodeB[CRANKSHAFT
N rpm] nodeB -->|Crank Pin Rotates| nodeC[CONNECTING ROD
Slider-Crank Mechanism] nodeC -->|Reciprocating Motion| nodeD[PISTON / PLUNGER
Inside Cylinder] nodeD -->|Suction Stroke: Vacuum Created| nodeE[SUCTION NON RETURN VALVE
NRV Inlet] nodeE -->|Liquid Rises| nodeF[SUCTION PIPE
From Sump / Well] nodeF -->|Atmospheric Pressure Pushes| nodeG[WATER SOURCE
Sump / Reservoir] nodeD -->|Delivery Stroke: Pressurization| nodeH[DELIVERY NON RETURN VALVE
NRV Outlet] nodeH -->|Liquid Forced Up| nodeI[DELIVERY PIPE
To Overhead Tank] nodeI -->|Discharged| nodeJ[OVERHEAD TANK / Process] nodeK[AIR VESSEL
Pressure Stabilizer] -.->|Dampens Pulsation| nodeH nodeK -.->|Dampens Pulsation| nodeE ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Solid arrows** = primary energy/fluid flow. - **Dotted arrows** = pressure-pulsation damping via the **air vessel** (mandatory for high-speed pumps). - The two NRVs ensure **unidirectional flow** (no backflow). --- ## 4.2 Process Flow: Suction and Delivery Stroke Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Pump Starts]) --> node1{Piston at TDC} node1 -->|Suction Begins| node2[Suction NRV: OPEN] node2 -->|Delivery NRV: CLOSED| node3[Liquid Rises Through Suction Pipe] node3 -->|Cylinder Fills A.L Volume| node4{Piston at BDC} node4 -->|Delivery Begins| node5[Suction NRV: CLOSED] node5 -->|Delivery NRV: OPEN| node6[Liquid Forced Through Delivery Pipe] node6 -->|Volume A.L Delivered| node7{Piston at TDC Again} node7 --> node1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Energy Pathway (Input → Output) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[INPUT
Shaft Power, P_sh] -->|Minus Mech. Losses| nodeB[WORK ON PISTON
W = w.Qth.H_total] nodeB -->|Minus Friction Losses
w.Qth.Hf| nodeC[USEFUL HYDRAULIC ENERGY
w.Qact.H_man] nodeB -->|Leakage/Slip| nodeD[SLIP LOSS
Volume Loss] nodeC -->|Output to Fluid| nodeE([Discharge to Delivery Tank]) nodeD -.->|Lost| nodeF[Internal Leakage
Past Piston/Valves] ``` **Engineering Interpretation of the Energy Chain:** - $P_{sh} \;\rightarrow\; W$ (mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech}$) - $W \;\rightarrow\; w \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_{man}$ (manometric efficiency $\eta_{man}$) - Overall: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{man}$ # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define *Slip* and *Negative Slip* in a reciprocating pump. Under what conditions does negative slip occur? **Model Answer:** **Slip:** Slip is defined as the **difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge** of a reciprocating pump. $$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$ It arises due to (i) delayed operation of the non-return valves, (ii) leakage past the piston/piston rings, and (iii) the compressibility of dissolved gases/air trapped in the cylinder clearance volume. **Negative Slip:** It is the phenomenon in which the **actual discharge exceeds the theoretical discharge**, i.e., $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$. Under this condition, the calculated slip value becomes negative. **Conditions for Negative Slip:** - Long, narrow, and highly frictional **suction pipe**. - **High crank speed (N)** — typically > 60–80 rpm. - **Suction head $H_s$ greater than ~6.5 m.** - The inertia and rebound of the decelerating liquid column in the suction pipe momentarily exceed the piston velocity, allowing extra liquid to be drawn in and pushed out via the partially-open delivery NRV. > *(3 marks: 1 mark for slip definition + 1 mark for negative slip definition + 1 mark for conditions)* --- > **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2022]** State the formula for theoretical discharge of a single-acting and a double-acting reciprocating pump. What is the role of an air vessel? **Model Answer:** **Theoretical Discharge Formulas:** - **Single-acting:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ m³/s - **Double-acting (neglecting rod area):** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ m³/s **Role of Air Vessel:** An air vessel is a **closed chamber partially filled with air**, fitted either on the suction pipe or delivery pipe near the pump cylinder. Its function is to: 1. **Dampen pressure pulsations** in the pipe flow, making the discharge more uniform. 2. Allow the pump to **run at higher speeds** without column separation (cavitation-like break) in the suction pipe. 3. **Reduce the work required** to accelerate the liquid column each stroke (energy saving). > *(3 marks: 1 mark each for two formulas + 1 mark for air vessel role)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]** > A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of **200 mm** and a stroke of **400 mm**. It runs at **50 rpm** and delivers water against a suction head of **5 m** and a delivery head of **25 m**. The suction pipe is **10 m long** with diameter **100 mm**, and the delivery pipe is **30 m long** with diameter **100 mm**. Take $f = 0.005$, and neglect minor losses. > > **(a)** Calculate the **theoretical discharge** and the **work done per second** by the pump. (7 marks) > > **(b)** If the actual discharge is found to be **10% less** than the theoretical, find the **percentage slip**, **coefficient of discharge**, and **manometric efficiency**. (7 marks) #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Theoretical Discharge & Work Done per Second **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of piston:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.200)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical Discharge (Single-Acting):** $$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{0.03142 \times 0.400 \times 50}{60}$$ $$Q_{th} = \frac{0.6283}{60} = 0.01047 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 10.47 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 3 — Mean velocity in pipes (using $Q_{th}$):** $$V = \frac{Q_{th}}{A_{pipe}} = \frac{0.01047}{\frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.100)^2} = \frac{0.01047}{0.007854} = 1.333 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Frictional head loss (Darcy-Weisbach):** $$H_f = \frac{4f L_s V^2}{2g D_s} + \frac{4f L_d V^2}{2g D_d}$$ $$H_f = \frac{4 \times 0.005 \times (10 + 30) \times (1.333)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.100}$$ $$H_f = \frac{4 \times 0.005 \times 40 \times 1.777}{1.962} = \frac{1.422}{1.962} = 0.725 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Work done per second by the pump:** $$W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)$$ $$W = 9810 \times 0.01047 \times (5 + 25 + 0.725)$$ $$W = 9810 \times 0.01047 \times 30.725 = 3155.5 \text{ W} \approx 3.156 \text{ kW}$$ > **[7 marks: 2 for Qth, 2 for V, 2 for Hf, 1 for W]** --- ##### Part (b) — Percentage Slip, $C_d$, and $\eta_{man}$ **Step 1 — Actual Discharge (10% less than theoretical):** $$Q_{act} = 0.90 \times Q_{th} = 0.90 \times 0.01047 = 0.009423 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Percentage Slip:** $$\%S = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{0.01047 - 0.009423}{0.01047} \times 100 = 10.0\%$$ **Step 3 — Coefficient of Discharge:** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 0.90 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$ **Step 4 — Manometric Efficiency:** $$\eta_{man} = \frac{Q_{act} \cdot (H_s + H_d)}{Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{man} = \frac{0.009423 \times 30}{0.01047 \times 30.725} \times 100 = \frac{0.2827}{0.3217} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{man} \approx 87.86\%}$$ > **[7 marks: 2 for Qact, 1 for %slip, 1 for Cd, 3 for ηman]** --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** > A double-acting reciprocating pump, with a piston diameter of **150 mm** and rod diameter of **30 mm**, has a stroke of **250 mm**. The pump runs at **40 rpm** and lifts water through a suction head of **4 m** and a delivery head of **20 m**. > > **(a)** Determine the **theoretical discharge** and the **slip percentage** if the actual discharge measured is **4.2 L/s**. (7 marks) > > **(b)** Compute the **work done per second by the pump** and the **manometric efficiency**. Take $H_f = 1.5$ m. (7 marks) #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Theoretical Discharge & Slip % **Step 1 — Piston area:** $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767$ m² **Step 2 — Rod area:** $a = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4}$ m² **Step 3 — Effective area on rod side:** $A - a = 0.01767 - 0.000707 = 0.01696$ m² **Step 4 — Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting, with rod):** $$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{(0.01767 \times 2 - 0.000707) \times 0.250 \times 40}{60}$$ $$Q_{th} = \frac{(0.03464) \times 0.250 \times 40}{60} = \frac{0.3464}{60} = 0.005773 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 5 — Actual Discharge:** $Q_{act} = 4.2 \text{ L/s} = 0.0042$ m³/s **Step 6 — Slip and Percentage Slip:** $$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.005773 - 0.0042 = 0.001573 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\%S = \frac{0.001573}{0.005773} \times 100 = 27.25\%$$ > **[7 marks: 2 for A and a, 3 for Qth, 1 for slip, 1 for %slip]** --- ##### Part (b) — Work Done per Second & Manometric Efficiency **Step 1 — Work Done per Second:** $$W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f) = 9810 \times 0.005773 \times (4 + 20 + 1.5)$$ $$W = 9810 \times 0.005773 \times 25.5 = 1444.1 \text{ W} \approx 1.444 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Manometric Efficiency:** $$\eta_{man} = \frac{Q_{act} \cdot (H_s + H_d)}{Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{man} = \frac{0.0042 \times 24}{0.005773 \times 25.5} \times 100 = \frac{0.1008}{0.1472} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{man} \approx 68.48\%}$$ > **[7 marks: 3 for W, 4 for ηman]** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks per Question:** > > 1. **Forgetting to use $Q_{th}$ in the work-done formula** (not $Q_{act}$!). The work done by the piston is calculated on the *theoretical* swept volume. Substituting $Q_{act}$ here is a very common error → marks deducted. > > 2. **Using $H_{man}$ in work-done formula** (not the total head). Friction head $H_f$ MUST be added. Board answers like "$W = w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot H_{man}$" lose full marks for the work part. > > 3. **Confusing $C_d$ with $C_v$ of orifices** — they are unrelated. $C_d$ in a pump is **volumetric efficiency**, defined purely as $Q_{act}/Q_{th}$. > > 4. **In double-acting formula, omitting rod area** — board full-marks require the term $(2A - a)$. Writing $2ALN/60$ directly will lose 1–2 marks if rod area is given. > > 5. **Not writing units** in the final answer → -0.5 mark deduction. Always state **m³/s, m, %, W or kW**. > > 6. **Stating "negative slip = zero slip"** — they are different. Zero slip is the borderline case; negative slip means $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must be at your fingertips before the exam):** - ✅ A reciprocating pump is a **positive displacement pump** with **pulsating discharge**. - ✅ **Single-acting:** 1 delivery/rev → $Q_{th} = ALN/60$. - ✅ **Double-acting (with rod):** $Q_{th} = (2A - a)LN/60$; (without rod) $= 2ALN/60$. - ✅ **Slip** = $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ (positive = loss; negative = gain). - ✅ **% Slip** = $(Q_{th} - Q_{act})/Q_{th} \times 100$. - ✅ **$C_d$ = $\eta_{vol}$ = $Q_{act}/Q_{th}$** (numerically equal, both dimensionless). - ✅ **Work done/sec** = $w \cdot Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)$ (use $Q_{th}$, not $Q_{act}$). - ✅ **Manometric efficiency** = $Q_{act} \cdot (H_s + H_d) / [Q_{th} \cdot (H_s + H_d + H_f)]$. - ✅ **Negative slip** requires: long suction pipe + narrow bore + high $N$ + $H_s > 6.5$ m. - ✅ **Air vessel** dampens pulsation and permits higher safe speeds. - ✅ Standard constants: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $w_{water} = 9810$ N/m³. - ✅ **Overall efficiency** = $\eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$. - ✅ **Indicator diagram** (mentioned in module context) plots **pressure vs. piston displacement** — used to derive mean effective pressure and diagnose pump health. - ✅ **Friction head** $H_f$ is computed using **Darcy-Weisbach**: $\dfrac{4fLV^2}{2gD}$ summed for suction + delivery. > 🎯 **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If the problem mentions "no air vessel" or "long pipe," assume the **friction head $H_f$ is non-zero and must be calculated**. If $H_f$ is not given, you must compute it from pipe length, diameter, velocity, and friction factor $f$.
